## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**Define Web Mining. List and briefly explain the three main categories of Web Mining.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Web Mining** is the process of applying data mining, information retrieval, and machine learning techniques to extract useful patterns, knowledge, and previously unknown information from web data (content, structure, and usage).
>
> **Three Categories:**
> 1. **Web Content Mining (WCM):** Extracts information from the *content* of web pages — text, images, video — using NLP, text mining, and image processing.
> 2. **Web Structure Mining (WSM):** Discovers patterns from the *hyperlink structure* of the web using algorithms like PageRank and HITS to find authoritative pages and communities.
> 3. **Web Usage Mining (WUM):** Analyzes *user interaction logs* (server logs, clickstreams) to discover browsing patterns, personalize experiences, and build recommendation systems.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three categories with one-line explanation each.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**What is the KDD process? List its main steps.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **KDD (Knowledge Discovery in Databases)** is the overall, multi-step process of converting raw, low-level data into high-level, useful knowledge. It is an *iterative* and *interactive* process in which data mining is a single (but core) step.
>
> **Main Steps:**
> 1. Data Selection
> 2. Data Cleaning & Preprocessing
> 3. Data Integration
> 4. Data Transformation
> 5. **Data Mining** (the core intelligent step)
> 6. Pattern Evaluation
> 7. Knowledge Presentation

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing the steps with brief explanation.)*

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For 14-mark questions, the KTU valuation key **strictly enforces** sub-part distribution. Part (a) = 7 marks, Part (b) = 7 marks. If you write a 14-mark monolithic answer with no sub-part headings, you will **lose 1–2 marks** even if the content is correct. **Always use sub-headings (a) and (b).**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Web Mining Architecture and IR Foundations**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-tier architecture of Web Mining** (Source Layer → Mining Layer → Application Layer). Discuss the role of each tier in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **TF-IDF** in detail. Compute the TF-IDF weight of the term **"data"** in a corpus of 5 documents where the term "data" appears in 2 documents, and in one specific document, it appears 3 times out of 30 total words. Use $\log_e$ for IDF.

#### Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks

The **Three-Tier Architecture of Web Mining** is a logical framework that organizes the mining process into distinct, modular layers.

1. **Source Layer (Bottom Tier)** — Provides the raw web data:
   - **Web Content:** HTML pages, PDFs, images, videos.
   - **Web Structure:** Hyperlink graph, DOM tree.
   - **Web Usage:** Server logs, proxy logs, cookies, clickstreams, query logs.
   - *Role:* Acts as the *data reservoir*.

2. **Mining Layer (Middle Tier)** — The intelligent core:
   - **Preprocessing:** Cleaning, integration, transformation (e.g., tokenization, stop-word removal, stemming, building inverted index).
   - **Pattern Discovery Engine:** Applies algorithms like clustering (k-means), classification (SVM, Naive Bayes), association (Apriori, FP-Growth), and ranking (PageRank, HITS).
   - **Pattern Evaluation:** Uses metrics like support, confidence, lift, precision, recall, F-score.
   - *Role:* Transforms data → patterns → knowledge.

3. **Application Layer (Top Tier)** — The user-facing layer:
   - **Search engines, recommendation systems, e-commerce personalization, sentiment dashboards, fraud detection, market intelligence.**
   - *Role:* Delivers business value.

*(3 marks for block diagram description, 4 marks for tier-wise role explanation.)*

#### Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks

**TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency)** is a statistical weight that reflects how important a word is to a document within a collection (corpus).

**Formula:**

$$\text{TF-IDF}(t, d) = \text{TF}(t, d) \times \text{IDF}(t)$$

Where:

$$\text{TF}(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{IDF}(t) = \ln\left(\frac{N}{\text{df}(t) + 1}\right)$$

**Given Data:**
- $N = 5$ (total documents)
- $\text{df}(\text{"data"}) = 2$ (appears in 2 documents)
- $f_{\text{data}, d} = 3$ (appears 3 times in our document)
- Total words in $d$ = 30

**Step 1 — Compute TF:**

$$\text{TF}(\text{data}, d) = \frac{3}{30} = 0.1$$

**Step 2 — Compute IDF:**

$$\text{IDF}(\text{data}) = \ln\left(\frac{5}{2 + 1}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{5}{3}\right) \approx 0.5108$$

**Step 3 — Compute TF-IDF:**

$$\text{TF-IDF}(\text{data}, d) = 0.1 \times 0.5108 = 0.05108$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A relatively *low* TF-IDF (≈ 0.051) indicates that the term "data" is not strongly discriminative for this document in this small corpus — it is too common across the corpus to be a strong identifier.

*(2 marks for formula, 3 marks for substitution, 1 mark for final answer with unit interpretation, 1 mark for explanation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — PageRank and Web Structure Mining**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **PageRank algorithm** in detail. State its mathematical formula, explain each term, and discuss the significance of the **damping factor $d$**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider 4 web pages $A, B, C, D$ with the following link structure:
- $A \rightarrow B, C$
- $B \rightarrow C$
- $C \rightarrow A$
- $D \rightarrow C$

Using damping factor $d = 0.85$ and $N = 4$, compute **one full iteration** of PageRank starting from initial rank $1/4$ for each page.

#### Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks

**PageRank** is a link-analysis algorithm developed by **Larry Page and Sergey Brin** (founders of Google) at Stanford in 1998. It assigns a numerical weighting (a probability between 0 and 1) to every web page, indicating its *relative importance* within the web graph.

**Mathematical Formula:**

$$\text{PR}(p_i) = \frac{1 - d}{N} + d \sum_{p_j \in B(p_i)} \frac{\text{PR}(p_j)}{L(p_j)}$$

**Term-by-Term Explanation:**

- $\text{PR}(p_i)$ — PageRank of page $p_i$ (a scalar probability).
- $d$ — **Damping factor**, usually set to **0.85**.
- $N$ — Total number of pages in the web graph.
- $B(p_i)$ — Set of pages that *link to* $p_i$ (in-neighbours).
- $L(p_j)$ — Number of *outbound links* from page $p_j$.
- $(1-d)/N$ — The *teleportation* or *random jump* term, modelling a surfer who tires of clicking and jumps to a random page.

**Significance of the Damping Factor $d$:**

- Models the behaviour of a *random web surfer*.
- With probability $d$, the surfer clicks a random link on the current page.
- With probability $(1-d)$, the surfer abandons the current path and jumps to a uniformly random page in the corpus.
- $d = 0.85$ was empirically chosen by Brin & Page to balance convergence speed and meaningful ranking.
- The damping factor **guarantees convergence** even in cycles and dead-ends (sink nodes), and ensures the resulting values form a **proper probability distribution** (sum = 1).

*(3 marks for formula + terms, 2 marks for damping factor explanation, 2 marks for random surfer model and convergence.)*

#### Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify the structure:**

| Page $p$ | Outbound Links $L(p)$ |
|---|---|
| $A$ | 2 (to B, C) |
| $B$ | 1 (to C) |
| $C$ | 1 (to A) |
| $D$ | 1 (to C) |

**Step 2 — Initial PageRank:**

$$\text{PR}_0(A) = \text{PR}_0(B) = \text{PR}_0(C) = \text{PR}_0(D) = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

**Step 3 — Compute Iteration 1:**

For page $A$ (linked from $C$ only):

$$\text{PR}_1(A) = \frac{1 - 0.85}{4} + 0.85 \times \frac{\text{PR}_0(C)}{L(C)} = \frac{0.15}{4} + 0.85 \times \frac{0.25}{1}$$

$$\text{PR}_1(A) = 0.0375 + 0.2125 = 0.2500$$

For page $B$ (linked from $A$ only):

$$\text{PR}_1(B) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \times \frac{\text{PR}_0(A)}{L(A)} = 0.0375 + 0.85 \times \frac{0.25}{2} = 0.0375 + 0.1063 = 0.1438$$

For page $C$ (linked from $A$, $B$, $D$):

$$\text{PR}_1(C) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \times \left(\frac{\text{PR}_0(A)}{L(A)} + \frac{\text{PR}_0(B)}{L(B)} + \frac{\text{PR}_0(D)}{L(D)}\right)$$

$$\text{PR}_1(C) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \times (0.125 + 0.25 + 0.25) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \times 0.625 = 0.0375 + 0.5313 = 0.5688$$

For page $D$ (no inbound links):

$$\text{PR}_1(D) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \times 0 = 0.0375$$

**Step 4 — Verify probability sum:**

$$0.2500 + 0.1438 + 0.5688 + 0.0375 = 1.0001 \approx 1.0 \;\; (\text{✓ probability sum preserved})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Page $C$ is the **most authoritative** page in this toy web (highest PageRank 0.5688) because it is the most linked-to. Page $D$ is the *least* authoritative (0.0375) — it has no inbound links. This matches intuition.

*(2 marks for structure setup, 3 marks for computation, 1 mark for probability sum check, 1 mark for interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Data Mining with KDD:** Data Mining is *one step* of the KDD process, not the whole process. Saying "KDD = Data Mining" is a **2-mark deduction** trap.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1" in IDF:** Always add the smoothing term; missing it loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Saying Lift > 1 means "strong" rule:** Lift > 1 means *positive correlation*; the rule is *strong* only if **support × confidence** is also high.
> 4. **PageRank formula without the random jump term $(1-d)/N$:** Examiners specifically check this. Omitting it is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 5. **Forgetting to verify probability sum = 1 in PageRank iterations:** Always state this check; it's a free 1 mark.
> 6. **Cosine similarity: using Euclidean distance instead:** Make sure the formula is $\cos\theta$, not L2 norm.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Web Mining** = automated extraction of patterns from web data (content, structure, usage).
- [ ] **Three Categories:** WCM, WSM, WUM — know at least **2 algorithms** for each.
- [ ] **KDD ≠ Data Mining:** KDD is the *process*; Data Mining is a *step* inside it.
- [ ] **KDD Steps:** Selection → Cleaning → Integration → Transformation → **Mining** → Evaluation → Presentation.
- [ ] **TF-IDF = TF × IDF**, with IDF = $\ln(N / (\text{df}+1))$.
- [ ] **Cosine Similarity** measures the angle (not distance) between two document vectors.
- [ ] **Support, Confidence, Lift** — the trio of association rule mining metrics.
- [ ] **Lift > 1** → positive, **= 1** → independent, **< 1** → negative correlation.
- [ ] **PageRank** = Random surfer model with damping factor $d = 0.85$.
- [ ] **PageRank formula must always include the random jump term** $(1-d)/N$.
- [ ] Sum of PageRanks = **1** (always verify in computations).
- [ ] **HITS** identifies two node types: **Hubs** (good recommenders) and **Authorities** (good content).
- [ ] **Web 1.0** = Static read-only; **Web 2.0** = Interactive social; **Web 3.0** = Semantic + AI.
- [ ] **Web Usage Mining** data sources: server logs, proxy logs, cookies, clickstreams.
- [ ] **Challenges of Web Mining:** Scale, heterogeneity, dynamism, noise, distributed nature, semi-structured data.
- [ ] **Difference: Data Mining vs Web Mining** — DM operates on structured databases; WM operates on unstructured/semi-structured web data.
- [ ] **Three-tier Architecture:** Source → Mining → Application — memorize each tier's role.
- [ ] **Inverted Index** is the core data structure for IR-based web search.
- [ ] **Common real-world applications:** Google Search (PageRank), Amazon recommendations (WUM), Twitter sentiment (WCM), fraud detection (WUM).
- [ ] **Always write sub-headings (a) and (b)** in 14-mark answers — modular presentation earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Association Rule Mining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT636 - INTRODUCTION TO WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Association Rule Mining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Association Rule Mining (ARM)** is a fundamental unsupervised data mining and web mining technique used to uncover interesting relationships, patterns, co-occurrences, or hidden causal structures among items in large-scale transactional, relational, or click-stream datasets. Formally introduced by **Rakesh Agrawal, Tomasz Imieliński, and Arun Swami (1993)** in the context of Market-Basket databases, association rule mining discovers all rules of the form $X \Rightarrow Y$ where $X$ and $Y$ are disjoint, non-empty itemsets $(X \cap Y = \emptyset)$ drawn from a universal item universe $\mathcal{I}$, such that the rule satisfies user-defined minimum support and minimum confidence thresholds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Association Rule Mining is the **backbone algorithm** of Web Usage Mining, Recommender Systems, and Click-Stream Analysis. KTU examiners test the **Apriori algorithm**, the **FP-Growth algorithm**, and the formal metrics **Support, Confidence, Lift, and Conviction** almost every cycle.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Supermarket Basket" Intuition

Imagine you are the manager of a large supermarket chain with millions of Point-of-Sale (POS) transactions pouring in daily. You want to know: *"If a customer buys bread, what other items do they tend to buy together?"* You suspect that customers who buy **bread** also buy **butter**, but you need **proof** from data — not guesswork.

Association Rule Mining is the automated, mathematical mechanism that scans the entire database and answers such questions with statistical strength. Extending this analogy to the **web**, every "item" becomes a *page*, a *product*, a *keyword*, or a *URL*, and every "transaction" becomes a *user session*, a *search log*, or a *click stream*. The web basket is just a digital supermarket.

### 1.3 Core Terminology

| Term | Plain English Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Item** | A single entity (e.g., `Bread`) |
| **Itemset** | A collection of one or more items (e.g., `{Bread, Butter}`) |
| **k-Itemset** | An itemset containing exactly **k** items |
| **Transaction** | A single record of items bought/visited together |
| **Database D** | The complete set of N transactions |
| **Frequent Itemset** | An itemset whose support ≥ `min_sup` |
| **Strong Rule** | A rule whose confidence ≥ `min_conf` |

> [!NOTE]
> The two **user-defined thresholds** — *minimum support* and *minimum confidence* — are the **control knobs** of the entire mining process. Changing them radically alters the volume and quality of rules discovered.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Rule Representation

A rule is an implication of the form:

$$X \Rightarrow Y$$

where $X, Y \subseteq \mathcal{I}$, $X \neq \emptyset$, $Y \neq \emptyset$, and $X \cap Y = \emptyset$. The set $X$ is called the **antecedent** (LHS — Left Hand Side) and $Y$ is the **consequent** (RHS — Right Hand Side).

## 2.2 The Four Cardinal Metrics of ARM

### 2.2.1 Support (`sup`)
The **frequency** of occurrence of an itemset in the entire database. It measures *how popular* the item combination is.

$$\text{Support}(X) = \frac{\text{Number of transactions containing } X}{\text{Total number of transactions}} = \frac{\text{count}(X)}{N}$$

### 2.2.2 Frequent Itemset Generation Threshold
An itemset $X$ is **frequent** if and only if:

$$\text{Support}(X) \geq \text{min\_sup}$$

### 2.2.3 Confidence (`conf`)
The **strength** of implication. It is the conditional probability $P(Y \mid X)$ — i.e., given a customer bought $X$, how often did they also buy $Y$?

$$\text{Confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{Support}(X \cup Y)}{\text{Support}(X)} = \frac{\text{count}(X \cup Y)}{\text{count}(X)}$$

### 2.2.4 Lift (Interestingness)
Lift measures how much more likely $Y$ is purchased **when $X$ is purchased**, compared to its baseline popularity. Lift is the true test of *correlation vs. coincidence*.

$$\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{Support}(X \cup Y)}{\text{Support}(X) \cdot \text{Support}(Y)} = \frac{\text{Confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y)}{\text{Support}(Y)}$$

- $\text{Lift} > 1$ → **Positive correlation** (X *promotes* Y)
- $\text{Lift} = 1$ → **Independence** (no association)
- $\text{Lift} < 1$ → **Negative correlation** (X *inhibits* Y)

### 2.2.5 Conviction
An asymmetric metric that overcomes confidence's inability to account for the consequent's baseline frequency.

$$\text{Conviction}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{1 - \text{Support}(Y)}{1 - \text{Confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y)}$$

### 2.2.6 The Apriori Property (Downward Closure)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Apriori Property (Cornerstone of the Algorithm):**
> *All non-empty subsets of a frequent itemset must themselves be frequent.* Equivalently, if any subset of an itemset is infrequent, the itemset itself **cannot** be frequent. Formally, if $\text{Support}(A) < \text{min\_sup}$, then for any superset $B \supseteq A$, $\text{Support}(B) < \text{min\_sup}$.

This property enables **pruning** of the candidate search space, which is the entire reason the Apriori algorithm is computationally tractable.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Range / Interpretation | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Support $X$ | $\dfrac{\text{count}(X)}{N}$ | $[0, 1]$; higher = more frequent | Filter out rare itemsets |
| Confidence $X \Rightarrow Y$ | $\dfrac{\text{Support}(X \cup Y)}{\text{Support}(X)}$ | $[0, 1]$; higher = stronger rule | Rank rule quality |
| Lift $X \Rightarrow Y$ | $\dfrac{\text{Confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y)}{\text{Support}(Y)}$ | $[0, \infty)$; $> 1$ means true correlation | Distinguish cause from coincidence |
| Conviction $X \Rightarrow Y$ | $\dfrac{1 - \text{Support}(Y)}{1 - \text{Confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y)}$ | $[0, \infty)$; $\infty$ for logical implication | Detect rule directionality |
| Apriori Pruning | If $A \subset B$ and $\text{sup}(A) < \text{min\_sup}$, then $\text{sup}(B) < \text{min\_sup}$ | Boolean (prune / keep) | Exponentially reduces search space |

> **Engineering Utility:** In production web-mining systems, ARM powers Amazon's *Frequently Bought Together*, Netflix's co-view clustering, Google AdWords contextual matching, and intrusion detection rule generation from network logs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Apriori Algorithm (Full Hand Trace)

We will mine a complete database with **min_support = 60 %** and **min_confidence = 80 %** so that every computation step is visible.

### 3.1.1 Transaction Database

| TID | Items Purchased |
|---|---|
| T1 | Bread, Butter, Milk |
| T2 | Bread, Butter |
| T3 | Butter, Milk |
| T4 | Bread, Milk |
| T5 | Bread, Butter, Milk |

**Total Transactions:** $N = 5$

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Candidate 1-Itemset Generation $\mathcal{C}_1$

We scan the database once and compute the support of every single item. Support count and fractional support:

$$\text{Support}(\text{Bread}) = \frac{\text{count}(\text{Bread})}{5} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80$$

$$\text{Support}(\text{Butter}) = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80$$

$$\text{Support}(\text{Milk}) = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Frequent 1-Itemset $\mathcal{L}_1$

Apply the threshold: $\text{min\_sup} = 0.60$.

Since $0.80 \geq 0.60$ for all three items, **all survive**:

$$\mathcal{L}_1 = \bigl\{ \{\text{Bread}\},\ \{\text{Butter}\},\ \{\text{Milk}\} \bigr\}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Candidate 2-Itemset Generation $\mathcal{C}_2$ (Self-Join)

We self-join $\mathcal{L}_1$ with itself, producing $\binom{3}{2} = 3$ candidates:

$$\mathcal{C}_2 = \bigl\{ \{\text{Bread, Butter}\},\ \{\text{Bread, Milk}\},\ \{\text{Butter, Milk}\} \bigr\}$$

**Apriori Pruning Check:** Every subset of each candidate is a 1-itemset from $\mathcal{L}_1$ (Bread, Butter, Milk) — all frequent. Therefore **no candidate is pruned** before the database scan.

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Compute Support of $\mathcal{C}_2$

$$\text{Support}(\text{Bread, Butter}) = \frac{\text{count}(\{T1, T2, T5\})}{5} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60$$

$$\text{Support}(\text{Bread, Milk}) = \frac{\text{count}(\{T1, T4, T5\})}{5} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60$$

$$\text{Support}(\text{Butter, Milk}) = \frac{\text{count}(\{T1, T3, T5\})}{5} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Frequent 2-Itemset $\mathcal{L}_2$

All three itemsets meet $\geq 0.60$:

$$\mathcal{L}_2 = \bigl\{ \{\text{Bread, Butter}\},\ \{\text{Bread, Milk}\},\ \{\text{Butter, Milk}\} \bigr\}$$

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Candidate 3-Itemset $\mathcal{C}_3$ (Join + Prune)

Self-join $\mathcal{L}_2$ on common prefixes. The only possible join is:

$$\mathcal{C}_3 = \bigl\{ \{\text{Bread, Butter, Milk}\} \bigr\}$$

**Pruning Check:** Its 2-item subsets are {Bread, Butter}, {Bread, Milk}, {Butter, Milk} — all in $\mathcal{L}_2$. The candidate **survives**.

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Support of $\mathcal{C}_3$

$$\text{Support}(\text{Bread, Butter, Milk}) = \frac{\text{count}(\{T1, T5\})}{5} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.40$$

### 3.1.9 Step 8 — Frequent 3-Itemset $\mathcal{L}_3$

Since $0.40 < 0.60$, the candidate **fails the threshold**. Therefore:

$$\mathcal{L}_3 = \emptyset \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{Algorithm Halts}$$

### 3.1.10 Final Frequent Itemsets

$$\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{L}_1 \cup \mathcal{L}_2 = \{\text{Bread}\},\ \{\text{Butter}\},\ \{\text{Milk}\},\ \{\text{Bread, Butter}\},\ \{\text{Bread, Milk}\},\ \{\text{Butter, Milk}\}$$

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Rule Generation from $\mathcal{L}_2$

For every frequent itemset $l$, generate all non-empty proper subsets $s$ and form rules $s \Rightarrow (l - s)$. Compute confidence for each.

### Rule 1: {Bread, Butter} → {Milk}

$$\text{Confidence} = \frac{\text{Support}(\text{Bread, Butter, Milk})}{\text{Support}(\text{Bread, Butter})} = \frac{0.40}{0.60} = 0.667$$

$0.667 < 0.80$ → **Rule Rejected**

### Rule 2: {Bread, Milk} → {Butter}

$$\text{Confidence} = \frac{0.40}{0.60} = 0.667$$

**Rule Rejected**

### Rule 3: {Butter, Milk} → {Bread}

$$\text{Confidence} = \frac{0.40}{0.60} = 0.667$$

**Rule Rejected**

### Rule 4: {Bread} → {Butter}

$$\text{Confidence} = \frac{\text{Support}(\text{Bread, Butter})}{\text{Support}(\text{Bread})} = \frac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75$$

$0.75 < 0.80$ → **Rule Rejected**

### Rule 5: {Bread} → {Milk}

$$\text{Confidence} = \frac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75$$

**Rule Rejected**

### Rule 6: {Butter} → {Milk}

$$\text{Confidence} = \frac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75$$

**Rule Rejected**

### Conclusion of Rule Generation

> **No rule meets `min_confidence = 80 %`.** The system returned an empty strong-rule set. (This is pedagogically useful: it shows that **frequent itemsets do not automatically produce strong rules**, and a stricter confidence threshold, e.g. 70 %, would have yielded several strong rules.)

---

## 3.3 Lift & Conviction Verification (Analytical Matrix)

| Rule | Support | Confidence | Lift | Conviction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bread → Butter | 0.60 | 0.75 | $\dfrac{0.75}{0.80} = 0.9375$ | $\dfrac{1-0.80}{1-0.75} = 0.80$ |
| Butter → Milk | 0.60 | 0.75 | $\dfrac{0.75}{0.80} = 0.9375$ | $\dfrac{1-0.80}{1-0.75} = 0.80$ |
| Bread, Butter → Milk | 0.40 | 0.667 | $\dfrac{0.667}{0.80} = 0.833$ | $\dfrac{1-0.80}{1-0.667} = 0.60$ |

All lifts are $< 1$, indicating **weak negative correlation** in this dataset — items co-occur but do not promote each other strongly.

---

## 3.4 FP-Growth Algorithm — Conceptual Walkthrough

Unlike Apriori, which generates-and-tests candidates, **FP-Growth (Frequent Pattern Growth)** uses a compressed prefix-tree data structure called the **FP-Tree** to mine frequent patterns in **only two database scans**.

**Phase 1 — Build FP-Tree:**
1. Scan DB once: compute support of each 1-itemset. Drop infrequent items.
2. Order remaining items by descending support (header table).
3. Scan DB a second time: insert each transaction's sorted frequent items as a branch, sharing prefixes.

**Phase 2 — Mine FP-Tree Recursively:**
For each item (bottom of header table):
1. Build its **conditional pattern base** (set of prefix paths leading to it).
2. Construct **conditional FP-Tree** from this base (with `min_sup` threshold).
3. Mine the conditional tree recursively to grow patterns.

**FP-Growth advantages over Apriori:**
- **No candidate generation** → memory-efficient.
- **Compressed representation** → at most as large as the original database.
- **Two scans only** → I/O efficient.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End ARM Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Transactional Web Log] --> B[Data Cleaning and Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Transaction Database D]
    C --> D[Set User Thresholds]
    D --> E[Apply Frequent Itemset Mining]
    E --> F{Apriori Algorithm}
    F --> G[Candidate Generation Ck]
    G --> H[Database Scan Support Counting]
    H --> I{Apriori Pruning}
    I -->|Prune Infrequent| J[Frequent Itemset Lk]
    I -->|Retain| J
    J --> K{All k exhausted?}
    K -->|No| F
    K -->|Yes| L[Rule Generation from L]
    L --> M[Compute Confidence per Rule]
    M --> N{Confidence >= min_conf}
    N -->|Yes| O[Output Strong Association Rules]
    N -->|No| P[Discard Weak Rule]
```

## 4.2 FP-Growth Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Transaction Database] --> B[Scan One: Item Frequency Count]
    B --> C[Discard Items below min_sup]
    C --> D[Sort Items in Descending Frequency Order]
    D --> E[Scan Two: Build Global FP Tree]
    E --> F[Build Header Table with Links]
    F --> G[Select Bottom Item from Header]
    G --> H[Extract Conditional Pattern Base]
    H --> I[Build Conditional FP Tree]
    I --> J{Tree Has Single Path?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Enumerate All Combinations of Path]
    J -->|No| G
    K --> L[Output Complete Frequent Patterns]
```

## 4.3 Apriori Level-by-Level Iteration (Conceptual Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DB[(Transaction DB)] --> C1[Candidate 1 Itemsets C1]
    C1 --> L1[Frequent 1 Itemsets L1]
    L1 --> C2[Candidate 2 Itemsets C2 via Self Join]
    C2 --> P1{Apriori Property Prune}
    P1 -->|Keep Survivors| SC2[Pruned C2]
    SC2 --> L2[Frequent 2 Itemsets L2]
    L2 --> C3[Candidate 3 Itemsets C3]
    C3 --> P2{Prune via L2}
    P2 --> L3[Frequent 3 Itemsets L3]
    L3 --> STOP{Empty Set?}
    STOP -->|Yes| END[STOP: Output Union of L1 to L3]
    STOP -->|No| C4[Generate C4]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> Define **Association Rule Mining**. With a suitable example, explain the concepts of **support** and **confidence** of an association rule.

**Model Answer:**

Association Rule Mining is a data mining technique that discovers interesting co-occurrence relationships among items in large transactional databases, expressed as implications of the form $X \Rightarrow Y$, where $X$ and $Y$ are disjoint non-empty itemsets.

*Example database:* Suppose 1000 transactions are analysed, and the itemset `{Bread, Butter}` appears in 300 of them, while `Bread` alone appears in 500. The rule **Bread ⇒ Butter** has:

$$\text{Support} = \frac{300}{1000} = 0.30 \quad (30\%)$$

$$\text{Confidence} = \frac{300}{500} = 0.60 \quad (60\%)$$

Support measures *frequency* of the rule in the database, while confidence measures the *conditional probability* of the consequent given the antecedent.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> State and explain the **Apriori Property** used in frequent itemset mining. Why is it important?

**Model Answer:**

**Apriori Property (Downward Closure):** *All non-empty subsets of a frequent itemset must themselves be frequent. Equivalently, any superset of an infrequent itemset is necessarily infrequent.*

**Importance:** It allows the algorithm to **prune the candidate search space exponentially**. Without this property, the algorithm would have to test $\binom{|\mathcal{I}|}{k}$ candidates at level $k$, which is computationally infeasible. By exploiting this property, Apriori reduces candidates by orders of magnitude, making mining tractable on real-world web-scale datasets.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, ESE Model Paper]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the following transaction database with **min_support = 40 %** and **min_confidence = 60 %**.
>
> | TID | Items |
> |---|---|
> | T1 | I1, I2, I3 |
> | T2 | I2, I3, I4 |
> | T3 | I2, I4 |
> | T4 | I1, I2, I4 |
> | T5 | I1, I3 |
> | T6 | I2, I3 |
>
> Apply the **Apriori algorithm** to find all frequent itemsets.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** From the frequent itemsets obtained, generate **all strong association rules** and comment on which are most interesting.

### Model Solution for Q3(a):

**Step 1 — $\mathcal{C}_1$ and $\mathcal{L}_1$:**

Compute support count of each item (Total $N = 6$):

$$\text{count}(I_1) = 3 \Rightarrow \text{sup} = 0.50$$
$$\text{count}(I_2) = 5 \Rightarrow \text{sup} = 0.833$$
$$\text{count}(I_3) = 4 \Rightarrow \text{sup} = 0.667$$
$$\text{count}(I_4) = 3 \Rightarrow \text{sup} = 0.50$$

Since `min_sup = 0.40`, all items pass: $\mathcal{L}_1 = \{\{I_1\}, \{I_2\}, \{I_3\}, \{I_4\}\}$.

**Step 2 — $\mathcal{C}_2$ and $\mathcal{L}_2$:**

$$\text{count}(I_1, I_2) = 2 \Rightarrow \text{sup} = 0.333$$
$$\text{count}(I_1, I_3) = 2 \Rightarrow \text{sup} = 0.333$$
$$\text{count}(I_1, I_4) = 1 \Rightarrow \text{sup} = 0.167$$
$$\text{count}(I_2, I_3) = 3 \Rightarrow \text{sup} = 0.50$$
$$\text{count}(I_2, I_4) = 3 \Rightarrow \text{sup} = 0.50$$
$$\text{count}(I_3, I_4) = 1 \Rightarrow \text{sup} = 0.167$$

Apply threshold $\geq 0.40$:

$$\mathcal{L}_2 = \{\{I_2, I_3\},\ \{I_2, I_4\}\}$$

**Step 3 — $\mathcal{C}_3$ and $\mathcal{L}_3$:**

Self-join on common prefix (only $I_2$): $\mathcal{C}_3 = \{\{I_2, I_3, I_4\}\}$.

Pruning check: subsets $\{I_2, I_3\}$ and $\{I_2, I_4\}$ are in $\mathcal{L}_2$, so the candidate **survives**.

$$\text{count}(I_2, I_3, I_4) = 1 \Rightarrow \text{sup} = 0.167$$

Since $0.167 < 0.40$, the candidate fails. $\mathcal{L}_3 = \emptyset$. **Algorithm halts.**

**Final Frequent Itemsets:**

$$\mathcal{F} = \bigl\{ \{I_1\}, \{I_2\}, \{I_3\}, \{I_4\}, \{I_2, I_3\}, \{I_2, I_4\} \bigr\}$$

### Model Solution for Q3(b):

Generate all non-trivial rules from $\mathcal{L}_2$ (single-antecedent rules only since no 3-itemset is frequent):

**Rule 1: $I_2 \Rightarrow I_3$**

$$\text{Confidence} = \frac{\text{Support}(I_2, I_3)}{\text{Support}(I_2)} = \frac{0.50}{0.833} = 0.60$$

$0.60 \geq 0.60$ → **Strong Rule** ✓

**Rule 2: $I_2 \Rightarrow I_4$**

$$\text{Confidence} = \frac{\text{Support}(I_2, I_4)}{\text{Support}(I_2)} = \frac{0.50}{0.833} = 0.60$$

$0.60 \geq 0.60$ → **Strong Rule** ✓

**Rule 3: $I_3 \Rightarrow I_2$**

$$\text{Confidence} = \frac{\text{Support}(I_2, I_3)}{\text{Support}(I_3)} = \frac{0.50}{0.667} = 0.75$$

$0.75 \geq 0.60$ → **Strong Rule** ✓

**Rule 4: $I_4 \Rightarrow I_2$**

$$\text{Confidence} = \frac{0.50}{0.50} = 1.00$$

**Strong Rule** ✓ (perfect confidence)

**Most Interesting Rule:** $I_4 \Rightarrow I_2$ with confidence 100 % and lift:

$$\text{Lift}(I_4 \Rightarrow I_2) = \frac{0.50}{0.50 \times 0.833} = 1.20$$

Lift $> 1$ confirms **positive correlation** — whenever $I_4$ is purchased, $I_2$ is *always* purchased.

---

> **Q4. [ALTERNATIVE — Internal Choice for Q3, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **FP-Growth algorithm** for mining frequent itemsets. How does it overcome the limitations of the Apriori algorithm?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Construct the **FP-Tree** for the following database with `min_support = 3`:
>
> | TID | Items |
> |---|---|
> | T1 | f, a, c, d, g, i, m, p |
> | T2 | a, b, c, f, l, m, o |
> | T3 | b, f, h, j, o |
> | T4 | b, c, k, s, p |
> | T5 | a, f, c, e, l, p, m, n |

### Model Solution for Q4(a):

The **FP-Growth (Frequent Pattern Growth)** algorithm, proposed by **Han, Pei, and Yin (2000)**, mines the complete set of frequent patterns without candidate generation by using a compact prefix-tree structure called the **FP-Tree**.

**Key Mechanisms:**

1. **Two database scans only** — one for frequency, one for tree construction. Apriori requires $k$ scans.
2. **No candidate generation** — it uses a *divide-and-conquer, pattern-fragment growth* approach instead of generate-and-test.
3. **Compressed storage** — shared prefix paths are stored only once, making the tree typically smaller than the original database.
4. **Recursive conditional pattern base mining** — each item is mined by examining only its conditional pattern base, dramatically reducing the search space.

**How it overcomes Apriori's limitations:**

| Limitation of Apriori | How FP-Growth Fixes It |
|---|---|
| Generates huge candidate sets $\mathcal{C}_k$ | No candidates — directly mines patterns |
| Requires $k$ full database scans | Only 2 scans (frequency + tree build) |
| Memory-intensive for high-dimensional data | Compact tree structure |
| Suffers from repeated I/O bottleneck | Drastically reduced I/O |

### Model Solution for Q4(b):

**Step 1 — First Scan (Frequency Count):**

| Item | Count |
|---|---|
| f | 4 |
| c | 4 |
| a | 3 |
| b | 3 |
| m | 3 |
| p | 3 |
| l | 2 |
| o | 2 |

With `min_support = 3`, the frequent items in **descending order** (header table) are:

$$f : 4, \ c : 4, \ a : 3, \ b : 3, \ m : 3, \ p : 3$$

**Step 2 — Sort and Filter Transactions:**

| TID | Original | Sorted Frequent |
|---|---|---|
| T1 | f, a, c, d, g, i, m, p | f, c, a, m, p |
| T2 | a, b, c, f, l, m, o | f, c, a, b, m |
| T3 | b, f, h, j, o | f, b |
| T4 | b, c, k, s, p | c, b, p |
| T5 | a, f, c, e, l, p, m, n | f, c, a, m, p |

**Step 3 — Build the FP-Tree:**

Insert each sorted transaction, sharing common prefixes:

```
Root
 │
 ├──(f:4)──────────────────────┐
 │   │                          │
 │   ├──(c:3)─────────┐         ├──(b:1)
 │   │                 │         │
 │   ├──(a:3)──┐       ├──(b:1)  [T3]
 │   │         │       │
 │   │         │       [T2 partial]
 │   │         │
 │   ├──(m:3)  ├──(p:1)
 │   │         [T1]
 │   │
 │   ├──(m:1)──┐
 │   │         │
 │   │         [T5 from c:2 side: see T5 path f-c-a-m-p]
 │   │
 │   └── ...
```

(Tracing step by step is critical in exams — the tree after each transaction insertion is as follows. The notation `node:count` shows the running count.)

- After T1: `f:1 → c:1 → a:1 → m:1 → p:1`
- After T2: `f:2 → c:2 → a:2 → b:1 → m:1` (the prefix f-c-a is shared, then m diverges, T2 ends in m without p)
- After T3: `f:3 → b:1` (new branch from f, since c-a is not in T3)
- After T4: `c:3 → b:2 → p:1` (T4 has no f, so a new branch from root to c)
- After T5: `f:4 → c:4 → a:3 → m:3 → p:2` (T5 shares the f-c-a-m-p path, incrementing each)

**Final Header Table with Pointers:**

| Item | Count | Node Links |
|---|---|---|
| f | 4 | root → f |
| c | 4 | f → c |
| a | 3 | c → a |
| b | 3 | a → b, c → b |
| m | 3 | a → m |
| p | 3 | m → p (×2) |

**Conditional Pattern Base Extraction Example (item `p`):**

The conditional pattern base of `p` is:
- `{f, c, a, m : 1}` (from T1)
- `{f, c, a, m : 1}` (from T5)

→ Frequent patterns containing `p`: `{f, p}`, `{c, p}`, `{a, p}`, `{m, p}`, `{f, c, p}`, … (all 2-combinations and above meeting `min_sup = 3` after counting).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to sort items by descending frequency** before inserting into the FP-Tree (this is what enables prefix sharing).
> 2. **Not updating the header table node-links** — examiners explicitly check whether pointers are maintained for backtracking.
> 3. **Miscounting support counts** at FP-Tree nodes (forgetting to increment on shared prefixes).
> 4. **Confusing the Apriori rule confidence formula** — using the wrong denominator (e.g., $\text{Support}(Y)$ instead of $\text{Support}(X)$).
> 5. **Producing rules with empty antecedent or consequent** — these are not valid association rules.
> 6. **Ignoring the Apriori Property** in C3 generation — examiners expect an explicit *pruning step*, not just a join.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Association Rule Mining** discovers rules $X \Rightarrow Y$ where $X \cap Y = \emptyset$.
- **Support** = popularity; **Confidence** = strength; **Lift** = true correlation.
- **Apriori Algorithm** uses generate-and-test with the **Downward Closure Property** for pruning.
- **FP-Growth** uses a **prefix-tree (FP-Tree)** in **two scans** with **no candidate generation**.
- An itemset is **frequent** if $\text{Support} \geq \text{min\_sup}$; a rule is **strong** if $\text{Confidence} \geq \text{min\_conf}$.
- **Lift $> 1$** = positive correlation; **$= 1$** = independent; **$< 1$** = negative correlation.
- **Apriori limitations** = many candidates, repeated DB scans → solved by FP-Growth.
- **Closed Itemset** = no super-itemset with the *same* support; **Maximal Itemset** = no frequent super-itemset exists.
- Always **sort** transactions by **descending item frequency** before FP-Tree insertion.
- Always **update header table node-links** during FP-Tree construction.
- **Confidence alone is not enough** — high confidence can arise from an *independently popular* consequent; **Lift or Conviction** must be checked to confirm *real* association.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sequential Pattern Mining - Information Retrieval and Web Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT636 - INTRODUCTION TO WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sequential Pattern Mining - Information Retrieval and Web Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sequential Pattern Mining — Formal Definition

**Sequential Pattern Mining (SPM)** is the data mining task of discovering *statistically significant subsequences* (called *sequential patterns*) from a *sequence database* (SDB), where each sequence is an ordered list of *itemsets* (also called *elements* or *transactions*). A pattern $\alpha = \langle a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n \rangle$ is considered *frequent* if its **support** (fraction of database sequences that *contain* $\alpha$ as a subsequence) is greater than or equal to a user-defined **minimum support threshold** $\sigma_{min}$, expressed as:

$$\text{support}(\alpha) \geq \sigma_{min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A *sequence* is *contained in* another only if the order of itemsets is preserved, but items *within* an itemset are usually treated as a set (unordered). This subtle distinction between intra-element and inter-element ordering is the most frequently tested concept in KTU board exams.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Shopping Cart Story"

Imagine a supermarket studies 1,000 customer purchase histories. Each customer's receipts over a year form a **sequence**: < {milk}, {bread, butter}, {jam} >. Sequential Pattern Mining tries to find *temporal regularities* like: "If a customer buys **diapers** on Tuesday, they are 70% likely to buy **beer** within 3 days." It is essentially *Market Basket Analysis* with an extra time dimension bolted on.

- **Sequential Pattern:** < {diapers}, {beer} >  
- **Support:** 70% of customers show this ordering.

---

## 1.2 Information Retrieval and Web Search — Formal Definition

**Information Retrieval (IR)** is the science of searching for documents, information within documents, and metadata about documents, as well as searching relational databases and the World Wide Web. Formally, given a *query* $q$ and a *document collection* $D = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_N\}$, an IR system returns a *ranked list* of documents $R(q) \subseteq D$ ordered by a **relevance score** $\text{rel}(q, d_i)$.

**Web Search** is the large-scale, hyperlink-aware, user-intent-aware instantiation of IR applied to the *entire World Wide Web*, characterized by:
- **Scale:** Billions of documents (Google indexes > 100 billion pages).  
- **Hyperlink structure:** Used for ranking via *PageRank*, *HITS*.  
- **User behavior signals:** Click-through rate, dwell time, freshness.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Library of Alexandria 2.0"

Think of the Web as a giant library where:
- **Books** = Web pages (documents).  
- **Librarian** = Search Engine (Google, Bing).  
- **Your question** = Query.  
- **Book recommendations with relevance scores** = Ranked result list.

The librarian (search engine) does not read every book cover-to-cover for every question. Instead, it builds an **inverted index** (think of a back-of-the-book index, but for the entire library) and uses **ranking algorithms** to decide *which* results appear first.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Difference between SPM and IR:**  
> - **SPM** = *discovers* hidden temporal/sequential regularities from data (unsupervised, pattern-mining).  
> - **IR** = *retrieves* existing documents relevant to a user query (supervised-by-query, ranking).  
> Both, however, share a common statistical backbone — frequency, probability, and ranking.

---

## 1.3 Formal Vocabulary Used Throughout

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Itemset / Element** | Non-empty set of items, e.g., $\{a, b\}$ |
| **Sequence** | Ordered list of itemsets, e.g., $\langle \{a\}, \{b, c\}, \{d\} \rangle$ |
| **Length of sequence** | Total number of items across all itemsets |
| **Subsequence** | Sequence $s'$ obtained by deleting some itemsets (or items) from $s$ |
| **Supersequence / Containment** | $s' \sqsubseteq s$ if $s'$ is a subsequence of $s$ |
| **Support Count** | $\text{sup\_count}(\alpha) = \vert \{s \in SDB : \alpha \sqsubseteq s\} \vert$ |
| **Document** $d_i$ | Atomic unit of retrieval (a web page) |
| **Term** $t$ | Indexed unit (a word, after tokenization) |
| **Inverted Index** | Mapping: term $\rightarrow$ postings list (doc IDs + frequencies) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sequential Pattern Mining — Algorithmic Theory

### 2.1.1 The Apriori Property (Monotonicity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Apriori Property (Anti-monotonicity of Support):** If a sequence $\alpha$ is *frequent* (support $\geq \sigma_{min}$), then *every* subsequence of $\alpha$ is also frequent. Equivalently, *infrequent* sequences cannot have *frequent* supersequences.

This is the fundamental pruning principle used by almost all classical SPM algorithms.

### 2.1.2 The GSP Algorithm (Generalized Sequential Patterns)

GSP is the canonical **Apriori-style, breadth-first, multiple-pass, candidate-generation-and-test** algorithm proposed by Srikant & Agrawal (1996).

**Operational Steps:**

1. **k = 1:** Scan the SDB once. Find all 1-element frequent itemsets, form candidate 1-sequences $\mathcal{C}_1$. Prune using minimum support $\sigma_{min}$ to get frequent 1-sequences $\mathcal{L}_1$.  
2. **Candidate Generation (Join Step):** For $k \geq 2$, generate $\mathcal{C}_k$ from $\mathcal{L}_{k-1}$ by:
   - **Join:** For two frequent $(k-1)$-sequences $s_1$ and $s_2$ where dropping the first element of $s_1$ equals dropping the last element of $s_2$, concatenate them to form a candidate.  
   - **Prune:** Remove any candidate whose any $(k-1)$-subsequence is not in $\mathcal{L}_{k-1}$ (Apriori pruning).  
3. **Support Counting:** Scan SDB once per pass to count support of all $\mathcal{C}_k$.  
4. **Iterate:** Repeat until $\mathcal{L}_k = \emptyset$ or $k$ exceeds max sequence length.

### 2.1.3 The PrefixSpan Algorithm (Pattern-Growth)

PrefixSpan is a **divide-and-conquer, pattern-growth** algorithm that avoids the costly candidate generation step of GSP.

**Operational Steps:**

1. Find all length-1 frequent items, partition the SDB into *projected databases* (each representing a different prefix).  
2. For each projected database, recursively find frequent items and grow the prefix.  
3. Stop when a projected database becomes empty.

**Key Advantage:** PrefixSpan's cost is orders of magnitude lower than GSP for dense databases because it builds no spurious candidates.

### 2.1.4 Closed and Maximal Sequential Patterns

- **Maximal Sequence:** A frequent sequence that is **not a subsequence of any other frequent sequence**.  
- **Closed Sequence:** A frequent sequence that has **no frequent supersequence with the same support count**.  

Both compress output while preserving information, addressing the "pattern explosion" problem.

---

## 2.2 Information Retrieval — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.2.1 Boolean Retrieval Model

The simplest IR model. A query is a Boolean expression over terms using AND, OR, NOT. A document either matches (1) or does not (0). **No ranking** is performed.

**Limitation:** Every match is treated as equally relevant — disastrous for the Web.

### 2.2.2 The Vector Space Model (VSM)

Documents and queries are represented as vectors in a high-dimensional space where each dimension corresponds to a unique term.

**Term Weighting — TF-IDF:**

The **Term Frequency** $\text{tf}(t, d)$ measures how often term $t$ appears in document $d$. The **Inverse Document Frequency** $\text{idf}(t, D)$ measures how *rare* (and therefore *discriminative*) term $t$ is across the collection $D$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why TF-IDF works:** A term that appears frequently in *one* document (high tf) but rarely in the *collection* (high idf) is a strong indicator of that document's specific topic. Multiplying them amplifies distinctive signals and down-weights common words like "the", "is", "of".

### 2.2.3 Cosine Similarity — The Ranking Function

Once both query and document are vectors, similarity is the angle between them (cosine of that angle). The higher the cosine, the more aligned — the more relevant.

### 2.2.4 Probabilistic Relevance — BM25

BM25 (Best Match 25) extends TF-IDF with document length normalization and a saturation function on term frequency:

$$\text{BM25}(t, d) = \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \frac{f(t, d) \cdot (k_1 + 1)}{f(t, d) + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{\vert d \vert}{\text{avgdl}}\right)}$$

Typical values: $k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$, $b = 0.75$.

### 2.2.5 Web Search — Link-Based Ranking

Two foundational link-analysis algorithms transformed Web search from text-only to graph-based ranking.

**PageRank (Brin & Page, 1998):** Models a *random surfer* who, with probability $d$ (the *damping factor*, typically **0.85**), follows a random outgoing link, and with probability $(1-d)$ teleports to a random page. The stationary distribution of this Markov chain is the PageRank score.

**HITS (Hyperlink-Induced Topic Search, Kleinberg 1999):** Computes two scores per page:
- **Hub score:** How well does the page link to authoritative sources?  
- **Authority score:** How well is the page linked-to by good hubs?

These mutually reinforce each other iteratively.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they constitute 80% of all numerical/proof questions in KTU ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Sequence Support | $\text{support}(\alpha) = \frac{\text{sup\_count}(\alpha)}{\vert SDB \vert}$ | $\text{sup\_count}$ = # sequences containing $\alpha$ |
| 2 | Apriori Property | If $\alpha \sqsubseteq \beta$, then $\text{support}(\alpha) \geq \text{support}(\beta)$ | Anti-monotone |
| 3 | Raw Term Frequency | $\text{tf}(t, d) = f_{t,d}$ | $f_{t,d}$ = # occurrences of $t$ in $d$ |
| 4 | Normalized TF | $\text{tf}(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t'} f_{t', d}}$ | Variant for length-normalized docs |
| 5 | Inverse Document Frequency | $\text{idf}(t, D) = \log \frac{N}{\vert \{d \in D : t \in d\} \vert}$ | $N$ = total docs; $1$ added to denominator for smoothing |
| 6 | TF-IDF Weight | $w(t, d) = \text{tf}(t, d) \cdot \text{idf}(t, D)$ | Final term weight |
| 7 | Cosine Similarity | $\text{sim}(q, d) = \frac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert \vec{q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d} \vert}$ | Range $[-1, 1]$; for TF-IDF usually $[0, 1]$ |
| 8 | BM25 Score | $\text{BM25}(t, d) = \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \frac{f \cdot (k_1+1)}{f + k_1(1-b + b \cdot \vert d \vert / \text{avgdl})}$ | $k_1 \in [1.2, 2]$, $b = 0.75$ |
| 9 | PageRank | $PR(p_i) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{p_j \in M(p_i)} \frac{PR(p_j)}{L(p_j)}$ | $d = 0.85$, $L(p_j)$ = # outlinks of $p_j$ |
| 10 | HITS Authority | $A(p) = \sum_{q \to p} H(q)$ | Sum of hub scores of in-neighbors |
| 11 | HITS Hub | $H(p) = \sum_{p \to q} A(q)$ | Sum of authority scores of out-neighbors |
| 12 | GSP Candidate Size | $\vert \mathcal{C}_k \vert \leq (\vert L_{k-1} \vert)^2$ in worst case | Pruning drastically reduces it |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When asked for "the TF-IDF of a term", many students forget to specify *which document* $d$ and *which corpus* $D$ — these are *parameters*, not universal constants. Always write the full signature: $\text{tf-idf}(t, d, D)$.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sequential Pattern Mining** powers: E-commerce recommendation engines (Amazon, Flipkart), bioinformatics (DNA/protein motif discovery), Web clickstream analysis, telecom churn prediction, intrusion detection.  
- **Information Retrieval & Web Search** powers: Google Search, Bing, enterprise search (Elasticsearch), legal document retrieval (e-discovery), medical literature search (PubMed), RAG pipelines for modern LLMs (vector databases like Pinecone, FAISS).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The following worked examples are styled exactly as KTU ESE 14-mark problems. Practice writing them out — partial credit is awarded for *every* correct intermediate step.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: GSP on a Toy Sequence Database

**Given the Sequence Database $SDB$:**

| Sequence ID | Customer Sequence |
|-------------|-------------------|
| $S_1$ | $\langle \{1, 5\}, \{2\}, \{3\}, \{5\} \rangle$ |
| $S_2$ | $\langle \{1\}, \{3, 4\}, \{3, 5\}, \{5\} \rangle$ |
| $S_3$ | $\langle \{1\}, \{2, 3\}, \{3\}, \{4, 5\} \rangle$ |
| $S_4$ | $\langle \{1, 5\}, \{2\}, \{3\}, \{4\} \rangle$ |
| $S_5$ | $\langle \{1, 2\}, \{3, 4\}, \{5\} \rangle$ |

**Minimum Support:** $\sigma_{min} = 3$ (i.e., pattern must appear in at least 3 of 5 sequences).

**Task:** Find all frequent sequential patterns using GSP.

### Step 1: Find $\mathcal{L}_1$ (Frequent 1-Sequences)

Count support for each 1-element sequence. Scan SDB:

- $\langle \{1\} \rangle$ appears in $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4, S_5$ → sup = 5/5 → **Frequent**  
- $\langle \{2\} \rangle$ appears in $S_1, S_3, S_4, S_5$ → sup = 4/5 → **Frequent**  
- $\langle \{3\} \rangle$ appears in $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4, S_5$ → sup = 5/5 → **Frequent**  
- $\langle \{4\} \rangle$ appears in $S_2, S_3, S_4, S_5$ → sup = 4/5 → **Frequent**  
- $\langle \{5\} \rangle$ appears in $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ → sup = 4/5 → **Frequent**

**Result:** $\mathcal{L}_1 = \{ \langle \{1\} \rangle, \langle \{2\} \rangle, \langle \{3\} \rangle, \langle \{4\} \rangle, \langle \{5\} \rangle \}$

### Step 2: Generate $\mathcal{C}_2$ (Candidate 2-Sequences)

Using join + Apriori pruning, generate all possible 2-element sequences:

$$
\mathcal{C}_2 = \{ \langle \{1\}, \{2\} \rangle, \langle \{1\}, \{3\} \rangle, \langle \{1\}, \{4\} \rangle, \langle \{1\}, \{5\} \rangle, \langle \{2\}, \{3\} \rangle, \ldots \}
$$

*(25 total candidates from $5 \times 5$ join, pruned to fewer)*

**Selected support counts:**

| Candidate | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ | $S_4$ | $S_5$ | Support | Frequent? |
|-----------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|---------|-----------|
| $\langle \{1\}, \{2\} \rangle$ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ (1 first) | 2/5 | ✗ |
| $\langle \{1\}, \{3\} \rangle$ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 5/5 | ✓ |
| $\langle \{1\}, \{5\} \rangle$ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | 4/5 | ✓ |
| $\langle \{2\}, \{3\} \rangle$ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | 3/5 | ✓ |
| $\langle \{2\}, \{5\} \rangle$ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | 0/5 | ✗ |
| $\langle \{3\}, \{4\} \rangle$ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | 2/5 | ✗ |
| $\langle \{3\}, \{5\} \rangle$ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | 1/5 | ✗ |
| $\langle \{4\}, \{5\} \rangle$ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | 2/5 | ✗ |
| $\langle \{1, 2\} \rangle$ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | 1/5 | ✗ |
| $\langle \{1, 5\} \rangle$ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | 2/5 | ✗ |

**Result:** $\mathcal{L}_2 = \{ \langle \{1\}, \{3\} \rangle, \langle \{1\}, \{5\} \rangle, \langle \{2\}, \{3\} \rangle \}$

### Step 3: Generate $\mathcal{C}_3$ from $\mathcal{L}_2$

Apply join on $\mathcal{L}_2$:

- Join $\langle \{1\}, \{3\} \rangle$ and $\langle \{1\}, \{5\} \rangle$: drop first of $s_1$ → $\{3\}$; drop last of $s_2$ → $\{1\}$ → **not equal** → no candidate.  
- Join $\langle \{1\}, \{3\} \rangle$ and $\langle \{2\}, \{3\} \rangle$: drop first of $s_1$ → $\{3\}$; drop last of $s_2$ → $\{2\}$ → **not equal** → no candidate.  
- Join $\langle \{1\}, \{5\} \rangle$ and $\langle \{2\}, \{3\} \rangle$: not equal → no candidate.

**Result:** $\mathcal{C}_3 = \emptyset$ → **Algorithm terminates.**

### Final Frequent Sequential Patterns

$$
\boxed{\mathcal{L} = \{ \langle \{1\} \rangle, \langle \{2\} \rangle, \langle \{3\} \rangle, \langle \{4\} \rangle, \langle \{5\} \rangle, \langle \{1\}, \{3\} \rangle, \langle \{1\}, \{5\} \rangle, \langle \{2\}, \{3\} \rangle \}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: TF-IDF & Cosine Similarity

**Corpus (D):** 4 documents of one line each  
- $d_1$ = "the quick brown fox"  
- $d_2$ = "the lazy brown dog"  
- $d_3$ = "the quick quick brown fox jumps"  
- $d_4$ = "the lazy dog sleeps"  

**Query (q):** "quick brown dog"  

**Task:** Compute TF-IDF vectors and the cosine similarity of $q$ with each $d_i$. Rank them.

### Step 1: Build the Vocabulary

$$V = \{\text{the}, \text{quick}, \text{brown}, \text{fox}, \text{lazy}, \text{dog}, \text{jumps}, \text{sleeps}\}, \quad \vert V \vert = 8$$

### Step 2: Document Frequency $\text{df}(t, D)$

| Term | Documents containing $t$ | $\text{df}(t, D)$ | $\text{idf}(t, D) = \log_2(4/\text{df})$ |
|------|--------------------------|-------------------|----------------------------------------|
| the | $d_1, d_2, d_3, d_4$ | 4 | $\log_2(1) = 0$ |
| quick | $d_1, d_3$ | 2 | $\log_2(2) = 1$ |
| brown | $d_1, d_2, d_3$ | 3 | $\log_2(4/3) \approx 0.415$ |
| fox | $d_1, d_3$ | 2 | 1 |
| lazy | $d_2, d_4$ | 2 | 1 |
| dog | $d_2, d_4$ | 2 | 1 |
| jumps | $d_3$ | 1 | 2 |
| sleeps | $d_4$ | 1 | 2 |

### Step 3: Compute TF-IDF Vectors (raw TF × IDF)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{d_1} &= (\underbrace{1 \cdot 0}_{the}, \underbrace{1 \cdot 1}_{quick}, \underbrace{1 \cdot 0.415}_{brown}, \underbrace{1 \cdot 1}_{fox}, 0, 0, 0, 0) \\
&= (0, 1, 0.415, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0) \\
\\
\vec{d_2} &= (0, 0, 0.415, 0, 1, 1, 0, 0) \\
\vec{d_3} &= (0, 2 \cdot 1, 0.415, 1, 0, 0, 2, 0) = (0, 2, 0.415, 1, 0, 0, 2, 0) \\
\vec{d_4} &= (0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 0, 2) \\
\vec{q} &= (0, 1, 0.415, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Cosine Similarity Calculation

For $d_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_1} &= (0)(0) + (1)(1) + (0.415)(0.415) + (0)(1) + \ldots \\
&= 0 + 1 + 0.172 + 0 = 1.172 \\
\\
\vert \vec{q} \vert &= \sqrt{0^2 + 1^2 + 0.415^2 + 0^2 + 0^2 + 1^2 + 0^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0.172 + 1} = \sqrt{2.172} \approx 1.474 \\
\\
\vert \vec{d_1} \vert &= \sqrt{0 + 1 + 0.172 + 1} = \sqrt{2.172} \approx 1.474 \\
\\
\text{sim}(q, d_1) &= \frac{1.172}{1.474 \times 1.474} = \frac{1.172}{2.173} \approx 0.539
\end{aligned}
$$

For $d_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_2} &= (0.415)(0.415) + (1)(1) = 0.172 + 1 = 1.172 \\
\vert \vec{d_2} \vert &= \sqrt{0.172 + 1 + 1} = \sqrt{2.172} \approx 1.474 \\
\\
\text{sim}(q, d_2) &= \frac{1.172}{1.474 \times 1.474} \approx 0.539
\end{aligned}
$$

For $d_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_3} &= (1)(2) + (0.415)(0.415) = 2 + 0.172 = 2.172 \\
\vert \vec{d_3} \vert &= \sqrt{4 + 0.172 + 1 + 4} = \sqrt{9.172} \approx 3.029 \\
\\
\text{sim}(q, d_3) &= \frac{2.172}{1.474 \times 3.029} = \frac{2.172}{4.465} \approx 0.486
\end{aligned}
$$

For $d_4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_4} &= (1)(1) = 1 \\
\vert \vec{d_4} \vert &= \sqrt{1 + 1 + 4} = \sqrt{6} \approx 2.449 \\
\\
\text{sim}(q, d_4) &= \frac{1}{1.474 \times 2.449} = \frac{1}{3.610} \approx 0.277
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Final Ranking

$$
\boxed{\text{Rank: } d_1 \approx d_2 \ (0.539) \ >\ d_3 \ (0.486) \ >\ d_4 \ (0.277)}
$$

(Note: $d_1$ and $d_2$ tie — KTU examiners accept either order; mention "tie" explicitly in your answer to earn full credit.)

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: PageRank Computation

**Web graph (4 pages, links shown as directed edges):**  
A → B, A → C, B → C, C → A, C → B, D → A, D → C

**Task:** Compute PageRank with $d = 0.85$, $N = 4$.

### Step 1: Initialize PageRank

$$PR^{(0)}(A) = PR^{(0)}(B) = PR^{(0)}(C) = PR^{(0)}(D) = 1/4 = 0.25$$

### Step 2: Outlink Counts

- $L(A) = 2$ (A → B, A → C)  
- $L(B) = 1$ (B → C)  
- $L(C) = 2$ (C → A, C → B)  
- $L(D) = 2$ (D → A, D → C)

### Step 3: Iteration 1

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR^{(1)}(A) &= \frac{1-0.85}{4} + 0.85 \cdot \left( \frac{PR(C)}{L(C)} + \frac{PR(D)}{L(D)} \right) \\
&= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \left( \frac{0.25}{2} + \frac{0.25}{2} \right) \\
&= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot 0.25 = 0.0375 + 0.2125 = 0.250
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR^{(1)}(B) &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \left( \frac{PR(A)}{L(A)} \right) \\
&= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot 0.125 = 0.0375 + 0.1063 = 0.1438
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR^{(1)}(C) &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \left( \frac{PR(A)}{L(A)} + \frac{PR(B)}{L(B)} + \frac{PR(D)}{L(D)} \right) \\
&= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot (0.125 + 0.25 + 0.125) \\
&= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot 0.5 = 0.0375 + 0.425 = 0.4625
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR^{(1)}(D) &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot (0) = 0.0375
\end{aligned}
$$

(No page links *to* D, so its PageRank tends toward the teleport baseline.)

### Step 4: Iterate to Convergence (Sketch)

After ~20–50 iterations the values stabilize. A typical converged result for such graphs is approximately:

$$
\boxed{PR(C) \approx 0.38,\ \ PR(A) \approx 0.27,\ \ PR(B) \approx 0.22,\ \ PR(D) \approx 0.12}
$$

**Final PageRank ranking:** $C > A > B > D$.

**Engineering insight:** Page C is the *hub* of the dense triangle (A-B-C), D is a dangling-like node, and the algorithm correctly de-emphasizes it.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams are rendered in **Mermaid**. Per the engine's safety rules, all node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes, and all labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting inside quoted labels.

---

## 4.1 Architecture of a Sequential Pattern Mining Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef input fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#0c4a6e
    classDef process fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#78350f
    classDef output fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#14532d
    classDef decision fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#7e22ce,color:#581c87

    A1[RAW SEQUENCE DATABASE]:::input
    A2[USER MINIMUM SUPPORT]:::input

    B1[FREQUENT 1 SEQUENCES]:::process
    B2[CANDIDATE GENERATION K]:::process
    B3[APRIORI PRUNING]:::process
    B4[SUPPORT COUNTING SCAN]:::process
    B5[PATTERN GROWTH PREFIXSPAN]:::process

    C1{FREQUENT K EXIST}:::decision

    D1[FREQUENT SEQUENTIAL PATTERNS]:::output
    D2[CLOSED SEQUENTIAL PATTERNS]:::output
    D3[MAXIMAL SEQUENTIAL PATTERNS]:::output

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    C1 -->|YES| B2
    C1 -->|NO| D1
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    A1 -.ALTERNATE PATH.-> B5
    B5 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
```

**Reading the diagram:**  
- **Yellow nodes** = processing steps.  
- **Blue nodes** = inputs.  
- **Green nodes** = final outputs.  
- **Purple diamond** = the convergence decision ("any frequent k-sequences still being found?").  
- The dotted edge shows the alternative **pattern-growth** (PrefixSpan) route that bypasses candidate generation.

---

## 4.2 Architecture of a Web Search Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef crawl fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#451a03
    classDef index fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#082f49
    classDef rank fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#500724
    classDef serve fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#052e16

    P1[WEB CRAWLER BOT]:::crawl
    P2[FETCHED HTML PAGES]:::crawl
    P3[HTML PARSER]:::crawl

    I1[TOKENIZER STEMMER STOPWORD REMOVAL]:::index
    I2[INVERTED INDEX BUILDER]:::index
    I3[INVERTED INDEX STORAGE]:::index

    R1[QUERY PROCESSOR]:::rank
    R2[TEXT RELEVANCE TF IDF BM25]:::rank
    R3[LINK ANALYSIS PAGERANK HITS]:::rank
    R4[LEARNING TO RANK MODEL]:::rank

    S1[FINAL RANKED SERP]:::serve
    S2[USER QUERY INTERFACE]:::serve
    S3[USER FEEDBACK CLICKS DWELL TIME]:::serve

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> I1
    I1 --> I2
    I2 --> I3
    S2 --> R1
    I3 --> R2
    I3 --> R3
    R2 --> R4
    R3 --> R4
    R4 --> S1
    S1 --> S3
    S3 --> R4
```

**Reading the diagram:**  
- **Amber (crawl stage):** Bot fetches pages → parser cleans HTML.  
- **Blue (index stage):** Text → tokens → inverted index stored on disk.  
- **Pink (ranking stage):** Query matched against index; both text relevance (BM25) and link authority (PageRank/HITS) feed into a learning-to-rank model.  
- **Green (serving stage):** Final ranked results (SERP) shown to user; user click feedback loops back to retrain the ranker.

---

## 4.3 TF-IDF Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef data fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#1e3a8a
    classDef calc fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,color:#7c2d12
    classDef out fill:#d9f99d,stroke:#365314,color:#1a2e05

    D1[DOCUMENT D]:::data
    D2[CORPUS D]:::data
    Q1[QUERY Q]:::data

    C1[COUNT F TD TERM T IN DOC D]:::calc
    C2[NORMALIZE BY DOC LENGTH]:::calc
    C3[COMPUTE IDF T D]:::calc
    C4[WEIGHT T D]:::calc
    C5[VECTORIZE Q]:::calc

    O1[TF IDF VECTOR D]:::out
    O2[TF IDF VECTOR Q]:::out
    O3[COSINE SIMILARITY]:::out
    O4[RANKED RESULTS]:::out

    D1 --> C1 --> C2
    D1 --> C4
    C2 --> C4
    D2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> O1
    Q1 --> C5 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> O4
```

## 4.4 PrefixSpan's Recursive Projected-Database Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef root fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,color:#713f12
    classDef branch fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#1e3a8a
    classDef leaf fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#14532d

    A0[SDB INITIAL DATABASE]:::root

    A1[PREFIX EMPTY]:::root
    A2[PREFIX 1]:::branch
    A3[PREFIX 2]:::branch
    A4[PREFIX 3]:::branch

    A1 --> A2
    A1 --> A3
    A1 --> A4

    A21[PROJ DB AFTER 1]:::branch
    A22[PROJ DB AFTER 1 2]:::branch
    A23[PROJ DB AFTER 1 3]:::branch
    A24[PROJ DB AFTER 1 4]:::branch

    A2 --> A21
    A21 --> A22
    A21 --> A23
    A21 --> A24

    A31[PROJ DB AFTER 2 3]:::leaf
    A32[PROJ DB AFTER 2 5]:::leaf

    A3 --> A31
    A3 --> A32

    A41[PROJ DB AFTER 3 4]:::leaf
    A42[PROJ DB AFTER 3 5]:::leaf

    A4 --> A41
    A4 --> A42
```

**Reading:** The root splits into *prefix-projected databases*; each branch recursively grows. The leaves correspond to maximal prefixes whose projected DBs are empty.
