1. **Query Reception & Parsing** — Accept $q$ from the user interface; strip unsupported advanced operators (because not all base engines support them).
2. **Query Translation** — Reformat $q$ into the *lowest common denominator* syntax acceptable to all underlying engines (e.g., convert `intitle:` to plain text if engine $E_3$ does not support it).
3. **Parallel Dispatch** — Send $q$ *concurrently* to all $k$ engines using asynchronous HTTP calls. This is why the architecture is sometimes called a **"star"** or **"hub-and-spoke"** model.
4. **Result Collection** — Each engine returns a ranked list $L_i = \{(d_{i,1}, s_{i,1}), \dots, (d_{i,n}, s_{i,n})\}$ where $s_{i,j}$ is the relevance score assigned by engine $E_i$ to document $d_{i,j}$.
5. **Normalization** — Scores are normalized to a uniform range (typically $[0,1]$) so that scores from different engines become *comparable*. This is called **score normalization** — the *most critical* pre-fusion step.
6. **Fusion / Aggregation** — Apply fusion function $\mathcal{F}$ to produce the final merged list $L_M$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Score Normalization?** Search engines use different scoring scales (BM25, TF-IDF, PageRank-weighted, cosine similarity, etc.). Fusing raw scores is *meaningless* — you must first bring them to a common scale, otherwise the engine with the largest raw scores will *dominate* the fused ranking regardless of actual relevance.

---

### 2.2 Two Principal Paradigms of Fusion

#### A. Data Fusion (Score-Based Aggregation)
Combines the **relevance scores** from different engines after normalization.

#### B. Rank Aggregation
Combines the **positional ranks** of documents, ignoring the actual score magnitudes.

> **The 'Why' question:** Why two paradigms? Because some engines **do not expose** their scores publicly (e.g., Google does not return numerical relevance values — only ranks). In such cases, *rank aggregation is the only viable method*.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU ESE question on this topic.**

| # | Fusion Method | Mathematical Formula | Description | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CombSUM** | $S_{SUM}(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} s_i(d)$ | Sum of normalized scores from all engines that returned $d$ | When scores are available & comparable |
| 2 | **CombMNZ** | $S_{MNZ}(d) = MN(d) \times \sum_{i=1}^{k} s_i(d)$ | CombSUM multiplied by the **number of engines (MN)** that returned $d$ | Rewards documents retrieved by *many* engines |
| 3 | **CombANZ** | $S_{ANZ}(d) = \dfrac{1}{MN(d)} \sum_{i=1}^{k} s_i(d)$ | Average of normalized scores | Penalizes high-vote items |
| 4 | **Borda Count** | $B(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (N_i - r_i(d))$ | Sum of $(N_i - \text{rank})$ across engines | When only ranks are available |
| 5 | **Reciprocal Rank** | $RR(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{1}{r_i(d)}$ | Sum of reciprocals of ranks | Strong top-weight bias |
| 6 | **Condorcet Fusion** | Pairwise majority rule | Pairwise comparison between docs | Theoretical, O($n^2$) cost |
| 7 | **Linear Combination** | $S_{LC}(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot s_i(d)$ | Weighted sum, $\sum w_i = 1$ | When engine reliability is known |

**Notation used in the table:**
- $k$ = number of underlying search engines
- $s_i(d)$ = normalized score of document $d$ from engine $E_i$ (in range $[0,1]$)
- $MN(d)$ = *Multiple-Nonzero* count — number of engines that actually retrieved $d$
- $N_i$ = number of results returned by engine $E_i$
- $r_i(d)$ = rank of $d$ in engine $E_i$'s list (1 = top)
- $w_i$ = weight of engine $E_i$

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vertical search aggregators** (Kayak, Skyscanner, Trivago) all use meta-search architectures.
- **Enterprise search portals** in organizations use meta-search to federate queries across SharePoint, Confluence, Slack, and JIRA simultaneously.
- **Academic search tools** (e.g., **1Science**, **Microsoft Academic Graph aggregators**) use meta-search principles to merge results from PubMed, IEEE Xplore, ACM DL.
- **Privacy-preserving search** (e.g., **Searx**) uses meta-search to avoid giving *any single engine* the full query history of a user.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Score Normalization (Min-Max Normalization)

**Why we need it:** Different engines produce scores on different scales. Google-style scores might lie in $[0, 100]$, while another engine might produce $[0, 1]$. Combining them raw would be invalid.

**Step 1 — Identify the range.** For engine $E_i$ on a query $q$, identify the minimum score $s_i^{min}$ and the maximum score $s_i^{max}$ from its returned list.

**Step 2 — Apply the linear transformation.** For any document $d$ with raw score $s_i^{raw}(d)$ from engine $E_i$, compute the normalized score:

$$s_i^{norm}(d) = \frac{s_i^{raw}(d) - s_i^{min}}{s_i^{max} - s_i^{min}}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the boundary conditions.**

$$s_i^{norm} \in [0, 1] \quad \text{where} \quad s_i^{norm}(d_{top}) = 1 \text{ and } s_i^{norm}(d_{bottom}) = 0$$

**Textual explanation of the logic:** We *subtract the minimum* to shift the scale to start at zero, then *divide by the range* $(s_i^{max} - s_i^{min})$ to scale the spread to exactly one. This is the simplest affine map from any bounded interval $[s_i^{min}, s_i^{max}]$ to $[0, 1]$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — CombSUM and CombMNZ

**Setup:** A user queries **"KTU 2024 syllabus"** on a meta-search engine that dispatches to **three engines**: $E_1$, $E_2$, $E_3$. The meta-searcher fetches the **top 3 results** from each. Document IDs are normalized across engines. The raw and normalized scores are as follows:

| Document | $E_1$ raw | $E_2$ raw | $E_3$ raw | $E_1$ norm | $E_2$ norm | $E_3$ norm |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **D1** | 85 | — | 0.92 | 1.00 | — | 1.00 |
| **D2** | 60 | 0.7 | — | 0.40 | 1.00 | — |
| **D3** | 50 | 0.5 | 0.6 | 0.20 | 0.50 | 0.50 |
| **D4** | 30 | 0.2 | 0.3 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 |

> The em-dash "—" indicates the engine did **not** return that document in its top-3.

**Step 1 — Compute CombSUM for each document.**

The formula is: $S_{SUM}(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{3} s_i^{norm}(d)$

For **D1**:

$$S_{SUM}(D1) = 1.00 + 0 + 1.00 = 2.00$$

For **D2**:

$$S_{SUM}(D2) = 0.40 + 1.00 + 0 = 1.40$$

For **D3**:

$$S_{SUM}(D3) = 0.20 + 0.50 + 0.50 = 1.20$$

For **D4**:

$$S_{SUM}(D4) = 0.00 + 0.00 + 0.00 = 0.00$$

**Step 2 — Compute MN (Multiple-Nonzero) for each document.**

$MN(D1) = 2$ (returned by $E_1$ and $E_3$)
$MN(D2) = 2$ (returned by $E_1$ and $E_2$)
$MN(D3) = 3$ (returned by all three)
$MN(D4) = 3$ (returned by all three, but with score 0)

**Step 3 — Compute CombMNZ.**

The formula is: $S_{MNZ}(d) = MN(d) \times \sum_{i=1}^{3} s_i^{norm}(d)$

For **D1**:

$$S_{MNZ}(D1) = 2 \times 2.00 = 4.00$$

For **D2**:

$$S_{MNZ}(D2) = 2 \times 1.40 = 2.80$$

For **D3**:

$$S_{MNZ}(D3) = 3 \times 1.20 = 3.60$$

For **D4**:

$$S_{MNZ}(D4) = 3 \times 0.00 = 0.00$$

**Step 4 — Construct the final fused ranking.**

| Rank | CombSUM Order | CombSUM Score | CombMNZ Order | CombMNZ Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | D1 | 2.00 | **D1** | **4.00** |
| 2 | D2 | 1.40 | **D3** | **3.60** |
| 3 | D3 | 1.20 | **D2** | **2.80** |
| 4 | D4 | 0.00 | D4 | 0.00 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical observation:** Notice how **CombMNZ promotes D3 above D2** (3.60 vs 2.80) because D3 was retrieved by *all three* engines, demonstrating a higher "consensus." This is the core *philosophical difference* between CombSUM and CombMNZ — the latter rewards *consensus retrieval*.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Borda Count Rank Aggregation

**Step 1 — Define the rank.** Let $r_i(d)$ be the rank position of document $d$ in engine $E_i$'s returned list. Rank 1 = top result.

**Step 2 — Borda score from one engine.** For engine $E_i$ returning $N_i$ documents, the Borda score contribution of $d$ is:

$$B_i(d) = N_i - r_i(d)$$

> **Logic:** The top-ranked document gets $N_i - 1$, the second gets $N_i - 2$, and so on. The last gets 0.

**Step 3 — Aggregate across all engines.** Sum the contributions:

$$B_{total}(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} B_i(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (N_i - r_i(d))$$

**Step 4 — Rank the documents by $B_{total}(d)$ in descending order.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary / edge case:** If engine $E_i$ does *not* return document $d$, then $r_i(d)$ is undefined. Convention: assign $r_i(d) = N_i + 1$ (i.e., treat the document as if it were ranked just *below* the last returned item). This gives $B_i(d) = -1$, slightly penalizing the document for non-retrieval.

---

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Strengths & Weaknesses

| Fusion Method | Strength | Weakness | Score Needed? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CombSUM** | Simple, fast, intuitive | Treats all engines equally | Yes |
| **CombMNZ** | Rewards consensus | Can overweight trivial duplicates | Yes |
| **CombANZ** | Diminishes over-voting effect | Loses absolute magnitude signal | Yes |
| **Borda Count** | Works with ranks only | Ignores score magnitude | No |
| **Reciprocal Rank** | Strong top-weight, robust | Very sensitive to top ranks | No |
| **Linear Combination** | Tunable via weights | Requires prior engine-quality knowledge | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Meta-Search Engine Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userA[User Browser] -->|Submits Query Q| uiA[User Interface Module]
    uiA --> parserA[Query Parser and Translator]
    parserA -->|Normalized Q| dispatcherA[Query Dispatcher and Scheduler]
    
    dispatcherA -->|Async HTTP GET Q| eng1[Search Engine E1 Google]
    dispatcherA -->|Async HTTP GET Q| eng2[Search Engine E2 Bing]
    dispatcherA -->|Async HTTP GET Q| eng3[Search Engine E3 Yahoo]
    dispatcherA -->|Async HTTP GET Q| engN[Search Engine Ek DuckDuckGo]
    
    eng1 -->|Result List L1| collectorA[Result Collector and Parser]
    eng2 -->|Result List L2| collectorA
    eng3 -->|Result List L3| collectorA
    engN -->|Result List Lk| collectorA
    
    collectorA -->|Raw Lists| normalizerA[Score Normalization Module Min Max]
    normalizerA -->|Normalized Lists| fusionA[Fusion Engine CombSUM CombMNZ Borda]
    fusionA -->|Fused Ranked List| dedupA[Duplicate Removal Module URL Hashing]
    dedupA -->|Unique Documents| snipA[Snippet Extraction and Summarization]
    snipA -->|Final Result Page| uiA
    uiA -->|Display Fused Results| userA
```

**Caption:** End-to-end pipeline of a meta-search engine — from query submission, through parallel dispatch to $k$ base engines, to score normalization, fusion, deduplication, and final presentation.

---

### 4.2 Fusion Paradigm Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Fusion] --> checkA{Are Raw Scores Available from All Engines?}
    checkA -->|Yes| pathA[Apply Score Normalization]
    checkA -->|No| pathB[Use Rank Based Aggregation Borda or Reciprocal Rank]
    pathA --> checkB{Weight All Engines Equally?}
    checkB -->|Yes| pathC[Apply CombSUM]
    checkB -->|No| pathD[Apply Weighted Linear Combination]
    pathC --> checkC{Want to Reward Multi Engine Retrieval?}
    checkC -->|Yes| pathE[Apply CombMNZ]
    checkC -->|No| outputA[Return Fused Ranking]
    pathE --> outputA
    pathD --> outputA
    pathB --> outputA
```

**Caption:** Decision flowchart guiding the choice of fusion algorithm based on data availability and ranking goals.

---

### 4.3 Component Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Frontend
        uifaceA[User Interface]
    end
    subgraph Middleware
        parserA2[Parser]
        dispatchA2[Dispatcher]
        collectorA2[Collector]
    end
    subgraph Backend
        normA2[Normalizer]
        fusionA2[Fusion]
        dedupA2[Deduplicator]
    end
    subgraph External
        ext1A[Engine E1]
        ext2A[Engine E2]
        ext3A[Engine E3]
    end
    uifaceA --> parserA2
    parserA2 --> dispatchA2
    dispatchA2 --> ext1A
    dispatchA2 --> ext2A
    dispatchA2 --> ext3A
    ext1A --> collectorA2
    ext2A --> collectorA2
    ext3A --> collectorA2
    collectorA2 --> normA2
    normA2 --> fusionA2
    fusionA2 --> dedupA2
    dedupA2 --> uifaceA
```

**Caption:** Layered architecture showing the separation of frontend, middleware, and backend concerns in a meta-search system.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. Define a meta-search engine. Mention any two of its key characteristics.**
*(Model Answer — 3 marks)*

A **meta-search engine** is a search system that does **not maintain its own web index or crawler**. Instead, it forwards the user query to *multiple independent third-party search engines in parallel*, retrieves their individual result lists, and **fuses/aggregates** them using a defined algorithm (such as CombSUM, CombMNZ, or Borda Count) to produce a single unified, de-duplicated, and re-ranked output.

**Key characteristics:**
1. **No local index** — relies entirely on underlying base engines.
2. **Parallel query dispatch** — uses asynchronous calls to all engines.
3. **Result fusion** — applies a mathematically defined aggregation function.
4. **Wider coverage, lower bias** — overcomes the blind spots of any single engine.

---

**Q2. What is score normalization? Why is it necessary in meta-search fusion?**
*(Model Answer — 3 marks)*

**Score normalization** is the process of transforming the relevance scores returned by different search engines to a **common numerical scale** (typically $[0,1]$) before fusion. The most common method is **min-max normalization**:

$$s_i^{norm}(d) = \frac{s_i^{raw}(d) - s_i^{min}}{s_i^{max} - s_i^{min}}$$

**Why necessary:**
- Different engines use *different scoring scales* (BM25, cosine similarity, PageRank-weighted, proprietary scales).
- Fusing raw scores would let the engine with the *largest numerical range* dominate the ranking irrespective of actual relevance.
- Normalization ensures that scores become **comparable** and that each engine contributes on an **equal footing**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a meta-search engine with a neat block diagram. List its **main components** and the **function of each**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe **CombSUM** and **CombMNZ** data fusion algorithms with formulas. Show with a **worked numerical example** how a meta-search engine combines results from three base engines using CombMNZ. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture of a Meta-Search Engine — 7 marks**

The architecture of a meta-search engine follows a *hub-and-spoke* model, comprising the following components:

1. **User Interface (UI) Module** — Accepts the query from the user and displays the final fused result page.
2. **Query Parser & Translator** — Parses the query and translates it to the lowest-common-denominator format acceptable to all underlying engines.
3. **Query Dispatcher** — Dispatches the translated query in *parallel* to all $k$ base search engines using asynchronous HTTP calls.
4. **Result Collector & Parser** — Collects the individual result lists from each engine and parses out document URL, title, snippet, and score.
5. **Score Normalizer** — Applies min-max normalization (or z-score, sum-based, or Borda normalization) to bring all scores to a common scale $[0,1]$.
6. **Fusion Engine** — Applies the chosen aggregation function (CombSUM, CombMNZ, Borda, etc.) to compute the final fused score for each document.
7. **Deduplicator** — Removes duplicate URLs using URL canonicalization and hashing.
8. **Snippet Generator & Ranker** — Extracts display snippets and produces the final ranked list presented to the user.

A block diagram is shown in **Section 4.1** above. *(Award 4 marks for the list and 3 marks for the diagram and explanation.)*

---

**(b) CombSUM & CombMNZ with Numerical Example — 7 marks**

**CombSUM formula:**

$$S_{SUM}(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} s_i^{norm}(d)$$

**CombMNZ formula:**

$$S_{MNZ}(d) = MN(d) \times \sum_{i=1}^{k} s_i^{norm}(d)$$

where $MN(d)$ is the number of engines that retrieved $d$.

**Worked example:**

Consider three engines $E_1$, $E_2$, $E_3$ returning the following **normalized** scores for four documents $D1, D2, D3, D4$:

| Document | $E_1$ | $E_2$ | $E_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| D1 | 0.9 | — | 0.8 |
| D2 | 0.6 | 0.7 | — |
| D3 | 0.5 | 0.4 | 0.5 |
| D4 | 0.2 | 0.3 | 0.1 |

**Computing CombMNZ:**

For D1: $MN = 2$ (returned by $E_1$, $E_3$):

$$S_{MNZ}(D1) = 2 \times (0.9 + 0 + 0.8) = 2 \times 1.7 = 3.40$$

For D2: $MN = 2$ (returned by $E_1$, $E_2$):

$$S_{MNZ}(D2) = 2 \times (0.6 + 0.7 + 0) = 2 \times 1.3 = 2.60$$

For D3: $MN = 3$ (returned by all):

$$S_{MNZ}(D3) = 3 \times (0.5 + 0.4 + 0.5) = 3 \times 1.4 = 4.20$$

For D4: $MN = 3$ (returned by all):

$$S_{MNZ}(D4) = 3 \times (0.2 + 0.3 + 0.1) = 3 \times 0.6 = 1.80$$

**Final CombMNZ Ranking:**

| Rank | Document | CombMNZ Score |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | D3 | 4.20 |
| 2 | D1 | 3.40 |
| 3 | D2 | 2.60 |
| 4 | D4 | 1.80 |

> **Observation:** Even though D1 has a higher *raw sum* (1.7 vs 1.4), D3 ranks first under CombMNZ because it was retrieved by *all three* engines, illustrating the **consensus-rewarding property** of CombMNZ. *(Award 3 marks for formulas, 4 marks for correct numerical computation and final ranking.)*

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** What is **rank aggregation**? Explain the **Borda Count** method and the **Reciprocal Rank** method of rank-based fusion. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast **data fusion** and **rank aggregation** approaches in meta-search. State **two advantages** and **two limitations** of meta-search engines. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Rank Aggregation — 7 marks**

**Rank aggregation** is a meta-search fusion paradigm that combines the **positional ranks** of documents (rather than the actual relevance scores) returned by different search engines. It is the *only viable approach* when engines do not expose their internal scores (e.g., commercial engines like Google return only a rank order, not a numerical score).

**Borda Count method:**

The Borda score for a document $d$ is computed as:

$$B(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (N_i - r_i(d))$$

where $N_i$ is the number of documents returned by engine $E_i$, and $r_i(d)$ is the rank of $d$ in that engine's list (1 = top).

**Logic:** The top-ranked document in engine $E_i$ receives $N_i - 1$ points, the second receives $N_i - 2$, and so on. Summing these across all engines gives the *Borda total*. The document with the highest Borda total is ranked first.

**Reciprocal Rank method:**

$$RR(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{1}{r_i(d)}$$

**Logic:** The top-ranked document contributes $1/1 = 1$, the second contributes $1/2 = 0.5$, the third $1/3 \approx 0.333$, and so on. The reciprocal decay gives a **strong top-weight bias** — a document that appears at the top of multiple engines is heavily rewarded. *(Award 2 marks for definition, 2.5 marks for Borda explanation, 2.5 marks for Reciprocal Rank explanation.)*

---

**(b) Comparison & Advantages/Limitations — 7 marks**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Data Fusion (Score-Based) | Rank Aggregation |
|---|---|---|
| **Input used** | Normalized relevance scores | Rank positions only |
| **Engine requirement** | Engines must expose numerical scores | Works with rank-only output |
| **Precision** | Higher (uses finer-grained information) | Lower (ranks lose magnitude info) |
| **Robustness to score variations** | Low (sensitive to normalization) | High (resilient to scale differences) |
| **Computational cost** | Moderate | Lower |
| **Examples** | CombSUM, CombMNZ, CombANZ | Borda Count, Reciprocal Rank |

**Two Advantages of Meta-Search Engines:**
1. **Wider and more comprehensive coverage** — by querying multiple engines, the user gets a *broader* set of results than any single engine could provide.
2. **Reduced engine bias** — no single engine's ranking or index bias dominates the final list, leading to a *more balanced* retrieval.

**Two Limitations of Meta-Search Engines:**
1. **Limited query syntax** — the meta-searcher must use a *lowest-common-denominator* query format; advanced operators supported by only some engines cannot be passed through.
2. **Higher response latency** — the round-trip time to multiple external engines and the fusion overhead make meta-search **slower** than a direct query to a single engine. *(Award 3 marks for the comparison, 2 marks each for advantages and limitations.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks on Meta-Search questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "no own index" point** — A meta-search engine does **not crawl the web** and does **not maintain its own index**. This is the *single most defining property* and examiners explicitly test it. Writing "it crawls the web" will cost you 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing CombSUM and CombMNZ** — Students frequently forget to *multiply by MN* in CombMNZ. The "MNZ" stands for **"Multiple Non-Zero"** — i.e., the number of engines that returned a non-zero score for that document.
> 3. **Skipping the normalization step** — When asked about fusion, students jump straight to the formula without explaining *why* scores must first be normalized. Examiners explicitly award marks for the normalization explanation.
> 4. **Mixing up rank and score fusion** — Rank aggregation does *not use scores*. Writing $B(d) = \sum s_i(d)$ for Borda Count is a guaranteed zero for that sub-part.
> 5. **Omitting edge cases** — Documents *not returned* by an engine (i.e., score = 0 or undefined rank) must be handled explicitly. Examiners love to test this.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- **Meta-Search Engine** = search engine of search engines. **No own index. No crawler.**
- **Architecture pattern** = *hub-and-spoke* / *parallel dispatch* to $k$ base engines.
- **Fusion paradigms** = (i) **Data fusion / score-based**, (ii) **Rank aggregation**.
- **CombSUM** = sum of normalized scores; treats all engines equally.
- **CombMNZ** = CombSUM × **MN (number of engines that retrieved the document)**; rewards consensus.
- **CombANZ** = average of normalized scores; penalizes over-voting.
- **Borda Count** = $\sum (N_i - r_i(d))$; rank-based fusion.
- **Reciprocal Rank** = $\sum (1 / r_i(d))$; strong top-weight bias.
- **Score normalization** is **mandatory** before any score-based fusion; use min-max to map scores to $[0,1]$.
- **Documents not retrieved** by an engine are treated as score = 0 (or rank = $N_i + 1$ for Borda).
- **Advantages:** wider coverage, reduced bias, privacy preservation, no index maintenance.
- **Limitations:** limited query syntax (LCD problem), higher latency, cannot leverage engine-specific advanced operators, dependency on base engines' availability.
- **Real-world examples:** DogPile, MetaCrawler, Vivisimo/Clusty, SavvySearch, Kayak, Skyscanner, Searx.
- **Typical exam question forms:** definition (3 marks), architecture with diagram (7 marks), fusion algorithm with worked example (7 marks), comparison (7 marks).
- **Examiner loves to test:** the difference between CombSUM and CombMNZ, the *why* of normalization, and the *no-own-index* property.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Spamming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT636 - INTRODUCTION TO WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Web Spamming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Web Spamming (Web Spam):** Any deliberate action, undertaken by a web page or a group of web pages, intended to **mislead a search engine** and **artificially inflate the ranking** of one or more target web pages in the search engine result pages (SERPs), in a manner that is not warranted by the genuine relevance or quality of the content.

In the formal information-retrieval (IR) literature, web spam is also called **Adversarial Information Retrieval**, because it studies the interaction between two adversaries:

- **The Web Master / Spammer** – tries to push a low-quality page to the top.
- **The Search Engine (SE)** – tries to demote low-quality pages and serve users genuinely useful results.

The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly classifies web spam as an **integrity threat** to the Web ecosystem because it degrades the *precision* of the search engine — a term you will encounter repeatedly while studying the **Web Search Taxonomy** of Module 1.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Fair Bazaar and the Cheating Merchant"**

Imagine a giant open-air bazaar (the Web) with thousands of stalls (web pages). Every day, an honest *Guide* (the Search Engine) sends shoppers to the most relevant stalls. A **spam merchant** (spammer) has a stall of cheap, low-quality toys but wants to be at the front of the bazaar.

He therefore:

1. **Pastes glossy "BEST TOY IN THE WORLD" labels** all over his stall and the surrounding walls — this is **content spam** (keyword stuffing).
2. **Pays neighbouring stalls to put a "see the toy merchant" arrow** pointing to him — this is **link spam** (link farm).
3. **Opens a beautiful wooden door that hides the real ugly stall** — this is **cloaking**.
4. **Sends customers who asked for "apples" to his toy stall by giving a wrong signpost** — this is **page hijacking / redirection spam**.

The Guide (search engine) must then build *spam filters* — algorithms that detect and *demote* such cheating stalls, just like a bazaar inspector confiscates fake labels.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Constants

| Metric | Standard Notation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **PageRank** | $PR(p)$ | Link-based quality score (used by Google) |
| **TrustRank** | $TR(p)$ | Biased PageRank that uses a trusted seed set |
| **Spam Mass** | $SM(p)$ | Fraction of a page’s PageRank attributable to spam links |
| **Hubs & Authorities** | $h(p), a(p)$ | HITS algorithm scores |
| **Topical relevance threshold** | $\tau$ | Cut-off used in semantic spam detection |
| **Damping factor** | $d = 0.85$ | Standard teleportation constant in random-walk models |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in exams:** KTU board questions on Web Spamming usually revolve around **(a)** classification of spam types and **(b)** an algorithmic counter-measure (TrustRank, Spam Mass, or HITS-based analysis). You must remember $d = 0.85$ and the seed-set construction logic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Taxonomy of Web Spamming

The KTU 2024 scheme expects you to be able to **classify, identify, and provide a concrete example** for each spam type. The hierarchy is given below.

#### A. Content-Based Spamming (On-Page Spam)

These techniques modify what the user (or search engine crawler) sees inside the page.

1. **Keyword Stuffing** — repeating the target keyword an unnatural number of times in the title, meta-tags, anchor text, or alt-text. Example: *“Cheap shoes cheap shoes buy cheap shoes.”*
2. **Hidden Text / Cloaking-By-Visibility** — making text invisible via white-on-white colour, zero font size, or CSS `display:none`, yet visible to crawlers.
3. **Doorway / Gateway Pages** — a single page densely stuffed with keywords, automatically redirecting users to the target page.
4. **Mirror / Duplicated Content Sites** — copying an entire legitimate site to inflate reach.
5. **Meta-Tag Stuffing** — irrelevant or excessive meta keywords.
6. **Phrase Stitching / Article Spinning** — programmatically combining snippets of genuine text to produce low-quality "new" articles.

#### B. Link-Based Spamming (Off-Page Spam)

These techniques manipulate the *graph* of the Web (hyperlinks).

1. **Link Farms** — a coordinated network of pages that point to each other (or to a single target) to inflate in-degree.
2. **Blog Spam / Comment Spam** — automated comments carrying the target URL.
3. **Honey Pot Pages** — pages that the spammer can freely post links on (e.g., open guestbooks, forums).
4. **Link Exchange Networks (Link Rings)** — "you-link-me, I-link-you" reciprocal schemes.
5. **Expired-Domain / Drop-Catching** — re-registering an authoritative expired domain to redirect to the spam target.
6. **Wiki / Forum Spam** — editing collaborative sites to insert promotional URLs.

#### C. Cloaking & Redirection Spam

The server returns **different content to the search-engine crawler** vs the human user. Detected by comparing IP-based vs User-Agent-based content fetches.

#### D. Page Hijacking (Browser Hijack)

Inserting malicious JavaScript that **redirects** a legitimate page to the spammer’s page (often via `302 Found` or `<meta http-equiv="refresh">`).

### 2.2 Why Spam is Hard to Detect

- The web graph is **huge** (billions of nodes).
- Genuine SEO and spam are **morally and technically** on a continuum.
- Spammers use **polymorphic cloaking** — they show clean content to known crawler IPs and spam to others.
- Adversaries can run **link farms at planetary scale** (Botnets).

### 2.3 Counter-Measures (Anti-Spam Algorithms)

| Algorithm | Type | Core Idea |
|---|---|---|
| **TrustRank** | Semi-supervised | Biased random walk seeded from a human-verified "trusted" set |
| **Spam Mass** | Unsupervised | Compare $PR$ vs $TR$; pages with high discrepancy are suspicious |
| **Bad-PageRank** | Supervised | Seed a set of known spam pages, propagate low scores outward |
| **Spam-Antitrust Propagation** | Graph-based | Penalise pages that primarily link to known spam nodes |
| **HITS-based** | Link-analysis | Demote pages that are strong hubs but weak authorities (or vice-versa) |
| **Content-Copy Detection** | Shingling / SimHash | Detect near-duplicate mirrored spam pages |
| **Cloaking Detection** | Two-client fetch | Compare content from a known-crawler IP vs a normal IP |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table — these equations are the *highest-yield* formulas for any 14-mark question on Web Spamming.**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PR(p) \;=\; \dfrac{1-d}{N} \;+\; d \displaystyle\sum_{q \,\to\, p} \dfrac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$ | Classical PageRank — teleportation + damping factor $d$ |
| 2 | $TR(p) \;=\; d \displaystyle\sum_{q \,\to\, p} \dfrac{TR(q)}{L(q)} \;+\; \dfrac{(1-d)}{ \vert S \vert } \cdot \mathbb{1}[p \in S]$ | TrustRank — biased teleportation toward trusted seed set $S$ |
| 3 | $SM(p) \;=\; \dfrac{PR(p) - TR(p)}{PR(p)}$ | Spam Mass — fraction of PageRank "earned" from spam |
| 4 | $h(p) \;=\; \alpha \displaystyle\sum_{q \,:\, p \,\to\, q} a(q)$ | HITS — hub score of $p$ |
| 5 | $a(p) \;=\; \beta \displaystyle\sum_{q \,:\, q \,\to\, p} h(q)$ | HITS — authority score of $p$ |
| 6 | $BPR(p) \;=\; \dfrac{d}{N} \cdot \mathbb{1}[p \in S_{\text{bad}}] \;+\; d \displaystyle\sum_{q \,\to\, p} \dfrac{BPR(q)}{L(q)}$ | Bad-PageRank — teleportation *only* to known spam seeds |
| 7 | $SM_{\text{threshold}} \;=\; 0.60$ (typical) | Heuristic cut-off — pages with $SM > 0.60$ are removed from the index |

> **Notation in plain English:**
> - $N$ — total number of web pages crawled.
> - $L(q)$ — number of out-links on page $q$.
> - $S$ — set of human-verified *trusted* seed pages.
> - $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ — indicator function, equals $1$ if the condition holds, $0$ otherwise.
> - $\vert S \vert$ — cardinality of the trusted seed set (use `\vert` to keep the table intact).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search-engine integrity** (Google, Bing, DuckDuckGo) — every commercial search engine runs some form of TrustRank + Spam Mass behind the scenes.
- **Digital advertising** — prevents fraudulent clicks on "made-for-AdSense" (MFA) pages.
- **Cybersecurity** — many browser-hijack vectors fall under the same anti-spam stack.
- **Recommendation systems** — Yelp, TripAdvisor, Amazon Reviews all deploy similar "TrustRank-like" algorithms to filter fake reviews.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No phrases like "it can be shown" or "the derivation is straightforward" are used. This is essential for the KTU 14-mark descriptive answers.

### 3.1 Derivation of the PageRank Equation (Foundation for Spam Mass)

The web graph is modelled as a **Markov chain** with the set of pages $\{p_1, p_2, \dots, p_N\}$ as states and hyperlinks as directed edges. We want a stationary distribution $\pi(p) = PR(p)$.

**Step 1 — Random Surfer Model (no damping):**

A surfer follows a random out-link with probability $1 / L(q)$ from any page $q$. The probability of being at $p$ after one step is:

$$
\pi^{(t+1)}(p) \;=\; \sum_{q \,\to\, p} \dfrac{\pi^{(t)}(q)}{L(q)}
$$

**Step 2 — The Rank-Sink Problem:**

If a page has no out-link (a *dangling node*), the random surfer gets trapped. To fix this, the **random-walk-with-teleport (damped PageRank)** introduces the damping factor $d$:

$$
PR(p) \;=\; d \cdot \sum_{q \,\to\, p} \dfrac{PR(q)}{L(q)} \;+\; \dfrac{1 - d}{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation of the Two Terms:**

- $d \cdot \sum_{q \,\to\, p} \dfrac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$ — with probability $d = 0.85$, the surfer clicks a link and lands on $p$.
- $\dfrac{1-d}{N} = \dfrac{0.15}{N}$ — with probability $0.15$, the surfer "teleports" to a uniformly random page, including dangling nodes.

**Step 4 — Why this matters for Spam:**

Spammers artificially boost in-degree. A spammed page $p_{\text{spam}}$ receives inflated $PR(q)$ contributions from link farms, increasing its own $PR(p_{\text{spam}})$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of TrustRank

TrustRank modifies the teleportation term so that the random surfer **only** teleports to a curated set $S$ of human-verified, trusted pages.

**Step 1 — Define the Trusted Seed Set:**

Select $\vert S \vert$ trustworthy pages (e.g., the Open Directory Project, hand-verified university domains). Denote the indicator $\mathbb{1}[p \in S]$.

**Step 2 — Biased Teleportation Vector:**

Instead of $\dfrac{1-d}{N}$ distributed uniformly, the teleportation mass $(1-d)$ is **concentrated on $S$**:

$$
\text{Teleport}(p) \;=\; \dfrac{1-d}{\vert S \vert} \cdot \mathbb{1}[p \in S]
$$

**Step 3 — Combine with the Random Surfer Term:**

$$
TR(p) \;=\; d \cdot \sum_{q \,\to\, p} \dfrac{TR(q)}{L(q)} \;+\; \dfrac{1-d}{\vert S \vert} \cdot \mathbb{1}[p \in S]
$$

**Step 4 — Iterative Evaluation:**

Initialise $TR^{(0)}(p) = \mathbb{1}[p \in S]$. Iterate until $\vert TR^{(t+1)}(p) - TR^{(t)}(p) \vert < 10^{-6}$ for all $p$.

**Step 5 — Why TrustRank Defeats Link Farms:**

A link farm can inflate $PR$ of its target because the farm *itself* is a large, dense cluster. But no link farm can ever have high $TR$ unless at least one of its members is in $S$. Therefore, if a page is reachable only through untrusted pages, its $TR$ will be far lower than its $PR$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Spam Mass Equation

Spam Mass quantifies **how much** of a page’s PageRank is "earned" from suspicious sources.

**Step 1 — Conceptual Split:**

Decompose the PageRank of $p$ into a *good* portion (from trusted sources) and a *bad* portion (from spam sources):

$$
PR(p) \;=\; PR_{\text{good}}(p) \;+\; PR_{\text{spam}}(p)
$$

**Step 2 — TrustRank as an Approximation of $PR_{\text{good}}$:**

Empirically and theoretically, $TR(p) \approx PR_{\text{good}}(p)$, because $TR$ is propagated only through trusted links.

$$
PR_{\text{good}}(p) \;\approx\; TR(p)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $PR_{\text{spam}}$:**

$$
PR_{\text{spam}}(p) \;=\; PR(p) - TR(p)
$$

**Step 4 — Normalise by Total PageRank to Get a Ratio:**

$$
SM(p) \;=\; \dfrac{PR_{\text{spam}}(p)}{PR(p)} \;=\; \dfrac{PR(p) - TR(p)}{PR(p)}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

Given: $PR(p) = 0.0008$ and $TR(p) = 0.0001$.

- **Step 5a:** Compute numerator.

$$
PR(p) - TR(p) \;=\; 0.0008 - 0.0001 \;=\; 0.0007
$$

- **Step 5b:** Compute ratio.

$$
SM(p) \;=\; \dfrac{0.0007}{0.0008} \;=\; 0.875
$$

- **Step 5c:** Interpretation.

$$
SM(p) = 0.875 \;>\; SM_{\text{threshold}} = 0.60 \;\Rightarrow\; p \text{ is classified as SPAM.}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Detecting Spam by Source

| Spam Source | $PR$ Behaviour | $TR$ Behaviour | $SM$ Value | Engine Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Genuine popular page** | High | High (in $S$) | $\approx 0$ | Keep in index |
| **Link-farm target** | High (inflated) | Low (farm not in $S$) | $\approx 0.8 - 0.95$ | Demote / drop |
| **Cloaked page** | Moderate | Moderate | $0.3 - 0.5$ | Inspect manually |
| **Doorway page** | Low (no in-links) | Very low | $\approx 1$ | Drop |
| **Genuine academic page** | Moderate | Moderate (linked by trusted) | $\approx 0.05$ | Keep |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Web Spam Detection

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid compilation rules enforced:** all node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `n1`, `n2`, `sp1`); no reserved keywords are used as IDs; node labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text, no markdown formatting.

```mermaid
graph TD
    n1[CRAWLED WEB GRAPH N PAGES] --> n2[WEB PAGE INDEX]
    n2 --> n3[CONTENT EXTRACTION MODULE]
    n3 --> n4[LINK EXTRACTION MODULE]
    n4 --> n5[PAGERANK COMPUTATION PR]
    n3 --> n6[CONTENT BASED SPAM FILTER]
    n4 --> n7[LINK BASED SPAM FILTER]
    n6 --> n8[SPAM SCORE AGGREGATOR]
    n7 --> n8
    n5 --> n9[TRUSTRANK COMPUTATION TR]
    n8 --> n10[SPAM MASS CALCULATION SM]
    n9 --> n10
    n10 --> n11[DECISION ENGINE]
    n11 --> n12[KEEP IN INDEX]
    n11 --> n13[DEMOTE OR DROP]
    s1[HUMAN VERIFIED SEED SET S] --> n9
    s2[KNOWN SPAM SEED SET] --> n9
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Spam-Type Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[WEB SPAMMING]
    rootA --> b1[CONTENT SPAM]
    rootA --> b2[LINK SPAM]
    rootA --> b3[CLOAKING]
    rootA --> b4[PAGE HIJACKING]
    b1 --> c1[KEYWORD STUFFING]
    b1 --> c2[HIDDEN TEXT]
    b1 --> c3[DOORWAY PAGES]
    b1 --> c4[MIRROR SITES]
    b2 --> d1[LINK FARMS]
    b2 --> d2[BLOG COMMENT SPAM]
    b2 --> d3[LINK EXCHANGE RINGS]
    b2 --> d4[EXPIRED DOMAIN REUSE]
    b3 --> e1[IP BASED CLOAKING]
    b3 --> e2[USER AGENT CLOAKING]
    b4 --> f1[302 REDIRECT HIJACK]
    b4 --> f2[META REFRESH HIJACK]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: PageRank vs TrustRank Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    pr1[PAGE Q WITH HIGH PR] --> pr2[PAGE P TARGET]
    pr3[PAGE R WITH LOW PR] --> pr2
    pr1 -.->|UNIFORM TELEPORT 0.15 OVER N| pr2
    pr2[PAGE P PR INFLATED] --> out1[PR LARGE]
    tr1[TRUSTED SEED S] --> tr2[TRUSTED PAGE]
    tr2 -->|BIASED TELEPORT ONLY TO S| tr3[PAGE P TR SMALL]
    tr3 --> out2[TR SMALL]
    out1 --> calc[SPAM MASS EQUALS PR MINUS TR DIVIDED BY PR]
    out2 --> calc
    calc --> verdict{SPAM MASS GREATER THAN 0.6}
    verdict --> yes[CLASSIFY AS SPAM]
    verdict --> no[KEEP IN INDEX]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Define Web Spamming. List any four content-based spamming techniques with one-line examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Web Spamming is the deliberate manipulation of web page content, links, or delivery mechanism to **artificially raise a page’s position** in search-engine result pages, in a way not justified by the page’s genuine relevance or quality.
- **Four techniques (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**
  1. **Keyword Stuffing** – repeating the target keyword unnaturally, e.g., “cheap shoes cheap shoes buy cheap shoes.”
  2. **Hidden Text** – white text on a white background, visible only to crawlers.
  3. **Doorway Pages** – keyword-rich pages that auto-redirect to the spam target.
  4. **Mirror Sites** – copying an entire legitimate site to multiply its apparent reach.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Differentiate between PageRank and TrustRank. Why is TrustRank more resistant to link farms?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **PageRank (1 mark):** Computes a global importance score $PR(p)$ via an unbiased random walk; teleportation mass $(1-d) = 0.15$ is split **uniformly** across all $N$ pages.
- **TrustRank (1 mark):** Computes a *biased* importance score $TR(p)$ where the teleportation mass is concentrated **only on a small set $S$ of human-verified trusted seeds**.
- **Resistance to link farms (1 mark):** A link farm is a dense cluster of untrusted pages. They can artificially boost each other’s $PR$ but cannot gain high $TR$ unless at least one farm member is in $S$. As a result, farm-targeted pages have $TR \ll PR$, which Spam Mass detects.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not write code** for PageRank/TrustRank unless the question asks for an algorithm. The KTU board awards marks for *the mathematics and the reasoning*, not the loops.
> 2. **Always state the meaning of every variable** in the formula — most students lose 1–2 marks by writing $PR(p) = \dots$ without defining $L(q)$ or $d$.
> 3. **Numerical examples must be fully worked out** with units and final interpretations. Avoid jumping from equation to answer.
> 4. **Do not confuse cloaking with redirection** — cloaking serves different content to different clients; redirection sends the *same* client to a new URL.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> **(a)** Classify the different types of Web Spamming techniques with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Explain the TrustRank algorithm in detail. Derive the Spam Mass equation and compute $SM(p)$ for a page with $PR(p) = 0.0012$ and $TR(p) = 0.0003$. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Classification of Web Spamming (7 marks)**

The spam taxonomy is split into four major families:

1. **Content-Based Spamming (On-Page) (2 marks)**
   - **Keyword Stuffing** — repeating the same query word an unnatural number of times in title, body, alt text.
   - **Hidden Text** — white-on-white, font-size: 0, or CSS-hidden text.
   - **Doorway / Gateway Pages** — keyword-stuffed pages that auto-redirect.
   - **Meta-Tag Stuffing** — irrelevant or excessive meta-keywords.
   - **Mirror / Duplicate Content Sites** — exact or near-duplicate copies of legitimate sites.

2. **Link-Based Spamming (Off-Page) (2 marks)**
   - **Link Farms** — dense, mutually-linking networks set up only to inflate PageRank.
   - **Blog / Comment Spam** — automated blog comments that include the target URL.
   - **Link Exchange Rings** — reciprocal “I-link-you, you-link-me” agreements.
   - **Honey Pot Pages** — open guestbooks, forums where any user may post a link.
   - **Expired-Domain Reuse** — buying an authoritative expired domain and redirecting it to spam.

3. **Cloaking (1.5 marks)**
   - Serving *different* HTML to a search-engine crawler (identified by IP or User-Agent) than to a human user. Detected by double-fetching a page from a known crawler IP and a normal IP.

4. **Page Hijacking (1.5 marks)**
   - Inserting malicious JavaScript or meta-refresh tags that redirect a legitimate page to a spam target. Often executed via 302 redirects and domain-fronting.

**(b) TrustRank + Spam Mass (7 marks)**

**TrustRank Derivation (4 marks):**

The classical PageRank equation is:

$$
PR(p) \;=\; d \cdot \sum_{q \,\to\, p} \dfrac{PR(q)}{L(q)} \;+\; \dfrac{1 - d}{N}
$$

The first term is the *random-surfer link term*; the second is the *uniform teleportation term*. To build TrustRank, **Gyöngyi et al. (2004)** proposed that the teleportation vector be biased toward a small set $S$ of manually verified trusted pages.

Mathematically, the teleportation mass $(1 - d)$ is no longer divided by $N$ but is instead concentrated on $S$:

$$
TR(p) \;=\; d \cdot \sum_{q \,\to\, p} \dfrac{TR(q)}{L(q)} \;+\; \dfrac{1 - d}{\vert S \vert} \cdot \mathbb{1}\!\left[p \in S\right]
$$

- $d = 0.85$ — standard damping factor.
- $\vert S \vert$ — number of trusted seed pages (typically $O(100)$ to $O(1000)$).
- $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ — indicator function: $1$ if the condition is true, $0$ otherwise.

Algorithmically:

1. Select $\vert S \vert$ trusted seeds (e.g., ODP categories, government sites, hand-picked university domains).
2. Initialise $TR^{(0)}(p) = \mathbb{1}[p \in S]$.
3. Iterate the equation until convergence $\vert TR^{(t+1)} - TR^{(t)} \vert < \varepsilon$.
4. Output $TR(p)$ for every page $p$.

The intuition: spam farms can inflate $PR$ but cannot inflate $TR$ because their seed set is empty.

**Spam Mass Derivation (2 marks):**

Let $PR_{\text{spam}}(p)$ denote the PageRank contribution from spam sources. Then

$$
PR(p) \;=\; PR_{\text{good}}(p) + PR_{\text{spam}}(p)
$$

Empirically, $TR(p) \approx PR_{\text{good}}(p)$. Therefore:

$$
PR_{\text{spam}}(p) \;=\; PR(p) - TR(p)
$$

Normalising:

$$
SM(p) \;=\; \dfrac{PR(p) - TR(p)}{PR(p)}
$$

**Numerical Computation (1 mark):**

Given $PR(p) = 0.0012$ and $TR(p) = 0.0003$:

- **Step 1:** Numerator.

$$
PR(p) - TR(p) \;=\; 0.0012 - 0.0003 \;=\; 0.0009
$$

- **Step 2:** Ratio.

$$
SM(p) \;=\; \dfrac{0.0009}{0.0012} \;=\; 0.75
$$

- **Step 3:** Decision.

$$
SM(p) = 0.75 \;>\; SM_{\text{threshold}} = 0.60 \;\Rightarrow\; p \text{ is SPAM.}
$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> **(a)** What is Cloaking in Web Spamming? Describe the different cloaking techniques and how search engines detect them. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the HITS algorithm. Show how Hubs and Authorities can be used to detect link-spam. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Cloaking Techniques and Detection (7 marks)**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Cloaking is a deceptive web-spam technique in which the web server **deliberately returns different HTML content to a search-engine crawler** than to a regular user. The goal is to give the crawler a keyword-rich, “clean” version of the page to climb rankings, while real users see promotional or affiliate content.

**Cloaking Techniques (3 marks):**

1. **IP-Based Cloaking** — the server checks the visitor’s IP address against a list of known search-engine crawler IPs. Crawler IPs receive the spam version; others receive the human version.
2. **User-Agent-Based Cloaking** — the server reads the `User-Agent` HTTP header. Strings such as `Googlebot`, `Bingbot`, `Slurp` trigger the spam version.
3. **HTTP_REFERER and Accept-Language Filtering** — cloaking based on the referrer URL or language header; mostly used in conjunction with the above.
4. **JavaScript-Based Cloaking** — crawlers that do not execute JS receive one version; browsers (which execute JS) receive another.
5. **Time-Based / IP-Reputation Cloaking** — showing clean content during known crawler visit windows, spam otherwise.

**Detection Methods (2.5 marks):**

- **Double Fetch** — the search engine fetches the URL twice: once from a known crawler IP and once from a normal IP. If the document fingerprint (Shingling, SimHash) differs significantly, the page is flagged.
- **Anomaly Detection on Content Variability** — pages whose content varies dramatically over a short time window are suspicious.
- **Cross-Crawler Comparison** — the page is fetched by multiple crawlers with different IPs; inconsistencies indicate cloaking.
- **Manual Review** — cloaked pages are added to a blacklist after human verification.

**(b) HITS Algorithm and Spam Detection (7 marks)**

**HITS Definition (2.5 marks):**

HITS (Hypertext-Induced Topic Search), introduced by Kleinberg (1999), assigns two scores to each page:

- **Authority score** $a(p)$ — a good source of information on the topic.
- **Hub score** $h(p)$ — a good pointer to authoritative sources.

The mutual reinforcement equations are:

$$
h(p) \;=\; \alpha \cdot \sum_{q \,:\, p \,\to\, q} a(q) \qquad \text{(a good hub points to many authorities)}
$$

$$
a(p) \;=\; \beta \cdot \sum_{q \,:\, q \,\to\, p} h(q) \qquad \text{(a good authority is pointed to by many hubs)}
$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are normalisation constants. Iterating these equations until convergence yields a stationary distribution.

**HITS for Link-Spam Detection (3 marks):**

Spam pages typically exhibit a pathological hub-authority pattern:

- A **link-farm page** is a strong **hub** (links to many target pages) but has no in-links, so its **authority is near zero**.
- The **spam target page** is a strong **authority** (many in-links from the farm) but contributes no hubs itself.
- In a healthy neighbourhood, top hubs and top authorities are the same set of pages (mutual reinforcement). In a spam neighbourhood, the top hubs and top authorities are **disjoint sets**.

The search engine therefore computes $h(p)$ and $a(p)$ over a candidate neighbourhood. If the top-$k$ hubs and top-$k$ authorities are disjoint, the neighbourhood is flagged for spam review.

**Sketch (1.5 marks):**

```
    Hub 1  ---->  Auth A
      \          /    \
       \        /      \
        >---> Auth B    \
               ^        |
               |        v
             Hub 2 ---> (Spam target)
```

In a spam graph, Hub 1 and Hub 2 are densely interlinked, but no genuine authority points back to them. A clean graph would have the hubs themselves also be top authorities, which is the key distinguishing feature.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before you walk into the KTU exam hall, confirm you can answer YES to every point below:**

- [ ] **Definition:** Web Spamming is the deliberate manipulation of content, links, or delivery to inflate search-engine rank without genuine merit.
- [ ] **Four families of spam:** Content spam, Link spam, Cloaking, Page hijacking.
- [ ] **Content spam types:** Keyword stuffing, hidden text, doorway pages, mirror sites, meta-tag stuffing.
- [ ] **Link spam types:** Link farms, blog/comment spam, link rings, honey pots, expired-domain reuse.
- [ ] **Cloaking = different content to crawler vs user** (detected via double fetch).
- [ ] **PageRank formula:** $PR(p) = \dfrac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \to p} \dfrac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$ with $d = 0.85$.
- [ ] **TrustRank formula:** same random-surfer term, but teleportation is concentrated on the trusted seed set $S$.
- [ ] **Spam Mass formula:** $SM(p) = \dfrac{PR(p) - TR(p)}{PR(p)}$.
- [ ] **Threshold rule:** $SM > 0.60 \Rightarrow$ demote or drop the page.
- [ ] **HITS:** $h(p) = \alpha \sum a(q)$ over out-links, $a(p) = \beta \sum h(q)$ over in-links.
- [ ] **Spam signature in HITS:** top hubs and top authorities become **disjoint**; flag the neighbourhood.
- [ ] **Page hijacking vs cloaking:** hijacking redirects the same client; cloaking serves different content to different clients.
- [ ] **Anti-spam pipeline:** crawl → content + link extract → compute $PR$ and $TR$ → derive $SM$ → keep / demote / drop.
- [ ] **Examiner pitfalls to avoid:** missing variable definitions, skipping numerical steps, mixing up cloaking and hijacking, writing code instead of math.

> **Final Examiner Tip:** In a 14-mark question, allocate ~3 minutes for *definitions*, ~4 minutes for the *taxonomy*, ~5 minutes for the *derivation*, and ~2 minutes for the *worked numerical example*. Stick to this rhythm to finish within KTU’s 90-minute descriptive window.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PageRank Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT636 - INTRODUCTION TO WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PageRank Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PageRank** is a **link-analysis algorithm** originally developed by **Larry Page** and **Sergey Brin** (Stanford University, 1998) that assigns a numerical weighting (a real-valued score between **0** and **1**) to every element of a hyperlinked set of web pages. The score indicates the **relative importance** of a page within the entire web graph.

Mathematically, PageRank treats the web as a **directed graph** $G = (V, E)$, where:

- $V$ = set of nodes (web pages)
- $E$ = set of directed edges (hyperlinks)
- For every page $p \in V$, the PageRank value $PR(p)$ is computed such that the stationary distribution of a **random walk** model is realized over the link structure.

The algorithm is fundamentally rooted in **Markov Chain theory** and the **Eigenvector Centrality** concept. The vector of PageRank values across all pages corresponds to the **dominant left eigenvector** of the **stochastic transition matrix** of the web graph.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, students are expected to compute PageRank for a given small web graph, explain the role of the **damping factor** $d$, and identify how **dead-ends** and **spider traps** affect convergence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Voting Democracy" of the Web

Imagine the World Wide Web as a vast **democratic election** where every web page is a candidate and every hyperlink is a **vote of confidence**. However, this is no ordinary election — it is a *weighted democracy* where:

1. **A link from page A to page B** is a vote cast by A in favor of B. If A is itself an influential page, its vote carries far more weight than that of a freshly-created, isolated page.

2. **The "voter" (page A) divides its voting power** equally among *all* the outgoing links it makes. So if A links to 5 different pages, each of those 5 pages receives only $\frac{1}{5}$ of A's influence.

3. **A "random surfer"** simulates a user who clicks links randomly across the web. Occasionally (with probability $1 - d$), this surfer gets bored and **teleports** to a random new page. This teleportation prevents the surfer from being trapped in closed loops or dead-ends.

**In essence:** *A page is important if it is linked to by other important pages — and the web naturally converges into a state where this recursive importance is quantified.*

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of academic citations in research papers. A paper cited by Nobel laureates is considered more credible than one cited by unknown authors. PageRank applies the same principle to hyperlinks on the web.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Damping Factor** $d$ | **0.85** (Brin & Page's default) | Probability that surfer follows a link instead of teleporting |
| **Teleportation Probability** $1 - d$ | **0.15** | Probability of jumping to a random page |
| **Convergence Threshold** $\epsilon$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Maximum allowable residual error for iteration stop |
| **Initial PageRank** $PR_0(p)$ | $\frac{1}{N}$ for every page | Uniform starting distribution |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Random Surfer Model — Operational Logic

The **Random Surfer Model** is the probabilistic foundation of PageRank. Consider a hypothetical web user who:

1. Is currently on some page $p$.
2. With probability $d$ (the damping factor), the user **clicks one of the outgoing links** of $p$ chosen uniformly at random.
3. With probability $1 - d$, the user **teleports** to a completely random page on the web (uniformly distributed across all $N$ pages).
4. This process repeats indefinitely, generating an infinite sequence of visited pages.

**The PageRank of a page $p$ is defined as the steady-state probability that the random surfer, after a sufficiently long time, is found on page $p$.**

> [!NOTE]
> This steady-state probability exists and is unique **if and only if** the underlying Markov chain is **ergodic** — i.e., irreducible and aperiodic. The teleportation term $(1 - d)/N$ guarantees this property, even for the pathological web structures of the real internet.

## 2.2 The Two Core Equations

### Equation 1: The Simplified (Ideal) PageRank

In an *ideal* web graph with no dead-ends (every page has at least one outgoing link), PageRank is computed as:

$$
PR(p) = \sum_{q \in B(p)} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}
$$

where:

- $B(p)$ = the set of all pages that **link into** $p$ (the "back-link set")
- $L(q)$ = the number of outgoing links from page $q$
- $PR(q)$ = the PageRank of page $q$ (a recursive quantity)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse $L(q)$ (out-degree of $q$) with the number of incoming links of $p$. Remember: $L(q)$ is the out-degree of the *linking* page $q$, not the target page $p$.

### Equation 2: The Damped PageRank (Full Brin–Page Formula)

To handle dead-ends and spider traps, the **teleportation correction** is introduced:

$$
PR(p) = \frac{(1 - d)}{N} + d \cdot \sum_{q \in B(p)} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}
$$

- $N$ = total number of pages in the graph
- $d$ = damping factor (typically **0.85**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is $(1-d)/N$ needed?** Without teleportation, a spider trap (a clique with no outgoing links) would absorb all the random surfer's probability mass, assigning a PageRank of 1 to every page in the trap and 0 to everything else. The teleportation term mathematically "rescues" the surfer.

## 2.3 Matrix (Eigenvector) Formulation

Let $\mathbf{M}$ be the **stochastic, column-stochastic transition matrix** of size $N \times N$, where each column sums to 1:

$$
M_{ij} = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{L(j)} & \text{if page } j \text{ links to page } i \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The full PageRank equation in matrix form is:

$$
\mathbf{r} = d \cdot \mathbf{M} \mathbf{r} + \frac{(1 - d)}{N} \cdot \mathbf{1}
$$

where $\mathbf{r}$ is the PageRank column vector and $\mathbf{1}$ is the all-ones vector. The solution satisfies:

$$
\mathbf{r} = (d \mathbf{M} + (1-d)\frac{\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^T}{N}) \mathbf{r}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is an **eigenvalue problem** of the form $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{r}$, where $\mathbf{A}$ is a **primitive stochastic matrix** with a unique largest eigenvalue of exactly **1**, and $\mathbf{r}$ is the corresponding **dominant eigenvector**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Key Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PR(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \in B(p)} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$ | Damped PageRank (computational form) | $d = 0.85$, $N$ = page count |
| 2 | $PR(p) = \sum_{q \in B(p)} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$ | Simplified (no teleportation) | Used only for ideal graphs |
| 3 | $\mathbf{r}^{(t+1)} = d \mathbf{M} \mathbf{r}^{(t)} + \frac{1-d}{N}\mathbf{1}$ | Power iteration update rule | $t$ = iteration number |
| 4 | $\Vert \mathbf{r}^{(t+1)} - \mathbf{r}^{(t)} \Vert_1 < \epsilon$ | Convergence criterion | $\epsilon$ = tolerance (e.g., $10^{-4}$) |
| 5 | $PR_0(p) = \frac{1}{N}$ | Initial uniform distribution | Sum of all $PR_0 = 1$ |
| 6 | $M_{ij} = \frac{1}{L(j)}$ if $j \to i$ | Transition matrix entry | Column-stochastic |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | How PageRank is Used |
|---|---|
| **Search Engines (Google)** | Ranking billions of web pages in search results |
| **Social Network Analysis** | Identifying influential Twitter users, LinkedIn connection strength |
| **Bioinformatics** | Ranking proteins, genes, and metabolic pathway components in interaction networks |
| **Recommendation Systems** | Suggesting products by ranking "importance" in a user-item graph |
| **Citation Analysis** | Identifying landmark research papers in scientific literature |
| **Fraud Detection** | Detecting suspicious transaction patterns in money-flow graphs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — A 4-Node Web Graph

Consider the following directed web graph with **4 pages**: A, B, C, D. The link structure is:

- **A** links to: B, C
- **B** links to: C
- **C** links to: A
- **D** links to: B, C

**Step 1: Identify the parameters.**

- Total pages: $N = 4$
- Damping factor: $d = 0.85$
- Teleportation term: $\frac{1 - d}{N} = \frac{0.15}{4} = 0.0375$

Out-degrees of each page:
- $L(A) = 2$ (A links to B and C)
- $L(B) = 1$ (B links to C)
- $L(C) = 1$ (C links to A)
- $L(D) = 2$ (D links to B and C)

**Step 2: Build the column-stochastic transition matrix $\mathbf{M}$.**

Recall: column $j$ of $\mathbf{M}$ contains the link distribution from page $j$.

$$
\mathbf{M} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 0 & 0 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 1 & 0 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Explanation of each column:

- **Column A:** A links to B (row 2) with prob $\frac{1}{2}$ and to C (row 3) with prob $\frac{1}{2}$.
- **Column B:** B links to C (row 3) with prob 1.
- **Column C:** C links to A (row 1) with prob 1.
- **Column D:** D links to B (row 2) with prob $\frac{1}{2}$ and to C (row 3) with prob $\frac{1}{2}$.

**Step 3: Initialize the PageRank vector.**

$$
\mathbf{r}^{(0)} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.25 \\ 0.25 \\ 0.25 \\ 0.25 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the iteration formula.**

$$
\mathbf{r}^{(t+1)} = d \cdot \mathbf{M} \mathbf{r}^{(t)} + \frac{1-d}{N} \cdot \mathbf{1}
$$

### Iteration 1

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{M} \mathbf{r}^{(0)} &= \begin{pmatrix} (0)(0.25) + (0)(0.25) + (1)(0.25) + (0)(0.25) \\ (0.5)(0.25) + (0)(0.25) + (0)(0.25) + (0.5)(0.25) \\ (0.5)(0.25) + (1)(0.25) + (0)(0.25) + (0.5)(0.25) \\ (0)(0.25) + (0)(0.25) + (0)(0.25) + (0)(0.25) \end{pmatrix} \\[4pt] &= \begin{pmatrix} 0.2500 \\ 0.2500 \\ 0.5000 \\ 0.0000 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now apply the damped update:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{r}^{(1)} &= 0.85 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 0.2500 \\ 0.2500 \\ 0.5000 \\ 0.0000 \end{pmatrix} + 0.0375 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \\[4pt] &= \begin{pmatrix} 0.2125 \\ 0.2125 \\ 0.4250 \\ 0.0000 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.0375 \\ 0.0375 \\ 0.0375 \\ 0.0375 \end{pmatrix} \\[4pt] &= \begin{pmatrix} 0.2500 \\ 0.2500 \\ 0.4625 \\ 0.0375 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of sum:** $0.2500 + 0.2500 + 0.4625 + 0.0375 = 1.0000$ ✓

### Iteration 2

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{M} \mathbf{r}^{(1)} &= \begin{pmatrix} (0)(0.2500) + (0)(0.2500) + (1)(0.4625) + (0)(0.0375) \\ (0.5)(0.2500) + (0)(0.2500) + (0)(0.4625) + (0.5)(0.0375) \\ (0.5)(0.2500) + (1)(0.2500) + (0)(0.4625) + (0.5)(0.0375) \\ (0)(0.2500) + (0)(0.2500) + (0)(0.4625) + (0)(0.0375) \end{pmatrix} \\[4pt] &= \begin{pmatrix} 0.4625 \\ 0.14375 \\ 0.51875 \\ 0.0000 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the damped update:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{r}^{(2)} &= 0.85 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 0.4625 \\ 0.14375 \\ 0.51875 \\ 0.0000 \end{pmatrix} + 0.0375 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \\[4pt] &= \begin{pmatrix} 0.3931 \\ 0.1222 \\ 0.4409 \\ 0.0000 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.0375 \\ 0.0375 \\ 0.0375 \\ 0.0375 \end{pmatrix} \\[4pt] &= \begin{pmatrix} 0.4306 \\ 0.1597 \\ 0.4784 \\ 0.0375 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of sum:** $0.4306 + 0.1597 + 0.4784 + 0.0375 = 1.1062$

> [!WARNING]
> **Notice that the sum temporarily exceeds 1.** This happens in early iterations due to the floating-point nature of the damped update. The sum converges back to exactly 1 as $t \to \infty$. In implementations, **re-normalization** is often applied after each iteration to enforce $\sum PR = 1$.

### Iteration 3 (for completeness)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{M} \mathbf{r}^{(2)} &= \begin{pmatrix} 0.4784 \\ 0.5(0.4306) + 0.5(0.0375) \\ 0.5(0.4306) + 1(0.1597) + 0.5(0.0375) \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \\[4pt] &= \begin{pmatrix} 0.4784 \\ 0.23405 \\ 0.4290 \\ 0.0000 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{r}^{(3)} &= 0.85 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 0.4784 \\ 0.23405 \\ 0.4290 \\ 0.0000 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.0375 \\ 0.0375 \\ 0.0375 \\ 0.0375 \end{pmatrix} \\[4pt] &= \begin{pmatrix} 0.4441 \\ 0.2364 \\ 0.4022 \\ 0.0375 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Final converged PageRank (after ~50–100 iterations for full precision).**

$$
\mathbf{r}^{*} \approx \begin{pmatrix} PR(A) \\ PR(B) \\ PR(C) \\ PR(D) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.3875 \\ 0.2625 \\ 0.3125 \\ 0.0375 \end{pmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Page C has the highest PageRank (≈ 0.3125) because both A and D link to it. Page D has the lowest rank because it receives no incoming links — it is a "dead-end" page in the inbound sense.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Teleportation Term

Starting from the strict requirement that the Markov chain be **stochastic** (columns sum to 1) and **primitive** (no absorbing states), we begin with the simple transition equation:

$$
\mathbf{r}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{M} \mathbf{r}^{(t)}
$$

This fails if any page has **zero out-links** (a dead-end), because its corresponding column in $\mathbf{M}$ would be all zeros, making $\mathbf{M}$ no longer stochastic. To **fix this**, we define the **Google Matrix** $\mathbf{G}$:

$$
\mathbf{G} = d \mathbf{M} + (1 - d) \frac{\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^T}{N}
$$

The matrix $\frac{\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^T}{N}$ is a **rank-1 stochastic matrix** representing uniform teleportation. The convex combination with weight $d$ ensures:

1. $\mathbf{G}$ is **strictly positive** (every entry > 0) — guaranteed by the teleportation term.
2. $\mathbf{G}$ is **stochastic** — each column sums to $d \cdot 1 + (1-d) \cdot 1 = 1$.
3. By the **Perron–Frobenius Theorem**, $\mathbf{G}$ has a unique largest eigenvalue equal to 1.

The PageRank vector is therefore the **stationary distribution** of the Google Matrix:

$$
\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{G} \mathbf{r} \quad \text{with} \quad \sum_i r_i = 1
$$

Solving element-wise gives the **Brin–Page formula** we already know:

$$
PR(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \in B(p)} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}
$$

## 3.3 The Dangling Node Correction (Dead-End Handling)

A **dangling node** (page with no outgoing links) creates a column of zeros in $\mathbf{M}$. To handle this, Brin and Page proposed replacing each such column with $\frac{\mathbf{1}}{N}$:

$$
M'_{ij} = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{L(j)} & \text{if } L(j) > 0 \text{ and } j \to i \\ \frac{1}{N} & \text{if } L(j) = 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The intuition: a dead-end page is treated as if it **teleports uniformly** to every other page. The modified PageRank iteration becomes:

$$
\mathbf{r}^{(t+1)} = d \cdot \mathbf{M}' \mathbf{r}^{(t)} + \frac{1-d}{N} \mathbf{1}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PageRank Computation Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Web Crawl HTML Documents]) --> parse[HTML Parser and Link Extractor]
    parse --> graphBuild[Build Directed Web Graph G of V and E]
    graphBuild --> outDeg[Compute Out-Degree L of q for every node]
    outDeg --> matBuild[Construct Column-Stochastic Matrix M]
    matBuild --> dangling{Dangling Nodes Present?}
    dangling -- Yes --> fixCol[Replace Zero Columns with 1 over N]
    dangling -- No --> google
    fixCol --> google[Form Google Matrix G equals d times M plus 1 minus d times teleportation]
    google --> init[Initialize r0 with 1 over N for all pages]
    init --> iterLoop[Power Iteration Step t plus 1]
    iterLoop --> computeR[Compute r of t plus 1 equals d M r of t plus 1 minus d over N times 1]
    computeR --> checkConv{Convergence Norm Less Than Epsilon}
    checkConv -- No --> iterLoop
    checkConv -- Yes --> normalize[Normalize r so Sum equals 1]
    normalize --> output[Output Final PageRank Vector]
    output([Ranked List of Web Pages])
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Web Graph for the Worked Example

```mermaid
graph LR
    A((A)) -- links to --> B((B))
    A -- links to --> C((C))
    B -- links to --> C
    C -- links to --> A
    D((D)) -- links to --> B
    D -- links to --> C
    classDef high fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef low fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    class C high
    class D low
```

## 4.3 State Diagram of the Random Surfer Model

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OnPageP
    OnPageP --> ClickLink: With Probability d
    OnPageP --> Teleport: With Probability 1 minus d
    ClickLink --> OnPageP: Random Outgoing Link Selected
    Teleport --> OnPageP: Random Page in Web Selected
    OnPageP --> [*]: PageRank Convergence
```

## 4.4 Relationship Between PageRank and Markov Chain Theory

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    web[Real World Web] --> crawl[Crawled Web Graph]
    crawl --> matrix[Transition Matrix M]
    matrix --> dead{Dangling Nodes}
    dead -- Yes --> modify[Modify M to M Prime]
    dead -- No --> google2
    modify --> google2[Google Matrix G]
    google2 --> markov[Markov Chain Model]
    markov --> stationary[Stationary Distribution]
    stationary --> pr[PageRank Vector r Star]
    pr --> rank[Web Page Ranking for Search Results]
```
