# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the PageRank algorithm. What is the role of the damping factor $d$ in PageRank computation?**

**Model Answer:**

PageRank is a link-analysis algorithm used by search engines to assign a numerical importance score to web pages based on the structure of incoming and outgoing hyperlinks. The score of a page $p$ is computed as:

$$
PR(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \in B(p)} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}
$$

**Role of the damping factor $d$:**

- $d$ (typically 0.85) represents the probability that a random surfer **continues clicking** links rather than teleporting.
- It **prevents the surfer from getting trapped** in spider traps or stuck in dead-end cycles.
- It **guarantees convergence** of the iterative computation to a unique stationary distribution.
- It ensures the **ergodicity** (irreducibility + aperiodicity) of the underlying Markov chain.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between a dangling node and a spider trap in the context of PageRank. How does the teleportation term handle them?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Dangling Node | Spider Trap |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A page with **zero outgoing links** | A tightly-connected cluster of pages with **no outgoing links to the rest of the web** |
| **Effect on Matrix** | Creates a column of zeros in $\mathbf{M}$ | Creates a sub-matrix that absorbs probability mass |
| **Effect on PR** | PageRank "leaks" out of the system | PageRank accumulates in the trap, giving 0 to outside pages |
| **Handling** | Replace zero column with $\frac{\mathbf{1}}{N}$ | Teleportation term $(1-d)/N$ redistributes probability |

The **teleportation term** $\frac{1-d}{N}$ acts as a mathematical safety valve: with probability $1-d$, the random surfer is **teleported to a random page**, preventing permanent absorption in either pathological structure.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Compute the PageRank for the following 4-page web graph using $d = 0.85$. Show at least 3 iterations and the final converged values. (14 marks)**

**Web Graph:**
- A links to B, D
- B links to C
- C links to A, B
- D links to C

#### Part (a) — Construct the transition matrix and explain dangling node handling. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify out-degrees.**
- $L(A) = 2$ (A → B, A → D)
- $L(B) = 1$ (B → C)
- $L(C) = 2$ (C → A, C → B)
- $L(D) = 1$ (D → C)

**Step 2: Build column-stochastic matrix $\mathbf{M}$.**

$$
\mathbf{M} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 0 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0.5 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Each column corresponds to a source page; entries in that column represent the probability distribution across target pages.

**Dangling node handling:** All 4 pages have at least one outgoing link — so **no dangling nodes** exist in this graph. The matrix $\mathbf{M}$ is already column-stochastic.

**Step 3: Form the Google Matrix $\mathbf{G} = d\mathbf{M} + \frac{1-d}{4}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^T$.**

The teleportation matrix is $\frac{0.15}{4}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^T = 0.0375 \cdot \mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^T$, which adds 0.0375 to **every entry** of $d\mathbf{M}$.

#### Part (b) — Execute the power iteration and compute the final PageRank. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Using the direct computational form:

$$
PR(p) = \frac{0.15}{4} + 0.85 \sum_{q \in B(p)} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}
$$

- $PR(A) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \left[ \frac{PR(C)}{2} \right]$
- $PR(B) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \left[ \frac{PR(A)}{2} + \frac{PR(C)}{2} \right]$
- $PR(C) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \left[ \frac{PR(B)}{1} + \frac{PR(D)}{1} \right]$
- $PR(D) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \left[ \frac{PR(A)}{2} \right]$

**Initialization:** $PR^{(0)} = (0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25)$.

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR^{(1)}(A) &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \frac{0.25}{2} = 0.0375 + 0.10625 = 0.1438 \\
PR^{(1)}(B) &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \left( \frac{0.25}{2} + \frac{0.25}{2} \right) = 0.0375 + 0.2125 = 0.2500 \\
PR^{(1)}(C) &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot (0.25 + 0.25) = 0.0375 + 0.4250 = 0.4625 \\
PR^{(1)}(D) &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \frac{0.25}{2} = 0.0375 + 0.10625 = 0.1438
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR^{(2)}(A) &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \frac{0.4625}{2} = 0.0375 + 0.1966 = 0.2341 \\
PR^{(2)}(B) &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \left( \frac{0.1438}{2} + \frac{0.4625}{2} \right) = 0.0375 + 0.2577 = 0.2952 \\
PR^{(2)}(C) &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot (0.2500 + 0.1438) = 0.0375 + 0.3348 = 0.3723 \\
PR^{(2)}(D) &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \frac{0.1438}{2} = 0.0375 + 0.0611 = 0.0986
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR^{(3)}(A) &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \frac{0.3723}{2} = 0.0375 + 0.1582 = 0.1957 \\
PR^{(3)}(B) &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \left( \frac{0.2341}{2} + \frac{0.3723}{2} \right) = 0.0375 + 0.2577 = 0.2952 \\
PR^{(3)}(C) &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot (0.2952 + 0.0986) = 0.0375 + 0.3348 = 0.3723 \\
PR^{(3)}(D) &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \frac{0.2341}{2} = 0.0375 + 0.0995 = 0.1370
\end{aligned}
$$

**Converged PageRank (after ~50 iterations):**

$$
\boxed{PR(A) \approx 0.21, \quad PR(B) \approx 0.30, \quad PR(C) \approx 0.36, \quad PR(D) \approx 0.13}
$$

**Final ranking:** C > B > A > D

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the Random Surfer Model in detail. How is it used to derive the PageRank formula? Derive the role of the damping factor mathematically. (7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution:

**Random Surfer Model Explanation:**

The Random Surfer Model posits a hypothetical user who navigates the web by:

1. **Clicking a hyperlink** (with probability $d$) chosen uniformly at random from the current page's outgoing links.
2. **Teleporting** (with probability $1 - d$) to a uniformly random page on the web.

This process defines a **time-homogeneous Markov chain** on the state space $V$ of all web pages.

**Derivation of the PageRank Formula:**

Let $X_t$ denote the page visited at time $t$. The transition probability is:

$$
P(X_{t+1} = i \mid X_t = j) = d \cdot M_{ij} + \frac{1-d}{N}
$$

The PageRank of page $i$ is defined as the **stationary probability** $\pi_i$ of this Markov chain:

$$
\pi_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \pi_j \cdot P(X_{t+1} = i \mid X_t = j)
$$

Substituting the transition probability:

$$
\pi_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \pi_j \left( d \cdot M_{ij} + \frac{1-d}{N} \right)
$$

Expanding:

$$
\pi_i = d \sum_{j=1}^{N} \pi_j M_{ij} + \frac{1-d}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \pi_j
$$

Since $\sum \pi_j = 1$ (probability distribution), the second term simplifies to $\frac{1-d}{N}$. Using $M_{ij} = 1/L(j)$ if $j \to i$, we get:

$$
\boxed{PR(i) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{j \in B(i)} \frac{PR(j)}{L(j)}}
$$

**Role of the Damping Factor $d$ — Mathematical Justification:**

- $d = 1$ → No teleportation → Markov chain may be non-ergodic (disconnected components, spider traps).
- $d < 1$ → The Google Matrix $\mathbf{G}$ becomes **strictly positive** (all entries $> 0$), guaranteeing by the **Perron–Frobenius Theorem** that:
  1. The spectral radius is exactly 1.
  2. The eigenvector is **unique** and **positive**.
  3. The power iteration **converges** from any initial vector.

Empirically, $d = 0.85$ provides the best trade-off between link-following fidelity and convergence speed.

#### Part (b) — Dead-ends, Spider Traps, and the Google Matrix. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Dead-Ends (Dangling Nodes):**

A page with $L(p) = 0$ creates a zero column in $\mathbf{M}$. If a random surfer lands on such a page, the next state is undefined. The fix proposed by Brin and Page is the **dangling node correction**:

$$
M'_{ij} = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{L(j)} & \text{if } L(j) > 0 \text{ and } j \to i \\ \frac{1}{N} & \text{if } L(j) = 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

This reformulation treats a dead-end page as if it teleports uniformly to every other page, preserving stochasticity.

**Spider Traps:**

A spider trap is a set $S \subset V$ such that for every $p \in S$, all outgoing links of $p$ point to pages within $S$. In the absence of teleportation, the Markov chain becomes **absorbing on $S$**, meaning $PR(p) = 0$ for all $p \notin S$.

**Solution via the Google Matrix:**

The **Google Matrix** $\mathbf{G}$ is defined as:

$$
\mathbf{G} = d \mathbf{M} + (1 - d) \frac{\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^T}{N}
$$

This matrix has three critical properties:

1. **Stochasticity:** Each column sums to $d + (1-d) = 1$.
2. **Strict positivity:** Every entry $G_{ij} \geq \frac{1-d}{N} > 0$, so the matrix is primitive.
3. **Unique Stationary Distribution:** By Perron–Frobenius, the principal eigenvector with eigenvalue 1 is unique.

**Numerical Illustration (Spider Trap Example):**

Consider pages A, B, C where A and B form a spider trap (A ↔ B) and C links only to A. Without $d$ correction, the PageRank would be $PR(A) = PR(B) = 0.5$, $PR(C) = 0$. With $d = 0.85$ and the teleportation term:

$$
PR(C) = \frac{0.15}{3} + 0.85 \cdot 0 = 0.05 \neq 0
$$

The teleportation "lifts" C out of zero, demonstrating the corrective power of the damping factor.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $(1-d)/N$ term:** Many students write only $PR(p) = d \sum \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$ and lose 3–4 marks. Always include the teleportation term.
> 2. **Wrong out-degree:** Confusing $L(q)$ (out-degree of the *source* page) with the in-degree of the target page. Mark the formula carefully.
> 3. **Not normalizing the final result:** The sum of PageRanks must equal 1. Always verify and renormalize.
> 4. **Confusing PageRank with HITS:** PageRank is a global, query-independent ranking, whereas HITS (Hyperlink-Induced Topic Search) is query-dependent with separate "authority" and "hub" scores.
> 5. **Skipping iteration counts:** KTU expects at least 3 explicit iterations for full marks on computational questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- [x] **PageRank = Random Walk + Teleportation**: It models a user who clicks links with prob $d$ and teleports with prob $1-d$.
- [x] **Default damping factor**: $d = 0.85$ (memorize this value).
- [x] **Full Brin–Page Formula**: $PR(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \in B(p)} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$ — **always include the teleportation term**.
- [x] **Out-degree $L(q)$ belongs to the source page $q$**, not the target page $p$.
- [x] **Initial PageRank** is always $\frac{1}{N}$ for every page (uniform distribution).
- [x] **Dangling nodes** (zero out-degree) require matrix column replacement with $\frac{1}{N}$.
- [x] **Spider traps** are handled by the teleportation term; without it, PageRank becomes degenerate.
- [x] **Convergence criterion**: $\Vert \mathbf{r}^{(t+1)} - \mathbf{r}^{(t)} \Vert_1 < \epsilon$ (e.g., $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$).
- [x] **Sum of PageRanks = 1** (probabilistic invariant).
- [x] **Computation method**: Power iteration using $\mathbf{r}^{(t+1)} = d \mathbf{M} \mathbf{r}^{(t)} + \frac{1-d}{N} \mathbf{1}$.
- [x] **Mathematical basis**: Markov Chain stationary distribution + Perron–Frobenius Theorem.
- [x] **Google Matrix**: $\mathbf{G} = d\mathbf{M} + (1-d)\frac{\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^T}{N}$ — strictly positive, stochastic, primitive.
- [x] **Difference from HITS**: PageRank is query-independent; HITS is query-dependent.
- [x] **Real-world uses**: Google Search, social network analysis, citation ranking, fraud detection, bioinformatics.
- [x] **Convergence speed**: Typically 50–100 iterations suffice for graphs of millions of nodes.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2 — Link-Based Similarity Search

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Link-Based Similarity Search** is a family of techniques in Web Mining that quantifies the *topological affinity* between two nodes (typically web pages, documents, or entities) of a web graph **exclusively from the hyperlink (edge) structure**, without relying on textual content. It operates on a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ is the set of nodes (pages) and $E$ is the set of directed hyperlinks, and produces a similarity score $S : V \times V \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ such that nodes linked through short, frequently traversed, or mutually-referenced paths receive higher scores.

Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEADT636), the term formally groups the following canonical algorithms:

1. **PageRank** and **Personalized PageRank** (Brin & Page, 1998)
2. **SimRank** (Jeh & Widom, 2002)
3. **Random Walk with Restart (RWR)**
4. **HITS — Hyperlink-Induced Topic Search** (Kleinberg, 1999)
5. **Jaccard / Dice / Overlap Coefficient on In-Link / Out-Link Sets**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2):** "Link-Based Similarity Search" covers (a) the co-citation and bibliographic coupling intuitions, (b) PageRank-style stationary distribution, (c) SimRank's recursive self-similarity, and (d) random-walk models including RWR. Students must be able to *derive* the iterative equations, *interpret* the damping/teleport constant, and *compute* scores on small toy graphs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Gossip Network"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine a college campus where students (web pages) point to other students' profiles via friend-links (hyperlinks). Two students $A$ and $B$ are considered *similar* not because their names appear together, but because:
> - **The same set of students point to both of them** (co-citation → "popular kids referenced by the same crowd").
> - **They both point to a similar set of students** (bibliographic coupling → "two students with the same taste in friends").
> - **A random walker, starting at $A$, frequently lands at $B$** before getting bored (PageRank / RWR → "A's neighbourhood of influence overlaps B's").
> - **A's friends-of-friends are also B's friends** (SimRank → "two people with identical social circles").

This is the *core shift* from **content-based** similarity (matching words) to **structure-based** similarity (matching relationships). The web is treated as a *graph signal processor* rather than a *text corpus*.

### 1.3 Why Link-Based Similarity Matters

| Driver | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| Anti-spam | A page with many quality in-links is structurally credible, regardless of keyword stuffing. |
| Recommendation | "Users who liked page $p$ also liked $q$" emerges naturally from link topology. |
| Deduplication / Near-Duplicate Detection | Mirrored sites share in-link patterns. |
| Social & Citation Networks | Bibliographic coupling is the original link-similarity measure (Marshakova, 1973; Small, 1973). |
| Knowledge Graphs (Google Knowledge Panel, Wikidata) | Entity similarity via co-occurrence in graph triples. |

### 1.4 Key Terms at a Glance

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Web graph | $G=(V,E)$ | Directed graph of $N = \vert V \vert$ pages |
| In-link set of $p$ | $I(p)$ | $\{ q \in V \mid (q,p) \in E\}$ |
| Out-link set of $p$ | $O(p)$ | $\{ q \in V \mid (p,q) \in E\}$ |
| Damping / teleport factor | $d$ or $c$ | Probability of following a link vs. teleporting |
| Random surfer | — | Walker teleporting with probability $1-d$ |
| Personalization vector | $\mathbf{e}$ | Probability distribution over query-relevant pages |
| Co-citation count | $C(p,q)$ | $\vert I(p) \cap I(q) \vert$ |
| Bibliographic coupling count | $B(p,q)$ | $\vert O(p) \cap O(q) \vert$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Foundational Idea — Co-Citation and Bibliographic Coupling

These two measures are the *intellectual ancestors* of every modern link-based similarity method.

### 2.1.1 Co-Citation Similarity

Two pages $p$ and $q$ are **co-cited** if some third page $r$ contains hyperlinks to *both* $p$ and $q$. The intuition: "If a referee $r$ cites both $p$ and $q$, then $p$ and $q$ are topically related."

The raw co-citation count is:

$$C(p,q) = \vert I(p) \cap I(q) \vert$$

To normalise, we divide by the geometric mean of their in-degrees (analogous to a cosine-style score), yielding the **Amsler-Culicover Normalised Co-Citation**:

$$S_{\text{co-cit}}(p,q) = \frac{\vert I(p) \cap I(q) \vert}{\sqrt{\vert I(p) \vert \cdot \vert I(q) \vert}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why normalise?** A page with 10,000 in-links will trivially co-cite almost every popular page. Normalisation removes this popularity bias.

### 2.1.2 Bibliographic Coupling

The dual notion — two pages $p$ and $q$ are bibliographically coupled if they *both link to* a common page $r$. Used heavily in bibliometrics (e.g., to detect research-front papers).

$$S_{\text{bib-coup}}(p,q) = \frac{\vert O(p) \cap O(q) \vert}{\sqrt{\vert O(p) \vert \cdot \vert O(q) \vert}}$$

## 2.2 PageRank — The Stationary Distribution of a Random Surfer

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic

1. A **random surfer** begins at a uniformly random page.
2. At each step, with probability $d$ the surfer follows a random out-link; with probability $(1-d)$ the surfer *teleports* to any page uniformly.
3. The **PageRank** of a page is the long-run fraction of time the surfer spends on that page.

### 2.2.2 Mathematical Formulation

Let $\mathbf{r} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$ be the PageRank vector, $\mathbf{M}$ the row-stochastic transition matrix with $M_{ij} = 1/\vert O(j) \vert$ if $j \rightarrow i$, and $\mathbf{e}$ the uniform teleportation vector with all entries $1/N$. Then:

$$\mathbf{r} = d \cdot \mathbf{M} \cdot \mathbf{r} + \frac{1-d}{N} \cdot \mathbf{e}$$

The scalar form (the equation examiners love):

$$PR(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \in I(p)} \frac{PR(q)}{\vert O(q) \vert}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Personalised PageRank (PPR)** replaces the uniform teleport vector $\mathbf{e}$ with a query-dependent vector $\mathbf{v}$ whose entry for the seed node is 1 (or proportional to relevance). This biases the random walk to a topic neighbourhood — the heart of modern *graph-based recommendation*.

## 2.3 SimRank — Recursive Self-Similarity

### 2.3.1 Core Philosophy

*"Two pages are similar if they are referenced by similar pages."* This single sentence is the entirety of SimRank. The recursion bottoms out at $S(p,p) = 1$ for all $p$.

### 2.3.2 Iterative Equation

$$S(a,b) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } a = b \\ \dfrac{C}{\vert I(a) \vert \cdot \vert I(b) \vert} \sum_{i \in I(a)} \sum_{j \in I(b)} S(i,j) & \text{if } a \neq b \end{cases}$$

where $C \in (0,1)$ is the **decay factor** (typically $C = 0.6$ or $0.8$).

### 2.3.3 Why SimRank Works

- It captures the *structural context* of a node, not just its degree.
- For nodes with no in-links, $S(a,b) = 0$ (intuitively correct — they have no structural context).
- The matrix form is $\mathbf{S} = C \cdot \mathbf{M}^T \cdot \mathbf{S} \cdot \mathbf{M} + (1-C) \cdot \mathbf{I}$, solved by simple iteration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Complexity Trap (KTU Alert):** Naïve SimRank is $O(N^4)$ per iteration. The optimisation exploits the sparse structure of $\mathbf{M}$ to bring it down to $O(N^2 \cdot k)$ for $k$ iterations — a frequent 14-mark question.

## 2.4 Random Walk with Restart (RWR)

Closely related to PPR, RWR defines a similarity from a *single source node* $u$ as the stationary probability of a walker that, at every step, has probability $c$ of returning to $u$:

$$\mathbf{r}_{u}^{(t+1)} = (1-c) \cdot \mathbf{M}^T \cdot \mathbf{r}_{u}^{(t)} + c \cdot \mathbf{e}_{u}$$

The final vector $\mathbf{r}_{u}$ is the **RWR relevance** of every node w.r.t. $u$; the pairwise similarity follows by comparing these vectors with cosine similarity.

## 2.5 HITS — Hubs and Authorities

Kleinberg's insight: in a topic-focused subgraph, two mutually reinforcing roles emerge.

- **Authority**: A good authority $p$ is pointed to by many hubs. $A(p) = \sum_{q \in I(p)} H(q)$
- **Hub**: A good hub $p$ points to many authorities. $H(p) = \sum_{q \in O(p)} A(q)$

Iterations:

$$\mathbf{a}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{M}^T \cdot \mathbf{h}^{(t)}, \qquad \mathbf{h}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{M} \cdot \mathbf{a}^{(t)}$$

with $\ell_2$-normalisation at every step to prevent divergence.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise the following eight equations verbatim** — they account for $\approx 70\%$ of the marks in any Module 2 question on link-based similarity.

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables | Boundary / Default |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PageRank (scalar) | $PR(p) = \dfrac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \in I(p)} \dfrac{PR(q)}{\vert O(q) \vert}$ | $d \in (0,1)$ | $d = 0.85$ |
| 2 | PageRank (matrix) | $\mathbf{r} = d \mathbf{M} \mathbf{r} + \tfrac{1-d}{N} \mathbf{e}$ | $\mathbf{M}$ row-stochastic | Converges if $d<1$ |
| 3 | Co-Citation | $S_{\text{co-cit}}(p,q) = \dfrac{\vert I(p) \cap I(q) \vert}{\sqrt{\vert I(p) \vert \cdot \vert I(q) \vert}}$ | In-link sets | $0 \le S \le 1$ |
| 4 | Bibliographic Coupling | $S_{\text{bib-coup}}(p,q) = \dfrac{\vert O(p) \cap O(q) \vert}{\sqrt{\vert O(p) \vert \cdot \vert O(q) \vert}}$ | Out-link sets | $0 \le S \le 1$ |
| 5 | SimRank | $S(a,b) = \dfrac{C}{\vert I(a) \vert \vert I(b) \vert} \sum_{i,j} S(i,j)$ for $a \ne b$ | Decay $C$ | $C = 0.6$ to $0.8$ |
| 6 | SimRank Matrix Form | $\mathbf{S} = C \mathbf{M}^T \mathbf{S} \mathbf{M} + (1-C)\mathbf{I}$ | $\mathbf{I}$ identity | $S(a,a)=1$ |
| 7 | RWR | $\mathbf{r}^{(t+1)} = (1-c)\mathbf{M}^T \mathbf{r}^{(t)} + c \mathbf{e}_u$ | Restart $c$ | $c \in (0,1)$ |
| 8 | HITS | $A(p)=\sum_{q \in I(p)}H(q),\; H(p)=\sum_{q \in O(p)}A(q)$ | Mutual iter. | $\ell_2$-normalise |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Search engines** (Google, Bing): PageRank is one of *hundreds* of ranking signals; PPR variants feed the "More like this" panels.
- **Social networks**: Twitter's "Who to follow" used personalised PageRank (Gupta et al., 2013).
- **E-commerce**: Amazon's item-to-item collaborative filtering is essentially a SimRank on a co-purchase graph.
- **Bio-informatics**: Protein-function similarity via RWR on Protein–Protein Interaction (PPI) networks.
- **Academic search**: Semantic Scholar and AMiner use bibliographic coupling to detect research fronts.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — The PageRank Power-Iteration Formula

### 3.1.1 Setup

We begin with a directed web graph $G=(V,E)$ with $N = \vert V \vert$ pages. We define a column-stochastic transition matrix $\mathbf{P}$ where $P_{ij} = 1/\vert O(j) \vert$ if $j \rightarrow i$, and $0$ otherwise. A random surfer at node $j$ moves to a uniformly chosen out-neighbour.

### 3.1.2 The Teleportation Patch

The matrix $\mathbf{P}$ is *not* a valid Markov chain for nodes with out-degree zero (**dangling nodes**), and may be disconnected. To handle both issues, we apply the Google matrix:

$$\mathbf{G} = d \mathbf{P} + (1-d) \cdot \frac{1}{N} \mathbf{1} \mathbf{1}^T$$

Here $\mathbf{1}$ is the all-ones column vector, so $\frac{1}{N}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^T$ is a rank-one matrix that maps every state to a uniform distribution.

### 3.1.3 Stationary Distribution Equation

We seek $\mathbf{\pi}$ such that $\mathbf{G} \mathbf{\pi} = \mathbf{\pi}$ with $\sum_i \pi_i = 1$. The damping $d<1$ guarantees that $\mathbf{G}$ is *primitive* and *stochastic*, hence has a unique stationary distribution by the **Perron–Frobenius theorem**.

Expanding:

$$\mathbf{\pi} = d \mathbf{P} \mathbf{\pi} + (1-d) \frac{1}{N} \mathbf{1} \mathbf{1}^T \mathbf{\pi}$$

Because $\mathbf{1}^T \mathbf{\pi} = 1$:

$$\mathbf{\pi} = d \mathbf{P} \mathbf{\pi} + \frac{1-d}{N} \mathbf{1}$$

Component-wise (with $\mathbf{e} = \frac{1}{N}\mathbf{1}$):

$$PR(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \in I(p)} \frac{PR(q)}{\vert O(q) \vert}$$

### 3.1.4 Power Iteration Algorithm

**Step 1** Initialise $\mathbf{\pi}^{(0)} = \frac{1}{N}\mathbf{1}$.

**Step 2** Repeat until convergence:
$$\mathbf{\pi}^{(t+1)} = d \mathbf{P} \mathbf{\pi}^{(t)} + \frac{1-d}{N}\mathbf{1}$$

**Step 3** Convergence criterion: $\Vert \mathbf{\pi}^{(t+1)} - \mathbf{\pi}^{(t)} \Vert_1 < \varepsilon$ (e.g., $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why power iteration works:** Because $\mathbf{G}$ is a convex combination of a stochastic matrix and a rank-one positive matrix, its spectral radius is $1$ with a unique dominant eigenvalue of multiplicity 1. Power iteration converges geometrically at rate $O(d)$ per step.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Worked Numerical Example of PageRank

Consider a 4-node graph: $1 \to 2$, $1 \to 3$, $2 \to 3$, $3 \to 1$, $3 \to 4$, $4 \to 1$. Take $d = 0.85$, $N = 4$.

### 3.2.1 Build the Matrix $\mathbf{P}$

Out-degrees: $O(1)=2$, $O(2)=1$, $O(3)=2$, $O(4)=1$.

$$
\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 1/2 & 1 \\
1/2 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
1/2 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1/2 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.2.2 Initialise and Iterate

Let $\mathbf{\pi}^{(0)} = (0.25,\, 0.25,\, 0.25,\, 0.25)^T$. The teleport contribution is $\frac{1-d}{N}\mathbf{1} = \frac{0.15}{4}\mathbf{1} = (0.0375,\, 0.0375,\, 0.0375,\, 0.0375)^T$.

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi^{(1)}_1 &= 0.85 \cdot \bigl(0 \cdot 0.25 + 0 \cdot 0.25 + 0.5 \cdot 0.25 + 1 \cdot 0.25\bigr) + 0.0375 \\
           &= 0.85 \cdot (0.125 + 0.25) + 0.0375 = 0.85 \cdot 0.375 + 0.0375 = 0.3563 \\
\pi^{(1)}_2 &= 0.85 \cdot (0.5 \cdot 0.25 + 0 + 0 + 0) + 0.0375 = 0.85 \cdot 0.125 + 0.0375 = 0.1438 \\
\pi^{(1)}_3 &= 0.85 \cdot (0.5 \cdot 0.25 + 1 \cdot 0.25 + 0 + 0) + 0.0375 = 0.85 \cdot 0.375 + 0.0375 = 0.3563 \\
\pi^{(1)}_4 &= 0.85 \cdot (0 + 0 + 0.5 \cdot 0.25 + 0) + 0.0375 = 0.85 \cdot 0.125 + 0.0375 = 0.1438
\end{aligned}
$$

Check: $\sum \pi^{(1)}_i = 0.3563 + 0.1438 + 0.3563 + 0.1438 = 1.0002 \approx 1$ ✓ (residual is rounding).

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi^{(2)}_1 &= 0.85 \cdot (0 \cdot 0.3563 + 0 \cdot 0.1438 + 0.5 \cdot 0.3563 + 1 \cdot 0.1438) + 0.0375 \\
           &= 0.85 \cdot (0.1782 + 0.1438) + 0.0375 = 0.85 \cdot 0.3220 + 0.0375 = 0.3112 \\
\pi^{(2)}_2 &= 0.85 \cdot (0.5 \cdot 0.3563) + 0.0375 = 0.85 \cdot 0.1782 + 0.0375 = 0.1889 \\
\pi^{(2)}_3 &= 0.85 \cdot (0.5 \cdot 0.3563 + 1 \cdot 0.1438) + 0.0375 = 0.85 \cdot 0.3220 + 0.0375 = 0.3112 \\
\pi^{(2)}_4 &= 0.85 \cdot (0.5 \cdot 0.3563) + 0.0375 = 0.85 \cdot 0.1782 + 0.0375 = 0.1889
\end{aligned}
$$

After sufficient iterations, the system converges to:

$$
\mathbf{\pi}^* \approx (0.297,\ 0.203,\ 0.297,\ 0.203)^T
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Nodes 1 and 3 are reciprocally linked (a 2-cycle), and they have *higher* PageRank than nodes 2 and 4. This is the **cycle-amplification effect** of link-based methods.

## 3.3 Derivation III — SimRank via the Matrix-Recursion Form

Starting from the scalar definition:

$$S(a,b) = \frac{C}{\vert I(a) \vert \vert I(b) \vert} \sum_{i \in I(a)} \sum_{j \in I(b)} S(i,j)$$

Define $\mathbf{S} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}$ and the in-degree-normalised transition matrix $\mathbf{W}$ where $W_{ab} = 1/\vert I(a) \vert$ if $b \to a$. Then the double sum is the $(a,b)$-entry of $\mathbf{W} \mathbf{S} \mathbf{W}^T$. For $a=b$, the recursion is pinned to $1$, achieved by the additive term $(1-C)\mathbf{I}$:

$$\mathbf{S}^{(k+1)} = C \cdot \mathbf{W} \mathbf{S}^{(k)} \mathbf{W}^T + (1-C) \cdot \mathbf{I}$$

with $\mathbf{S}^{(0)} = \mathbf{I}$. This is a fixed-point iteration that provably converges to the unique SimRank matrix (proof: $\Vert C \mathbf{W} \otimes \mathbf{W} \Vert_\infty < C < 1$).

## 3.4 Derivation IV — RWR as a Special Case of Personalised PageRank

Let $\mathbf{e}_u$ be the indicator vector for node $u$. Set $c = 1 - d$ and $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{e}_u$. Then the RWR equation is identical to PPR:

$$\mathbf{r}_u = d \mathbf{P}^T \mathbf{r}_u + (1-d) \mathbf{e}_u$$

Solving by the Neumann series (a standard trick):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{r}_u &= (1-d) \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} d^k (\mathbf{P}^T)^k \mathbf{e}_u
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the closed form: the $k$-th term is the probability of a walk of *exactly* $k$ steps from $u$ to any node $v$, with a forced restart at step 0 — directly the Kolmogorov–Chapman expansion of the random walk.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Co-Citation | Bib-Coupling | PageRank | SimRank | RWR | HITS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output type | Pairwise score | Pairwise score | Global score per node | Pairwise score | Per-seed score vector | H/A pair per node |
| Symmetric? | Yes | Yes | N/A (1-ary) | Yes | Optional (cosine) | N/A (1-ary) |
| Direction | In-link | Out-link | In-link | In-link (recursive) | In-link | In-link + Out-link |
| Damping | None | None | $d$ | $C$ | $c$ | None (norm) |
| Complexity (naïve) | $O(N^2)$ | $O(N^2)$ | $O(N^2 k)$ | $O(N^3 k)$ | $O(N^2 k)$ | $O(N^2 k)$ |
| Sensitive to dangling? | No | No | Yes (handled) | No | Yes (handled) | No |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Link-Based Similarity Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Web Crawler] --> B[Graph Builder]
    B --> C[Adjacency Representation]
    C --> D[Algorithm Selector]
    D --> E1[PageRank Engine]
    D --> E2[SimRank Engine]
    D --> E3[RWR Engine]
    D --> E4[HITS Engine]
    D --> E5[Co-Citation / Bib-Coupling Engine]
    E1 --> F[Score Aggregator]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G[Top-K Ranker]
    G --> H[Output: Ranked Node List]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style F fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style H fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

**Read the diagram top-down:** The crawler supplies HTML/JSON documents; the **Graph Builder** parses anchors and emits an edge list; the **Adjacency Representation** layer picks sparse CSR/CSC formats; the **Algorithm Selector** routes to one of the five engines depending on the query modality (single-node importance, pairwise similarity, topic-restricted search, etc.); scores are merged in the **Score Aggregator** and trimmed by a **Top-K Ranker**.

## 4.2 State Machine of the PageRank Power Iteration

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialise
    Initialise --> Compute: pi_0 = 1 over N times 1
    Compute --> Normalise: pi_tilde = d M pi_t
    Normalise --> CheckDiff: add teleport term
    CheckDiff --> Compute: L1 diff > epsilon
    CheckDiff --> Converged: L1 diff le epsilon
    Converged --> Output: emit pi_star
    Output --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Dependency Graph Among Link-Based Similarity Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    COCIT[Co-Citation]
    BIBCOUP[Bibliographic Coupling]
    PAGERANK[PageRank]
    PPR[Personalised PageRank]
    RWR[Random Walk with Restart]
    SIMRANK[SimRank]
    HITS[HITS]
    JAC[Set Jaccard]

    COCIT --> JAC
    BIBCOUP --> JAC
    PAGERANK --> PPR
    PPR --> RWR
    PAGERANK --> SIMRANK
    HITS --> SIMRANK

    style COCIT fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style PAGERANK fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style SIMRANK fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style RWR fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style HITS fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Reading the dependency graph:** PageRank is the *ur-algorithm* — every method (PPR, RWR, SimRank's normalised recursion) reduces to a *modified* PageRank iteration. Co-citation and bibliographic coupling are non-iterative, set-theoretic ancestors that motivate the entire family.
