In a bipartite $(k, l)$-core, two **independent thresholds** ($k$ for $U$, $l$ for $V$) define the structural density requirement, which captures asymmetric participation patterns common in two-mode networks. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Book Club" Intuition Imagine a **book club website** with two types of members: **Readers** ($U$) and **Genres** ($V$). An edge exists if a reader likes a genre. A bipartite $(3, 2)$-core community would be the largest group where: - Every reader in the group likes **at least 3** genres within the group. - Every genre in the group is liked by **at least 2** readers within the group. If a reader only likes 2 genres, they get "peeled off" from the community. If a genre is only liked by 1 reader, it also gets removed. This recursive removal continues until the density criterion is satisfied for all remaining vertices — yielding a tightly-knit community of mutual interest. ## 1.4 Formal Definition: $p$-Core (K-Core) A **$p$-core** (or $k$-core) of a graph is a maximal subgraph in which every vertex has degree at least $p$. When applied to a bipartite graph with a uniform threshold $k = l = p$, it reduces to the **bipartite $p$-core** (symmetric case). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Property:** The $k$-core of a graph is **unique** for any given $k$. This uniqueness is critical for deterministic community detection in web mining pipelines. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Bipartite Cores Matter Web mining frequently encounters **two-mode network data**: - **Customer–Product** purchase networks - **User–Tag** annotation networks - **Author–Conference** publication networks - **Actor–Movie** collaboration networks In these networks, traditional unipartite community detection **fails** because vertices on each side play structurally different roles. The **bipartite core** approach respects this asymmetry. ## 2.2 Hierarchical Structure of Cores Cores exhibit a **nested hierarchy** (similar to Russian dolls): $$ (k, l)\text{-core} \;\subseteq\; (k', l')\text{-core} \quad \text{whenever} \quad k' \leq k \;\text{and}\; l' \leq l $$ This means the **$(3, 2)$-core contains the $(4, 3)$-core**, which in turn contains the $(5, 4)$-core. As thresholds increase, the core shrinks toward the "densest backbone" of the network. ## 2.3 The Peeling Algorithm (Core Decomposition) Computing the bipartite $(k, l)$-core follows a **recursive deletion** strategy: 1. **Initialize:** Compute the degree of every vertex in $G$. 2. **Identify violations:** Find all vertices with degree below threshold (i.e., $u \in U$ with $\deg(u) < k$, or $v \in V$ with $\deg(v) < l$). 3. **Delete:** Remove these violating vertices and all their incident edges. 4. **Recompute:** After deletion, recompute degrees of remaining neighbors. 5. **Iterate:** Repeat steps 2–4 until no violating vertices remain. 6. **Output:** The remaining subgraph is the $(k, l)$-core. > [!NOTE] > **Why Peeling Works:** Removing a low-degree vertex cannot *increase* the degree of any remaining vertex. Therefore, the process converges monotonically to a unique fixed point in $O(|E|)$ time using a queue-based implementation. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Symbol / Term** | **Definition** | **Constraint / Range** | |---|---|---| | $G = (U, V, E)$ | Bipartite graph with two vertex partitions | $U \cap V = \emptyset$ | | $k$ | Minimum degree threshold for $U$ vertices | $k \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}, \; 1 \leq k \leq \vert U \vert$ | | $l$ | Minimum degree threshold for $V$ vertices | $l \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}, \; 1 \leq l \leq \vert V \vert$ | | $\deg_H(u)$ | Degree of $u$ within subgraph $H$ | $\deg_H(u) \geq k$ | | $\deg_H(v)$ | Degree of $v$ within subgraph $H$ | $\deg_H(v) \geq l$ | | $C_{k,l}$ | The unique $(k, l)$-core of $G$ | Always exists, unique | | $\vert E_{k,l} \vert$ | Number of edges in the $(k, l)$-core | $\vert E_{k,l} \vert \geq 0$ | | Complexity | Time: $O(\vert E \vert)$, Space: $O(\vert U \vert + \vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Deterministic | ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Web Mining - **Recommendation Systems:** $(k, l)$-cores identify dense user–item subgraphs for collaborative filtering. - **Spam Detection:** Web pages with very low bipartite degree in a host–link network are likely spam farms. - **Community Detection in Twitter/X:** User–hashtag bipartite cores reveal topical micro-communities. - **Citation Network Analysis:** Author–paper bipartite cores expose prolific research groups. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The $(k, l)$-core decomposition provides an **efficient pre-processing filter** for expensive graph mining tasks like frequent subgraph mining and link prediction, reducing search space dramatically. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Constructing a $(2, 2)$-Core **Given Bipartite Graph $G$:** Let $U = \{u_1, u_2, u_3, u_4\}$ and $V = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4\}$ with edges: $$ E = \{(u_1, v_1), (u_1, v_2), (u_2, v_1), (u_2, v_2), (u_2, v_3), (u_3, v_2), (u_3, v_3), (u_4, v_4)\} $$ **Initial Degree Calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \deg(u_1) &= 2, \quad \deg(u_2) = 3, \quad \deg(u_3) = 2, \quad \deg(u_4) = 1 \\ \deg(v_1) &= 2, \quad \deg(v_2) = 3, \quad \deg(v_3) = 2, \quad \deg(v_4) = 1 \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Applying the Peeling Process for $(k, l) = (2, 2)$ **Iteration 1: Identify Violations** $$ \begin{aligned} u_4: \quad \deg(u_4) = 1 &< 2 \;\; \Rightarrow \text{REMOVE } u_4 \\ v_4: \quad \deg(v_4) = 1 &< 2 \;\; \Rightarrow \text{REMOVE } v_4 \end{aligned} $$ **After Iteration 1:** $$ \begin{aligned} U' &= \{u_1, u_2, u_3\}, \quad V' = \{v_1, v_2, v_3\} \\ E' &= \{(u_1, v_1), (u_1, v_2), (u_2, v_1), (u_2, v_2), (u_2, v_3), (u_3, v_2), (u_3, v_3)\} \end{aligned} $$ **Iteration 2: Recompute Degrees** $$ \begin{aligned} \deg(u_1) &= 2, \quad \deg(u_2) = 3, \quad \deg(u_3) = 2 \\ \deg(v_1) &= 2, \quad \deg(v_2) &= 3, \quad \deg(v_3) = 2 \end{aligned} $$ All degrees satisfy the threshold: $\deg(u_i) \geq 2$ and $\deg(v_j) \geq 2$. **Iteration 3: Termination Check** No further violations. Process converges. ## 3.3 Final $(2, 2)$-Core Output $$ C_{2,2} = \bigl(\{u_1, u_2, u_3\}, \{v_1, v_2, v_3\}, \{(u_1,v_1),(u_1,v_2),(u_2,v_1),(u_2,v_2),(u_2,v_3),(u_3,v_2),(u_3,v_3)\}\bigr) $$ $$ \boxed{\vert E_{C_{2,2}} \vert = 7, \quad \text{with } \vert U' \vert = 3, \;\; \vert V' \vert = 3} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Core Types | **Core Type** | **Threshold** | **Symmetry** | **Vertex Constraint** | **Use Case** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Unipartite $k$-core | Single $k$ | Symmetric | $\deg(v) \geq k$ for all $v$ | General graph backbone | | Bipartite $p$-core | Single $p$ | Symmetric across $U, V$ | $\deg(u) \geq p, \; \deg(v) \geq p$ | Balanced two-mode networks | | Bipartite $(k, l)$-core | Dual $(k, l)$ | Asymmetric | $\deg(u) \geq k, \; \deg(v) \geq l$ | Real-world heterogeneous networks | | Truss | $k$-truss | Edge-based | Each edge in $\geq k$ triangles | Triangle-dense subgraphs | | Clique | Size $k$ | Symmetric | Fully connected | Smallest, densest units | ## 3.5 Derivation of Time Complexity The peeling algorithm processes each vertex at most once and each edge at most once (when one of its endpoints is removed): $$ \begin{aligned} T(n, m) &= \sum_{v \in U \cup V} O(1) + \sum_{e \in E} O(1) \\ &= O(\vert U \vert + \vert V \vert) + O(\vert E \vert) \\ &= O(\vert E \vert) \quad \text{(since } \vert E \vert \geq \max(\vert U \vert, \vert V \vert) \text{ in non-trivial graphs)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Complexity Insight:** The $O(\vert E \vert)$ bound makes bipartite core decomposition **linear time**, making it scalable to massive web-scale graphs with billions of edges. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of $(k, l)$-Core Peeling ```mermaid flowchart TD startA["START: Input Bipartite Graph G"] --> startB["Initialize degree counters
degU and degV for all vertices"] startB --> startC["Push all U-vertices with deg less than k
and V-vertices with deg less than l
into QUEUE"] startC --> startD{"Queue empty?"} startD -- "No" --> startE["Dequeue violating vertex v"] startE --> startF["Mark v as REMOVED"] startF --> startG["For each neighbor w of v:
Decrement deg of w"] startG --> startH{"w violated threshold?"} startH -- "Yes" --> startI["Enqueue w"] startH -- "No" --> startJ["Continue to next neighbor"] startI --> startD startJ --> startD startD -- "Yes" --> endA["Collect all UNREMOVED vertices
and their incident edges"] endA --> endB["Output: The k-l-Core Subgraph Ckl"] endB --> endC["END"] ``` ## 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram: Asymmetric Density Structure ```mermaid graph LR subgraph sideU["Partition U: Users"] user1["u1"] user2["u2"] user3["u3"] user4["u4: REMOVED
(deg less than k)"] end subgraph sideV["Partition V: Items"] item1["v1"] item2["v2"] item3["v3"] item4["v4: REMOVED
(deg less than l)"] end user1 --- item1 user1 --- item2 user2 --- item1 user2 --- item2 user2 --- item3 user3 --- item2 user3 --- item3 user4 -. "edge deleted" .-> item4 ``` ## 4.3 Nested Hierarchy of Cores (Hasse Diagram) ```mermaid graph TB core00["(1,1)-core: Entire Graph G"] core12["(1,2)-core"] core21["(2,1)-core"] core22["(2,2)-core"] core33["(3,3)-core"] core44["(4,4)-core: Maximal Dense Core"] core00 --> core12 core00 --> core21 core12 --> core22 core21 --> core22 core22 --> core33 core33 --> core44 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the Hasse Diagram:** Lower cores contain higher cores. The $(1, 1)$-core is the entire graph; the $(4, 4)$-core is the densest irreducible substructure. Each level-up discards vertices failing the stricter degree requirements. ## 4.4 Algorithm Pseudocode Block (Reference) > [!NOTE] > The following reference structure is included for conceptual understanding only and aligns with standard bipartite core decomposition literature. ```mermaid flowchart TD algoA["Function ComputeBipartiteCore G, k, l"] algoB["Initialize: degMap for all vertices"] algoC["Build queue Q of violating vertices"] algoD["While Q is not empty:"] algoE["Pop vertex v from Q"] algoF["For each neighbor w of v in G:"] algoG["Decrement deg of w"] algoH["If deg of w now violates threshold
and w not in Q: Push w to Q"] algoI["Mark v as removed"] algoJ["Return induced subgraph on unremoved vertices"] algoA --> algoB algoB --> algoC algoC --> algoD algoD --> algoE algoE --> algoF algoF --> algoG algoG --> algoH algoH --> algoD algoD --> algoJ ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **Define a bipartite $(k, l)$-core. How does it differ from a unipartite $k$-core?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A bipartite $(k, l)$-core of a graph $G = (U, V, E)$ is the maximal subgraph $H$ such that every vertex in $U$ has degree at least $k$ and every vertex in $V$ has degree at least $l$ within $H$. **Difference:** A unipartite $k$-core applies a single threshold $k$ to all vertices uniformly, while a bipartite $(k, l)$-core uses two independent thresholds ($k$ for partition $U$ and $l$ for partition $V$), allowing asymmetric density requirements suited to two-mode networks. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **State two real-world applications of bipartite core decomposition in web mining.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Recommendation Systems:** Identifying dense user–item subgraphs for collaborative filtering algorithms. **(1.5 marks)** 2. **Hashtag/Topic Community Detection:** Finding engaged user–hashtag subgraphs on platforms like Twitter/X to detect trending topic clusters. **(1.5 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **a)** Define a bipartite graph and a $(k, l)$-core. Explain the peeling algorithm used to compute the $(k, l)$-core with its time complexity. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** A **bipartite graph** $G = (U, V, E)$ has vertex set partitioned into two disjoint sets $U$ and $V$, with all edges connecting $U$ to $V$. **(1 mark)** A **$(k, l)$-core** is the maximal subgraph $H \subseteq G$ where every $u \in U$ satisfies $\deg_H(u) \geq k$ and every $v \in V$ satisfies $\deg_H(v) \geq l$. **(1 mark)** **Peeling Algorithm Steps: (4 marks)** 1. Compute degree of every vertex in $G$. 2. Enqueue all $u \in U$ with $\deg(u) < k$ and all $v \in V$ with $\deg(v) < l$. 3. While queue non-empty: dequeue vertex $v$, mark removed, decrement degree of each neighbor. 4. If neighbor's new degree falls below threshold, enqueue it. 5. Terminate when queue is empty. Return the subgraph induced by unmarked vertices. **Time Complexity:** Each vertex and edge is processed at most once, yielding $O(\vert E \vert)$ time and $O(\vert U \vert + \vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ space. **(1 mark)** --- **b)** Consider the bipartite graph with $U = \{u_1, u_2, u_3, u_4, u_5\}$, $V = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4\}$ and edges: $(u_1, v_1), (u_1, v_2), (u_2, v_1), (u_2, v_2), (u_2, v_3), (u_3, v_2), (u_3, v_3), (u_4, v_3), (u_4, v_4), (u_5, v_4)$. Compute the $(2, 2)$-core. Show all iterations. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Initial Degrees: (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} \deg(u_1) = 2, \; \deg(u_2) = 3, \; \deg(u_3) = 2, \; \deg(u_4) = 2, \; \deg(u_5) = 1 \\ \deg(v_1) = 2, \; \deg(v_2) = 3, \; \deg(v_3) = 3, \; \deg(v_4) = 2 \end{aligned} $$ **Iteration 1:** $u_5$ has $\deg(u_5) = 1 < 2$. Remove $u_5$ and edge $(u_5, v_4)$. **(1 mark)** **Recompute:** $\deg(v_4)$ decreases from 2 to 1. Now $v_4$ has $\deg(v_4) = 1 < 2$. **(1 mark)** **Iteration 2:** Remove $v_4$ and edges $(u_4, v_4)$. This reduces $\deg(u_4)$ from 2 to 1. **(1 mark)** **Iteration 3:** $\deg(u_4) = 1 < 2$. Remove $u_4$ and edge $(u_4, v_3)$. This reduces $\deg(v_3)$ from 3 to 2 (still valid). **(1 mark)** **Iteration 4:** Check all remaining: $u_1(2), u_2(3), u_3(2), v_1(2), v_2(3), v_3(2)$. All satisfy threshold. **(1 mark)** **Final $(2, 2)$-Core: (1 mark)** $$ C_{2,2} = \bigl(\{u_1, u_2, u_3\}, \{v_1, v_2, v_3\}, \{(u_1,v_1),(u_1,v_2),(u_2,v_1),(u_2,v_2),(u_2,v_3),(u_3,v_2),(u_3,v_3)\}\bigr) $$ $$\boxed{\vert E_{C_{2,2}} \vert = 7}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **a)** Discuss the nested hierarchical property of bipartite cores. Prove that if $k_1 \geq k_2$ and $l_1 \geq l_2$, then the $(k_1, l_1)$-core is a subgraph of the $(k_2, l_2)$-core. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Definition Recall:** The $(k, l)$-core is the maximal subgraph satisfying the degree constraints for thresholds $(k, l)$. **(1 mark)** **Property Statement:** Higher thresholds yield smaller (nested) cores. **(1 mark)** **Proof: (5 marks)** Let $C_{k_1, l_1}$ denote the $(k_1, l_1)$-core and $C_{k_2, l_2}$ denote the $(k_2, l_2)$-core. *Claim:* If $k_1 \geq k_2$ and $l_1 \geq l_2$, then $C_{k_1, l_1} \subseteq C_{k_2, l_2}$. *Proof by contradiction:* Assume there exists a vertex $u \in U_{C_{k_1, l_1}}$ such that $u \notin U_{C_{k_2, l_2}}$. Then $u$ is removed during the $(k_2, l_2)$-core peeling process, meaning at some stage $\deg(u) < k_2$ in the remaining graph. But $u$ survived the $(k_1, l_1)$-core peeling, meaning $\deg(u) \geq k_1$ throughout. Since $k_1 \geq k_2$, we have $\deg(u) \geq k_1 \geq k_2$, which contradicts $\deg(u) < k_2$. The symmetric argument holds for $v \in V$ with $l_1 \geq l_2$. Therefore, every vertex surviving the stricter peeling must also survive the relaxed peeling, proving $C_{k_1, l_1} \subseteq C_{k_2, l_2}$. $\blacksquare$ --- **b)** Explain how bipartite core communities are used in real-world web mining applications. Provide two specific examples with the type of threshold parameters typically used. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Conceptual Framework:** Bipartite core decomposition extracts structurally dense subgraphs from two-mode networks, which serve as proxies for meaningful communities in web mining. **(1 mark)** **Example 1: Movie Recommendation (Collaborative Filtering) — (3 marks)** - **Network:** User–Movie bipartite graph where an edge indicates a rating. - **Application:** The $(k, l)$-core reveals groups of users who collectively rate a set of movies. - **Typical Thresholds:** $k = 5$ (each user rated $\geq 5$ movies in the core), $l = 50$ (each movie received $\geq 50$ ratings in the core). - **Utility:** Provides reliable neighborhood for collaborative filtering since each user has sufficient overlap with each movie's rater base. **Example 2: Academic Collaboration Analysis — (3 marks)** - **Network:** Author–Publication bipartite graph where an edge indicates authorship. - **Application:** The $(k, l)$-core identifies prolific research groups where authors have multiple joint publications. - **Typical Thresholds:** $k = 3$ (each author has $\geq 3$ papers in the core), $l = 2$ (each paper has $\geq 2$ core authors). - **Utility:** Detects active research laboratories and helps bibliometric analysis. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to Recompute Degrees After Deletion:** Many students remove a vertex but fail to update the degree of its neighbors, leading to an incorrect final core. Always recompute degrees **after every deletion round**. **(−2 marks typical)** > 2. **Confusing Partitions:** Mixing up $U$ and $V$ thresholds. Remember: $k$ applies to $U$, $l$ applies to $V$. Do not apply the same threshold to both sides unless explicitly stated. **(−1 mark)** > 3. **Skipping Iteration Steps:** Examiners award partial credit per peeling iteration. Show **every iteration explicitly** with degrees before and after. **(−2 to −3 marks if skipped)** > 4. **Ignoring Cascade Effects:** When a $V$-vertex is removed, all $U$-neighbors' degrees drop. This may cause a cascade of $U$-removals. Always recheck after every deletion. **(−2 marks)** > 5. **Stating the Core is Non-Unique:** The $(k, l)$-core is **always unique** for any given $(k, l)$. Writing otherwise loses a mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Bipartite Graph:** $G = (U, V, E)$ with $U \cap V = \emptyset$ and all edges crossing partitions. - **Bipartite $(k, l)$-Core:** Maximal subgraph where $\deg(u) \geq k$ for all $u \in U$ and $\deg(v) \geq l$ for all $v \in V$. - **Peeling Algorithm:** Iteratively remove vertices violating the degree threshold; recompute and repeat. - **Uniqueness:** The $(k, l)$-core is **always unique** for any threshold pair. - **Nested Hierarchy:** $C_{k_1, l_1} \subseteq C_{k_2, l_2}$ whenever $k_1 \geq k_2$ and $l_1 \geq l_2$. - **Time Complexity:** $O(\vert E \vert)$ — linear in number of edges. - **Space Complexity:** $O(\vert U \vert + \vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$. - **Web Mining Applications:** Recommendation systems, hashtag community detection, spam farm identification, academic collaboration mining. - **Bipartite vs Unipartite:** Two thresholds $(k, l)$ vs single $k$ — enables asymmetric density modeling. - **Implementation Tip:** Use a queue or stack with a "marked removed" array to achieve $O(\vert E \vert)$ time. - **Key Edge Cases:** If $k > \max(\deg(u))$ for any $u$, core is empty. If $k = 1$ and $l = 1$, core is the entire graph. - **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never apply peeling without recomputing neighbor degrees after each removal. - **Comparison Anchor:** $(k, l)$-core is to bipartite graphs what $k$-core is to unipartite graphs — a structural backbone extraction tool. - **Module Context:** This topic is part of Module 2 on Community Detection in Web Mining; relate it to other methods like modularity-based detection and spectral clustering. - **Exam Strategy:** When asked to "compute the $(k, l)$-core," always show the **initial degree table**, **each peeling iteration**, and the **final core with edge count**.

topics.title: Web Crawling - A Basic Crawler Algorithm: Breadth-First Crawlers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT636 - INTRODUCTION TO WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Web Crawling - A Basic Crawler Algorithm: Breadth-First Crawlers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Web Crawler:** A *web crawler* (also known as a *spider*, *bot*, or *web robot*) is an automated software agent that systematically traverses the World Wide Web by following hyperlinks from one web page to another, with the primary objective of downloading and indexing web content for search engines, data mining, or archival purposes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Breadth-First Crawler (BFS Crawler):** A *Breadth-First Crawler* is a web crawling strategy that explores the web graph in a **level-by-level** manner, processing all URLs at the current crawl depth before descending to the next level. It uses a **FIFO (First-In-First-Out)** queue as its frontier data structure, ensuring that pages closer to a seed URL are fetched before pages that are further away.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Throwing a Stone in a Pond" 🪨💧**
>
> Imagine you drop a stone into the center of a still pond. The ripples spread outward in **concentric circles**. The water at distance **1 meter** is touched first, then the water at **2 meters**, then **3 meters**, and so on. The stone is your *seed URL*, and each ripple represents a "depth" of web pages.
>
> A **Breadth-First Crawler** behaves exactly like this! It first visits all pages that are **one link away** from the seed (first ripple), then all pages **two links away** (second ripple), and so on. It does **not** dive deep down a single chain of links like a Depth-First Crawler would.

### 1.3 Why BFS for Web Crawling? (The Intuitive Justification)

| Reason | Plain-English Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Coverage of Important Pages** | Important/popular pages tend to have many pages linking to them → they are reached early in BFS. |
| **Load Distribution** | BFS naturally distributes requests across many servers, avoiding hammering a single host. |
| **Politeness** | A wide-but-shallow crawl respects the bandwidth of individual web servers better than deep chains. |
| **Duplicate Avoidance** | Level-by-level processing pairs cleanly with a *URL-seen* hash table. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics in Web Crawling (bolded for KTU reference):**
> - **Crawl Frontier** — the waiting list of URLs pending download.
> - **Seed Set (S)** — the initial set of URLs given to the crawler.
> - **Politeness Policy** — delay between successive requests to the same server (typically **1 to 10 seconds**).
> - **Robots.txt** — a standard file at the root of a website that tells crawlers which paths to avoid.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Anatomy of a Breadth-First Crawler

A BFS Crawler is built from **four essential logical components** (as per KTU Module 2 syllabus):

1. **Seed Loader** — injects initial URLs into the frontier.
2. **URL Frontier (FIFO Queue)** — the waiting area; guarantees BFS ordering.
3. **Fetcher / Downloader** — issues an HTTP `GET` request and stores the HTML.
4. **Extractor & Link Parser** — parses `<a href="...">` tags, normalizes URLs, and adds unseen ones back to the queue.
5. **URL-Seen Data Structure** — typically a **Hash Set** or **Bloom Filter** that records visited URLs to prevent revisits.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (The "Why" and "How")

> [!NOTE]
> **BFS Crawler — High-Level Trace**

- **Step 1 — Initialization:** Place all seed URLs in the FIFO queue `Q` and mark them in the `Seen` set.
- **Step 2 — Dequeue:** Remove the URL at the *front* of `Q` (the oldest one) — this is what gives BFS its level-order property.
- **Step 3 — Fetch:** Download the page using HTTP.
- **Step 4 — Parse:** Extract all outgoing hyperlinks from the downloaded page.
- **Step 5 — Normalize:** Resolve relative URLs to absolute form, strip fragments (`#section`), and lowercase the host.
- **Step 6 — Deduplicate:** For each new URL, check against `Seen`; reject if already present.
- **Step 7 — Enqueue:** Append the unseen URL to the *rear* of `Q`.
- **Step 8 — Termination:** Stop when `Q` is empty, a maximum page count is reached, or a time/depth limit is exceeded.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes every quantitative relationship you may need for BFS crawler problems in KTU 2024 exams.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Meanings | Typical Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BFS Time Complexity | $T(n) = \mathcal{O}(V + E)$ | $V$ = number of pages fetched, $E$ = number of edges (hyperlinks) crawled | Pages + Links |
| 2 | BFS Space Complexity | $S(n) = \mathcal{O}(V)$ | $V$ = number of pages stored in `Q` + `Seen` | URLs in memory |
| 3 | Frontier Size (Worst Case) | $\vert Q \vert_{\max} = \sum_{i=1}^{d} L^{i}$ | $L$ = avg. out-degree (links/page), $d$ = current depth | Geometric growth |
| 4 | Page Growth per Level | $P_{d+1} \approx L \cdot P_{d}$ | $P_d$ = pages at depth $d$, $L$ = avg. out-degree | Doubles-like growth |
| 5 | Politeness Delay | $D = \dfrac{1}{R_{\text{per-host}}}$ | $D$ = delay in seconds, $R$ = requests per host per second | Seconds (e.g., $D=1$) |
| 6 | Duplicate Detection | $\text{isSeen}(u) \Rightarrow \text{skip}$ | $u$ = candidate URL | Constant-time via hash |
| 7 | Bloom Filter False Positive | $P_{fp} \approx \left(1 - e^{-\frac{kn}{m}}\right)^{k}$ | $k$ = hash functions, $n$ = items, $m$ = bit array size | Probability |
| 8 | Bandwidth Requirement | $BW = N \cdot \bar{S}_{\text{page}}$ | $N$ = pages, $\bar{S}$ = average page size (KB) | KB or MB |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where BFS Crawlers Are Actually Used:**
> - **Search Engine Indexing (e.g., Googlebot, Bingbot):** BFS-like policies ensure that "hub" pages with high PageRank are discovered early.
> - **Web Archiving (e.g., Internet Archive's Heritrix):** BFS gives predictable, even coverage of domains.
> - **SEO Auditing Tools (e.g., Screaming Frog):** BFS lets them map every URL within a few clicks of the homepage.
> - **Data Mining Pipelines:** BFS crawling is the first stage before content extraction, sentiment analysis, or graph mining.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Formal BFS Crawler Algorithm (Traceable Form)

Let us define the formal trace used by a BFS Crawler.

**Input:**
- $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_k\}$ — a set of seed URLs.
- $D_{\max}$ — maximum depth allowed.
- $C_{\max}$ — maximum number of pages to crawl.

**Data Structures:**
- $Q$ — FIFO queue of tuples $(u, d)$ where $u$ is a URL and $d$ is its depth.
- $\text{Seen}$ — hash set of visited URLs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Algorithm Body — Exhaustive Step Trace**

**Step 1 — Initialize the frontier.**

For every seed $s_i \in S$:

$$\text{Enqueue}(Q, (s_i, 0))$$
$$\text{Seen} \leftarrow \text{Seen} \cup \{s_i\}$$

*Why?* This sets the crawl's starting frontier at depth 0, and prevents any seed from being fetched twice.

**Step 2 — Loop guard.**

While $Q$ is non-empty AND pages-fetched $< C_{\max}$:

**Step 3 — Dequeue (FIFO).**

$$(u, d) \leftarrow \text{Dequeue}(Q)$$

*Why FIFO?* It guarantees that URLs enqueued at depth $d$ are dequeued before any URL at depth $d+1$ — the structural definition of BFS.

**Step 4 — Depth filter.**

If $d > D_{\max}$, **continue** to next iteration.

**Step 5 — Fetch the page.**

$$P \leftarrow \text{HTTP\_GET}(u)$$

**Step 6 — Extract hyperlinks.**

Let $\text{links}(P) = \{l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_m\}$ be the set of all anchor-tag URLs in page $P$.

**Step 7 — For each extracted link $l_i$, normalize and dedupe.**

The normalized form is:

$$l_i^{\text{norm}} = \text{Resolve}(\text{base}(u), l_i) \;\mid\; \text{StripFragment}(l_i) \;\mid\; \text{LowercaseHost}(l_i)$$

If $l_i^{\text{norm}} \notin \text{Seen}$:

$$\text{Enqueue}(Q, (l_i^{\text{norm}}, d+1))$$
$$\text{Seen} \leftarrow \text{Seen} \cup \{l_i^{\text{norm}}\}$$

**Step 8 — Repeat Step 2 until termination.**

### 3.2 Worked Example — Frontier Trace on a Toy Web Graph

> [!TIP]
> **Toy Web Graph:** Suppose the seed is `A`. Page `A` links to `B` and `C`. Page `B` links to `D`. Page `C` links to `E`. Page `D` links to nothing new. Page `E` links to `A` (already seen).

Let us trace the BFS frontier.

**Iteration 1:**
- Initial: $Q = [(A, 0)]$, $\text{Seen} = \{A\}$
- Dequeue $(A, 0)$, fetch `A`, links extracted: $\{B, C\}$
- After enqueue: $Q = [(B, 1), (C, 1)]$, $\text{Seen} = \{A, B, C\}$

**Iteration 2:**
- Dequeue $(B, 1)$, fetch `B`, links extracted: $\{D\}$
- After enqueue: $Q = [(C, 1), (D, 2)]$, $\text{Seen} = \{A, B, C, D\}$

**Iteration 3:**
- Dequeue $(C, 1)$, fetch `C`, links extracted: $\{E\}$
- After enqueue: $Q = [(D, 2), (E, 2)]$, $\text{Seen} = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$

**Iteration 4:**
- Dequeue $(D, 2)$, fetch `D`, links: $\{\}$ (nothing new)
- $Q = [(E, 2)]$

**Iteration 5:**
- Dequeue $(E, 2)$, fetch `E`, links: $\{A\}$ → **already in `Seen`**, skip.
- $Q = [\,]$ → **Termination.**

**Order of pages fetched (BFS order):** $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow E$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Matrix — BFS vs DFS Crawler Behavior**

| Property | BFS Crawler | DFS Crawler |
|---|---|---|
| Data Structure | FIFO Queue | LIFO Stack |
| Crawl Pattern | Layered ripples | One chain deep |
| Early Discovery of Hubs | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Poor |
| Memory Footprint | Higher (stores full frontier) | Lower |
| Politeness | Naturally better | Can hammer one host |
| Risk of Infinite Traps | Low | High (crawler traps) |

### 3.3 Complexity Derivation

Let $V$ be the number of unique pages crawled and $E$ be the number of unique hyperlinks traversed.

Each URL is enqueued and dequeued **exactly once** (due to `Seen`):

$$T_{\text{queue-ops}} = \mathcal{O}(V)$$

Each hyperlink is examined **once** for dedup:

$$T_{\text{link-checks}} = \mathcal{O}(E)$$

Summing the two:

$$T_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{O}(V) + \mathcal{O}(E) = \mathcal{O}(V + E)$$

Space is dominated by the queue + the `Seen` set, both of which can hold up to $V$ URLs:

$$S_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{O}(V)$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — BFS Crawler Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Load Seed URLs] --> B[Initialize FIFO Queue Q and Seen Set]
    B --> C{Is Q empty OR limit reached}
    C -- Yes --> Z[Stop: Return Crawled Pages]
    C -- No --> D[Dequeue URL u with depth d from front of Q]
    D --> E{Is d greater than Dmax}
    E -- Yes --> C
    E -- No --> F[HTTP GET: Fetch page P from u]
    F --> G[HTML Parser: Extract all href links from P]
    G --> H[URL Normalizer: Resolve, strip fragment, lowercase host]
    H --> I{Is normalized URL in Seen set}
    I -- Yes --> C
    I -- No --> J[Add URL to Seen Set]
    J --> K[Enqueue URL with depth d plus 1 at rear of Q]
    K --> C
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a BFS Web Crawler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        seed1[Seed URL 1]
        seed2[Seed URL 2]
        seedN[Seed URL N]
    end

    subgraph CoreEngine[Crawler Core Engine]
        frontier[FIFO URL Frontier]
        seenStore[URL Seen Hash Set]
        fetcher[HTTP Fetcher Module]
        parser[HTML Link Parser]
        normalizer[URL Normalizer]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        store1[Downloaded Pages Disk]
        indexer[Indexer Passes to Search Engine]
    end

    seed1 --> frontier
    seed2 --> frontier
    seedN --> frontier
    frontier --> fetcher
    fetcher --> parser
    parser --> normalizer
    normalizer --> seenStore
    seenStore --> frontier
    fetcher --> store1
    store1 --> indexer
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — One Crawl Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as URL Frontier
    participant F as Fetcher
    participant P as Parser
    participant N as Normalizer
    participant S as Seen Set

    U->>F: Dequeue next URL
    F->>F: HTTP GET request
    F-->>P: Return raw HTML
    P->>N: Extract and send href list
    N->>S: Check normalized URL
    S-->>N: Return false if unseen
    N->>U: Enqueue unseen URL at rear
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When a question says *"Draw the architecture of a web crawler"*, you may replace the explicit component names with the four canonical blocks — **Frontier, Fetcher, Parser, Deduper** — and you will receive full credit.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define a web crawler. Mention the role of the URL frontier.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A *web crawler* is an automated software agent that systematically browses the World Wide Web by following hyperlinks, primarily to download and index pages for search engines. The **URL frontier** is the waiting data structure (queue or priority queue) that holds URLs pending download. It decouples the *discovery* of new URLs from the *fetching* of already-discovered ones, and is the key component that enforces the crawl order (FIFO for BFS, LIFO for DFS). *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. Why is BFS the default strategy for most general-purpose web crawlers?**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
BFS is preferred because: **(i)** it discovers *hub* pages (high in-degree) early, since they are reached within a few hops of any seed; **(ii)** it distributes load across many hosts, which is *polite* to web servers; **(iii)** it pairs naturally with a `Seen` hash set, making duplicate detection efficient ($\mathcal{O}(1)$ per check); and **(iv)** it avoids crawler traps that plague depth-first strategies. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Pattern: Internal Choice between Q11A and Q11B]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Comprehensive BFS Crawler Design*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **basic crawler algorithm** with a neat block diagram. List its four main components. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A basic web crawler is a loop-driven agent that repeatedly (1) picks a URL, (2) fetches it, (3) parses it for new URLs, and (4) adds the new URLs back to the frontier. The four canonical components are:

1. **URL Frontier** — the waiting list of URLs.
2. **Fetcher** — issues HTTP requests and stores responses.
3. **Parser** — extracts hyperlinks from the downloaded HTML.
4. **URL Filter / Deduper** — removes duplicates and enforces inclusion/exclusion rules (e.g., `robots.txt`).

The block diagram is given in **Section 4.2** above. *(7 marks — full credit requires the block diagram, the four-component list, and a one-line description of each.)*

---

**(b)** Describe the **Breadth-First Crawling algorithm** with its FIFO frontier. Trace it on the following web graph: `Seed = {A}`, where `A→{B, C}`, `B→{D}`, `C→{E, A}`, `D→{E}`, `E→{}`. Show the contents of the queue and the `Seen` set at each iteration. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **BFS Algorithm Summary (refer Section 3.1 for full trace template):** Initialize $Q$ with seeds, mark them in `Seen`, then repeatedly dequeue the front, fetch, extract links, normalize, dedupe, and enqueue unseen links at the rear.

**Trace Table:**

| Iteration | Dequeued (URL, depth) | Fetched | Links Found | Q (after enqueue) | Seen (after) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (A, 0) | A | B, C | [(B,1), (C,1)] | {A, B, C} |
| 2 | (B, 1) | B | D | [(C,1), (D,2)] | {A, B, C, D} |
| 3 | (C, 1) | C | E, A | [(D,2), (E,2)] | {A, B, C, D, E} |
| 4 | (D, 2) | D | E | [(E,2)] | {A, B, C, D, E} |
| 5 | (E, 2) | E | — | [ ] | {A, B, C, D, E} |

**BFS Fetch Order:** $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow E$

Note: `A` was correctly skipped at Iteration 3 because it was already in `Seen`. *(7 marks — full credit requires: (i) algorithm restatement, (ii) complete trace table, (iii) explicit BFS order, (iv) note on the dedup of A.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice: BFS Properties & Complexity*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare **Breadth-First** and **Depth-First** crawling strategies. Which one is more suitable for a general-purpose search engine crawler and why? *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The comparison matrix from **Section 3.2** should be reproduced here for full marks.

BFS uses a **FIFO queue**, visiting pages in concentric layers around the seed, while DFS uses a **LIFO stack** and dives deep down one chain of links before backtracking. For a general-purpose search engine crawler, **BFS is more suitable** because: (1) it discovers *hub pages* (high PageRank) early, improving index quality; (2) it distributes load across many web servers, satisfying the *politeness policy*; (3) it is less prone to *crawler traps* (e.g., dynamically generated calendars) that can capture a DFS crawler in an infinite chain; and (4) it is easier to bound by depth, making it more predictable. *(7 marks)*

---

**(b)** Derive the **time and space complexity** of a BFS web crawler that fetches $V$ unique pages and traverses $E$ unique hyperlinks. Justify each term. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Time Complexity:**

Let $V$ = number of unique pages fetched, and $E$ = number of unique hyperlinks examined.

Each URL is enqueued and dequeued exactly once (the `Seen` set guarantees no duplicate enqueues). So queue operations cost:

$$T_Q = \mathcal{O}(V)$$

Each extracted hyperlink is checked against the `Seen` set exactly once (hash check). So link-processing cost is:

$$T_L = \mathcal{O}(E)$$

Total time:

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_Q + T_L = \mathcal{O}(V) + \mathcal{O}(E) = \mathcal{O}(V + E)$$

**Space Complexity:**

Both the FIFO queue and the `Seen` set can hold up to $V$ URLs in the worst case. So:

$$S_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{O}(V)$$

These are the standard BFS bounds, identical to the classical graph BFS. *(7 marks — full credit requires: (i) explicit definitions of $V$ and $E$, (ii) separate treatment of queue ops and link checks, (iii) summed expression, (iv) the $\mathcal{O}(V)$ space argument.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Before You Write!**
> 1. **Forgetting the `Seen` check:** Many students describe BFS but omit the `Seen` set. This will cause the crawler to revisit URLs and your algorithm to be marked *incorrect*. Always include a `Seen` (or `Visited`) check after URL normalization.
> 2. **Confusing BFS with DFS in the trace:** When the examiner gives a graph and asks for a *BFS trace*, students often produce a *DFS trace* by mistake. Remember: BFS = process **all** depth-`d` nodes **before** any depth-`(d+1)` node.
> 3. **Skipping URL normalization:** Listing "extract links" without mentioning *normalization* (relative → absolute, fragment stripping, lowercasing) loses 1–2 easy marks.
> 4. **Omitting the politeness / `robots.txt` discussion:** For full credit on architecture questions, always mention that real crawlers respect `robots.txt` and add per-host delays.
> 5. **Wrong complexity statement:** Writing $T = \mathcal{O}(V \cdot E)$ is a classic mistake. The correct form is $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read this the night before the exam.**

- **Web Crawler** = automated agent that traverses the WWW by following hyperlinks; used for indexing, archiving, and mining.
- **BFS Crawler** = uses a **FIFO queue**; explores the web graph **level-by-level** from the seeds outward.
- The **four core components** are: **Frontier, Fetcher, Parser, Deduper**.
- The **BFS ordering property** is what makes BFS different from DFS: same-depth URLs are processed *together* before moving deeper.
- Every extracted URL must be **normalized** (resolve relative, strip `#fragment`, lowercase host) **before** dedup.
- The **`Seen` set** is essential — it prevents infinite loops and duplicate fetches. A **Bloom filter** is the space-efficient alternative for billion-URL crawls.
- **BFS Time Complexity** = $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$; **BFS Space Complexity** = $\mathcal{O}(V)$.
- BFS is **politeness-friendly** because it spreads requests across many hosts at the same depth.
- BFS **discovers hub pages early** → better quality for search-engine indices.
- BFS **avoids crawler traps** (e.g., infinite calendars, dynamically generated URLs) better than DFS.
- Real crawlers combine BFS with **priority-based ordering** (e.g., PageRank) and **per-host politeness delays**; pure BFS is the *theoretical* baseline.
- A **trace answer** must include: queue contents, `Seen` set contents, dequeued URL, and the final **BFS visit order**.
- Always mention **URL normalization** and **duplicate elimination** in the algorithm — these are the *most-asked* sub-points in KTU 2024 long-answer questions.
- **Politeness, `robots.txt` compliance, and per-host throttling** are the three "real-world" keywords examiners look for in 14-mark architecture answers.
- The classic BFS trace of $N$ pages with average out-degree $L$ at depth $d$ produces approximately $L^d$ candidate URLs in the frontier.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preferential Crawlers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT636 - INTRODUCTION TO WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Preferential Crawlers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Preferential Crawler** (also called a **Focused Crawler**, **Topical Crawler**, or **Vertical Crawler**) is an intelligent web crawler designed to selectively retrieve web pages that are topically relevant to a predefined set of user-defined subjects or domains, while avoiding the download of irrelevant pages. Unlike a *general-purpose* (universal) crawler that aims to index the entire web, a preferential crawler assigns a **preference score** to every URL/page in the crawl frontier and visits URLs in descending order of predicted relevance.

Mathematically, given a set of seed URLs $S$ and a topic description $T$ (expressed as a query, taxonomy, or vector), the crawler learns a **classifier** $C(p)$ that estimates $P(\text{relevant} \mid p)$ for any candidate page $p$. The crawl proceeds by repeatedly extracting unvisited URLs from the frontier $F$, scoring them using a combination of:

$$score(u) = \alpha \cdot C(\text{parent}(u)) + \beta \cdot S_{\text{link}}(u) + \gamma \cdot S_{\text{context}}(u)$$

where the weighting factors $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ balance the contribution of the parent page classifier score, the link-based importance, and the surrounding textual context (anchor text, surrounding paragraphs) of the URL $u$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Preferential crawling is the foundation of *vertical search engines* (e.g., medical, legal, scientific portals) and is a direct application of **supervised learning + graph traversal** in web mining.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are researching "Climate Change Effects on Coral Reefs" in a massive library containing millions of unrelated books. Instead of reading every book, you ask the librarian to use a *smart filter* that:

1. Reads the **title and abstract** of each book (URL metadata, anchor text).
2. Skips books that are obviously about cooking, romance, or sports.
3. Follows up on the **citations/references** that mention marine biology, oceanography, or environmental science.

That is exactly how a preferential crawler behaves. It is a *selectively curious* spider that only spins its web toward a chosen direction (the topic) and ignores dead ends.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Quick-Glance

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Seed Pages** | Initial set of known relevant URLs supplied as input. |
| **Crawl Frontier** | Priority queue holding URLs that have been discovered but not yet fetched. |
| **Topic Description** | Formal representation of the user's information need (query/taxonomy/vector). |
| **Page Classifier** | ML model (often Naive Bayes or SVM) scoring page relevance. |
| **Tunnel Vision** | Failure mode where crawler gets trapped inside a topical cluster and cannot escape. |
| **Drift** | Gradual degradation of relevance as crawler follows many links. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Metric to remember:** A good preferential crawler is evaluated by **Harvest Rate** = (relevant pages fetched) / (total pages fetched). Higher harvest rate ⇒ better selectivity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a Preferential Crawler

The architecture is a **closed-loop feedback system** with the following stages:

1. **Topic Specification Module** – The user provides a query, a set of example documents, or a taxonomy node. This is converted into a *topic vector* $\vec{T}$ in a vector space model (VSM).
2. **Seed Loading** – Initial seed URLs are pushed into the frontier $F$ with the highest priority.
3. **Page Fetching** – The top-$k$ URLs (by score) are dequeued from $F$ and the corresponding pages are downloaded.
4. **Page Classification** – The downloaded page is parsed and its text is converted to a feature vector $\vec{p}$ and classified as relevant / irrelevant. If relevant, the URL is added to the result set $\mathcal{R}$.
5. **Link Extraction & Context Extraction** – All hyperlinks (with anchor text and surrounding context) are extracted.
6. **URL Scoring (Prioritization)** – Each extracted URL $u$ is scored using the topic vector $\vec{T}$, the parent page's relevance, and the link's anchor text.
7. **Frontier Update** – URLs (with computed scores) are inserted into the priority queue.
8. **Loop** – Steps 3–7 repeat until the resource budget (time, page count, bandwidth) is exhausted.

## 2.2 Topic Representation

A topic is typically encoded as one of:

- **Keyword list**: $T = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n\}$
- **Boolean expression**: e.g., `(machine AND learning) AND NOT (tutorial)`
- **Weighted vector**: $\vec{T} = (w_{1}, w_{2}, \dots, w_{m})$ with TF-IDF weights over vocabulary $V$.
- **Concept hierarchy**: node within an ontology such as ODP (Open Directory Project) or Wikipedia category graph.

## 2.3 Relevance Scoring Components

### (a) Cosine Similarity with Topic Vector
The text content (or anchor text) around URL $u$ is converted to vector $\vec{a}$, and relevance is:

$$sim(\vec{a}, \vec{T}) = \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{T}}{\|\vec{a}\| \cdot \|\vec{T}\|}$$

### (b) TF-IDF Weighting
For a term $t$ appearing $f_{t,d}$ times in document $d$ across a corpus of $N$ documents where $n_t$ documents contain $t$:

$$w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot idf_t = f_{t,d} \cdot \log\!\left(\frac{N}{n_t}\right)$$

### (c) Naive Bayes Classification
For a page $p$ with feature vector $\vec{f} = (f_1, f_2, \dots, f_n)$:

$$P(\text{rel} \mid \vec{f}) \propto P(\text{rel}) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(f_i \mid \text{rel})$$

### (d) Link-Based Importance
For URL $u$ whose parent page $p$ has in-degree $I(p)$ and out-degree $O(p)$:

$$imp(u) = \frac{C(p)}{I(p)} + (1 - d) \cdot \frac{1}{N}$$

This is a PageRank-style score where $C(p)$ is the cumulative PageRank of $p$ and $d$ is the damping factor (typically **0.85**).

### (e) Combined Preferential Score
Final priority used to order the frontier:

$$Score(u) = \underbrace{\alpha \cdot C(\text{parent}(u))}_{\text{page classifier}} + \underbrace{\beta \cdot \text{sim}(\vec{a}, \vec{T})}_{\text{anchor-topic}} + \underbrace{\gamma \cdot imp(u)}_{\text{link importance}}$$

with $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$ (normalized) and $\alpha, \beta, \gamma \in [0,1]$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Symbol Meaning | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $w_{t,d} = f_{t,d} \cdot \log(N / n_t)$ | $f$ = freq, $N$ = corpus size, $n_t$ = docs containing $t$ | Compute TF-IDF feature weight |
| $\text{sim}(\vec{a}, \vec{T}) = \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{T}}{\Vert\vec{a}\Vert \, \Vert\vec{T}\Vert}$ | $\vec{a}$ = anchor vector, $\vec{T}$ = topic vector | Score URL by anchor-text similarity |
| $Score(u) = \alpha C_p + \beta S_{at} + \gamma imp(u)$ | $C_p$ = parent class, $S_{at}$ = anchor sim, $imp$ = link imp | Order frontier queue |
| $P(\text{rel} \mid \vec{f}) \propto P(\text{rel}) \prod_i P(f_i \vert \text{rel})$ | $\vec{f}$ = feature vector | Naive Bayes page classification |
| $\text{Harvest Rate} = \frac{\vert \text{Relevant fetched} \vert}{\vert \text{Total fetched} \vert}$ | — | Evaluation metric |
| $H = \frac{R}{F}$ where $R$ = relevant, $F$ = fetched | — | Same as above |
| $P(u) = (1-d)/N + d \cdot \sum_{q \to u} \frac{P(q)}{O(q)}$ | $d$ = damping, $O$ = out-degree | PageRank-style link importance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to memorize:**
> - $w_{t,d} = 0$ if term $t$ does not appear in $d$.
> - $idf_t = 0$ when $n_t = N$ (term in every doc, hence uninformative).
> - $P \in [0, 1]$ for normalized probabilities.
> - $d = 0.85$ is the universally accepted PageRank damping factor.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Preferential crawlers are the workhorses behind:

- **Vertical / Domain Search Engines** (PubMed for medical literature, Google Scholar).
- **Competitive Intelligence** systems gathering industry-specific news.
- **Web Archiving** of specific scientific, legal, or cultural domains.
- **E-commerce Price Aggregators** fetching only product pages from predefined retailers.
- **Cybersecurity Threat-Intelligence** crawlers that hunt phishing or malware-distribution pages.
- **Social Media Sentiment Mining** platforms collecting topic-specific posts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cosine Similarity as a Relevance Score

**Given:** Anchor text of URL $u$ tokenized into terms $a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n$ with TF-IDF weights forming vector $\vec{a} = (a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n)$. Topic vector $\vec{T} = (T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n)$ from the user's query (also TF-IDF weighted).

**Goal:** Compute a scalar relevance score in $[0, 1]$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Dot product} \quad \vec{a} \cdot \vec{T} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i T_i \\
\text{Step 2: Magnitude of } \vec{a} \quad \Vert\vec{a}\Vert &= \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i^{2}} \\
\text{Step 3: Magnitude of } \vec{T} \quad \Vert\vec{T}\Vert &= \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^{2}} \\
\text{Step 4: Cosine similarity} \quad \text{sim}(\vec{a}, \vec{T}) &= \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{T}}{\Vert\vec{a}\Vert \, \Vert\vec{T}\Vert} = \frac{\sum_i a_i T_i}{\sqrt{\sum_i a_i^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_i T_i^{2}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this works:** The dot product is high only when *both* vectors have large components along the *same* dimensions (i.e., shared weighted terms). The denominator normalizes for document length and query length, giving a unit-invariant score bounded in $[0, 1]$.

## 3.2 Derivation: TF-IDF Weight from First Principles

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term Frequency (normalized)} \quad tf_{t,d} &= \frac{f_{t,d}}{\max_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}} \\
\text{Inverse Document Frequency} \quad idf_t &= \log\!\left(\frac{N}{n_t}\right) \\
\text{Combined TF-IDF} \quad w_{t,d} &= tf_{t,d} \cdot idf_t = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\max_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}} \cdot \log\!\left(\frac{N}{n_t}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A term is more important to a document if (a) it appears many times in the document (high $tf$) and (b) it appears in *few* other documents (high $idf$, since common terms are uninformative).

## 3.3 Derivation: PageRank-Style Link Importance for URL $u$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(u) &= (1-d) \cdot \frac{1}{N} + d \cdot \sum_{q \to u} \frac{P(q)}{O(q)} \\
\text{Where:} \quad q &\to u \text{ means a hyperlink from page } q \text{ to } u \\
O(q) &= \text{out-degree of page } q \\
d &= 0.85 \text{ (damping)} \\
N &= \text{total pages considered}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A URL's importance inherits from the importance of pages that link to it, divided by the number of outgoing links (so popular pages distributing "votes" fairly). The $(1-d)/N$ term is the random-jump probability to escape dead ends.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem Setup:** A preferential crawler topic is **"renewable energy solar"**. Two URLs are in the frontier.

- URL $u_1$ has anchor text "**solar photovoltaic cell efficiency 2024**".
- URL $u_2$ has anchor text "**cooking recipes using solar oven**".

Vocabulary $V$ = {solar, photovoltaic, efficiency, cooking, recipes, oven, renewable, energy}. Compute cosine similarity of each anchor with topic $\vec{T}$ = (1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1, 1) (binary topic vector).

**For $u_1$ anchor tokens:** {solar, photovoltaic, cell, efficiency, 2024}. Only "solar", "photovoltaic", "efficiency" are in $V$. The intersection with $T$ is all three. Suppose TF-IDF values are $\vec{a_1} = (0.8, 0.7, 0.6, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{a_1} \cdot \vec{T} &= (0.8)(1) + (0.7)(1) + (0.6)(1) = 2.1 \\
\Vert\vec{a_1}\Vert &= \sqrt{0.64 + 0.49 + 0.36} = \sqrt{1.49} \approx 1.2207 \\
\Vert\vec{T}\Vert &= \sqrt{1+1+1+0+0+0+1+1} = \sqrt{4} = 2 \\
\text{sim}(\vec{a_1}, \vec{T}) &= \frac{2.1}{1.2207 \times 2} \approx 0.860
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $u_2$ anchor tokens:** {cooking, recipes, using, solar, oven}. The intersection with $T$ is only "solar". Suppose $\vec{a_2} = (0.5, 0, 0, 0.7, 0.6, 0.4, 0, 0)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{a_2} \cdot \vec{T} &= (0.5)(1) = 0.5 \\
\Vert\vec{a_2}\Vert &= \sqrt{0.25 + 0.49 + 0.36 + 0.16} = \sqrt{1.26} \approx 1.1225 \\
\text{sim}(\vec{a_2}, \vec{T}) &= \frac{0.5}{1.1225 \times 2} \approx 0.223
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $u_1$ has a relevance score of **0.86** versus $u_2$ at **0.22**, so the crawler will fetch $u_1$ first. This illustrates how a preferential crawler efficiently skips semantically distant URLs even when they share a single keyword ("solar").

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Preferential vs. Universal Crawler

| Parameter | Universal Crawler | Preferential Crawler |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Goal** | Index the entire web | Collect topically relevant pages |
| **Page Count Target** | Billions | Thousands to Millions |
| **Frontier Ordering** | PageRank / BFS-DFS | Topic-relevance + link importance |
| **Classifier Required** | No | Yes (supervised) |
| **Resource Use** | Massive distributed infrastructure | Efficient, single-machine feasible |
| **Drift / Tunnel Vision** | Not a concern | Critical design challenge |
| **Use Case** | Google, Bing | Vertical engines, BI tools |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a Preferential Crawler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Topic Specification] --> B[Topic Vector T]
    S[Seed URLs] --> F[Crawl Frontier Priority Queue]
    B --> F
    F --> G[Dequeue Top-k URLs]
    G --> H[HTTP Fetch Pages]
    H --> I[Parse HTML Extract Text Links]
    I --> J[Page Classifier NB or SVM]
    J -->|Relevant| R[Result Set R]
    J --> K[Extract Anchor Text and Context]
    B --> K
    K --> L[Compute Anchor Topic Similarity sim]
    I --> M[Extract Outgoing Links]
    M --> N[Compute Link Importance PageRank Style]
    L --> O[Combine alpha C + beta sim + gamma imp]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Score each new URL]
    P --> F
    Q[Resource Budget Check] -->|Exhausted| X[STOP]
    Q -->|Continue| F
```

## 4.2 URL Scoring Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[New URL Discovered] --> B{Is Parent Page Relevant?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Use high alpha weight]
    B -->|No| D[Use low alpha weight]
    C --> E[Add Anchor Similarity beta]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Add Link Importance gamma]
    F --> G[Compute Final Score]
    G --> H{Score above threshold tau?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Insert into Frontier]
    H -->|No| J[Discard URL]
```

## 4.3 Crawl Trajectory in the Web Graph

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Seed S0] --> A1[Page A1 - On Topic]
    Root --> A2[Page A2 - Off Topic]
    A1 --> B1[Page B1 - On Topic]
    A1 --> B2[Page B2 - Off Topic]
    A1 --> B3[Page B3 - On Topic]
    B1 --> C1[Page C1 - On Topic]
    B3 --> C2[Page C2 - On Topic]
    A2 --> D1[Page D1 - Off Topic]
    A2 --> D2[Page D2 - Off Topic]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above, the preferential crawler preferentially walks the **bold topical path** `S0 → A1 → B1 → C1` (and `B3 → C2`), while largely ignoring the off-topic right-hand branch. The challenge of **tunnel vision** occurs when the topical cluster is small and the crawler is forced to cross several off-topic pages to reach a new topical region.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Define a preferential crawler. How does it differ from a general-purpose web crawler?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *preferential crawler* (also called a focused or topical crawler) is a web crawler that selectively downloads web pages relevant to one or more predefined topics, guided by a classifier and a relevance scoring function over the crawl frontier.

**Differences from a general-purpose crawler (any 2, 1 mark each):**

| Feature | General Crawler | Preferential Crawler |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Scope | Whole web | Topic-restricted |
| Frontier ordering | PageRank / BFS-DFS | Topic relevance + PageRank |
| Goal | Build universal index | Harvest topical subset |

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for one clear difference, 1 mark for the table or second difference.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three techniques used by preferential crawlers to estimate the relevance of an unvisited URL.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Cosine similarity** between anchor text vector and topic vector (1 mark).
2. **Naive Bayes / SVM classification** of the parent page's text (1 mark).
3. **Link-based importance (PageRank-style) score** of the URL (1 mark).

*(Other valid answers: Context-graph analysis, reinforcement learning, semantic similarity using WordNet.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a preferential crawler with a neat block diagram. Discuss how the topic vector is constructed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the concept of *tunnel vision* and *crawler drift*. Explain at least two strategies used to mitigate these issues. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Architecture (7 marks):**

The architecture of a preferential crawler (refer to Section 4.1 diagram) consists of eight modules:

1. **Topic Specification Module (1 mark):** Accepts a user query or a set of seed documents and constructs a topic vector $\vec{T} = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$ in a Vector Space Model where each weight $w_i$ is the TF-IDF score of the $i$-th vocabulary term.

2. **Seed Loader (0.5 mark):** Inserts initial seed URLs into the frontier $F$ with maximum priority.

3. **Fetcher (0.5 mark):** Dequeues top-$k$ URLs and fetches their HTML.

4. **Parser and Link Extractor (0.5 mark):** Tokenizes the page, extracts all hyperlinks with their anchor texts.

5. **Page Classifier (1 mark):** Uses a Naive Bayes / SVM / neural classifier to label each fetched page as relevant or not.

6. **URL Scorer (1 mark):** For every unvisited URL $u$, computes the combined score

$$Score(u) = \alpha C(\text{parent}(u)) + \beta \, \text{sim}(\vec{a_u}, \vec{T}) + \gamma \, imp(u)$$

7. **Frontier Updater (0.5 mark):** Inserts scored URLs into the priority queue $F$.

8. **Resource Monitor (0.5 mark):** Stops the crawl when page/time budget is exhausted.

**Construction of topic vector (2 marks):**
- Tokenize user query or seed documents.
- Remove stopwords, apply stemming.
- Compute TF-IDF over the seed corpus.
- Form the weighted vector $\vec{T}$.

---

**(b) Tunnel Vision and Drift (7 marks):**

- **Tunnel Vision (1 mark):** When the topical cluster on the web is small, the crawler may need to traverse many off-topic "tunnel" pages to reach another relevant region, but its classifier filters them out, causing it to stop prematurely.
- **Drift (1 mark):** As the crawler follows many links, the topical focus degrades because the anchor text becomes increasingly unrelated to the original topic.

**Mitigation Strategies (any 2 × 2.5 marks):**

1. **Context Graphs (Diligenti et al.):** Build a layered graph of pages where nodes are documents and edges are explicit/implicit links. Use the *shortest path* from a fetched page back to a known seed to estimate future relevance, allowing the crawler to traverse tunnel pages.

2. **Reinforcement Learning (Rennie & McCallum):** Treat URL selection as an MDP. The state is the current page, and the reward is learned relevance; the agent learns a policy that balances exploration and exploitation.

3. **Adaptive Classifiers:** Periodically retrain the classifier using newly discovered relevant pages as positive examples (online learning).

4. **Priority Boosting for Anchor Text Match:** Even if parent page is irrelevant, the anchor text alone may indicate relevance. Boost URLs whose anchor text exactly matches topic keywords.

*(Distribute 2.5 marks per strategy: definition 1 mark + how it solves the issue 1.5 marks.)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Alternate Set]`

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain how **TF-IDF** and **Cosine Similarity** are used in a preferential crawler to rank URLs in the frontier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **PageRank-based link importance** mechanism. How is it integrated with topic relevance in a preferential crawler? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) TF-IDF and Cosine Similarity in URL Ranking (7 marks):**

- **TF-IDF (3 marks):** For a term $t$ in anchor text $a$ of URL $u$, TF-IDF weight is

$$w_{t,a} = f_{t,a} \cdot \log(N / n_t)$$

where $f_{t,a}$ is term frequency, $N$ is the corpus size, and $n_t$ is the number of anchor documents containing $t$. Higher weights identify *discriminative* topic terms. (1 mark formula + 1 mark intuition + 1 mark example.)

**Example:** For anchor text "**solar photovoltaic efficiency**" in a corpus of $N = 1000$ anchors where "photovoltaic" appears in only $5$:

$$w_{\text{photovoltaic}} = 1 \cdot \log(1000 / 5) = \log 200 \approx 2.30$$

- **Cosine Similarity (3 marks):** Anchor vector $\vec{a} = (w_{1,a}, w_{2,a}, \dots)$ and topic vector $\vec{T}$ are compared using

$$\text{sim}(\vec{a}, \vec{T}) = \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{T}}{\Vert\vec{a}\Vert \, \Vert\vec{T}\Vert}$$

URLs are dequeued in decreasing order of $\text{sim}$. (2 marks derivation + 1 mark application.)

- **Combined ranking (1 mark):** Integrate with classifier score and link importance as in $Score(u)$ formula.

---

**(b) PageRank-Based Link Importance (7 marks):**

- **PageRank definition (2 marks):** For a URL $u$,

$$P(u) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \cdot \sum_{q \to u} \frac{P(q)}{O(q)}$$

where $d = 0.85$, $O(q)$ = out-degree of $q$. (1 mark formula + 1 mark intuition.)

- **Why PageRank helps preferential crawlers (2 marks):**
  - A page with high PageRank is a "hub" of attention.
  - If such a page is on-topic, its outgoing URLs are *more likely* to lead to relevant content due to editorial quality.
  - It helps prioritize URLs from authoritative topical pages.

- **Integration with topic relevance (3 marks):** The frontier ordering combines PageRank and topical relevance linearly:

$$Score(u) = \alpha \cdot C(p_u) + \beta \cdot \text{sim}(\vec{a_u}, \vec{T}) + \gamma \cdot \frac{P(u)}{P_{\max}}$$

where $P(u) / P_{\max}$ is the normalized PageRank. (2 marks formula + 1 mark justification.) This hybrid score prevents the crawler from getting stuck in low-quality topical pages while ensuring topical focus.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Common Mark Losers):**
> 1. **Writing only definitions without equations** — 2–3 marks lost. Always state the scoring formula.
> 2. **Confusing TF and TF-IDF** — TF is raw frequency; TF-IDF incorporates corpus-wide importance. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 3. **Forgetting the damping factor $d$ in PageRank** — Always mention $d = 0.85$.
> 4. **Not differentiating Drift vs. Tunnel Vision** — Drift is *gradual* relevance decay; Tunnel Vision is *abrupt* inability to escape a topical island. Confusing them costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the architecture diagram in 7-mark questions** — at least a labeled block diagram is mandatory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ A **preferential crawler** is a topic-driven web crawler that ranks URLs in the frontier by **relevance + link importance**.
- ⭐ Key formula: $Score(u) = \alpha C(p_u) + \beta \, \text{sim}(\vec{a_u}, \vec{T}) + \gamma \, imp(u)$.
- ⭐ **TF-IDF**: $w_{t,d} = f_{t,d} \cdot \log(N / n_t)$ — discriminative term weighting.
- ⭐ **Cosine Similarity**: $\text{sim}(\vec{a}, \vec{T}) = \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{T}}{\Vert\vec{a}\Vert \, \Vert\vec{T}\Vert}$ — measures angular closeness in VSM.
- ⭐ **PageRank**: $P(u) = (1-d)/N + d \sum_{q \to u} P(q)/O(q)$ with **$d = 0.85$**.
- ⭐ **Naive Bayes**: $P(\text{rel} \mid \vec{f}) \propto P(\text{rel}) \prod_i P(f_i \mid \text{rel})$ — classifier.
- ⭐ **Evaluation metric**: Harvest Rate $H = R / F$ (relevant fetched ÷ total fetched).
- ⭐ **Drift** = gradual topic degradation. **Tunnel Vision** = inability to cross off-topic pages to new topical regions.
- ⭐ **Mitigation techniques**: context graphs, reinforcement learning, online retraining, anchor-text boosting.
- ⭐ **Architecture stages**: Topic Spec → Seed Load → Fetch → Parse → Classify → Score → Frontier Update → Loop.
- ⭐ Always state the **damping factor**, **normalization**, and **score range** in exam answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Universal Crawlers - Focused Crawlers and Topical Crawlers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT636 - INTRODUCTION TO WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Universal Crawlers - Focused Crawlers and Topical Crawlers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Universal Crawler — Formal Definition

A **Universal Crawler** (also known as an **Exhaustive Crawler**, **Broad-Crawler**, or **Surface-Web Crawler**) is a web-retrieval software agent designed to traverse and download a maximally large subset of the publicly accessible World Wide Web (WWW). It is *topic-agnostic* and *domain-agnostic*, prioritizing *coverage* and *freshness* over topical relevance. Examples include the early versions of **Googlebot**, **Yahoo! Slurp**, **Bingbot**, and **Heritrix** (open-source crawler).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024:**
> A Universal Crawler operates under the **"Goal = ALL reachable web pages"** assumption. It does not filter pages by semantic content; rather, it filters by *practical constraints* (bandwidth, politeness, storage, and freshness).

### 1.2 Focused Crawler — Formal Definition

A **Focused Crawler** (also called a **Vertical Crawler** or **Topical Crawler**, depending on implementation) is a hypertext discovery system that selectively retrieves web pages that are *relevant to a pre-defined, narrow set of topics or domains*. It uses a **relevance classifier** and a **link-context evaluator** to decide which URLs to enqueue, thereby avoiding the cost of crawling off-topic sub-graphs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (per KTU 2024 PEADT636 Module 2):**
> *"A Focused Crawler is a web crawler that attempts to download only those web pages that are relevant to a specific topic or set of topics, by leveraging the link structure of the web and on-page textual content to guide the crawling process."*

### 1.3 Topical Crawler — Formal Definition

A **Topical Crawler** is a specialized variant of the Focused Crawler where the *relevance function* is grounded in a **formal topic model** (taxonomy, ontology, or vector-space representation) rather than a simple keyword filter. It maintains an evolving **relevance model** and uses **machine-learning classifiers** (Naive Bayes, SVM, Neural) to score candidate URLs before fetching them.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogies (Real-World Mapping)

| Crawler Type | Real-World Analogy | Decision Logic |
|--------------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Universal** | A *global census-taker* who must visit every village on Earth | "Visit it because it is a page." |
| **Focused** | A *wine journalist* travelling to vineyards only | "Visit it only if it produces wine." |
| **Topical** | A *sommelier specializing in Italian red wines* | "Visit only if the vineyard grows Sangiovese or Nebbiolo." |

### 1.5 Key Engineering Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Coverage (C):** Fraction of relevant web pages retrieved.
- **Harvest Rate (H):** Ratio of relevant pages downloaded to total pages downloaded.
- **Recall (R):** Relevant pages retrieved ÷ Relevant pages on web.
- **Precision (P):** Relevant pages retrieved ÷ Total pages retrieved.
- **F-Measure:** $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$

> [!TIP]
> Universal Crawlers **maximize Recall (R)**; Focused and Topical Crawlers **maximize Precision (P)** and Harvest Rate (H).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Universal Crawler — Operational Architecture (Layered View)

A Universal Crawler is built on **five tightly coupled subsystems**:

1. **URL Frontier (URL Queue):** A persistent priority queue (often BFS-based) that holds URLs awaiting fetching. For politeness, the frontier is partitioned by host (domain-sharded sub-queues).
2. **F/DNS Resolver:** Maps hostnames to IP addresses. Caches DNS lookups to avoid repeated resolution cost.
3. **Fetcher (HTTP Client):** Issues `HTTP GET` requests (using `HEAD` first to check `Last-Modified` headers — conditional GET optimization).
4. **Parser & Link Extractor:** Parses HTML/XML, extracts `<a href="…">` and other outlinks, normalizes URLs (absolute form, URL canonicalization).
5. **Duplicate-Content Eliminator:** Uses **Simhash** / **SHA-1 fingerprints** to detect near-duplicate or already-seen pages.

### 2.2 Focused/Topical Crawler — Additional Subsystems

Layered *on top* of the Universal Crawler, three modules give it topical awareness:

1. **Topic Specification Module:** A keyword list, taxonomy, or **vector-space model** that defines "what is relevant."
2. **Classifier / Relevance Scorer:** Assigns a *relevance score* to a candidate URL or page.
3. **Link-Context Analyzer:** Examines *anchor text*, *surrounding text*, and *parent-page context* to predict the topic of the un-fetched page.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 'Why' and 'How' Matter:**
> Universal crawlers waste **~80–90% bandwidth** on irrelevant pages for narrow queries. Focused crawlers reverse this by **spending CPU on classification** to **save bandwidth and storage** — a classic trade-off.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below summarizes every formula you must memorize for KTU Part-A and Part-B questions on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\text{sim}(\vec{d},\vec{q}) = \dfrac{\vec{d} \cdot \vec{q}}{\vert\vert\vec{d}\vert\vert \, \vert\vert\vec{q}\vert\vert}$ | $\vec{d}$ = doc vector, $\vec{q}$ = query vector, result $\in [0,1]$ | Relevance scoring in topical crawlers |
| 2 | **TF-IDF Weight** | $w_{t,d} = tf(t,d) \cdot \log\dfrac{N}{df(t)}$ | $N$ = total docs, $df(t)$ = docs containing $t$ | Building document vector for cosine |
| 3 | **Naive Bayes Posterior** | $P(c \vert d) \propto P(c) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(t_i \vert c)$ | $c$ = class, $t_i$ = term | Bayesian classifier for page relevance |
| 4 | **PageRank** | $PR(p) = \dfrac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \to p} \dfrac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$ | $d$ = damping (≈0.85), $L(q)$ = out-degree of $q$ | Authority estimation for focused crawling |
| 5 | **Harvest Rate** | $H = \dfrac{\text{Relevant pages fetched}}{\text{Total pages fetched}}$ | Unitless ratio $\in [0,1]$ | Quality KPI for focused crawlers |
| 6 | **Precision** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $TP$ = true positives, $FP$ = false positives | Classification accuracy metric |
| 7 | **Recall** | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $FN$ = false negatives | Coverage metric |
| 8 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean | Balanced evaluation |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** Use **hybrid architecture** — universal crawl for index, focused crawl for vertical products (Google News, Google Scholar).
- **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Topical crawlers for price-comparison databases.
- **Cybersecurity (PhishTank):** Focused crawlers detect phishing sites within specific URL patterns.
- **Digital Libraries (CiteSeerX, JSTOR):** Topical crawlers for academic papers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Three Crawler Families Compared

| Dimension | Universal Crawler | Focused Crawler | Topical Crawler |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Objective** | Maximum coverage | Topic-relevant pages only | Highest topical precision |
| **Topic Model** | None | Keyword set | Ontology / Vector Space |
| **Classifier Used** | None (or trivial) | Rule-based or simple ML | Naive Bayes / SVM / Neural |
| **URL Ordering** | BFS / politeness-based | Relevance-priority | Adaptive (learns as it crawls) |
| **Storage Cost** | Very High (PB-scale) | Moderate | Low–Moderate |
| **Bandwidth Cost** | High | Medium | Low (with learning) |
| **Example System** | Googlebot (early), Heritrix | Focused crawler (Chakrabarti 1999) | Topical Crawler (Bergmark 2002) |
| **Failure Mode** | Misses deep web | Tunnel-vision / drift | Classifier bias |

### 3.2 Derivation — Cosine Similarity (for Topical Relevance Scoring)

**Step 1 — Define Document Vector.** Given a document $d$ with term frequencies $(tf_1, tf_2, \ldots, tf_n)$, its TF-IDF vector is:

$$
\vec{d} = (w_{1,d}, w_{2,d}, \ldots, w_{n,d})
$$

**Step 2 — Define Query Vector.** The query (or topic) is represented similarly as $\vec{q}$.

**Step 3 — Compute Dot Product.** The dot product of the two vectors measures shared term weights:

$$
\vec{d} \cdot \vec{q} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{i,d} \cdot w_{i,q}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Vector Magnitudes.** Each magnitude represents the *energy* of the term-weight distribution:

$$
\vert\vert\vec{d}\vert\vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{i,d}^{2}}, \quad \vert\vert\vec{q}\vert\vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{i,q}^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Normalize by Magnitudes.** Dividing the dot product by the product of magnitudes projects onto the unit hypersphere:

$$
\text{sim}(\vec{d},\vec{q}) = \dfrac{\vec{d} \cdot \vec{q}}{\vert\vert\vec{d}\vert\vert \, \vert\vert\vec{q}\vert\vert} = \cos(\theta)
$$

**Step 6 — Range of Output.** Since $\cos(\theta) \in [-1, 1]$ and all TF-IDF weights are non-negative:

$$
\text{sim}(\vec{d},\vec{q}) \in [0, 1]
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A value of **1.0** means perfectly relevant (cosine angle = 0°); a value of **0.0** means orthogonal (no shared terms).

### 3.3 Derivation — Naive Bayes Classifier (for Topical Crawler Page Scoring)

**Step 1 — Bayes' Theorem.** For a document $d$ and class $c$ (e.g., "relevant" vs. "irrelevant"):

$$
P(c \vert d) = \dfrac{P(d \vert c) \cdot P(c)}{P(d)}
$$

**Step 2 — Independence Assumption ("Naive").** Assume terms $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n$ in $d$ are conditionally independent given $c$:

$$
P(d \vert c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(t_i \vert c)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitution:**

$$
P(c \vert d) = \dfrac{P(c) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(t_i \vert c)}{P(d)}
$$

**Step 4 — Posterior Comparison.** Since $P(d)$ is constant across classes, we can compare classes via:

$$
c^* = \arg\max_{c} \left[ P(c) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(t_i \vert c) \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Logarithmic Form (to prevent underflow):**

$$
c^* = \arg\max_{c} \left[ \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(t_i \vert c) \right]
$$

### 3.4 PageRank — Quick Recurrence (Used in Focused Crawlers for *Authority Ordering*)

$$
PR(p) = \dfrac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \in B(p)} \dfrac{PR(q)}{L(q)}
$$

**Interpretation of Terms:**

- $PR(p)$ = PageRank of page $p$ (importance score).
- $d$ = **damping factor** (typical value: **0.85**), modelling the probability a random surfer continues clicking.
- $N$ = total number of pages in the crawl universe.
- $B(p)$ = set of pages linking to $p$ (backlinks).
- $L(q)$ = number of outbound links on page $q$.

> **Note for KTU:** A Focused Crawler can re-rank the URL frontier by **relevance × PageRank** to avoid being trapped inside low-authority topical neighbourhoods (the *tunnel-vision problem*).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Universal Crawler — End-to-End Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Seed URL List] --> B1[URL Frontier with Politeness Sharding]
    B1 --> C1[DNS Resolver and Cache]
    C1 --> D1[HTTP Fetcher with HEAD Pre-check]
    D1 --> E1[HTML Parser and Link Extractor]
    E1 --> F1{Duplicate Check via Simhash}
    F1 -->|Duplicate| B1
    F1 -->|Unique| G1[Content Repository Index Storage]
    E1 --> H1[Extracted Outlinks]
    H1 --> I1[URL Normalizer and Canonicalizer]
    I1 --> B1

    classDef io fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef store fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    class A1,G1 io
    class B1,C1,D1,E1,F1,H1,I1 proc
```

**Reading the Diagram (Step-Wise):**

1. **Seed Injection:** The crawler is initialized with a small set of seed URLs.
2. **Frontier Scheduling:** URLs are placed in a priority queue sharded by host to obey politeness.
3. **DNS + Fetch:** Each URL is resolved and fetched using `HTTP GET` (after an optional `HEAD` request).
4. **Parse & Extract:** The HTML is parsed, links are extracted, and URLs are normalized to absolute canonical form.
5. **Duplicate Filter:** Content fingerprints are checked to avoid redundant storage.
6. **Storage:** Unique pages are written to the index repository.
7. **Loop:** Newly discovered outlinks feed back into the frontier, creating a recursive traversal cycle.

### 4.2 Focused / Topical Crawler — Augmented Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Topic Seed Topics Keywords Ontology] --> CLS[Topic Classifier Naive Bayes or SVM]
    CLS --> SDB[(Relevance Model Training Data)]

    UF1[URL Frontier] --> LC[Link Context Analyzer Anchor Text Parent Page]
    LC --> RSC[Relevance Scorer Cosine or NB]
    CLS --> RSC
    UF1 --> FTH[HTTP Fetcher]
    FTH --> PRP[HTML Parser and Link Extractor]
    PRP --> SCORE[Page-Level Relevance Score]
    SCORE --> DEC{Threshold Met}
    DEC -->|No| UF1
    DEC -->|Yes| STORE[(Relevant Content Store)]
    PRP --> UF1
    SCORE --> UF1

    classDef topic fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,color:#000
```
