```mermaid
    classDef core fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef store fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    class S1,CLS,SDB topic
    class UF1,LC,RSC,FTH,PRP,SCORE,DEC core
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

1. **Topic Definition:** A topic model is loaded (keywords, ontology, or training corpus).
2. **Classifier Training:** A model is built and continuously updated as new pages are confirmed relevant.
3. **Link-Context Analysis:** Even *before* fetching a page, anchor text and parent-page context are scored for predicted relevance.
4. **Threshold Gate:** Only pages with relevance score above a tunable threshold are stored and their outlinks enqueued.
5. **Feedback Loop:** Confirmed-relevant pages refine the classifier, producing *adaptive* crawling.

### 4.3 Comparative Trajectory — Coverage vs. Relevance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[Universal Crawler Maximize Recall Explore All Topics] -->|Tradeoff| F[Focused Crawler Balance Recall and Precision Topic Bound]
    F -->|Tradeoff| T[Topical Crawler Maximize Precision Narrow and Deep]

    classDef uni fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#003399
    classDef foc fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600
    classDef top fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d7a2d
    class U uni
    class F foc
    class T top
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> - Part A: 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks
> - Part B: Choice-based, 1 out of 2 × 14 marks = 14 marks
> - **Total: 20 marks per module in ESE.**

---

### 📘 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Style]**
**Differentiate between a Universal Crawler and a Focused Crawler. List any two real-world examples for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Universal Crawler | Focused Crawler |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Objective | Crawl the entire web | Crawl pages relevant to a specific topic |
| Topic Filter | None | Yes (keyword/ontology-based) |
| Page Storage | All reachable pages | Only relevant pages |
| Resource Cost | Very High (PB-scale) | Moderate |
| Examples | **Googlebot**, **Heritrix** | **CiteSeerX crawler**, **NewsBot** |

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for clear difference, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for distinguishing criterion.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style]**
**Define a Topical Crawler. What is the role of a "link-context analyzer" in it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Topical Crawler** is a web crawler that uses a **formal topic model** (taxonomy, ontology, or vector-space representation) to guide the traversal, retrieving only pages whose content matches the topic distribution defined by the model.

The **link-context analyzer** examines the **anchor text**, **surrounding paragraph text**, and **parent-page topical score** to *predict* the relevance of an un-fetched URL — *before* consuming bandwidth to download it. This is the core optimization that makes topical crawlers bandwidth-efficient.

*(Examiner's Note: 2 marks for definition, 1 mark for the role of link-context analyzer.)*

---

### 📕 PART B — Choice-Based 14-Mark Questions

> **Instructions:** Answer **ONE** of the following two full questions (each 14 marks).

---

#### **Q3A. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) Explain the architecture of a Universal Crawler with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the URL Frontier and the Duplicate-Content Eliminator.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **Universal Crawler** is composed of the following subsystems operating in a continuous feedback loop:

1. **Seed Loader:** Provides initial URLs.
2. **URL Frontier:** A **priority queue**, often sharded by host to enforce **politeness** (one connection per host, with a delay between requests).
3. **DNS Resolver:** Resolves hostnames to IPs; caches results to avoid repeated lookups.
4. **Fetcher:** Uses **HTTP GET**; may use `HEAD` to check `Last-Modified` headers for conditional fetches.
5. **Parser & Link Extractor:** Tokenizes HTML, extracts `<a href="…">` and other references; normalizes URLs.
6. **Duplicate-Content Eliminator:** Computes a **Simhash** or **SHA-1 fingerprint** of each page; pages with fingerprints already in the seen-set are discarded.
7. **Content Repository:** Stores unique pages for indexing.

**Role of the URL Frontier:** The frontier is the *scheduling engine* of the crawler. It decides which URL to fetch next. For politeness, the frontier is partitioned into per-host sub-queues. A common implementation is the **Mercator**-style multi-queue with a *front-queue* (priority-based) and *back-queue* (BFS-based).

**Role of the Duplicate-Content Eliminator:** The web contains massive redundancy (mirrors, syndicated articles, alias domains). The eliminator uses **Simhash** (a locality-sensitive hash) to flag near-duplicates in $O(1)$ time. Without it, the storage cost would explode by an order of magnitude.

*(Marks: 3 marks for diagram/architecture listing, 2 marks for frontier explanation, 2 marks for duplicate eliminator.)*

---

**(b) Discuss the major challenges faced by Universal Crawlers. How does a Universal Crawler decide which URL to fetch next from the frontier?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Major Challenges of Universal Crawlers:**

1. **Scale:** The web has **>50 billion pages** (post-2023 estimates). Crawling all of them is physically infeasible.
2. **Bandwidth & Politeness:** Aggressive crawling violates *robots.txt* and harms target servers. Crawlers must enforce per-host delays.
3. **Freshness:** Web content changes dynamically. Crawlers must re-visit important pages more often — a **refresh policy** is needed.
4. **Dee-Web Pages:** Pages behind forms, JavaScript, or login walls are not reachable via simple `GET` requests.
5. **Duplicate & Near-Duplicate Content:** ~30% of web pages are duplicates.
6. **Spider Traps:** Servers generating infinite URL spaces (e.g., calendars) can trap naive crawlers.
7. **Storage & Indexing:** Even after deduplication, storage is **petabyte-scale**.

**URL Scheduling Strategy:**

The frontier typically uses a **multi-queue (Mercator-style) algorithm**:
- URLs are placed in a **back queue** in BFS order.
- They are promoted to a **front queue** only when their host is eligible to be contacted.
- Within the front queue, a **priority function** (often $\text{Priority} = \text{PageRank-like score}$) determines the fetch order.

*(Marks: 4 marks for challenges, 3 marks for scheduling algorithm.)*

---

#### **Q3B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) Explain the working of a Focused Crawler with a clear diagram. How is the relevance of a candidate URL determined *before* it is fetched?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **Focused Crawler** works on the principle of *selective expansion*. Given a topic definition, it expands the crawl frontier only along links whose *predicted* relevance exceeds a threshold.

**Pre-Fetch Relevance Determination (the Key Innovation):**

Before a URL is enqueued for fetching, the crawler computes a **predicted relevance score** using:

$$
\text{PredictedRelevance}(u) = f(\text{AnchorText}(u), \text{ParentScore}(p), \text{ContextWindow}(u))
$$

- **Anchor Text:** The text inside `<a href="u">` is treated as a brief summary of the target page.
- **Parent Page Score:** The topical score of the page $p$ that contains link $u$.
- **Context Window:** The text surrounding the anchor in $p$.

The crawler builds a feature vector for $u$, then computes **cosine similarity** against the topic vector:

$$
\text{sim}(u, T) = \dfrac{\vec{f}_u \cdot \vec{T}}{\vert\vert\vec{f}_u\vert\vert \, \vert\vert\vec{T}\vert\vert}
$$

If $\text{sim}(u, T) \geq \theta$ (threshold), $u$ is enqueued. This *pre-filter* saves bandwidth.

**Post-Fetch Refinement:** Once fetched, the page is parsed and its full body is classified. The classifier updates its parameters using the confirmed-relevant page (online learning).

*(Marks: 4 marks for pre-fetch relevance, 3 marks for cosine similarity application.)*

---

**(b) Describe the role of classifiers and link-context analysis in topical crawling. List any two classifier algorithms used.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Classifiers:**

In a Topical Crawler, the **classifier** is the *brain* of the system. It serves two functions:

1. **Page-Level Classification:** After a page is fetched, the classifier assigns it a category label (relevant / not relevant), which determines whether the page is stored.
2. **URL-Level Prediction:** Before fetching, the classifier uses anchor text and context to predict the topic of the un-fetched URL.

**Role of Link-Context Analysis:**

The **link-context analyzer** exploits the *local graph structure* around a URL:
- It builds a **feature vector** for the URL from its anchor text, the surrounding text in the parent page, and the headings near the link.
- This vector is fed to the classifier to obtain a *relevance probability*.

**Why It Matters:** Without link-context analysis, the crawler would either crawl blindly (universal) or rely on full-page download (expensive). Link-context analysis makes the crawl **proactive** — the crawler *predicts* the destination before downloading it.

**Two Classifier Algorithms Used:**

1. **Naive Bayes Classifier:** Probabilistic, fast to train, assumes conditional independence among terms. Works well for high-dimensional sparse text data.
2. **Support Vector Machine (SVM):** Finds the optimal hyperplane separating relevant from irrelevant pages in feature space; often outperforms Naive Bayes in precision.

*(Marks: 3 marks for classifier role, 2 marks for link-context role, 2 marks for algorithm enumeration with brief notes.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — DO NOT LOSE MARKS HERE:**
> 1. **Confusing Topical with Focused:** Many students treat "Topical" and "Focused" as synonyms. They are *not identical* — Topical Crawlers use a **formal ontology/vector model**, while Focused Crawlers may use a simpler keyword filter.
> 2. **Skipping the Diagram:** Part B (a) questions *always* reward a labelled diagram. Skipping it costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the Formula:** Even in 7-mark sub-parts, examiners expect at least *one* formula (e.g., cosine similarity or PageRank). Memorize all eight from the Formula Sheet in Section 2.
> 4. **No Mark for Examples Alone:** When asked to "differentiate," tabular or bulleted *differences* are mandatory. Examples alone earn only 1 mark out of 3 in Part A.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Universal Crawler** = *coverage-first, topic-agnostic* (e.g., Googlebot, Heritrix).
- [x] **Focused Crawler** = *topic-restricted, keyword-driven*, balances precision and recall.
- [x] **Topical Crawler** = *ontology/vector-space driven, classifier-guided*, maximizes precision.
- [x] **Five Universal Crawler Subsystems:** Seed Loader, URL Frontier, DNS Resolver, Fetcher, Parser + Duplicate Eliminator.
- [x] **Three Focused/Topical Additions:** Topic Specification, Classifier/Relevance Scorer, Link-Context Analyzer.
- [x] **Cosine Similarity** is the *de facto* relevance metric in topical crawlers.
- [x] **Naive Bayes** and **SVM** are the most commonly tested classifiers.
- [x] **PageRank** is used in *authority-aware* focused crawlers to avoid tunnel-vision.
- [x] **Harvest Rate (H)** = KPI for focused crawlers; **Coverage (C)** = KPI for universal crawlers.
- [x] **Politeness Policy** = per-host delay enforced via sharded URL frontier (Mercator architecture).
- [x] **Simhash** = locality-sensitive hash for near-duplicate detection.
- [x] **Tunnel-Vision Problem** = focused crawler trapped in a deep but narrow topical sub-graph; mitigated by **PageRank blending**.
- [x] **Damping factor $d$** in PageRank = **0.85** (memorize this number).
- [x] **$\log(N / df(t))$** = IDF component; memorize the formula exactly.
- [x] **KTU 2024 favourite 14-mark question type:** *"Explain Universal Crawler architecture with diagram" + "Discuss challenges"* (a universal favourite).
- [x] **KTU 2024 favourite 14-mark alternative:** *"Focused Crawler with pre-fetch relevance" + "Classifiers in topical crawling"*.
- [x] Always cite the *formula* in the answer; KTU examiners reward mathematical precision.

> 📌 **Final Tip:** When answering a KTU question on this topic, always lead with the **diagram** (worth 2–3 marks), follow with the **subsystem list**, and close with **one formula + one real-world example** — this structure has consistently earned full marks in past KTU evaluations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wrapper Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEADT636 - INTRODUCTION TO WEB MINING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Wrapper Generation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wrapper (Web Data Extraction Context):** A *wrapper* is a software procedure / program module that is designed to **systematically locate, recognize, extract, and structure data** residing within semi-structured or unstructured web pages (typically HTML / XML / JSON) and transform it into a **machine-readable, relational format** (e.g., tuples, XML records, JSON objects, database rows). > **Wrapper Generation** is the broader engineering process of *automatically* (or semi-automatically) *constructing* these extraction rules — known as **extraction rules** or **wrapper rules** — so that data can be pulled from a target web source in a scalable, repeatable, and maintainable way without writing a new parser for every page. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, wrapper generation falls under the broader umbrella of **Information Extraction (IE)** and is a foundational operation in **Web Mining → Structured Data Extraction** workflows used in search engines, price comparators, business intelligence, and knowledge-graph construction. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a **wrapper as a "smart magnetic fishing net"** 🎣 cast into the chaotic ocean of the World Wide Web. - The ocean (**the Web**) is filled with **fish of every shape and size** (**heterogeneous HTML pages**) — Amazon listings, IRCTC train schedules, university notices, cricket scorecards. - A **regular net** (**a hard-coded parser / regex**) works only on *one specific boat's catch pattern* (one fixed site). - A **smart magnetic net with attractors** (**a wrapper**) is pre-configured with *magnets* (extraction rules) — *"catch anything that looks like a price near a rupee sign"*, *"catch anything inside ``"*. - The **art of *designing* and *attaching the right magnets* to the net** is **Wrapper Generation**. - The **magnet design** itself is learned by observing a few sample catches (training pages) — this is called **Wrapper Induction**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The wrapper does *not* change the original web page. It only *reads* the page, identifies regions of interest using learned patterns, and *projects* them into a structured table. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (Bolded for Quick Recall) - **Precision (P):** Fraction of extracted items that are *correct* = $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ - **Recall (R):** Fraction of *all correct* items that are extracted = $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ - **F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of P and R = $\frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ - **Concept Drift:** Site structure change rate (forces wrapper re-learning) - **Token:** A lexical unit between delimiters (e.g., a word, tag, or punctuation). - **Landmark:** A delimiter string in the page that occurs just *before* (prefix landmark) or *after* (suffix landmark) the target data region. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** Wrapper Generation directly serves **CO3 – "Apply extraction techniques to mine structured information from the web"** under **Module 3 – Web Information Extraction**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 Why Are Wrappers Needed? The web was originally designed **for humans**, not machines. Consequently: 1. **Heterogeneity** — Thousands of templates, no universal schema. 2. **Semi-structured content** — A mix of free text, tags, scripts, images. 3. **High redundancy** — Header, footer, ads repeated on every page. 4. **Concept drift** — Sites get redesigned every few months. 5. **Scale** — Billions of pages; manual extraction is infeasible. A wrapper abstracts this chaos by **separating the *what to extract* (declarative rules) from the *how to extract it* (procedural engine)**. ### 2.2 Categories of Wrapper Generation | # | Category | Learning Signal | Human Effort | Typical Algorithm | |---|----------|-----------------|--------------|-------------------| | 1 | **Manual Wrapper** | None | Very High (hand-coded) | ad-hoc scripts / regex | | 2 | **Supervised (Wrapper Induction)** | Labeled pages | Medium | HLRT, SoftMealy, Stalker, WIEN | | 3 | **Semi-Supervised** | Few labels + heuristics | Low | Co-training, dual-view learners | | 4 | **Unsupervised** | None | None | RoadRunner, EXALG, DEPTA | | 5 | **Visual / Interactive** | User clicks on sample items | Low | Lixto, ViPER, iWrap | ### 2.3 The Wrapper Generation Pipeline (High-Yield for KTU) 1. **Page Fetcher / Pre-processor** — Downloads the target page, resolves JavaScript, removes noise. 2. **Tokenizer / Tree Builder** — Converts the page into a DOM tree (HTML Parser) or token stream. 3. **Page Classifier** — Distinguishes *list/detail/index* pages. 4. **Sample Annotator** (only in supervised) — User labels a few fields. 5. **Induction / Learning Engine** — Learns extraction rules. 6. **Rule Validator** — Tests rules on held-out pages. 7. **Wrapper Deployer** — Applies learned rules to unseen pages, emits structured records. ### 2.4 KTU Cheat Sheet — Key Algorithms at a Glance | Algorithm | Approach | Rule Type | Strength | Limitation | |-----------|----------|-----------|----------|------------| | **WIEN** | Supervised | LR / HLRT (k-tuples around target) | Simple, provable | Fixed k | | **SoftMealy** | Supervised | Finite-State Transducer (FST) | Handles context | Complex training | | **Stalker** | Supervised | Landmark Automata (landmark tree) | Hierarchical, scalable | Needs tree | | **HLRT** | Supervised | Head–Left–Right–Tail delimiters | Easy to visualize | Single slot | | **RoadRunner** | Unsupervised | ACME (automata) via tag-set comparison | Fully automatic | Nested tags fail | | **EXALG** | Unsupervised | Equivalence classes of tokens | Robust to noise | Computationally heavy | | **DEPTA** | Unsupervised (partial) | Tree-edit + record alignment | Good for tables | Misses free text | ### 2.5 Engineering Utility (Real-World) - **E-commerce aggregators** (e.g., Google Shopping) → compare prices across vendors. - **Job portals** → Indeed, Naukri unify postings from thousands of company career sites. - **Cricket/finance dashboards** → ESPN, MoneyControl pull live data via wrappers. - **Research** → Build scholarly knowledge bases (DBLP, CiteSeer). - **Enterprise ETL** → Replacing expensive API subscriptions with deterministic web scrapers. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Mapping:** Google's acquisition and continued operation of *Google Product Search* relied on scalable wrapper generation for over a decade before the rise of structured feeds. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — The HLRT Wrapper Induction Algorithm Because HLRT (Head–Left–Right–Tail) is the most frequently asked algorithm in KTU examinations, we provide a complete, step-by-step worked example. ### 3.1 Problem Setup Consider the following **unstructured HTML list** from a hypothetical *Used-Car Portal*: ```html
  • Sedan | Honda City | 2019 | 750000 | Mumbai
  • SUV | Toyota Fortuner | 2021 | 2800000 | Delhi
  • Hatchback | Maruti Swift | 2020 | 550000 | Bengaluru
``` **Goal:** Extract the *Price* attribute (the 4th token, after the 3rd `|`) into a structured tuple. The HLRT rule for a single field is a **4-tuple** `(H, L, R, T)`: - **H (Head)** — the *closing* delimiter that marks the *start* of the target field. - **L (Left)** — the *opening* delimiter of the *preceding* separator. - **R (Right)** — the *closing* delimiter of the *target* (or the *opening* of the next separator). - **T (Tail)** — delimiter marking the *end* of the region of interest. ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation (Exhaustive) We will extract the **4th field** (`Price`) from each line. **Step 1 — Tokenize the page** Each `
  • ...
    • ` line is a list of tokens separated by `|`. The page-level delimiters include `
  • `, `
    • `, `
      `, `
    `, and the `|` character. **Step 2 — Compute the *Left* context** (the delimiter that *opens* the price slot) Looking at every occurrence of the 4th `|`, the *token immediately to its left* is the 3rd field (`Year`). So we look for the **most specific common left delimiter** across all training examples. Across the three lines, the substring **immediately before** the price is consistently the pattern `" | 20"` or `" | 19"` — i.e., the *closing* delimiter of the year, which is a digit followed by `" | "`. For induction simplicity, the algorithm records: $$L_{\text{price}} = \text{``}\vert\text{ ``}$$ (the literal pipe-plus-space substring, which is the closing of the previous field). **Step 3 — Compute the *Right* context** (the delimiter that *closes* the price slot) The substring immediately after the price in *all* training rows is `" | "`, which opens the next (City) field. So: $$R_{\text{price}} = \text{``} \vert \text{ ``}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the *Head* and *Tail*** (start and end of the *search region*) Since this is a *non-disjunctive* extraction, we set: $$H = \text{``the 3rd `}\vert\text{ after the start of the
  • ''} \quad ; \quad T = \text{``the 4th `}\vert\text{''}$$ **Step 5 — Final Rule** $$ \text{Rule}_{4} = \big( H,\; L,\; R,\; T \big) \;=\; \big( \text{3rdPipe},\; \text{``} \vert \text{ ``},\; \text{``} \vert \text{ ``},\; \text{4thPipe} \big) $$ **Step 6 — Apply to a new unseen line** ```html
  • Coupe | BMW M2 | 2023 | 6800000 | Pune
    • ``` Execution trace: 1. Start scanning after `
  • `. 2. Skip past 1st `|`, 2nd `|`, 3rd `|` → cursor now at the *start* of `2023`. 3. Apply left delimiter `L` → *consume* `" | "` and reach `2023`. 4. Apply right delimiter `R` → *stop* at the next `" | "` (between price and city). 5. Capture the substring between cursor and stop → `"6800000"`. 6. Emit tuple. ### 3.3 Generalization to k-Slots (LR-Wrappers) For a page containing *k* data fields, the induction algorithm produces **k HLRT rules**, one per field. The rules are executed sequentially from slot 1 to slot *k*, with the *Tail* of rule *i* serving as the *Head* of rule *i+1*. This chaining is the heart of **LR-Wrappers** (Kushmerick et al., 1997 — foundational, no specific edition cited per anti-hallucination protocol). ### 3.4 Worked Calculation — Precision and Recall of a Learned Wrapper Suppose a learned wrapper is applied to a *test set* of **200** product pages and the confusion matrix is as follows: | | Actually a record (TP/FN) | Actually not (FP/TN) | |---|---:|---:| | Extracted | 150 | 10 | | Not extracted | 5 | 35 | **Step-by-step computation:** $$ P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{150}{150 + 10} = \frac{150}{160} = 0.9375 $$ $$ R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{150}{150 + 5} = \frac{150}{155} = 0.9677 $$ $$ F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.9375 \cdot 0.9677}{0.9375 + 0.9677} $$ Evaluating the numerator and denominator step by step: $$ F_1 = \frac{1.8145}{1.9052} \approx 0.9523 $$ Hence the wrapper's quality: $$ P \approx 93.75\%,\quad R \approx 96.77\%,\quad F_1 \approx 95.23\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently give a 2×2 confusion matrix and ask for P, R, F1. Always write the formula first, plug in the values, and show the final numeric result with two decimal places. ### 3.5 Disjunctive HLRT — Handling Variability In real sites, the left context may *vary* across pages (e.g., a field can be preceded by either `" | "` or `" - "`). The algorithm stores a *set* of alternative delimiters, forming a **disjunctive rule**: $$ L = \{\, \text{``} \vert \text{ ``},\; \text{``} - \text{ ``},\; \text{``}, \text{``} \,\} $$ The wrapper engine tries each alternative left-to-right; the *first successful match* wins. This is the formal mechanism by which HLRT handles layout variation in wrapper induction. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 End-to-End Wrapper Generation Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Raw Web Page URL] --> B[HTTP Fetcher and HTML Parser] B --> C[DOM Tree Builder] C --> D[Page Classifier - List vs Detail] D --> E[Labeled Sample Pages] E --> F[Wrapper Induction Engine] F --> G[Extraction Rules: HLRT / FST / Landmark Tree] G --> H[Rule Validator on Hold-Out Pages] H --> I{Acceptable F1 Score?} I -- No --> J[Re-train with More Samples] J --> F I -- Yes --> K[Deploy Wrapper to Production] K --> L[Apply to Unseen Pages] L --> M[Structured Records DB or JSON] M --> N[Downstream Apps: Search, BI, KG] ``` ### 4.2 Wrapper Induction vs Wrapper Construction ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph M1[Manual Approach] M1A[Human Engineer] --> M1B[Hand-Coded Regex or XSLT] M1B --> M1C[Wrapper for Site S1] end subgraph M2[Supervised Induction] M2A[Few Labeled Pages] --> M2B[Induction Engine] M2B --> M2C[Learned Rules] M2C --> M2D[Wrapper for Site S1] end subgraph M3[Unsupervised / Automatic] M3A[Pages S1, S2, S3] --> M3B[Pattern Discovery] M3B --> M3C[ACME / Equivalence Classes] M3C --> M3D[Wrapper Generalized] end ``` ### 4.3 HLRT Rule Anatomy (Per-Slot) ```mermaid flowchart LR X1[Slot Start = Head H] --> X2[Scan until Left L matches] X2 --> X3[Capture target region] X3 --> X4[Stop when Right R matches] X4 --> X5[Slot End = Tail T] X5 --> X6[Emit Field Value] X6 --> X7[Cursor advances to T] X7 --> X8{More slots?} X8 -- Yes --> X1 X8 -- No --> X9[Emit Complete Record] ``` ### 4.4 Wrapper Lifecycle with Concept-Drift Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Wrapper Deployed] --> P2[Daily Crawl of Target Site] P2 --> P3{Quality KPIs OK?} P3 -- Yes --> P2 P3 -- No --> P4[Drift Detector Flags Anomaly] P4 --> P5[Re-annotate Sample Pages] P5 --> P6[Re-induce Rules] P6 --> P7[Versioned Wrapper Update] P7 --> P2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Note:** Mermaid diagrams above satisfy the Alpha-Node rule (all node IDs are alphanumeric such as `P1`, `X8`, `M1A`) and contain no markdown inside labels, ensuring clean rendering. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3] *Define a wrapper in the context of web mining. Mention any two limitations of hand-coded (manual) wrappers.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** A *wrapper* is a software module that systematically extracts and structures data from semi-structured web pages by recognizing delimiters and patterns around target fields, converting the output into a relational or hierarchical form. > > **Limitations of manual wrappers (2 × 0.75 = 1.5 marks):** > 1. **Brittleness** — any change in the site's HTML template breaks the wrapper (no resilience to *concept drift*). > 2. **Poor scalability** — writing and maintaining one parser per site becomes infeasible across thousands of sources. > 3. **High human cost** — skilled engineers are required for routine extraction tasks. --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest Pattern] *Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised wrapper generation with one example algorithm for each.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised | |---|---|---| | Training data | Labeled sample pages | Unlabeled pages | | Human effort | Medium (labeling) | None | | Output rule | Induced from labels | Discovered from regularities | | Example algorithm | **HLRT / Stalker / SoftMealy** | **RoadRunner / EXALG / DEPTA** | | Robustness to noise | High (labels disambiguate) | Variable | *(1.5 marks for the contrast table + 1.5 marks for the two named algorithms.)* ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 14 Marks) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Wrapper Induction using HLRT* **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern, Module 3]** **(a)** Explain the **HLRT (Head–Left–Right–Tail)** rule formalism for wrapper induction. Using a clearly stated example HTML list of 4 records (each record containing exactly 5 fields separated by a common delimiter), demonstrate how the algorithm extracts the **3rd field** by writing out the 4-tuple rule and showing the step-by-step extraction on **one of the records**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss **two key limitations** of the HLRT approach. For each limitation, briefly describe how **Stalker** or **SoftMealy** mitigates it. **(7 Marks)** ##### Model Solution **(a) HLRT Rule Formalism (7 Marks)** - **Definition (2 marks):** A single-slot HLRT rule is a 4-tuple `(H, L, R, T)`, where *H* and *T* are the *head* and *tail* delimiters that bracket the region of interest, and *L*, *R* are the *left* and *right* *context delimiters* identifying where the target field begins and ends inside that region. A multi-slot wrapper is a sequence of such rules, one per field, chained by passing *T* of rule *i* as the *H* of rule *i+1*. - **Example HTML (1.5 marks):** ```html
    1AsusLaptop45000Kochi
    2AppleTablet78000Trivandrum
    3SamsungPhone22000Kozhikode
    4LenovoLaptop55000Kollam
    ``` - **Induced rule for the 3rd field (2 marks):** $$ \text{Rule}_{3} = \big( H,\; L,\; R,\; T \big) = \big( \text{2nd}\langle\text{td}\rangle,\; \text{``}\langle\text{td}\rangle\text{``},\; \text{``}\langle\text{/td}\rangle\text{``},\; \text{3rd}\langle\text{td}\rangle \big) $$ - **Step-by-step extraction on Record 2 — `"Apple"` (1.5 marks):** 1. *H* = advance to the **2nd ``** opening tag. 2. *L* = match `` and place cursor at start of cell content. 3. Capture text up to *R* = `` → captured text is `"Apple"`. 4. *T* = advance cursor to the **3rd ``** opening tag (start of next cell). 5. Emit `"Apple"` as the 3rd field. **(b) Limitations and Mitigations (7 Marks)** | # | Limitation of HLRT | Mitigation by Stalker / SoftMealy | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Single linear slot** — cannot naturally express nested or hierarchical records. | **Stalker** uses a *landmark tree* that follows the HTML DOM tree, supporting nested record extraction. **(3.5 marks)** | | 2 | **Fixed delimiter assumption** — k delimiters per slot, struggles with optional or missing fields. | **SoftMealy** represents rules as *finite-state transducers* with *skip-to* actions that tolerate missing or variable tokens. **(3.5 marks)** | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *RoadRunner and Unsupervised Wrapper Generation* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern, Module 3]** **(a)** Describe the **RoadRunner** algorithm for *automatic (unsupervised) wrapper generation*. Clearly explain the role of **ACME (Automated Content Match Engine)** and how it handles *disjunctive* page templates. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Consider a test set of 150 web pages processed by a learned wrapper. Of these, 120 are correctly identified as data pages, 15 are *false positives* (extracted junk), and 5 data pages are *missed*. Compute **Precision, Recall, and F1-Score**, showing every substitution step. **(7 Marks)** ##### Model Solution **(a) RoadRunner Algorithm (7 Marks)** - **Concept (2 marks):** RoadRunner takes *multiple sample pages* from the same template and **iteratively refines a generalized regular expression (the "wrapper-as-regex")** that matches all observed variants. - **ACME (2 marks):** The *Automata-based Content Matching Engine* drives the induction. It performs **tag-set comparisons** between the current hypothesized template and a new sample. When a *mismatch* is found (e.g., an optional `` or an extra `
    `), ACME *generalizes* the template by replacing fixed substrings with regex operators like `+` (one or more) or `*` (zero or more) — and inserts *disjunctions* where two samples disagree. - **Disjunctive templates (2 marks):** When Page 1 has `
    ` and Page 2 has ``, ACME produces the disjunctive token `(
    \mid )`, allowing the wrapper to handle both layouts. - **Iteration & termination (1 mark):** The process repeats until the regex matches *all* (or a configurable fraction of) training pages with no further mismatches. **(b) Precision / Recall / F1 Computation (7 Marks)** Given: - $TP = 120$ (correct extractions) - $FP = 15$ (junk extracted) - $FN = 5$ (data missed) - $TN$ is unused for these metrics. **Step 1 — Precision:** $$ P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{120}{120 + 15} = \frac{120}{135} = 0.8889 $$ **Step 2 — Recall:** $$ R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{120}{120 + 5} = \frac{120}{125} = 0.96 $$ **Step 3 — F1-Score:** $$ F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.8889 \cdot 0.96}{0.8889 + 0.96} = \frac{1.7067}{1.8489} \approx 0.9230 $$ **Final Answer (1 mark):** $$ P \approx 88.89\%,\quad R = 96.00\%,\quad F_1 \approx 92.30\% $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > 1. Do **not** include $TN$ in P, R, or F1 formulas — these metrics are computed from a *retrieval* confusion matrix, not a classifier 2×2 with class-balance terms. > 2. Always **show the substitution step** (`120/135 = 0.8889`) before giving the final decimal. Examiners award partial marks for *each* correct substitution, not just the final number. > 3. In Q-A-(a), students frequently confuse *Head* with *Left*. Remember: *Head* and *Tail* **bracket the search region**; *Left* and *Right* **locate the target inside it**. ### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Wrapper** = extraction rule + execution engine that converts web pages → structured records. - ✅ **Wrapper Generation** = the act of *learning or hand-coding* these rules. - ✅ **Categories:** Manual, Supervised (Induction), Semi-supervised, Unsupervised (Automatic), Visual. - ✅ **Key supervised algorithms:** HLRT, WIEN, SoftMealy, Stalker. - ✅ **Key unsupervised algorithms:** RoadRunner, EXALG, DEPTA. - ✅ **HLRT Rule:** 4-tuple `(H, L, R, T)` — **H**ead, **L**eft, **R**ight, **T**ail delimiters, chained per slot. - ✅ **Stalker strength:** landmark *tree* supports nested/hierarchical records. - ✅ **SoftMealy strength:** finite-state transducer tolerates missing/variable tokens. - ✅ **RoadRunner / ACME:** unsupervised, produces a *generalized regex* template; handles disjunctions automatically. - ✅ **Quality Metrics:** Precision $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$, Recall $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$, F1 $\frac{2PR}{P+R}$ — do NOT include $TN$. - ✅ **Concept Drift** = inevitable; every deployed wrapper needs a *drift monitor* and *re-induction trigger*. - ✅ **Real-world applications:** price comparison engines, job aggregators, knowledge-graph population, financial intelligence dashboards. - ✅ **KTU 2024 alignment:** Module 3, CO3 — *Apply extraction techniques to mine structured information from the web*.

topics.title: Preliminaries - Wrapper Induction - Instance Based Wrapper Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEADT636 - INTRODUCTION TO WEB MINING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Preliminaries - Wrapper Induction - Instance Based Wrapper Learning # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Wrapper? — Formal Definition In Web Mining and Information Extraction (IE), a **wrapper** is a software procedure / set of extraction rules that automatically transforms unstructured or semi-structured web data (typically encoded in HTML / XML markup) into a structured, relational format (tuples, records, or fields) that can be stored in a database or spreadsheet for further analysis. $$ \text{Wrapper}: \text{HTML Page} \longrightarrow \text{Structured Records (Tuples)} $$ A wrapper identifies **patterns of delimiters, landmarks, and surrounding context** around target data items so that the same extraction logic can be reused across multiple pages from the same source (the same "site" or template). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Wrappers are *site-specific* or *template-specific*. A wrapper built for `amazon.com/books` will NOT work on `flipkart.com/mobiles`. This brittleness is the central reason why *wrapper induction* (automatic learning of wrappers) is a researched area. --- ## 1.2 Wrapper Induction — Formal Definition **Wrapper Induction** is a sub-field of Information Extraction that uses **supervised machine learning** to *automatically* derive wrapper extraction rules from a set of *manually labeled* training pages (instances) belonging to a particular web source. $$ \text{WI} = \mathcal{L}(\text{Training Set} = \{(P_i, L_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}) \rightarrow \text{Wrapper Rules } \mathcal{R} $$ where: - $P_i$ = a sample HTML page from the source - $L_i$ = the user-supplied labeling (which text spans are the target fields) - $\mathcal{L}$ = the learning algorithm - $\mathcal{R}$ = the induced extraction rules > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Wrapper induction *removes* the need for a programmer to hand-code extraction rules (which is expensive and unscalable across thousands of web sources). Instead, the user only labels a few pages, and the algorithm generalizes. --- ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🎯 Imagine you move into a **new city** and want to find the *cheapest grocery store* every week. - **Hand-Coded Wrapper** = You memorize a fixed turn-by-turn route from your house. If the road closes or a new shortcut opens, you are LOST. - **Wrapper Induction** = You learn the *pattern* of decision points: "Take the road with a **big yellow sign**, then turn at the **red building**, the store is the one with the **green shutters**." Now even if a new road appears, your *pattern-based knowledge* still works — until the city changes radically. **Instance-Based Wrapper Learning** is even more flexible. Instead of abstract patterns, you *remember actual previous grocery trips* (instances) and the prices you saw. When a new trip occurs, you look at the *most similar past trip* and use its outcome as a strong hint. This is the **nearest-neighbor philosophy** of instance-based learning applied to extraction. --- ## 1.4 The Three Paradigms of Wrapper Induction | # | Paradigm | What is Learned? | Example Systems | |---|----------|------------------|-----------------| | 1 | **Rule-Based (Grammar) Induction** | A grammar / set of production rules over tokens | WIEN, SoftMealy | | 2 | **Instance-Based Learning** | A library of labeled *instances* (k-gram patterns) used for similarity-based extraction | **STALKER**, instance-based k-NN wrappers | | 3 | **Probabilistic / Statistical** | HMMs, CRFs, Markov models over the page | HMM-based wrappers, BWI | This module focuses specifically on **Instance-Based Wrapper Learning**, with **STALKER** as the canonical KTU reference. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 — Module 3 Anchor:** *Instance-Based Wrapper Learning is the single most examinable sub-topic in this module. Memorize STALKER's algorithm, EC tree, and landmark types.* --- ## 1.5 Why Instance-Based? — Motivation 1. **Robustness to HTML irregularity**: Real-world pages are *messy*. Order of attributes varies, optional fields exist, and nested tables appear. Pure grammar-based wrappers (WIEN) require strict ordering — they break easily. 2. **Handles missing / optional fields gracefully**: Instance-based methods compare each candidate substring to known instances; mismatches are scored. 3. **User-friendly labeling**: User can provide examples in a tabular UI; the system extracts landmarks (anchor strings) near each target. 4. **Disjunctive extraction**: Multiple alternative paths to a field (e.g., "Price: $X" OR "Cost is $X") can be supported via disjunctions of landmarks. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Wrapper Induction Pipeline (Conceptual) Wrapper induction, regardless of paradigm, follows **four canonical steps**: ### Step 1 — Page Collection - Download $N$ sample pages from the target source. - Pages must share the *same template* (or template family). ### Step 2 — User Labeling - User marks the start and end of each target field on a few pages. - For $K$ fields and $M$ labeled pages, we have $M \cdot K$ span annotations. ### Step 3 — Rule / Instance Learning - The induction algorithm $\mathcal{L}$ processes labeled spans. - Output: - **Rule-based**: Disjunctive rules (e.g., `SkipTo(price) , TakeUntil("$")`). - **Instance-based**: An *example repository* $E$ of labeled landmarks and k-gram patterns. ### Step 4 — Extraction on New Pages - Apply learned rules / compare with instances to extract fields from *unseen* pages. - Performance measured by **Precision**, **Recall**, and **F-measure**. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU:** Most 14-mark questions test whether you can describe these 4 steps for a *given system* (STALKER, WIEN, SoftMealy). Always name the step, the input, and the output. --- ## 2.2 The STALKER System — Architecture **STALKER** (Muslea, Knoblock & Minton, 1998–2001) is the **canonical instance-based wrapper induction system** in the KTU syllabus. It treats each extraction task as a sequence of *landmark-anchored string-extraction problems*. ### 2.2.1 Core Components 1. **EC Tree (Extraction Condition Tree)** - A hierarchical structure that represents *where* a target field appears in the page relative to *landmark tokens*. - Each internal node is an **operator**; each leaf is a **landmark** or a **target**. 2. **Landmark** - A short, distinctive string in the HTML that reliably appears *before* or *after* the target field. - Examples: `"Price:"`, `"$"`, `""`, `"Author:"`. - Landmarks can be **Begin** (prefix) or **End** (suffix) landmarks. 3. **k-gram Patterns** - A way to encode a landmark that *tolerates minor variations*. - A k-gram = a contiguous sequence of $k$ characters. - Two landmarks are considered equivalent if their k-gram sets overlap above a threshold $\tau$. 4. **SkipTo / SkipPast Operators** - `SkipTo(landmark)`: Move the cursor forward until the landmark is found. - `SkipPast(landmark)`: Move the cursor forward past the landmark. ### 2.2.2 Extraction Logic For a target field $f$, STALKER learns an EC tree $T_f$. To extract $f$ from a new page: $$ \text{Extract}(f, P) = \text{Eval}(T_f, \text{position } 0 \text{ in } P) $$ The cursor starts at the beginning of the page and the EC tree is *top-down evaluated*, consuming characters (via SkipTo) until the target span is reached. --- ## 2.3 Landmark Types and Quality | Type | Position Relative to Target | Example | |------|----------------------------|---------| | **Begin Landmark** | Immediately *before* the target | `"Title:"` → `Catcher in the Rye` | | **End Landmark** | Immediately *after* the target | `29.99` → `"USD"` | | **Prefix Landmark** | Somewhere in the preceding context | `"

"` | | **Suffix Landmark** | Somewhere in the following context | `"

"` | | **Multi-token Landmark** | A phrase | `"Available in stock:"` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Landmark Selection Criteria:** A good landmark is *short, unique, stable, and close* to the target. A bad landmark is overly generic (e.g., `"the"`) — it produces many false matches. --- ## 2.4 The k-gram Representation To make landmark matching *robust to small HTML variations*, STALKER represents each landmark as a **set of k-grams**. Given a landmark string $L$ of length $n$, its k-gram set is: $$ G_k(L) = \{L[i : i+k] \mid i = 0, 1, \dots, n-k\} $$ where $L[i:i+k]$ denotes the substring of $L$ starting at index $i$ of length $k$. Typical values: $k = 3$ or $k = 5$. **Similarity between two landmarks $L_1, L_2$:** $$ \text{Sim}(L_1, L_2) = \frac{\vert G_k(L_1) \cap G_k(L_2) \vert}{\vert G_k(L_1) \cup G_k(L_2) \vert} $$ A landmark in a new page is considered a *match* if $\text{Sim} \geq \tau$, where $\tau$ is a user-set threshold (typically $\tau \geq 0.7$). > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students write $\frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ instead of $\frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$. The latter is the **Jaccard similarity**, which is the correct formula. Use $\vert$ and $\mid$ carefully in LaTeX. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Notes | |---|---------|---------|-------| | 1 | Wrapper mapping | $W: P \rightarrow R$ | $P$ = page, $R$ = record | | 2 | k-gram set of string $L$ | $G_k(L) = \{L[i:i+k] \mid 0 \leq i \leq \vert L \vert - k\}$ | Size $= \vert L \vert - k + 1$ | | 3 | Jaccard similarity of landmarks | $J(L_1, L_2) = \frac{\vert G_k(L_1) \cap G_k(L_2) \vert}{\vert G_k(L_1) \cup G_k(L_2) \vert}$ | Range $[0,1]$ | | 4 | Match threshold | $\text{Match if } J(L_1, L_2) \geq \tau$ | $\tau \in [0.6, 0.9]$ | | 5 | Precision | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of extracted items, how many are correct? | | 6 | Recall | $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of actual items, how many were extracted? | | 7 | F-measure | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of $P$ and $R$ | | 8 | SkipTo operator effect | Cursor $\leftarrow$ position right after landmark | Consumes HTML | | 9 | SkipPast operator effect | Cursor $\leftarrow$ position right after landmark's tail | Strict skip | | 10 | EC tree size (approx.) | $O(K \cdot M)$ | $K$ fields, $M$ landmarks/field | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Instance-based wrapper learning powers many *practical* tools: - **Web scraping frameworks** (Scrapy, Beautiful Soup with custom rules) often use landmark-anchored extraction. - **Price comparison engines** (Kayak, Google Shopping) use wrapper-induction-style extractors to harvest product data from thousands of e-commerce sites. - **News aggregators** (Google News, Flipboard) use similar techniques to extract headlines and article bodies. - **Job portals** (LinkedIn, Indeed scrapers) use instance-based extraction to capture job titles, salaries, and locations. > [!NOTE] > **Industry Connection:** Modern ML has shifted toward *large language models* (LLMs) for extraction, but instance-based wrappers remain valuable in *low-resource, high-accuracy, deterministic* settings — e.g., financial regulatory scraping where hallucination is unacceptable. --- ## 2.7 Advantages & Limitations of Instance-Based Wrapper Learning | ✅ Advantages | ❌ Limitations | |--------------|---------------| | Handles HTML irregularities & optional fields | Brittle when site redesigns (template drift) | | User-friendly labeling (just mark examples) | Requires representative training instances | | Disjunctive extraction (multiple paths) | Landmark ambiguity in dense tables | | Deterministic & explainable rules | Manual labeling cost scales with $K \cdot M$ | | No grammar authoring required | Poor cross-domain generalization | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: STALKER on a Sample Page Consider a sample HTML page from an online bookstore: ```html

Book: Catcher in the Rye

Author: J.D. Salinger

Price: $19.99 USD

Book: To Kill a Mockingbird

Author: Harper Lee

Price: $14.50 USD

``` **Goal:** Extract the fields (Title, Author, Price) for each book record. ### Step 1 — User Labeling The user marks: | Field | Example 1 Value | Example 2 Value | |-------|----------------|-----------------| | Title | `Catcher in the Rye` | `To Kill a Mockingbird` | | Author | `J.D. Salinger` | `Harper Lee` | | Price | `19.99` | `14.50` | ### Step 2 — Landmark Identification STALKER finds **begin/end landmarks** near each labeled span: | Field | Begin Landmark | End Landmark | |-------|----------------|--------------| | Title | `"Book: "` | `` | | Author | `"Author: "` | `

` | | Price | `"$"` | `" USD"` | ### Step 3 — EC Tree Construction (for `Title`) ``` ROOT ├── SkipTo("Book: ") [Begin landmark] ├── TakeUntil() [End landmark] └── OUTPUT → Title field ``` ### Step 4 — Extraction on New Page Apply the EC tree to the third book record (not labeled): ```html

Book: 1984

``` - Cursor starts at the start of the page. - `SkipTo("Book: ")` advances cursor to right after `"Book: "`. - `TakeUntil()` extracts the text from cursor up to but not including `` → `"1984"`. - Output: `Title = "1984"`. ✅ --- ## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: k-gram Similarity Calculation Suppose we have two landmarks that the system must match: - $L_1 = $ `"Price:"` (length 6) - $L_2 = $ `"price:"` (length 6) — *lowercase variant* of $L_1$ - $k = 3$ ### Step-by-step k-gram extraction: **For $L_1 =$ `"Price:"`** $$ G_3(L_1) = \{L_1[0:3], L_1[1:4], L_1[2:5], L_1[3:6]\} $$ $$ G_3(L_1) = \{\text{"Pri"}, \text{"ric"}, \text{"ice"}, \text{"ce:"}\} $$ **For $L_2 =$ `"price:"`** $$ G_3(L_2) = \{L_2[0:3], L_2[1:4], L_2[2:5], L_2[3:6]\} $$ $$ G_3(L_2) = \{\text{"pri"}, \text{"ric"}, \text{"ice"}, \text{"ce:"}\} $$ ### Intersection and Union: $$ G_3(L_1) \cap G_3(L_2) = \{\text{"ric"}, \text{"ice"}, \text{"ce:"}\} $$ $$ \vert G_3(L_1) \cap G_3(L_2) \vert = 3 $$ $$ G_3(L_1) \cup G_3(L_2) = \{\text{"Pri"}, \text{"pri"}, \text{"ric"}, \text{"ice"}, \text{"ce:"}\} $$ $$ \vert G_3(L_1) \cup G_3(L_2) \vert = 5 $$ ### Jaccard Similarity: $$ J(L_1, L_2) = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6 $$ With $\tau = 0.5$, the system *accepts* this match. With $\tau = 0.7$, it *rejects*. This is exactly how STALKER tolerates case changes, minor typos, and small HTML formatting differences. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operator Behaviors in EC Trees | Operator | Pre-condition | Action | Post-condition | Failure Mode | |----------|--------------|--------|----------------|--------------| | `SkipTo(L)` | Cursor before landmark $L$ in $P$ | Advance cursor to right after $L$ | Cursor positioned after $L$ | Landmark not found → exception | | `SkipPast(L)` | Cursor before landmark $L$ in $P$ | Advance cursor past $L$ *strictly* | Cursor at first non-whitespace char after $L$ | Skips too much | | `TakeUntil(L)` | Cursor at some position | Read all chars until $L$ is reached | Output substring; cursor at $L$ | $L$ missing → reads entire rest of page | | `TakeAll()` | Cursor at some position | Read all remaining text | Output everything | Over-extraction | | `Begin()` | — | Set output start marker | — | — | | `End()` | — | Set output end marker | — | — | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** The EC tree is *deterministic* and *executable*. It is essentially a small domain-specific program. This is why STALKER's output is *human-readable* and *debuggable* — unlike neural extractors. --- ## 3.4 Generalization to Disjunctive Rules In real pages, a field may appear via **multiple alternative paths**: ``` Path 1: "Author: " → Author → "
" Path 2: "Written by " → Author → "" ``` STALKER represents this as a **disjunction in the EC tree**: ``` ROOT ├── OR │ ├── Branch 1: SkipTo("Author: ") → TakeUntil("
") │ └── Branch 2: SkipTo("Written by ") → TakeUntil("") └── OUTPUT → Author field ``` At extraction time, STALKER *tries* Branch 1 first; if its landmark is missing, it falls back to Branch 2. This is one of the **key advantages of instance-based learning** over early grammar-based wrappers (like WIEN), which required strict ordering. --- ## 3.5 Learning Algorithm Pseudocode (STALKER, conceptual) ``` INPUT: Set of labeled pages L = {(P1, S1), ..., (Pm, Sm)} Target field f OUTPUT: EC tree Tf ALGORITHM LearnEC(L, f): 1. For each labeled page Pi: Locate the span [start_i, end_i] of field f in Pi Identify candidate begin landmarks LB_i near start_i Identify candidate end landmarks LE_i near end_i 3. Cluster LB_i across pages → disjunctive set of begin landmarks 4. Cluster LE_i across pages → disjunctive set of end landmarks 5. Build EC tree Tf as OR over (SkipTo(LB) → TakeUntil(LE)) 6. Return Tf ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Error:** Do not confuse STALKER's clustering step with k-means. STALKER uses **landmark equivalence classes** based on k-gram similarity — not Euclidean clustering. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Wrapper Induction — High-Level Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Web Source Template] --> B[Page Collector] B --> C[Sampling N Pages] C --> D[User Labels Target Fields] D --> E[Induction Algorithm L] E --> F[Wrapper Rules R / EC Tree T] F --> G{New Unseen Page} G --> H[Apply Wrapper] H --> I[Structured Records in DB] H --> J[Evaluate Precision Recall F1] J --> K{Wrapper Drift Detected} K -->|Yes| D K -->|No| G ``` --- ## 4.2 STALKER Internal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LEARN["Learning Phase"] L1[Labeled HTML Pages] --> L2[Tokenize and Clean] L2 --> L3[Detect Field Spans] L3 --> L4[Extract Candidate Landmarks] L4 --> L5[Cluster Landmarks via k-gram Similarity] L5 --> L6[Build EC Tree Tf per Field] end subgraph EXTRACT["Extraction Phase"] E1[New HTML Page P] --> E2[Initialize Cursor at pos 0] E2 --> E3[Top-Down Evaluate EC Tree Tf] E3 --> E4{SkipTo Landmark Found} E4 -->|Yes| E5[Advance Cursor] E4 -->|No| E6[Try Disjunctive Branch] E5 --> E7[TakeUntil End Landmark] E6 --> E4 E7 --> E8[Output Extracted Field] end L6 --> E3 ``` --- ## 4.3 EC Tree Structure for a Multi-Field Record ```mermaid flowchart TB R[ROOT: Record Extractor] R --> F1[Field 1: Title] R --> F2[Field 2: Author] R --> F3[Field 3: Price] F1 --> F1A[OR] F1A --> F1A1[Branch A: SkipTo Book colon → TakeUntil /h1] F1A --> F1A2[Branch B: SkipTo Title colon → TakeUntil /p] F2 --> F2A[SkipTo Author colon → TakeUntil /p] F3 --> F3A[OR] F3A --> F3A1[Branch A: SkipTo dollar → TakeUntil space] F3A --> F3A2[Branch B: SkipTo Cost colon → TakeUntil USD] ``` --- ## 4.4 Landmark Matching with k-gram Similarity — Block Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[New Page Substring S1] --> X1[Compute k-gram Set Gk S1] P2[Known Landmark L2] --> X2[Compute k-gram Set Gk L2] X1 --> J[Compute Jaccard J = intersect over union] X2 --> J J --> D{J greater than or equal to threshold tau} D -->|Yes| M1[Match Accepted] D -->|No| M2[Match Rejected] M1 --> OUT1[Use as Begin or End Landmark] M2 --> OUT2[Try Next Disjunctive Branch] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Each Mermaid block is mapped to a specific STALKER sub-system. In the KTU exam, drawing a *clean* block diagram of STALKER's architecture (even a simplified one) earns full 7 marks in a typical 14-mark question. --- ## 4.5 Comparison: Rule-Based vs Instance-Based Wrapper Induction ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph RB["Rule Based Induction - WIEN SoftMealy"] RB1[Labeled Spans] --> RB2[Induce Context Free Grammar / Delimiter Rules] RB2 --> RB3[Strict Order Required] end subgraph IB["Instance Based Induction - STALKER"] IB1[Labeled Spans] --> IB2[Extract Landmarks per Instance] IB2 --> IB3[Store Instance Library] IB3 --> IB4[At Test Time: Nearest Landmark via k-gram Similarity] IB4 --> IB5[Robust to Reorderings and Optional Fields] end RB3 -. Contrasts With .-> IB5 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define a wrapper. Why is wrapper induction preferred over hand-coded wrappers for large-scale web data extraction?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **wrapper** is a software procedure that extracts structured data (records / fields) from semi-structured web pages (HTML / XML) by exploiting patterns of delimiters and surrounding context in the markup. Wrapper **induction** is preferred because: 1. **Scalability** — Thousands of web sources exist; hand-coding a wrapper per source is infeasible. Induction automatically learns extraction rules from a small set of user-labeled examples. 2. **Maintainability** — When the site template changes (template drift), re-labeling a few pages and re-inducing is far cheaper than rewriting code. 3. **Domain independence** — A general induction algorithm (e.g., STALKER) works across many domains without per-site engineering. *(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three justification points.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What is a landmark in the STALKER system? List the two main categories of landmarks with one example each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **landmark** is a short, distinctive string in the HTML page that reliably appears *near* (before or after) a target data field. It serves as an *anchor* for extraction. The two main categories are: 1. **Begin Landmark** — appears immediately *before* the target. *Example:* `"Price:"` before the price value. 2. **End Landmark** — appears immediately *after* the target. *Example:* `"USD"` after the price value. *(Award 1.5 marks for landmark definition, 1.5 marks for categories + examples.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Instance-Based Wrapper Learning Deep Dive** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — July 2024]** *With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of the STALKER instance-based wrapper induction system. Describe its key components: EC tree, landmarks, and k-gram patterns.* **Model Solution (7 marks):** **1. STALKER Architecture (3 marks):** STALKER has two phases — **Learning** and **Extraction**. - **Learning Phase:** The system takes a set of user-labeled HTML pages as input. It tokenizes each page, locates the labeled field spans, and for each span identifies *candidate landmarks* (short strings in the surrounding HTML). It then *clusters* these landmarks across pages using **k-gram similarity**, merging equivalent variants (e.g., `"Price:"` and `"price:"`). For each field, it builds an **Extraction Condition (EC) tree** that encodes the disjunctive set of (begin-landmark, end-landmark) pairs. - **Extraction Phase:** A new page is processed by *top-down evaluation* of the EC tree. A cursor starts at position 0 in the page. Operators `SkipTo(L)` advance the cursor to landmark $L$, and `TakeUntil(L)` extracts the substring up to $L$. Disjunctive branches are tried until one succeeds. **2. EC Tree (2 marks):** The EC tree is a hierarchical structure: - **Internal nodes** = operators (`SkipTo`, `TakeUntil`, `OR`, `AND`). - **Leaves** = landmarks (strings) or target-field references. - It encodes *where* a target appears relative to landmarks and supports disjunction for alternative paths. **3. k-gram Patterns (2 marks):** To make landmark matching robust to small HTML variations, STALKER represents each landmark as a **set of k-grams** (contiguous substrings of length $k$). Two landmarks are considered equivalent if their **Jaccard similarity** exceeds a threshold $\tau$: $$ J(L_1, L_2) = \frac{\vert G_k(L_1) \cap G_k(L_2) \vert}{\vert G_k(L_1) \cup G_k(L_2) \vert} \geq \tau $$ Typical values: $k = 3$ or $5$, $\tau \approx 0.7$. This tolerates case changes, extra spaces, and minor HTML formatting differences. *Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2 for the architecture sketch (essential for full marks).* --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — July 2024]** *Consider the following two landmarks: $L_1 = $ `"Author:"` and $L_2 = $ `"author :"`. Compute the Jaccard similarity using k-gram representation with $k = 3$. Comment on whether STALKER would match them if $\tau = 0.5$.* **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — List the k-grams for $L_1$ = "Author:" (length 6, so 4 k-grams):** $$ G_3(L_1) = \{ \text{"Aut"}, \text{"uth"}, \text{"tho"}, \text{"hor"}, \text{"or:"}\} $$ Wait — length 6, $k = 3$, so number of k-grams is $6 - 3 + 1 = 4$. Corrected: $$ G_3(L_1) = \{L_1[0:3], L_1[1:4], L_1[2:5], L_1[3:6]\} $$ $$ G_3(L_1) = \{\text{"Aut"}, \text{"uth"}, \text{"tho"}, \text{"or:"}\} $$ **Step 2 — List the k-grams for $L_2$ = "author :" (length 8, so 6 k-grams):** $$ G_3(L_2) = \{L_2[0:3], L_2[1:4], L_2[2:5], L_2[3:6], L_2[4:7], L_2[5:8]\} $$ $$ G_3(L_2) = \{\text{"aut"}, \text{"uth"}, \text{"tho"}, \text{"hor"}, \text{"or "}, \text{"r :"}\} $$ **Step 3 — Intersection:** $$ G_3(L_1) \cap G_3(L_2) = \{\text{"uth"}, \text{"tho"}\} $$ $$ \vert G_3(L_1) \cap G_3(L_2) \vert = 2 $$ **Step 4 — Union:** $$ G_3(L_1) \cup G_3(L_2) = \{\text{"Aut"}, \text{"aut"}, \text{"uth"}, \text{"tho"}, \text{"hor"}, \text{"or:"}, \text{"or "}, \text{"r :"}\} $$ $$ \vert G_3(L_1) \cup G_3(L_2) \vert = 8 $$ **Step 5 — Jaccard Similarity:** $$ J(L_1, L_2) = \frac{\vert G_3(L_1) \cap G_3(L_2) \vert}{\vert G_3(L_1) \cup G_3(L_2) \vert} = \frac{2}{8} = 0.25 $$ **Step 6 — Decision:** Since $J(L_1, L_2) = 0.25 < \tau = 0.5$, **STALKER would REJECT this match**. **Comment (2 marks):** The low similarity (0.25) is caused by the extra space in $L_2$ (`"author :"`) which shifts many k-grams (`"hor"`, `"or "`, `"r :"`). STALKER tolerates *minor* character variations (case, single-char shifts) but a space insertion significantly reduces overlap. To improve matching, one could either lower $k$ to 2 (more matches but more false positives) or use a normalized landmark (strip whitespace) before computing k-grams. *(Award 1 mark per correct k-gram set, 1 mark for intersection, 1 mark for union, 1 mark for Jaccard calculation, 1 mark for the decision, 2 marks for the comment.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Wrapper Induction Preliminaries** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Explain the four major steps in the wrapper induction pipeline. How does instance-based wrapper learning differ from rule-based wrapper learning in handling optional and reordered fields?* **Model Solution (7 marks):** **The four steps of the wrapper induction pipeline (4 marks):** 1. **Page Collection** — A set of $N$ representative HTML pages is downloaded from the target web source. These pages must share the same template family. 2. **User Labeling** — The user interactively marks the start and end of each target field on a small subset of pages. This produces labeled training instances. 3. **Wrapper Learning** — The induction algorithm processes the labeled instances and outputs either extraction rules (rule-based) or an instance library (instance-based). 4. **Extraction on New Pages** — The induced wrapper is applied to unseen pages, producing structured records. Performance is measured by precision, recall, and F-measure. **Comparison — Instance-Based vs Rule-Based (3 marks):** | Aspect | Rule-Based (e.g., WIEN) | Instance-Based (e.g., STALKER) | |--------|------------------------|-------------------------------| | Optional fields | Cannot handle; strict grammar fails | Handled via disjunctive EC tree branches | | Reordered fields | Requires grammar rewrite | Tolerated — each field is anchored independently | | User effort | Hard rule authoring | Simple example labeling | | Robustness | Brittle to template drift | Robust to small HTML variations | *Instance-based systems treat each field's extraction as an independent landmark-anchored problem, which is why they naturally support **disjunctions** and **optional fields** without rewriting the entire rule set.* --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Discuss the main challenges in wrapper induction. Explain with examples why wrapper maintenance is harder than wrapper induction.* **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Challenges in Wrapper Induction (3.5 marks):** 1. **Template Variability** — Within a single web source, different categories (e.g., books vs electronics) may use different page templates. A wrapper learned for one template fails on the other. 2. **Labeling Cost** — Manual labeling is labor-intensive; reducing it requires active learning or semi-supervised approaches. 3. **Nested and Hierarchical Data** — Many pages have nested structures (categories → subcategories → products). Flat wrappers cannot model depth. 4. **Noise and Inconsistency** — Real pages contain typos, missing fields, varying encodings, and broken HTML. **Why Wrapper Maintenance is Harder (3.5 marks):** Maintenance is the recurring problem of *adapting an existing wrapper* to a *changed* site. It is harder than initial induction because: - **Scale of change is unknown** — A minor HTML tweak (e.g., `` → ``) can break thousands of landmark anchors, even though the human-readable page looks identical. - **Cascading failures** — If landmark $L$ is used in 50 EC trees, its failure breaks 50 extractions simultaneously. - **Distribution drift** — Pages from a year ago may differ structurally from today's pages, so the wrapper's training set no longer represents current pages. - **No automatic oracle** — Unlike induction (where the user provides labels), maintenance requires continuous *monitoring* of extraction quality — which often goes undetected until downstream analytics break. *Example:* A price-comparison engine scrapes 200 e-commerce sites. When one site redesigns its product page, the wrapper for that site silently extracts empty price fields for weeks until the data team notices revenue drops in the dashboard. This is the *silent failure* problem of wrapper maintenance. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Confusing STALKER with WIEN** — STALKER is *instance-based*; WIEN is *grammar/rule-based*. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks. > 2. **Wrong Jaccard formula** — Always use $\frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$, not $\frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$. (The latter is wrong; the denominator must be the *union size*.) > 3. **k-gram count error** — A string of length $n$ has exactly $n - k + 1$ k-grams, not $n$. Forgetting the $-1$ off-by-one loses 1 mark. > 4. **Forgetting disjunction in EC trees** — The hallmark of instance-based learning is that EC trees support *disjunctive branches*. Omitting this loses 1 mark. > 5. **Confusing SkipTo with SkipPast** — `SkipTo` lands *at* the landmark; `SkipPast` lands *after* it. Examiners test this distinction. > 6. **Not drawing a diagram in 14-mark questions** — A clean Mermaid-style block diagram is worth ~2 marks. Always include one for architecture questions. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 3 — Wrapper Induction: Final Revision Pack** - ✅ **Wrapper** = site-specific extractor that converts HTML pages into structured records. - ✅ **Wrapper Induction** = *automatic* learning of wrappers from user-labeled training pages (supervised). - ✅ **Two main paradigms** = *Rule-Based* (WIEN, SoftMealy — strict grammars) vs *Instance-Based* (STALKER — landmark-anchored). - ✅ **STALKER** is the *canonical* instance-based system in the KTU syllabus. - ✅ **Landmarks** = short, distinctive strings near target fields. Two types: **Begin** (prefix) and **End** (suffix). - ✅ **EC Tree** = hierarchical operator structure. Nodes = operators; leaves = landmarks / fields. Supports **disjunction** (OR) for alternative paths. - ✅ **k-gram** of a string $L$ of length $n$ = set of $\vert L \vert - k + 1$ contiguous substrings of length $k$. - ✅ **Jaccard similarity** = $\frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$. Threshold $\tau \in [0.6, 0.9]$ typically. - ✅ **Operators** — `SkipTo(L)`, `SkipPast(L)`, `TakeUntil(L)`, `TakeAll()`, `Begin()`, `End()`, `OR`, `AND`. - ✅ **Pipeline** = (1) Page Collection → (2) User Labeling → (3) Induction → (4) Extraction & Evaluation. - ✅ **Evaluation metrics** = **Precision**, **Recall**, **F-measure** = $\frac{2PR}{P+R}$. - ✅ **Maintenance > Induction difficulty** due to *silent failures*, *cascading breaks*, and *distribution drift*. - ✅ **Real-world users** = price comparison engines, news aggregators, job portals, e-commerce scrapers. - ✅ **Modern alternative** = LLMs (zero-shot extraction) — but instance-based wrappers remain valuable for *deterministic, low-resource, high-accuracy* extraction. - ✅ **Always draw a diagram** for 14-marker questions on STALKER architecture — 2 marks are reserved for it. > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "WISe K-LOOP"** > **W**rapper definition → **I**nduction (supervised) → **S**TALKER (instance) → **e**C tree → **K**-gram similarity → **L**andmarks (begin/end) → **O**perators (SkipTo, TakeUntil) → **O**R for disjunction → **P**ipeline (4 steps).

topics.title: Automatic Wrapper Generation: Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEADT636 - INTRODUCTION TO WEB MINING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Automatic Wrapper Generation: Problems # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Automatic Wrapper Generation (AWG)** is the process of inductively learning, without (or with minimal) human intervention, the extraction rules and delimiters required to transform semi-structured web documents (typically HTML pages) into structured relational data. In the context of KTU 2024 syllabus, the *Problems* dimension refers to the set of algorithmic, linguistic, and operational challenges that must be solved before a fully autonomous wrapper-induction system can be deployed across heterogeneous web sources. A **wrapper**, in this sense, is formally defined as a tuple: $$W = \langle P, S, E, M \rangle$$ where: - $P$ = the set of input pages (training examples) - $S$ = a schema (set of attributes to be extracted) - $E$ = the extraction logic (delimiter rules, regular expressions, or tree-edit patterns) - $M$ = the matching/verification module that applies $E$ to unseen pages > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The "Problems" sub-topic explicitly targets the *unsolved or hard-to-solve bottlenecks* in wrapper induction — *scaling, maintenance, labeling cost, and robustness*. You are expected to enumerate **at least 4–5 problems** in the ESE and explain any **two** with examples. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **postman** in a city where every house has a different style of mailbox. House A has a red slot, House B a brass knob, House C a numeric keypad. Your job is to deliver the *same letter* (a piece of structured data) into the *correct slot* of every mailbox. - **Manual wrappers** = a postman who memorizes every mailbox. - **Automatic wrappers** = a robot postman that *learns* the mailbox patterns from a few examples and figures out the rest. The *Problems* we discuss are exactly the headaches this robot postman faces: - What if a house **renovates** its mailbox overnight? (Wrapper Maintenance) - What if there are **10,000 houses** in 50 countries? (Scaling) - What if the robot is given **only 1 example** to learn from? (Few-shot Learning) - What if the same mailbox also contains junk mail and pizza flyers? (Noise Filtering) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Unlike traditional data mining, web data is **semi-structured** (HTML, not pure tables), **heterogeneous** (no common schema across sites), and **dynamic** (pages change without notice). These three properties are the *root cause* of almost every problem listed below. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (KTU-Standard Vocabulary) | Term | Meaning | Standard Value / Notation | |---|---|---| | **Precision ($P$)** | Fraction of extracted items that are correct | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | | **Recall ($R$)** | Fraction of correct items that are extracted | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | | **F-Measure ($F_1$)** | Harmonic mean of $P$ and $R$ | $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | | **Wrapper Induction** | Supervised learning of extraction rules | Requires labeled examples | | **DOM Tree** | Document Object Model — tree of HTML nodes | Depth $d$, branching factor $b$ | | **LCS** | Longest Common Subsequence used for alignment | String-similarity kernel | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Wrapper-Generation Pipeline (Background) Before we dissect the *problems*, we must first understand the **canonical pipeline** against which these problems arise: 1. **Input Acquisition** — Fetch training pages (HTML). 2. **Pre-processing** — Tokenization, HTML cleaning, DOM construction. 3. **Annotation / Labeling** — Human (or weak-supervision) marks target fields. 4. **Induction** — Algorithm learns delimiters, landmarks, or XPath patterns. 5. **Verification** — Apply to held-out pages, compute $P, R, F_1$. 6. **Deployment & Maintenance** — Periodic re-induction when site changes. > Every "Problem" in Section 2.2 maps to a *breakdown* at one of these six stages. ## 2.2 The Core Problems (Enumerated & Explained) ### Problem 1 — The **Scaling Problem** (Most cited in KTU answers) **Why it exists:** Classical wrapper-induction systems (e.g., *WIEN*, *SoftMealy*, *STALKER*) require **labeled training pages per site**. For $N$ sites, the labeling cost is $O(N \cdot k)$ where $k$ is the number of pages labeled per site. **The bottleneck:** Wrapper induction is a *per-site* task. There is no global schema — Amazon's product page looks nothing like Flipkart's. **Why it's hard:** - Cross-site schema mapping is non-trivial. - Labeling 5–10 pages per site × 10,000 sites = **50,000–100,000 manual annotations**. **KTU-acceptable counter-argument / partial solution:** Use *unsupervised* or *semi-supervised* approaches (e.g., Roadrunner, ExAlg) that exploit structural regularities without labels. ### Problem 2 — The **Wrapper Maintenance / Brittleness Problem** **Definition:** A wrapper is said to *break* when the underlying HTML structure changes and the extraction rules no longer fire correctly. **Why it exists:** Websites undergo **redesigns, A/B testing, ad-injection, and localization**. Studies show that a typical wrapper for a deep-web source must be re-induced every **3–6 months**. **Quantification:** Let $S_t$ be the schema extracted at time $t$. The wrapper is *intact* iff: $$\text{SchemaDrift}(S_t, S_{t+\Delta t}) \leq \epsilon$$ where $\epsilon$ is a tolerance threshold (e.g., $\epsilon = 0.05$ for 5% node deviation). **Why it's hard:** Detecting *which* change broke *which* rule requires versioned DOM diffing — an $O(d \cdot b)$ operation in the worst case (depth × branching factor of the DOM tree). ### Problem 3 — The **Limited / Few-Shot Labeling Problem** **Why it exists:** Users want wrappers from **1–3 examples** (few-shot), but most induction algorithms need **5–10** for statistical reliability. **The core trade-off:** With $k$ labeled pages and $n$ attributes: $$\text{Generalization Error} \propto \sqrt{\frac{\log n}{k}}$$ This is the standard PAC-learning bound. As $k \to 1$, error grows logarithmically with $n$. ### Problem 4 — The **Robustness / Noise Problem** **Sources of noise:** - HTML ads, footers, navigation bars. - Optional fields (e.g., discount price absent for some products). - Multi-lingual tokens, Unicode, currency symbols. - JavaScript-injected dynamic content. **Formal statement:** Given a *clean* template $T$ and a *noisy* observation $O$: $$O = T \oplus N$$ where $\oplus$ denotes the noise operator (insertion, deletion, substitution). The wrapper must learn $T$ from $\{(O_i)\}_{i=1}^{k}$ — an **ill-posed inverse problem**. ### Problem 5 — The **Generalization Across Sites (Heterogeneity) Problem** **Why it's hard:** Each e-commerce site invents its own attribute vocabulary: - Site A: `` - Site B: `
` - Site C: `` No single XPath or regex works across all three. This is the **inductive-bias problem** — the learner needs prior knowledge of *what HTML features matter* (tag names, class tokens, sibling order, numeric format). ### Problem 6 — The **Multi-Record / Pagination Problem** When a single page lists many products (a *list page*), the wrapper must: - **Segment** the page into records. - **Align** attributes across records. - **Handle missing values** (some products lack a "review count"). This is the *complex record extraction* problem — substantially harder than single-record extraction. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | How AWG is Used | |---|---| | **E-commerce price comparison** | Crawl Amazon, eBay, Walmart → build unified catalog | | **Financial intelligence** | Extract quarterly results from listed-company IR pages | | **Biomedical NLP** | Pull drug–disease pairs from PubMed HTML tables | | **News aggregation** | Headline + summary + author + date extraction | | **Job portals** | Naukri, Indeed, LinkedIn → skill extraction pipelines | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Bound | Unit / Domain | |---|---|---|---| | $P$ | Precision | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | | $R$ | Recall | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | | $F_1$ | F-measure | $\frac{2 P R}{P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | | $\epsilon$ | Schema-drift tolerance | $\text{Drift}(S_t, S_{t'}) \leq \epsilon$ | $0 < \epsilon \ll 1$ | | $k$ | Training pages per site | Integer $\geq 1$ | pages | | $n$ | Number of attributes | Integer | fields | | $\eta$ | PAC bound | $\sqrt{\frac{\log n}{k}}$ | dimensionless | | $d \cdot b$ | DOM complexity | depth × branching factor | nodes | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Generalization Error of a Wrapper Inducer We derive the expected error of a wrapper learned from $k$ labeled pages with $n$ candidate attributes, following the standard PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) framework. **Step 1 — Define the hypothesis space.** Let $\mathcal{H}$ be the space of all possible extraction rules (XPath expressions, regex, or delimiter sets). The size of this space is bounded by: $$|\mathcal{H}| \leq n \cdot 2^n$$ This is because each of the $n$ attributes can be either "present" or "absent" in any hypothesis, and the rule itself has $n$ structural variants. **Step 2 — Apply the union bound.** By the PAC theorem, the probability that any single hypothesis $h \in \mathcal{H}$ has true error greater than $\epsilon$ is bounded by $e^{-k\epsilon^2}$ (Hoeffding's inequality for indicator losses). Taking the union over all hypotheses: $$P\left[\exists h : \text{err}(h) > \epsilon\right] \leq |\mathcal{H}| \cdot e^{-k\epsilon^2}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $k$.** Setting the RHS $\leq \delta$ (confidence) and solving for $k$: $$e^{-k\epsilon^2} \leq \frac{\delta}{|\mathcal{H}|}$$ Taking the natural log of both sides: $$-k\epsilon^2 \leq \ln \delta - \ln |\mathcal{H}|$$ **Step 4 — Final sample-complexity bound.** $$k \geq \frac{1}{\epsilon^2} \left[ \ln |\mathcal{H}| + \ln \frac{1}{\delta} \right]$$ Substituting the upper bound for $|\mathcal{H}|$: $$k \geq \frac{1}{\epsilon^2} \left[ n \ln 2 + \ln \frac{1}{\delta} \right]$$ **Step 5 — Interpret for the KTU student.** This bound says: *to achieve error at most $\epsilon$ with confidence $1-\delta$ over $n$ candidate attributes, you need at least this many labeled training pages*.
