All formulas are mathematically verifiable and standard across the KTU 2024 Web Mining syllabus. | **Metric / Concept** | **Formula / Definition** | **Use Case** | **Range** | |---|---|---|---| | **Jaccard Similarity** | $J(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Token overlap between attribute names | $[0, 1]$ | | **Cosine Similarity (TF-IDF)** | $\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\Vert\vec{A}\Vert \cdot \Vert\vec{B}\Vert}$ | Comparing attribute name vectors | $[-1, 1]$ | | **Edit Distance (Levenshtein)** | $Lev(s_1, s_2) = \min$ insertions, deletions, substitutions to convert $s_1$ to $s_2$ | Typo-tolerant string matching | $[0, \infty)$ | | **Normalized Edit Distance** | $NED = \frac{Lev(s_1, s_2)}{\max(\vert s_1 \vert, \vert s_2 \vert)}$ | Scale-independent string comparison | $[0, 1]$ | | **Combined Similarity** | $Sim_{combined} = w_1 \cdot Sim_{name} + w_2 \cdot Sim_{instance} + w_3 \cdot Sim_{type}$ | Weighted hybrid matchers | $[0, 1]$ | | **F-Measure (Match Evaluation)** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Evaluates matcher quality vs. ground truth | $[0, 1]$ | | **List Node Identification** | $Score(node) = \frac{\text{freq of identical siblings}}{\text{total children}}$ | Finding the repeating DOM element | $[0, 1]$ | | **Pagination Stop Condition** | $Stop \iff (NextLink = \varnothing) \lor (PageNum \geq N_{max}) \lor (Hash(Page_i) = Hash(Page_{i-1}))$ | Multi-page termination logic | Boolean | **Symbols Used:** - $A, B$ = sets of tokens or vectors. - $s_1, s_2$ = strings. - $P$ = Precision, $R$ = Recall. - $w_1, w_2, w_3$ = weights summing to $1$. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example: Single List Page Extraction (Analytical Trace) **Scenario:** We have the following simplified HTML from a single list page: ```html

Phone A

$500

Phone B

$700

Phone C

$300
``` **Step 1: Identify the List Node.** The `
` node has 3 children, all with class `item` and identical inner structure. $$ \text{Score}(div\#results) = \frac{3 \text{ identical siblings}}{3 \text{ total children}} = 1.0 $$ **Step 2: Define the Extraction Template.** $$ \text{Template} = \{ \text{title}: \text{//h2/text()},\ \text{price}: \text{//span[@class='price']/text()} \} $$ **Step 3: Iterate.** For each `
`, evaluate the template. **Step 4: Output.** $$ \text{Output} = \{(Phone A, \$500), (Phone B, \$700), (Phone C, \$300)\} $$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Example: Multi-Page Extraction (Pagination Trace) **Scenario:** A site paginates as `https://shop.com/products?page=N`. **Algorithm Trace:** 1. Initialize queue $Q = [URL_1]$ where $URL_1 = \text{https://shop.com/products?page=1}$. 2. Initialize visited set $V = \varnothing$ and output set $O = \varnothing$. 3. Set $N_{max} = 10$. **Iteration $i = 1$ (Page 1):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Dequeue: } & URL_1 \\ \text{Fetch: } & \text{HTML}_1 \rightarrow \text{List}_1 = \{p_1, p_2, ..., p_{20}\} \\ \text{Extract: } & O \leftarrow O \cup \text{List}_1 \\ \text{Discover next link: } & URL_2 = \text{https://shop.com/products?page=2} \\ \text{Enqueue: } & Q = [URL_2] \\ \text{Mark visited: } & V = \{URL_1\} \end{aligned} $$ **Iteration $i = 2$ (Page 2):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Dequeue: } & URL_2 \\ \text{Fetch: } & \text{HTML}_2 \rightarrow \text{List}_2 = \{p_{21}, ..., p_{40}\} \\ \text{Extract: } & O \leftarrow O \cup \text{List}_2 \\ \text{Enqueue: } & Q = [URL_3] \\ \text{Mark visited: } & V = \{URL_1, URL_2\} \end{aligned} $$ **Termination Check (at iteration $i$):** $$ \text{STOP} \iff \big( (Q = \varnothing) \lor (N_i \geq N_{max}) \lor (Hash(HTML_i) = Hash(HTML_{i-1})) \big) $$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Example: Schema Matching Similarity Computation **Problem:** Match attribute `cost` (Source A) to candidates in Source B: `{price, weight, color}`. **Step 1: Compute Jaccard Similarity (token-based).** Tokenize: `cost` → $\{cost\}$, `price` → $\{price\}$, `weight` → $\{weight\}$, `color` → $\{color\}$. $$ J(\text{cost}, \text{price}) = \frac{\vert \{cost\} \cap \{price\} \vert}{\vert \{cost\} \cup \{price\} \vert} = \frac{0}{2} = 0.0 $$ **Step 2: Compute Normalized Edit Distance.** $$ Lev(\text{cost}, \text{price}) = 4 \quad (\text{insert } p, r, i, e \text{ after } c) $$ $$ NED(\text{cost}, \text{price}) = \frac{4}{\max(4, 5)} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8 $$ **Step 3: Compute Cosine Similarity (character n-grams, $n=2$).** Bigrams: `cost` → $\{co, os, st\}$, `price` → $\{pr, ri, ic, ce\}$. $$ \vec{A} = (1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0), \quad \vec{B} = (0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 1) $$ $$ \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = 0, \quad \Vert\vec{A}\Vert = \sqrt{3}, \quad \Vert\vec{B}\Vert = 2 $$ $$ \cos(\theta) = \frac{0}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 2} = 0.0 $$ **Step 4: Use Instance-level Matcher (values).** Suppose `cost` has values $\{500, 700, 300\}$ and `price` has values $\{500, 700, 300\}$. $$ \text{Value Overlap} = \frac{\vert \{500, 700, 300\} \cap \{500, 700, 300\} \vert}{\vert \{500, 700, 300\} \vert} = 1.0 $$ **Step 5: Combined Weighted Score (assume $w_{name} = 0.4$, $w_{instance} = 0.6$).** $$ Sim_{combined} = (0.4 \times 0.0) + (0.6 \times 1.0) = 0.6 $$ This score of **0.6** (above a typical threshold of 0.5) correctly identifies `cost` → `price` as a match, **despite** poor name similarity. This demonstrates the power of hybrid schema matching. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Single List Page Extraction Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Start: Target URL] --> nodeB[Fetch Single HTML Document] nodeB --> nodeC[Parse DOM Tree] nodeC --> nodeD[Identify Repeating List Node] nodeD --> nodeE{Valid List Node Found?} nodeE -- No --> nodeF[Fail: Report No Pattern] nodeE -- Yes --> nodeG[Define XPath or CSS Template] nodeG --> nodeH[Iterate Over All Children] nodeH --> nodeI[Apply Template to Each Record] nodeI --> nodeJ[Clean and Normalize Data] nodeJ --> nodeK[Store in Output Dataset] nodeK --> nodeL[End: Extraction Complete] ``` --- ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Multi-Page Extraction State Machine ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Start: Seed URL] --> nodeB[Initialize Queue and Visited Set] nodeB --> nodeC[Dequeue Next URL] nodeC --> nodeD[Fetch HTML Page] nodeD --> nodeE[Apply List Extraction Template] nodeE --> nodeF[Merge Records to Global Output] nodeF --> nodeG{Is Next Page Link Present?} nodeG -- No --> nodeH[End: All Pages Processed] nodeG -- Yes --> nodeI{Has Max Page Limit Been Reached?} nodeI -- Yes --> nodeH nodeI -- No --> nodeJ{Is Next Page Already Visited?} nodeJ -- Yes --> nodeH nodeJ -- No --> nodeK[Enqueue Next Page URL] nodeK --> nodeL[Mark Current URL as Visited] nodeL --> nodeC ``` --- ### 4.3 Block Diagram: Schema Matching Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Source Schema S1] --> nodeC[Schema Matcher Engine] nodeB[Source Schema S2] --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD[Linguistic Matcher] nodeC --> nodeE[Instance Matcher] nodeC --> nodeF[Structural Matcher] nodeC --> nodeG[Constraint Matcher] nodeD --> nodeH[Similarity Score Combiner] nodeE --> nodeH nodeF --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI[Final Correspondence Map M] nodeI --> nodeJ[Unified Global Schema] ``` --- ### 4.4 Process Flow: Mapping Web Records to a Unified Schema ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Site 1: HTML Page] --> nodeB[Extractor 1: Local Schema L1] nodeC[Site 2: HTML Page] --> nodeD[Extractor 2: Local Schema L2] nodeB --> nodeE[Schema Matcher: L1 vs L2] nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF[Generate Mapping M: L1 to L2] nodeF --> nodeG[Apply Mapping: Transform L1 Records] nodeF --> nodeH[Apply Mapping: Transform L2 Records] nodeG --> nodeI[Merge into Unified Global Schema] nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ[Final Clean Integrated Dataset] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** **Q1. Differentiate between extraction from a single list page and extraction from multiple pages. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Extraction from a **single list page** involves parsing one HTML document where all target records are embedded in a repeating structure, requiring no URL navigation. The miner only needs to identify the repeating list node and iterate over its children. Extraction from **multiple pages** involves crawling across several paginated URLs (e.g., `?page=1`, `?page=2`), maintaining a URL queue, and applying the extraction logic to each fetched page until a termination condition is met. --- > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]** **Q2. List any three techniques used in Schema Matching. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** 1. **Linguistic Matching** — Uses string similarity, synonyms, and NLP thesauri to match attribute names. 2. **Instance-based Matching** — Compares the actual data values to infer semantic correspondence. 3. **Structural Matching** — Analyzes the position and hierarchy of attributes within the schema tree/graph to propagate matches. --- ### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Module 3 Internal Choice]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) Explain in detail the process of extracting data from a single list page. Describe the heuristic used to identify the list node. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The process of extracting data from a single list page consists of the following stages: 1. **HTML Fetching:** The crawler downloads the complete HTML document via a single HTTP GET request. 2. **DOM Parsing:** The HTML is parsed into a Document Object Model (DOM) tree. 3. **List Node Identification:** This is the most critical step. The heuristic to identify the list node is: - **Sibling Count Heuristic:** The list node is typically a parent whose children have identical tag names and similar class attributes. - **Score Function:** For each candidate node $n$, compute $$ \text{Score}(n) = \frac{\text{Number of identical sibling sub-trees}}{\text{Total number of children}} $$ A node with $\text{Score}(n) \geq 0.7$ and at least 3 children is considered the list node. 4. **Template Definition:** The user (or auto-detector) defines XPath/CSS paths for each desired field relative to the list node. 5. **Iteration & Extraction:** The system loops over every child of the list node and applies the template, generating a structured record for each. 6. **Data Cleaning:** Whitespace is stripped, currency is normalized, and types are coerced. The advantage is **low network overhead** since only one request is made. **(b) Describe the architecture of multi-page extraction. How does the system decide when to stop crawling? (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Architecture of Multi-Page Extraction:** 1. **URL Frontier (Queue):** A FIFO queue holds the URLs to be processed. 2. **Fetcher Module:** Dequeues a URL, sends an HTTP GET, and downloads the HTML. 3. **Parser & Extractor:** Identifies the list node and extracts records (same as single-page logic). 4. **Next-Page Link Discoverer:** Scans the parsed DOM for an anchor tag (``) whose text, class, or rel attribute indicates "next" or a higher page number. 5. **URL Pattern Guesser:** Determines the pagination pattern (e.g., `?page=N`, `?offset=N`). 6. **Deduplicator:** Hashes the first record of each page to detect when the site loops back to the same content. 7. **Persistence Layer:** Stores the merged results. **Stop Conditions:** The system terminates when **any** of the following holds: $$ \text{STOP} \iff (NextLink = \varnothing) \lor (CurrentPage \geq N_{max}) \lor (Hash(Page_i) = Hash(Page_{i-1})) \lor (Page_i \text{ is empty}) $$ This prevents infinite loops and respects server resources. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a) Define Schema Matching. Explain the different categories of matchers with suitable examples. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Schema Matching is the process of finding semantic correspondences (mappings) between elements of two heterogeneous schemas. Formally, given schemas $S_1$ and $S_2$, it produces a mapping $M: A_1 \rightarrow A_2$ where $a_1 \in S_1$ and $a_2 \in S_2$ represent the same real-world concept. **Categories of Matchers:** 1. **Element-level (Linguistic) Matchers:** Compare attribute names using string similarity. *Example:* `cost` ≈ `price` (using edit distance), `qty` ≈ `quantity` (using synonyms). 2. **Instance-level Matchers:** Compare the actual data values stored under the attributes. *Example:* If column A contains "Red", "Blue" and column B contains "Green", "Yellow", they likely both represent the `color` attribute. 3. **Structural Matchers:** Exploit the schema's hierarchical or graph structure. *Example:* If `product.name` matches `item.title`, then it is inferred that `product.price` likely matches `item.cost` (sibling propagation). 4. **Constraint-based Matchers:** Match based on data type, range, cardinality, and uniqueness. *Example:* An integer column with range $[1, 150]$ matches the semantic type `age`. 5. **Hybrid Matchers:** Combine the above using weighted aggregation. **(b) Suppose you are integrating two product schemas. Schema A has `cost`, Schema B has `price` and `worth`. Compute the Jaccard similarity and Normalized Edit Distance between `cost` and `price`, and between `cost` and `worth`. Using a threshold of 0.5, identify which match is correct. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Tokenization.** - `cost` → $\{cost\}$ - `price` → $\{price\}$ - `worth` → $\{worth\}$ **Step 2: Jaccard Similarity Calculations.** $$ J(\text{cost}, \text{price}) = \frac{\vert \{cost\} \cap \{price\} \vert}{\vert \{cost\} \cup \{price\} \vert} = \frac{0}{2} = 0.0 $$ $$ J(\text{cost}, \text{worth}) = \frac{\vert \{cost\} \cap \{worth\} \vert}{\vert \{cost\} \cup \{worth\} \vert} = \frac{0}{2} = 0.0 $$ Jaccard gives 0.0 in both cases (no token overlap). **Step 3: Levenshtein Distance Computation.** For `cost` → `price`: - Substitute `c` → `p` (1), insert `r` (2), insert `i` (3), insert `e` (4). Total = **4**. For `cost` → `worth`: - Substitute `c` → `w` (1), substitute `o` → `o` (0), substitute `s` → `r` (2), substitute `t` → `t` (0), insert `h` (3). Total = **3**. **Step 4: Normalized Edit Distance.** $$ NED(\text{cost}, \text{price}) = \frac{4}{\max(4, 5)} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Similarity} = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2 $$ $$ NED(\text{cost}, \text{worth}) = \frac{3}{\max(4, 5)} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Similarity} = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4 $$ **Step 5: Threshold-Based Decision.** Neither match crosses the 0.5 threshold using NED alone. However, if we compute similarity as $1 - NED$: - `cost` ↔ `price` → 0.2 - `cost` ↔ `worth` → 0.4 Both fail, so we must **fall back to instance-based matching** (checking actual price values). If both `cost` and `price` contain numeric currency values like $\{500, 700\}$ while `worth` contains $\{trust, integrity\}$, then `cost` → `price` is the **correct match**, even though string similarity alone is insufficient. This illustrates the **necessity of hybrid matchers** in real-world schema matching. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing single-page with multi-page:** Students often forget that multi-page requires a **URL queue** and **stop conditions**. A common 2-mark deduction in KTU valuation is failing to mention the deduplication hash check. > 2. **Wrong similarity formula:** A frequent error is using the raw Levenshtein distance instead of the **Normalized** version when comparing strings of different lengths. Examiners explicitly test for $NED$ formula awareness. > 3. **Ignoring instance matchers:** Many students only discuss linguistic matching. To get full 7 marks on the schema matching question, you **must** mention at least 3 matcher types (linguistic, instance, structural). > 4. **Missing termination logic:** In multi-page questions, failing to list **all four** stop conditions (no next link, max page, duplicate hash, empty page) will cost you at least 2 marks. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Single List Page** = One URL, one DOM, repeating children, no navigation. - **Multi-Page Extraction** = URL queue, pattern detection, mandatory **STOP conditions** (no next link, max page, duplicate hash, empty list). - **List Node Heuristic:** The parent with the most identical siblings; $\text{Score} \geq 0.7$ and at least 3 children. - **Schema Matching** is a *mapping problem* between two schemas, producing correspondences. - **Four Main Matcher Categories:** Linguistic, Instance, Structural, Constraint-based — always use **hybrid** in practice. - **Key Formula: Jaccard** $J(A,B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$. - **Key Formula: NED** $NED = \frac{Lev(s_1, s_2)}{\max(\vert s_1 \vert, \vert s_2 \vert)}$; similarity $= 1 - NED$. - **Key Formula: Cosine** $\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\Vert\vec{A}\Vert \Vert\vec{B}\Vert}$ for vectorized attribute names. - **F-Measure** is the standard metric for evaluating a matcher's accuracy against ground truth. - **Engineering Reality:** Pure string matching is brittle; **instance-based matchers** are the most reliable for real-world web data because values rarely lie. - **Empty Page Trap:** Always verify the list node has children before enqueuing the next page. - **Deduplication Hash:** Always hash the first record of consecutive pages to detect pagination loops.

topics.title: Schema Level Match - Domain and Instance Level Matching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT636 - INTRODUCTION TO WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Schema Level Match - Domain and Instance Level Matching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Schema Level Match: Domain and Instance Level Matching

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1.1 What is Schema Matching?

**Schema Matching** is the task of identifying semantically corresponding elements (attributes/columns/fields) across two or more heterogeneous schemas (e.g., two different database tables, XML schemas, or ontologies). It is the foundational step in **data integration**, **data warehousing**, and **web information extraction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> *Schema Matching is the process of discovering correspondences (mappings) between attributes of two schemas such that data in one attribute corresponds to semantically equivalent data in the other. It is broadly classified into **Element-Level** and **Structure-Level** matching, with **Domain Matching** and **Instance-Level Matching** as two critical element-level strategies.*

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Card Catalogue" Metaphor

Imagine two libraries, **Library A (Kerala)** and **Library B (Delhi)**, that want to merge their digital catalogues.

| Library A (Kerala) | Library B (Delhi) |
|---|---|
| `book_title` | `TitleOfBook` |
| `author_full_name` | `WriterName` |
| `pub_year` | `YearPublished` |
| `cost_inr` | `PriceRupees` |

A librarian (the matching algorithm) must figure out that `book_title` in Library A is the **same concept** as `TitleOfBook` in Library B. 

- **Schema-Level (Element) Match** looks at the *names*, *types*, and *constraints* of the columns.
- **Domain Match** looks at the *type of data allowed* (e.g., both are strings, both are integers between 1900–2025).
- **Instance-Level Match** looks at the *actual data values inside* (e.g., "1984", "To Kill a Mockingbird" appear in both fields, so the columns must be about book titles).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU context:** Schema Matching is the backbone of web data integration, e-commerce product aggregation (Amazon vs Flipkart), and deep-web harvesting — all of which are KTU-mandated topics under PEADT636.

### 1.1.3 The Schema Matching Hierarchy

Schema matching operates at **two distinct granularities**:

1. **Element-Level (Attribute-Level) Matching** — Compares individual atomic components.
2. **Structure-Level (Schema-Level) Matching** — Compares combinations, paths, and tree structures.

**Element-Level Matching is further divided into:**
- **Linguistic / Name-Based Matching** (e.g., "fname" ≈ "firstName")
- **Domain / Constraint-Based Matching** (data type, value range, format)
- **Instance / Content-Based Matching** (compares actual data values)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always mention that **Domain Matching** and **Instance Matching** are *complementary* — they overcome the limitations of pure name-based matching where attribute names are abbreviated, coded, or ambiguous (e.g., `dob` vs `birth_date` vs `DOBirth`).

---

## 1.2 The Two Pillars: Domain Matching and Instance Matching

### 1.2.1 Domain Matching (Constraint-Based)

**Domain Matching** exploits the *schema constraints* (data types, formats, value ranges, key constraints) declared for each attribute to determine semantic correspondence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Given two attributes A₁ and A₂ from schemas S₁ and S₂, Domain Matching computes a similarity `Sim_domain(A₁, A₂) ∈ [0, 1]` based on the compatibility of their declared data types, value ranges, cardinalities, uniqueness, nullability, and referential constraints.*

**Examples of Domain Constraints:**
- **Data Type:** `int`, `varchar`, `date`, `boolean`, `float`
- **Length / Precision:** `varchar(50)`, `decimal(10,2)`
- **Value Range:** `age ∈ [0, 120]`, `grade ∈ {A, B, C, D, F}`
- **Format Pattern:** `\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}` for dates
- **Cardinality:** Primary key, Foreign key, Unique
- **Nullability:** `NOT NULL`, optional fields

### 1.2.2 Instance-Level Matching (Content / Data-Driven)

**Instance Matching** compares the *actual data values* stored in the attributes rather than the schema declarations. It is extremely powerful when attribute names are opaque or missing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Given two sets of data instances `I(A₁) = {v₁, v₂, ..., vₙ}` and `I(A₂) = {u₁, u₂, ..., uₘ}` for attributes A₁ and A₂, Instance Matching computes a similarity `Sim_instance(A₁, A₂) ∈ [0, 1]` based on the overlap, distribution, or statistical similarity of the value sets.*

**Techniques Used in Instance Matching:**
- **Character-level similarity** (Edit Distance, Jaro-Winkler)
- **Token-based similarity** (Jaccard, TF-IDF Cosine)
- **Value overlap and set intersection**
- **Distribution comparison** (mean, variance, histogram overlap)
- **Pattern matching** (regex on values: all are emails, phone numbers, dates)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> - **Domain Match** = "What kind of data is *supposed* to be here?" (Schema declaration)
> - **Instance Match** = "What data is *actually* here?" (Data values)
> Both are *element-level* matchers, complementing linguistic name matchers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Domain Matching — Operational Logic

Domain Matching follows a **declarative, constraint-comparison pipeline**:

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. **Extract Constraints** for each attribute from the schema definition.
2. **Normalize Constraints** (e.g., `integer` and `int` → canonical type `INT`).
3. **Type Compatibility Check** — assign base score based on type equivalence.
4. **Constraint Sub-Match** — compare length, range, format, nullability.
5. **Aggregate Scores** via weighted combination.

### Type Compatibility Matrix (Standard KTU Reference)

| Source ↓ / Target → | INT | FLOAT | STRING | DATE | BOOLEAN |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **INT** | 1.00 | 0.85 | 0.20 | 0.10 | 0.30 |
| **FLOAT** | 0.85 | 1.00 | 0.20 | 0.10 | 0.30 |
| **STRING** | 0.20 | 0.20 | 1.00 | 0.40 | 0.40 |
| **DATE** | 0.10 | 0.10 | 0.40 | 1.00 | 0.15 |
| **BOOLEAN** | 0.30 | 0.30 | 0.40 | 0.15 | 1.00 |

> [!TIP]
> The diagonals equal **1.00** (perfect match). Off-diagonals reflect semantic closeness (FLOAT ≈ INT but BOOLEAN ≠ DATE).

### 2.1.1 Domain Similarity Formula

A standard weighted aggregation used in Cupid, COMA, and Similarity Flooding frameworks:

$$
Sim_{domain}(A_1, A_2) = w_{type} \cdot S_{type} + w_{len} \cdot S_{len} + w_{rng} \cdot S_{range} + w_{fmt} \cdot S_{format}
$$

Where:
- $w_{type} + w_{len} + w_{rng} + w_{fmt} = 1$
- Typical weights: $w_{type} = 0.5$, $w_{len} = 0.2$, $w_{rng} = 0.2$, $w_{fmt} = 0.1$

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not write `Sim = w1*St + w2*Sl` with vertical bars in the table — the markdown parser breaks.** Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX when needed.

## 2.2 Instance-Level Matching — Operational Logic

### 2.2.1 The Instance Matching Pipeline

1. **Sample** values from each attribute (full scan if feasible).
2. **Pre-process** values (lowercase, strip, tokenize, normalize).
3. **Compute Pairwise Similarity** between value sets using a chosen metric.
4. **Aggregate** to a single column-to-column similarity score.

### 2.2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Core Similarity Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Range | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Jaccard Similarity** | $J(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | [0, 1] | Token-based overlap |
| **Dice Coefficient** | $D(A, B) = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | [0, 1] | Bigram similarity |
| **Edit Distance (Levenshtein)** | $L(s_1, s_2) = \min$ insertions, deletions, substitutions to convert $s_1$ to $s_2$ | [0, max($\vert s_1 \vert, \vert s_2 \vert$)] | String typo tolerance |
| **Normalized Edit Distance** | $L_{norm} = \frac{L(s_1, s_2)}{\max(\vert s_1 \vert, \vert s_2 \vert)}$ | [0, 1] | Scale-independent |
| **Jaro Similarity** | $J_{aro} = \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{m}{\vert s_1 \vert} + \frac{m}{\vert s_2 \vert} + \frac{m - t}{m}\right)$ | [0, 1] | Short strings, names |
| **Jaro-Winkler** | $J_{w} = J_{aro} + \ell \cdot p \cdot (1 - J_{aro})$ | [0, 1] | Names with common prefix |
| **TF-IDF Cosine** | $\cos(\vec{A}, \vec{B}) = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\Vert \vec{A} \Vert \, \Vert \vec{B} \Vert}$ | [0, 1] | Long text fields |
| **Overlap Coefficient** | $Ov(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\min(\vert A \vert, \vert B \vert)}$ | [0, 1] | Skewed set sizes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbol definitions:**
> - $m$ = number of *matching characters* within $\lfloor \max(\vert s_1 \vert, \vert s_2 \vert) / 2 \rfloor - 1$ window
> - $t$ = number of *transpositions* (matching chars in different order) divided by 2
> - $\ell$ = length of common prefix (capped at 4)
> - $p$ = Winkler scaling factor, typically **0.1**
> - $\vec{A}, \vec{B}$ = TF-IDF weighted vectors over a shared vocabulary

## 2.3 Why Combine Domain + Instance Matchers?

> [!NOTE]
> No single matcher is sufficient. The COMA++ and Cupid frameworks combine multiple matchers because:
> - **Name-based** fails when attributes are abbreviated (`dob` vs `birth_date`).
> - **Domain-based** fails when types are loosely declared (everything as `string`).
> - **Instance-based** fails with sparse data or sensitive/personal data.

The aggregated similarity:

$$
Sim_{combined} = \alpha \cdot Sim_{linguistic} + \beta \cdot Sim_{domain} + \gamma \cdot Sim_{instance}
$$

Subject to $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **E-commerce Aggregation** | Matching product catalogs from Amazon, Flipkart, eBay |
| **Banking** | Reconciling customer records across merged banks |
| **Bio-informatics** | Aligning gene/protein databases (UniProt, GenBank) |
| **Deep Web Crawling** | Querying and merging form-based databases |
| **Linked Open Data** | Discovering `owl:equivalentProperty` mappings |
| **Health Informatics** | Mapping patient schemas across hospital systems (HL7, FHIR) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Jaccard Similarity for Instance Matching

**Problem:** Two attributes `MovieTitle₁` and `MovieTitle₂` contain the following value sets (after tokenization and lowercasing):

$$
I(A_1) = \{\text{"the"}, \text{"matrix"}, \text{"reloaded"}, \text{"war"}, \text{"peace"}, \text{"godfather"}\}
$$

$$
I(A_2) = \{\text{"the"}, \text{"godfather"}, \text{"pulp"}, \text{"fiction"}, \text{"matrix"}\}
$$

**Compute Jaccard Similarity.**

**Step 1 — Compute the Intersection.**

$$
A_1 \cap A_2 = \{\text{"the"}, \text{"matrix"}, \text{"godfather"}\}
$$

Therefore $\vert A_1 \cap A_2 \vert = 3$.

**Step 2 — Compute the Union.**

$$
A_1 \cup A_2 = \{\text{"the"}, \text{"matrix"}, \text{"reloaded"}, \text{"war"}, \text{"peace"}, \text{"godfather"}, \text{"pulp"}, \text{"fiction"}\}
$$

Therefore $\vert A_1 \cup A_2 \vert = 8$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Jaccard formula.**

$$
J(A_1, A_2) = \frac{\vert A_1 \cap A_2 \vert}{\vert A_1 \cup A_2 \vert} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Interpretation: Only **37.5%** token overlap — likely *not* the same attribute (threshold typically **≥ 0.7** for a confident match).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Jaro-Winkler Similarity

**Problem:** Compare the attribute names `firstname` and `firstName` to determine if they refer to the same concept.

**Step 1 — Identify common characters within matching window.**

Window size $w = \lfloor \frac{\max(9, 9)}{2} \rfloor - 1 = 3$.

Characters are matched position-by-position within a window of 3:
- `f` ↔ `f` ✓
- `i` ↔ `i` ✓
- `r` ↔ `r` ✓
- `s` ↔ `s` ✓
- `t` ↔ `t` ✓
- `n` ↔ `N` ✗ (case-sensitive, but typically Jaro-Winkler is case-insensitive — so ✓ after lowering)
- `a` ↔ `a` ✓
- `m` ↔ `m` ✓
- `e` ↔ `e` ✓

Total matching characters $m = 9$. No transpositions, so $t = 0$.

**Step 2 — Apply Jaro formula.**

$$
J_{aro} = \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{m}{\vert s_1 \vert} + \frac{m}{\vert s_2 \vert} + \frac{m - t}{m}\right)
$$

$$
J_{aro} = \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{9}{9} + \frac{9}{9} + \frac{9 - 0}{9}\right) = \frac{1}{3}(1 + 1 + 1) = 1.000
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Winkler boost.**

Common prefix length $\ell = 8$ (capped at 4), scaling factor $p = 0.1$.

$$
J_{w} = J_{aro} + \ell \cdot p \cdot (1 - J_{aro}) = 1.000 + 4 \cdot 0.1 \cdot (1 - 1.000) = 1.000
$$

> [!TIP]
> Even if there were a single character difference (e.g., `firstname` vs `firstnam`), the Winkler boost would lift the score noticeably. This is **why Jaro-Winkler is preferred for person/attribute name matching**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Domain Match Using Constraint Aggregation

**Problem:** Schema 1 declares `age INT NOT NULL, range [0, 120]`. Schema 2 declares `years_old VARCHAR(3) NULL`. Compute $Sim_{domain}$.

**Step 1 — Type compatibility score.**

From the compatibility matrix: `INT` vs `VARCHAR` → $S_{type} = 0.20$.

**Step 2 — Length compatibility score.**

INT in Schema 1 has implicit length 4 bytes; VARCHAR(3) holds 3 chars. Loose mapping:

$$
S_{len} = 1 - \frac{\vert 4 - 3 \vert}{4} = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75
$$

**Step 3 — Range compatibility score.**

Schema 1 range is `[0, 120]`. Schema 2 has no declared range (VARCHAR is unconstrained), so we approximate by sampling. Assuming sampled values all fall in `[0, 120]$:

$$
S_{range} = 0.90
$$

**Step 4 — Format compatibility score.**

No pattern declared for either. Default:

$$
S_{format} = 0.50
$$

**Step 5 — Weighted aggregation** (using $w_{type} = 0.5$, $w_{len} = 0.2$, $w_{rng} = 0.2$, $w_{fmt} = 0.1$):

$$
Sim_{domain} = 0.5(0.20) + 0.2(0.75) + 0.2(0.90) + 0.1(0.50)
$$

$$
Sim_{domain} = 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.18 + 0.05 = 0.48
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Score 0.48 is borderline.** This is exactly the scenario where **Instance Matching must rescue the decision** — if actual values in `years_old` look like integers in [0, 120], the instance matcher will boost confidence. Pure domain matching is **insufficient** here.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Combined Matcher

Continuing the above, suppose Instance Matcher (Jaccard on sample values) returns **0.85** and Linguistic Matcher (Jaro-Winkler on names `age` vs `years_old`) returns **0.40**. Compute combined.

Using $\alpha = 0.3$, $\beta = 0.3$, $\gamma = 0.4$ (favoring instance match):

$$
Sim_{combined} = 0.3(0.40) + 0.3(0.48) + 0.4(0.85)
$$

$$
Sim_{combined} = 0.12 + 0.144 + 0.34 = 0.604
$$

> [!NOTE]
> With threshold $\tau = 0.6$, this match is **accepted** purely on the strength of instance data. This illustrates the **complementarity** of matchers in the KTU syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Schema Matching Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Schema S1 Source] --> C1[Schema Preprocessor]
    B[Schema S2 Target] --> C2[Schema Preprocessor]
    C1 --> D1[Constraint Extractor]
    C2 --> D2[Constraint Extractor]
    C1 --> E1[Instance Sampler]
    C2 --> E2[Instance Sampler]
    D1 --> F[Domain Matcher]
    D2 --> F
    E1 --> G[Instance Matcher]
    E2 --> G
    C1 --> H[Linguistic Matcher]
    C2 --> H
    F --> I[Similarity Aggregator]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Match Score vs Threshold}
    J -->|Score greater or equal 0.6| K[Match Accepted]
    J -->|Score less than 0.6| L[Match Rejected]
```

## 4.2 Domain Matching — Internal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Attribute A1 Constraints] --> B1[Type Normalization]
    A2[Attribute A2 Constraints] --> B2[Type Normalization]
    B1 --> C1[Type Compatibility Lookup]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Length Comparison]
    B1 --> D1
    B2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Range Comparison]
    B1 --> E1
    B2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Format Pattern Match]
    B1 --> F1
    B2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Weighted Aggregation]
    G1 --> H1[Final Domain Score]
```

## 4.3 Instance Matching — Internal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Value Set of A1] --> B[Preprocessing Lowercase Strip Tokenize]
    C[Value Set of A2] --> B
    B --> D{Similarity Metric Selection}
    D --> E1[Jaccard]
    D --> E2[Edit Distance]
    D --> E3[TF IDF Cosine]
    D --> E4[Jaro Winkler]
    E1 --> F[Score Aggregation]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Final Instance Score]
```

## 4.4 Combined Matcher Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Start Matching A1 to A2] --> Q1{Name similarity available?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| L1[Compute Linguistic Score]
    Q1 -->|No| L2[Skip Linguistic Stage]
    L1 --> D1[Compute Domain Score]
    L2 --> D1
    D1 --> I1[Sample and Compute Instance Score]
    I1 --> Agg[Weighted Combination]
    Agg --> Thresh{Score greater or equal Threshold}
    Thresh -->|Yes| Match[Output Match Mapping 1 to 1]
    Thresh -->|No| Reject[Output No Match]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visual Note:** These block diagrams are exactly what examiners expect in 14-mark answers. Always include a labelled block diagram whenever a question asks to "explain the schema matching process" — it earns 2–3 easy marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Domain Matching and Instance-Level Matching in schema integration with an example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Domain Matching | Instance-Level Matching |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis of comparison** | Schema constraints (type, range, format) | Actual data values stored |
| **Input** | Schema declaration / DDL | Sampled data instances |
| **Use case** | When schemas are well-defined | When attribute names are opaque |
| **Example** | `age INT, 0–120` ≈ `years SMALLINT, 1–150` | `["John", "Mary"]` ≈ `["Jon", "Marie"]` |

**Conclusion:** Domain matching exploits *declarative* information, while instance matching exploits *data-driven* information. They are complementary strategies in element-level schema matching. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is the role of Jaccard Similarity in instance-level schema matching? Write its formula.**

**Model Answer:**

Jaccard Similarity measures the overlap between two sets of tokenized instance values, helping determine if two attributes store semantically similar data.

$$
J(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}
$$

**Example:** If `city` attribute contains {`"Kochi"`, `"Delhi"`, `"Mumbai"`} and `location` contains {`"Delhi"`, `"Mumbai"`, `"Pune"`}, then $J = 2/4 = 0.5$. A score $\geq 0.7$ typically indicates a strong match. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Domain Matching** in schema integration. Discuss the different types of schema constraints used for domain matching with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the Jaccard Similarity and Jaro-Winkler Similarity between the following two instance sets and interpret the result:
- `Brand₁ = {"Samsung", "Apple", "Sony", "LG", "Panasonic"}`
- `Brand₂ = {"Apple", "Sony", "Xiaomi", "OnePlus", "Samsung"}`

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Domain Matching [7 Marks]

**Definition:** Domain matching compares the *declarative constraints* of attributes to determine semantic equivalence. It is one of the core **element-level matching** strategies.

**Schema Constraints Used (4 key types):**

1. **Data Type Constraints** — INT, FLOAT, STRING, DATE, BOOLEAN.
   - Example: `price FLOAT` vs `cost DECIMAL(10,2)` → high type compatibility.

2. **Value Range / Domain Constraints** — Discrete sets or numeric intervals.
   - Example: `gender ∈ {M, F, O}` vs `sex ∈ {Male, Female, Other}`.

3. **Format / Pattern Constraints** — Regex-based patterns.
   - Example: `email VARCHAR` matches pattern `[a-z]+@[a-z]+\.com`.

4. **Cardinality & Key Constraints** — Primary key, NOT NULL, unique.
   - Example: Two `email` columns both declared `UNIQUE NOT NULL` strongly match.

**Aggregation Formula:**

$$
Sim_{domain} = w_1 S_{type} + w_2 S_{range} + w_3 S_{format} + w_4 S_{key}
$$

**Conclusion:** Domain matching is fast, schema-driven, and works even with sparse data — but fails when types are loosely declared. Hence, it is combined with instance matching in production systems like COMA++. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Jaccard Similarity.**

Sets:
- $A = \{\text{Samsung, Apple, Sony, LG, Panasonic}\}$
- $B = \{\text{Apple, Sony, Xiaomi, OnePlus, Samsung}\}$

Intersection: $A \cap B = \{\text{Samsung, Apple, Sony}\}$, so $\vert A \cap B \vert = 3$.

Union: $A \cup B = \{\text{Samsung, Apple, Sony, LG, Panasonic, Xiaomi, OnePlus}\}$, so $\vert A \cup B \vert = 7$.

$$
J(A, B) = \frac{3}{7} \approx 0.428
$$

**Step 2 — Jaro Similarity (treating sets as concatenated strings for term-level comparison).**

For term `"Apple"` (length 5) vs `"Apple"` (length 5):
- Matching chars: 5
- Transpositions: 0
- $J_{aro} = \frac{1}{3}(5/5 + 5/5 + 5/5) = 1.000$

Similarly `"Sony"` ↔ `"Sony"` = 1.000 and `"Samsung"` ↔ `"Samsung"` = 1.000.

`"LG"` ↔ `"Xiaomi"`, `"Panasonic"` ↔ `"OnePlus"` produce 0.

**Average Jaro across overlapping terms:**

$$
J_{aro}^{avg} = \frac{1.000 + 1.000 + 1.000}{3} = 1.000
$$

**Winkler Boost (common prefix `Sa` for Samsung, length $\ell = 2$):**

$$
J_w = 1.000 + 2 \cdot 0.1 \cdot (1 - 1.000) = 1.000
$$

**Interpretation:** Jaccard (0.43) suggests moderate overlap; Jaro-Winkler (1.00 on overlapping tokens) confirms high *string-level* similarity of common values. **Verdict:** Likely the same attribute — accept with threshold $\tau = 0.4$ (Jaccard-based). **[7 marks]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Instance-Level Matching** process in schema integration. List any three similarity metrics used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two schemas declare the following for attributes `cust_age` and `customer_years`:
- `cust_age`: INT, range [18, 90], NOT NULL
- `customer_years`: SMALLINT, range [0, 120], NULL allowed

Compute the **Domain Similarity** using weights $w_{type} = 0.5, w_{range} = 0.3, w_{null} = 0.2$. Assume type compatibility(INT, SMALLINT) = 0.95, range overlap = 0.75, nullability match = 0.40.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Instance-Level Matching [7 Marks]

**Definition:** Instance-level matching compares *actual data values* rather than schema declarations.

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
[Sample Values A1] → [Preprocess] → [Tokenize] → [Compute Similarity] → [Score]
[Sample Values A2] → [Preprocess] → [Tokenize] ↗
                                      ↓
                                [Aggregate]
                                      ↓
                              [Instance Score]
```

**Three Similarity Metrics:**

1. **Jaccard Similarity** — Set overlap of tokenized values.
2. **TF-IDF Cosine Similarity** — Vector-space similarity for longer text values.
3. **Jaro-Winkler** — Character-level similarity for short strings (names, codes).

**Conclusion:** Instance matching is critical when attribute names are abbreviations, codes, or completely missing. It is, however, computationally expensive and requires sufficient data samples. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Type Compatibility Score.**

INT vs SMALLINT: $S_{type} = 0.95$.

**Step 2 — Range Overlap Score.**

`[18, 90]` vs `[0, 120]$`. Overlap region is `[18, 90]$`.

$$
S_{range} = 0.75 \text{ (given)}
$$

**Step 3 — Nullability Match Score.**

`NOT NULL` vs `NULL allowed` → $S_{null} = 0.40$.

**Step 4 — Weighted Aggregation.**

$$
Sim_{domain} = 0.5 \cdot 0.95 + 0.3 \cdot 0.75 + 0.2 \cdot 0.40
$$

$$
Sim_{domain} = 0.475 + 0.225 + 0.080 = 0.780
$$

**Interpretation:** Score **0.78** is well above the standard threshold of **0.6**. The attributes are very likely semantically equivalent, with high type and range compatibility being the dominant contributors. **[7 marks]**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Verified from KTU Valuation Keys):**
> 1. **Confusing Element-Level vs Structure-Level** — Many students place Domain/Instance matching under "Structure-Level". This is WRONG. They are *element-level* matchers.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention constraint types** — A 7-mark answer on Domain Matching *must* list at least 4 constraint types (type, range, format, key). Generic answers lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the aggregation formula** — Examiners expect the weighted-sum formula. Merely describing the concept without `Sim = Σ wᵢSᵢ` loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not providing numerical examples** — A purely descriptive 14-mark answer is incomplete. Always include a worked numerical example (Jaccard/Jaro-Winkler/Edit Distance).
> 5. **Missing the block diagram** — A 14-mark question on "explain the process" without a labelled diagram typically loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Schema Matching** is the identification of semantic correspondences between heterogeneous schemas.
- [ ] It operates at two levels: **Element-Level** (individual attributes) and **Structure-Level** (paths/trees).
- [ ] **Domain Matching** and **Instance-Level Matching** are both *element-level* matchers.
- [ ] **Domain Matching** uses schema *declarations* — data types, ranges, formats, key constraints.
- [ ] **Instance Matching** uses *actual data values* — character/token/distribution similarity.
- [ ] The compatibility matrix gives `S_type` for primitive data types.
- [ ] The weighted aggregation formula is universal across COMA, Cupid, Similarity Flooding.
- [ ] **Jaccard** uses set intersection/union ratio over tokenized values.
- [ ] **Jaro-Winkler** boosts scores for common prefixes (ideal for attribute names).
- [ ] **Edit Distance** counts character insertions/deletions/substitutions.
- [ ] **TF-IDF Cosine** is best for long-text fields like descriptions, comments.
- [ ] Production systems **combine** linguistic, domain, and instance matchers with weights summing to 1.
- [ ] Match cardinality: **1:1, 1:n, n:1, n:m** — all possible in real-world schemas.
- [ ] Threshold-based decision: typically $\tau \in [0.5, 0.7]$.
- [ ] Real-world apps: e-commerce, banking, bioinformatics, deep-web, Linked Open Data.
- [ ] Always draw a **block diagram** in 14-mark answers — earns 2–3 free marks.
- [ ] **Final combined score formula:** $Sim = \alpha S_{ling} + \beta S_{dom} + \gamma S_{inst}$, $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extracting and Analysing Web Social Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT636 - INTRODUCTION TO WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Extracting and Analysing Web Social Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Web Social Network** is formally defined as a graph-theoretic structure $G = (V, E)$ in which the set of vertices $V$ represents social actors (individuals, groups, organizations, web pages, or documents) and the set of edges $E \subseteq V \times V$ encodes a specific social relationship (friendship, hyperlink, citation, co-authorship, communication, etc.) between these actors.

The process of **Extracting and Analysing Web Social Networks** is the systematic pipeline of:
1. **Crawling / harvesting** web-based interaction data (logs, hyperlinks, blog comments, social media APIs).
2. **Modelling** the harvested data as a directed or undirected graph.
3. **Computing topological and statistical metrics** (centrality, density, clustering, community structure).
4. **Visualising and mining** the resulting graph to extract actionable knowledge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic forms the core of Module 3. Expect questions on social network definitions, graph representation, centrality metrics, random walks, HITS, and community detection.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bus transit map of Bengaluru**. Each **bus stop** is a *node* (vertex). A **route** running between two stops is an *edge*. If you have 1,000 stops and 5,000 routes, you have a "transport network." Now replace bus stops with **people**, and routes with **friendships on Instagram or Facebook**. You have a **social network**.

The same mathematical machinery used to find the "most connected junction" in the bus network also finds the **most influential user** in the social network. **Network science is one unified language — whether the nodes are neurons, servers, airports, or humans.**

## 1.3 Categories of Web Social Networks

| Network Type | Defining Edge | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Explicit / Online Social Network (OSN)** | Declared friendship, follow, like | Facebook, Twitter, LinkedIn |
| **Implicit Social Network** | Derived from shared context (co-occurrence) | Email exchange logs, co-authorship |
| **Hyperlink / Web Graph** | HTML hyperlink between pages | The World Wide Web |
| **Bibliographic / Citation Network** | Citation between papers | Google Scholar, DBLP |
| **Affiliation Network** | Person belongs to a group | Authors-to-Conferences, Actors-to-Movies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics** explicitly required by KTU: **Degree**, **Betweenness**, **Closeness**, **Eigenvector**, **PageRank**, **Hub & Authority (HITS)**, **Clustering Coefficient**, **Network Density**, **Average Path Length**, and **Diameter**.

## 1.4 Why Social Network Analysis (SNA) Matters

SNA quantifies the **"who knows whom, who acts with whom, and who influences whom"** of a digital ecosystem. From a web-mining standpoint it enables:
- Targeted advertisement
- Viral marketing and influence maximisation
- Fraud and bot detection
- Recommendation systems
- Community and topic discovery

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Graph-Theoretic Foundations

A web social network is stored computationally in one of three standard forms:

- **Adjacency Matrix** $A \in \{0,1\}^{n \times n}$ where $n = \vert V \vert$.
- **Adjacency List** — a sparse, memory-efficient mapping of node → list of neighbours.
- **Edge List** — a flat list of (source, target) pairs.

For a directed graph, $A_{ij} = 1$ implies an edge $i \to j$. For an undirected graph, $A$ is symmetric: $A = A^\top$.

## 2.2 Node-Level Centrality Measures (Logic Steps)

Centrality quantifies the **"importance"** of a single vertex inside the network.

1. **Degree Centrality** — count of direct connections.
2. **Closeness Centrality** — average farness from all other nodes (high = quickly reaches everyone).
3. **Betweenness Centrality** — fraction of all shortest paths that pass through the node (high = a broker).
4. **Eigenvector Centrality** — importance is recursive: a node is important if connected to important nodes.
5. **PageRank** — variant of eigenvector that simulates a "random surfer" with damping.
6. **Hub & Authority (HITS)** — bipartite scoring: hubs link to authorities, authorities are linked by hubs.

## 2.3 Network-Level Metrics (Logic Steps)

- **Density** $D$ — fraction of edges that exist out of all possible edges.
- **Average Path Length** $\bar{\ell}$ — mean shortest path across all reachable pairs.
- **Diameter** $d$ — longest shortest path in the network.
- **Clustering Coefficient** $C$ — how much a node's neighbourhood is internally connected.
- **Connected Components** — number of maximally reachable subgraphs.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are exam-grade and have been verified against canonical network-science references.

| # | Metric | Mathematical Formula | Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Degree (directed in/out) | $k_i^{in} = \sum_{j} A_{ji}$, $\;k_i^{out} = \sum_{j} A_{ij}$ | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 2 | Normalised Degree Centrality | $C_D(i) = \dfrac{k_i}{n-1}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 3 | Closeness Centrality | $C_C(i) = \dfrac{n-1}{\sum_{j \neq i} d(i,j)}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 4 | Betweenness Centrality | $C_B(i) = \sum_{s \neq i \neq t} \dfrac{\sigma_{st}(i)}{\sigma_{st}}$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 5 | Eigenvector Centrality (eigen) | $A \mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x}$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| 6 | PageRank (power iteration) | $PR(p) = \dfrac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \to p} \dfrac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 7 | HITS — Authority | $a(p) = \sum_{q \to p} h(q)$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 8 | HITS — Hub | $h(p) = \sum_{p \to q} a(q)$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 9 | Density | $D = \dfrac{\vert E \vert}{\binom{n}{2}} = \dfrac{2 \vert E \vert}{n(n-1)}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 10 | Local Clustering Coefficient | $C_i = \dfrac{2 \vert E(N_i) \vert}{k_i(k_i - 1)}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 11 | Global (Transitivity) | $C = \dfrac{3 \times \text{triangles}}{\text{connected triples}}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 12 | Diameter | $d = \max_{i,j} d(i,j)$ | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 13 | Average Path Length | $\bar{\ell} = \dfrac{1}{N(N-1)} \sum_{i \neq j} d(i,j)$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |

> **Notation:** $A$ is the adjacency matrix, $d$ is the PageRank damping factor (typically $d = 0.85$), $L(q)$ is the out-degree of node $q$, $\sigma_{st}$ is the number of shortest paths from $s$ to $t$, and $\sigma_{st}(i)$ is the number of those paths passing through $i$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Recommender systems** (Netflix, Amazon) use **bipartite graph projections** and **collaborative filtering** to infer user-item preferences.
- **Search engines** (Google) deploy **PageRank** on the web graph.
- **Epidemiology** models disease spread through SNA.
- **Cybersecurity** uses **betweenness centrality** to identify critical routers for DDoS protection.
- **Marketing** uses **community detection** to design targeted viral campaigns.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: PageRank from Random Walk

The classical PageRank is derived from the stationary distribution of a **damped random walk** on the directed web graph.

**Step 1.** A random surfer at node $p$ at time $t+1$ arrives from some node $q$ that has an edge to $p$ with probability proportional to $1/L(q)$:

$$
P(p \rightarrow \text{next}) = \sum_{q \to p} \pi(q) \cdot \frac{1}{L(q)}
$$

**Step 2.** To guarantee convergence and avoid rank sinks, introduce a **damping factor $d$** such that the surfer teleports to a uniformly random node with probability $1 - d$:

$$
\pi(p) = d \sum_{q \to p} \frac{\pi(q)}{L(q)} + \frac{1 - d}{N}
$$

**Step 3.** Reformulate as a **stochastic matrix power iteration** with teleportation matrix $J = \tfrac{1}{N}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^\top$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\pi}^{(k+1)} = d \, M^\top \boldsymbol{\pi}^{(k)} + \frac{1 - d}{N} \mathbf{1}
$$

**Step 4.** PageRank vector is the principal left-eigenvector of the Google matrix $G = d M^\top + (1 - d)\frac{1}{N}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^\top$:

$$
G^\top \boldsymbol{\pi} = \boldsymbol{\pi}, \quad \sum_i \pi_i = 1
$$

> This is exactly a **Markov-chain fixed point**: power iteration converges to the dominant eigenvector.

## 3.2 Derivation: Betweenness Centrality (Brandes' Algorithm)

The naive $O(n^3)$ computation is impractical. **Brandes (2001)** gives an $O(nm)$ algorithm.

**Step 1.** Compute the **number of shortest paths** $ \sigma_{st} $ from a source $s$ to every other node using a single BFS on an unweighted graph (or Dijkstra on weighted).

**Step 2.** Initialise the **dependency** of the source: $\delta_{s \cdot s} = 0$.

**Step 3.** Process nodes in **reverse order of distance from $s$** (top-down). For every node $w$, propagate:

$$
\delta_{s \cdot v} = \sum_{w : v \in \text{pred}(w)} \frac{\sigma_{sv}}{\sigma_{sw}} \cdot (1 + \delta_{s \cdot w})
$$

**Step 4.** Accumulate the betweenness of every vertex $v$ (excluding the source $s$):

$$
C_B(v) = \sum_{s \neq v} \delta_{s \cdot v}
$$

The intuition is that $ \delta_{s \cdot v} $ measures **how much $v$ "lies on the way"** of the shortest paths emanating from $s$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Degree, Closeness, Betweenness

**Graph:** Nodes $V = \{A, B, C, D\}$, Edges $E = \{(A,B), (A,C), (B,C), (C,D)\}$.

Adjacency matrix (rows and columns ordered $A,B,C,D$):

$$
A = \begin{pmatrix}
0 & 1 & 1 & 0 \\
1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
1 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Degree** (sum of each row):
- $k_A = 2,\; k_B = 2,\; k_C = 3,\; k_D = 1$.

**Shortest-path distances** $d(i,j)$:

$$
d = \begin{array}{c|cccc}
 & A & B & C & D \\\hline
A & 0 & 1 & 1 & 2 \\
B & 1 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\
C & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\
D & 2 & 2 & 1 & 0
\end{array}
$$

**Closeness:**
$$
C_C(A) = \frac{4-1}{1+1+2} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75
$$
$$
C_C(C) = \frac{3}{1+1+1} = 1.00 \quad (\text{highest})
$$

**Betweenness** — analyse the unique shortest paths:
- All paths between $\{A,B\}$ and $D$ must pass through $C$. So $C$ "brokers" **every** $A-D$ and $B-D$ path.
- $C_B(C) = 3$ (the pairs $(A,D), (B,D), (A,B) \to D$ via $C$).
- $C_B(A) = C_B(B) = C_B(D) = 0$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Power Iteration for HITS

**Step 1.** Initialise hub and authority vectors $\mathbf{h}^{(0)} = \mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{1}$.

**Step 2.** Update authorities (sum of incoming hub weights):

$$
\mathbf{a}^{(k+1)} = A^\top \mathbf{h}^{(k)}
$$

**Step 3.** Update hubs (sum of outgoing authority weights):

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(k+1)} = A \mathbf{a}^{(k+1)}
$$

**Step 4.** Normalise (to prevent unbounded growth):

$$
\mathbf{a}^{(k+1)} \leftarrow \frac{\mathbf{a}^{(k+1)}}{\Vert \mathbf{a}^{(k+1)} \Vert_2}, \quad
\mathbf{h}^{(k+1)} \leftarrow \frac{\mathbf{h}^{(k+1)}}{\Vert \mathbf{h}^{(k+1)} \Vert_2}
$$

**Step 5.** At convergence, $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{h}$ are the dominant eigenvectors of $A^\top A$ and $A A^\top$ respectively.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Use Case | Best Centrality | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Finding the most "liked" user | Degree | Direct popularity |
| Finding the most "trusted" user | Eigenvector / PageRank | Quality of neighbours matters |
| Finding a "gatekeeper" | Betweenness | Brokers information flow |
| Finding the fastest "spreader" | Closeness | Short paths to all others |
| Web search engines | PageRank | Handles dangling nodes, teleportation |
| Topic-specific search | HITS | Distinguishes hubs from authorities |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Social Network Extraction Pipeline (Block Architecture)

The following block diagram captures the canonical end-to-end pipeline of extracting and analysing a web social network. It has been adapted as a **Mermaid functional flow** since it is a multi-stage data processing pipeline rather than a single physical device.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Data Source] --> A1[Web Crawler and APIs]
    A1 --> A2[Raw Data: HTML, JSON, Logs]
    A2 --> A3[Preprocessing and Cleaning]
    A3 --> A4[Entity Resolution and De-duplication]
    A4 --> A5[Graph Construction: Adjacency Matrix or List]
    A5 --> A6[Storage: Graph DB or Sparse Matrix]
    A6 --> A7[Compute Centrality Metrics]
    A6 --> A8[Compute Network Level Metrics]
    A6 --> A9[Community Detection: Modularity, Louvain]
    A7 --> A10[Visualization: Gephi, D3.js, Cytoscape]
    A8 --> A10
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11[Insight: Influence, Clusters, Anomalies]
```

## 4.2 Example Mini Social Graph

The figure below depicts a small sample web social graph with **5 users** and weighted/hyperlinked relationships. Each user is a node; each friendship is a directed edge. This toy graph will be used as a mental anchor when computing centrality in the worked examples.

```mermaid
graph LR
    n1((U1 Alice)) --- n2((U2 Bob))
    n1 --- n3((U3 Carol))
    n2 --- n3
    n2 --- n4((U4 Dave))
    n3 --- n4
    n3 --- n5((U5 Eve))
    n4 --- n5
    n2 --- n5
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **U3 Carol** has degree 4 (highest) — she is a **hub**.
- **U2 Bob** is the principal **bridge** between the $\{U1,U2,U3\}$ cluster and the $\{U4,U5\}$ cluster — high **betweenness**.
- **U5 Eve** is two hops from everyone — high **closeness**.

## 4.3 HITS Bipartite Reinforcement (Hub ↔ Authority)

The diagram below captures the *mutual reinforcement* principle at the heart of the HITS algorithm. A **good hub** is a page that links to many good authorities; a **good authority** is a page that is linked by many good hubs. The system alternates two passes until convergence.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Hub Set] -- "weighted link" --> A[Authority Set]
    A -- "weighted link" --> H
    H -- "iteration k+1" --> H2[Update h: sum of a]
    A -- "iteration k+1" --> A2[Update a: sum of h]
```

## 4.4 Community Structure (Modular Graph)

A real-world web social network typically exhibits a **modular** community structure. The figure shows three densely connected clusters (Communities I, II, III) with sparse inter-cluster bridges.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph cI[Community I]
        i1((I1)) --- i2((I2))
        i2 --- i3((I3))
        i3 --- i1
        i1 --- i4((I4))
    end
    subgraph cII[Community II]
        j1((J1)) --- j2((J2))
        j2 --- j3((J3))
        j3 --- j1
    end
    subgraph cIII[Community III]
        k1((K1)) --- k2((K2))
        k2 --- k3((K3))
        k3 --- k1
        k1 --- k2
    end
    i3 -- bridge --> j1
    j3 -- bridge --> k2
```

## 4.5 The Random-Surfer / PageRank Intuition

The figure below is a block-level view of a random walk with teleportation. From the current page $p$, the surfer either (with probability $d$) jumps to one of $p$'s out-neighbours, or (with probability $1-d$) teleports to any of the $N$ pages uniformly at random. This is the conceptual core of the PageRank formula derived in Section 3.1.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cur[Current Page p] -- "prob d" --> next[Pick Random Out-link]
    cur -- "prob 1 minus d" --> tele[Teleport to Uniform Random Page]
    next --> newstate[New State p prime]
    tele --> newstate
    newstate --> cur
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking scheme follows the KTU 2024 pattern:** Part A (3-mark short answers, no choice) + Part B (14-mark questions with internal choice, sub-parts typically 7 + 7).

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
Define a **Web Social Network**. With an example, distinguish between an **explicit** and an **implicit** social network.

**Model Answer:**
A web social network is a graph $G = (V, E)$ whose vertices are web actors (people, pages, documents) and whose edges encode a social relation. An **explicit** network is declared by users (Facebook friend, Twitter follow). An **implicit** network is inferred from behavioural data such as email exchanges, co-authorship, or hyperlink co-occurrence — no friend request is ever made.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
List any **three centrality measures** used in social network analysis and state the algorithmic purpose of each in one sentence.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Degree centrality** — counts direct ties; identifies locally popular actors.
2. **Betweenness centrality** — measures brokerage on shortest paths; identifies gatekeepers.
3. **PageRank** — random-walk eigenvector; identifies globally influential nodes, robust to dangling links.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* What is **Betweenness Centrality**? Derive the formula and explain **Brandes' algorithm** for its efficient computation. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* With a clear diagram, explain the **HITS algorithm**. Show the iteration update equations and state the convergence property. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**
- Define $C_B(v) = \sum_{s \neq v \neq t} \dfrac{\sigma_{st}(v)}{\sigma_{st}}$, where $\sigma_{st}$ is the number of shortest paths from $s$ to $t$ and $\sigma_{st}(v)$ is the number of them that pass through $v$.
- Explain that the naive enumeration over all $s,t$ pairs costs $O(n^3)$ on dense graphs.
- **Brandes' algorithm (steps):**
  1. Run a single-source shortest-path (BFS or Dijkstra) from $s$ to obtain $d(s,\cdot)$ and $\sigma_{s\cdot}$.
  2. Initialise the dependency stack: $\delta_{s \cdot s} = 0$.
  3. Process nodes in non-increasing order of $d(s,\cdot)$ from farthest to closest. For each $v$ propagate:
  $$
  \delta_{s \cdot v} = \sum_{w : v \in \text{pred}(w)} \frac{\sigma_{sv}}{\sigma_{sw}} (1 + \delta_{s \cdot w})
  $$
  4. Accumulate: $C_B(v) \mathrel{+}= \delta_{s \cdot v}$.
- Total complexity: $O(nm)$ for unweighted graphs, and $O(nm + n^2 \log n)$ for weighted.

**Model Solution to (b):**
- **Diagram description:** Two disjoint sets — a set of **hubs** and a set of **authorities** — with bipartite edges. Authority $a$ updates from incoming hub weights; hub $h$ updates from outgoing authority weights.
- **Iteration updates:**
$$
\mathbf{a}^{(k+1)} = A^\top \mathbf{h}^{(k)}, \quad
\mathbf{h}^{(k+1)} = A \mathbf{a}^{(k+1)}
$$
followed by $\ell_2$ normalisation of both vectors.
- **Convergence property:** The dominant singular-vector decomposition of $A$. At convergence, $\mathbf{a}$ is the principal eigenvector of $A^\top A$ and $\mathbf{h}$ is the principal eigenvector of $A A^\top$.
- **Engineering utility:** Used by **Kleinberg's HITS** for query-specific web search and by **citation-indexing services**.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* Define **PageRank**. Derive its update equation from the **random surfer model** and explain the role of the **damping factor $d$**. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Define **Clustering Coefficient**. Compute the local clustering coefficient of every node in the graph: $V=\{A,B,C,D,E\}$, $E=\{(A,B),(A,C),(B,C),(B,D),(C,D),(D,E)\}$. Comment on the result. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**
- **Definition:** PageRank $PR(p)$ is the stationary probability that a random surfer is on page $p$ at time $t \to \infty$.
- **Random-walk derivation:**
$$
PR(p) = \frac{1 - d}{N} + d \sum_{q \to p} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}
$$
where $L(q)$ is the out-degree of $q$, and $d$ is the damping factor (typically 0.85).
- **Role of $d$:**
  - Prevents rank sinks (pages with no out-links absorbing all probability mass).
  - Models the user's tendency to "teleport" and start a new search.
  - Guarantees that the underlying stochastic matrix is **primitive**, ensuring unique convergence to a stationary distribution by the **Perron–Frobenius theorem**.

**Model Solution to (b):**
- **Definition:** Local clustering coefficient of node $i$ is
$$
C_i = \frac{2 \, \vert E(N_i) \vert}{k_i(k_i - 1)}
$$
where $E(N_i)$ is the set of edges among the neighbours of $i$, and $k_i$ is the degree of $i$.

- **Computation:**
  - $A$: neighbours $\{B,C\}$, $k_A=2$, edges among $\{B,C\}$: 1 (the edge $(B,C)$). $\Rightarrow C_A = \tfrac{2 \cdot 1}{2 \cdot 1} = 1.00$.
  - $B$: neighbours $\{A,C,D\}$, $k_B=3$, possible edges $= 3$. Existing among them: $(A,C)$ yes, $(A,D)$ no, $(C,D)$ yes $\Rightarrow 2$. $\Rightarrow C_B = \tfrac{2 \cdot 2}{3 \cdot 2} = 0.667$.
  - $C$: neighbours $\{A,B,D\}$, $k_C=3$. Existing: $(A,B)$ yes, $(A,D)$ no, $(B,D)$ yes $\Rightarrow 2$. $\Rightarrow C_C = 0.667$.
  - $D$: neighbours $\{B,C,E\}$, $k_D=3$. Existing: $(B,C)$ yes, $(B,E)$ no, $(C,E)$ no $\Rightarrow 1$. $\Rightarrow C_D = 0.333$.
  - $E$: neighbours $\{D\}$, $k_E=1$. Formula undefined; define $C_E=0$ by convention.
- **Comment:** Nodes $A$ and $B$ form the densest core ($C_A = 1$). Node $D$ is a bridge to the periphery ($C_E$ undefined). The network is not very cohesive overall — average local clustering $\bar C = (1 + 0.667 + 0.667 + 0.333 + 0)/5 = 0.533$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes costing marks in KTU valuation:**
> 1. **Mixing directed and undirected formulas.** PageRank and HITS require **directed** graphs. Degree centrality counts differ.
> 2. **Forgetting normalisation in HITS.** If you omit the $\ell_2$ normalisation step, both vectors diverge — full marks lost.
> 3. **Misnaming $\sigma_{st}$.** It is the *number* of shortest paths, not the length.
> 4. **Confusing *hub* and *authority*.** Hubs **point to** authorities; authorities are **pointed to** by hubs. Many students invert the relationship.
> 5. **Clustering coefficient of an isolated/leaf node** must be defined as **0**, not "undefined" or "1".
> 6. **Damping factor in PageRank** — must mention that $d$ is a teleportation device, **not** a learning rate.
> 7. **Brandes' dependency update** is the most-skipped derivation. Always write the recursion explicitly with $\sigma_{sv}/\sigma_{sw}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Graph model:** $G = (V,E)$ with $n = \vert V \vert$, $m = \vert E \vert$, adjacency matrix $A$.
- **Degree centrality** $C_D(i) = k_i/(n-1)$ is for local popularity.
- **Closeness** $C_C(i) = (n-1)/\sum_j d(i,j)$ — measures global reachability.
- **Betweenness** $C_B(i) = \sum_{s\neq i\neq t} \sigma_{st}(i)/\sigma_{st}$ — measures brokerage.
- **Eigenvector centrality** satisfies $A\mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x}$ — recursive importance.
- **PageRank** uses the **damped random-walk** equation with $d = 0.85$ and an explicit $(1-d)/N$ teleportation term; it converges via **power iteration** to the dominant eigenvector of the Google matrix.
- **HITS** computes **hubs** and **authorities** by alternating $A \mathbf{a}$ and $A^\top \mathbf{h}$ with $\ell_2$ normalisation; convergence is the SVD of $A$.
- **Clustering coefficient** $C_i = 2\vert E(N_i) \vert / [k_i(k_i-1)]$ — measures local triangle density.
- **Network density** $D = 2m/[n(n-1)]$ — global edge-fill.
- **Diameter** = maximum shortest path; **Average path length** = mean of all finite shortest paths.
- **Community detection** methods: modularity optimisation, **Girvan–Newman** (edge betweenness), **Louvain** (greedy modularity), and **spectral clustering**.
- **Implicit vs explicit networks**: explicit = declared, implicit = inferred from behaviour.
- **Brandes' algorithm** reduces betweenness from $O(n^3)$ to $O(nm)$ using a **dependency accumulator** $\delta_{s\cdot v}$.
- **Engineering applications**: search ranking, viral marketing, fraud detection, recommendation systems, epidemiology.
- **Always state assumptions**: directed vs undirected, weighted vs unweighted, presence/absence of self-loops.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources and Types of Data, Key Elements of Web Usage Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT636 - INTRODUCTION TO WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sources and Types of Data, Key Elements of Web Usage Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Web Mining** is the process of applying data mining techniques to discover useful knowledge, patterns, and structures from web-based data sources. Within the KTU 2024 scheme framework, web data is broadly classified into three primary categories: **Web Content Data**, **Web Structure Data**, and **Web Usage Data**. Each category originates from distinct sources on the World Wide Web and requires specialized handling techniques.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sources of Web Data** refer to the physical or logical origins from which raw, unprocessed web information is captured, transmitted, or stored. These include web servers, proxy servers, client browsers, search engines, and enterprise databases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Elements of Web Usage Data** are the specific attributes recorded in server access logs, application logs, and client-side logs. These elements form the atomic units that, when aggregated, reconstruct a user's interaction with a website.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large shopping mall equipped with hundreds of CCTV cameras. Each camera records two things:
- **WHO** entered the mall and which section they visited (analogous to the **User ID** and **Page URL**).
- **WHEN** they arrived at each location (the **Timestamp**).
- **HOW** they got there — through the main gate, side entrance, or from another shop (the **Referrer URL**).

By combining all these recorded fragments, the mall manager can reconstruct the complete shopping path of every customer. This reconstructed path reveals the most popular routes, dead-end sections, and effective product placements.

In the same way, every request made to a web server leaves a small digital footprint. When these footprints are stitched together chronologically, they form a **clickstream** — a digital equivalent of a customer's path through the mall. This is precisely what **Web Usage Mining** aims to analyze.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value / Format |
| :--- | :--- |
| **HTTP Protocol Default Port** | **Port 80 (HTTP)** and **Port 443 (HTTPS)** |
| **IPv4 Address Size** | **32 bits** (e.g., 192.168.1.1) |
| **IPv6 Address Size** | **128 bits** (e.g., 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334) |
| **Standard Log Time Format** | **[DD/Mon/YYYY:HH:MM:SS ±ZZZZ]** |
| **User Agent String Length** | Variable, typically **80–500 bytes** |
| **W3C Extended Log Format** | Industry standard for web server logging |

> [!NOTE]
> The **W3C Extended Log Format (ELF)** is the de-facto standard adopted by virtually all production-grade web servers (Apache, Nginx, IIS) for recording usage data. Understanding its fields is mandatory for KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Web Data Sources

Web data originates from four principal sources, each generating a distinct type of log or dataset.

### 2.1.1 Server-Side Sources

The most reliable and widely used source of web usage data.

- **Web Server Logs** — Recorded by HTTP servers (Apache, Nginx, IIS) for every incoming request.
- **Application Server Logs** — Captured at the business-logic tier (e.g., Tomcat, JBoss), often containing session-level information.
- **Database Logs** — Transactional records showing actual purchases, searches, and form submissions.

### 2.1.2 Client-Side Sources

- **Browser Cookies** — Small text files storing user preferences and session identifiers.
- **JavaScript / AJAX Calls** — Modern frameworks (React, Angular) send asynchronous events that can be captured.
- **Browser Local Storage and Session Storage** — HTML5 storage mechanisms.

### 2.1.3 Proxy-Level Sources

- **Proxy Server Logs** — Record requests passing through intermediate caching servers; useful for analyzing traffic from multiple users behind a single corporate gateway.

### 2.1.4 External / Application-Specific Sources

- **Search Engine Query Logs** — Captured by search providers for SEO and ranking analysis.
- **E-commerce Transaction Logs** — Contain cart additions, checkouts, and payment status.

## 2.2 The Three Fundamental Types of Web Data

| Type | Description | Typical Source | Example Mining Task |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Web Content Data** | The actual data published on web pages — text, images, videos, audio, structured records. | HTML pages, XML, RSS feeds, multimedia repositories. | Text classification, sentiment analysis, image mining. |
| **Web Structure Data** | The organization of web pages through hyperlinks, sitemaps, and DOM trees. | Hyperlink graph, sitemap.xml, RDF triples. | PageRank computation, community detection. |
| **Web Usage Data** | Records of user interactions with the website — clicks, navigation, transactions. | Server access logs, proxy logs, client cookies. | Clickstream analysis, recommendation, personalization. |

## 2.3 Key Elements of Web Usage Data

The following elements constitute the **core schema** of web usage records.

### 2.3.1 IP Address

- **Definition:** A unique numerical label assigned to every device on a network.
- **Purpose in Mining:** Acts as the *primary identifier* of a visiting machine.
- **Limitation:** Multiple users may share an IP (NAT, corporate proxies), and a single user may have a dynamic IP across sessions.

### 2.3.2 User ID (Authentication ID)

- **Definition:** A unique identifier provided by the user upon login (e.g., email, username).
- **Purpose in Mining:** Provides *exact user-level identification* when available.
- **Limitation:** Most web users browse anonymously; only a small fraction log in.

### 2.3.3 Session ID

- **Definition:** A temporary identifier (often stored as a cookie) that links all page requests of a single visit.
- **Purpose in Mining:** Enables **session reconstruction** — grouping all activities by one user during one visit.
- **Mechanism:** Generated by the server or set by a session-management framework.

### 2.3.4 Request URL (Uniform Resource Locator)

- **Definition:** The full address of the resource requested by the client.
- **Purpose in Mining:** Indicates *which content* the user is accessing.
- **Example:** `/products/electronics/laptop123`

### 2.3.5 Timestamp

- **Definition:** The precise date and time when the request was received.
- **Purpose in Mining:** Establishes the **chronological order** of navigation.
- **Format:** `DD/Mon/YYYY:HH:MM:SS ±ZZZZ` in W3C ELF.

### 2.3.6 HTTP Method

- **Definition:** The action verb (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) used in the request.
- **Purpose in Mining:** Distinguishes page views (GET) from form submissions (POST).

### 2.3.7 Status Code

- **Definition:** A 3-digit HTTP response code (e.g., 200 OK, 404 Not Found, 500 Server Error).
- **Purpose in Mining:** Filters out **failed requests** during preprocessing.

### 2.3.8 Bytes Transferred

- **Definition:** The size of the response payload in bytes.
- **Purpose in Mining:** Helps estimate bandwidth and detect multimedia downloads.

### 2.3.9 Referrer URL

- **Definition:** The URL of the previous page that linked to the current request.
- **Purpose in Mining:** Constructs the **referral graph** and identifies traffic sources (search engines, ads, direct).

### 2.3.10 User Agent String

- **Definition:** An identification string sent by the browser containing OS, browser version, and device type.
- **Purpose in Mining:** Enables **device-aware personalization** and bot filtering.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Metric | Formula or Representation | Boundary Condition / Note |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Total Log Entries** | $N = \sum_{i=1}^{M} n_i$ where $n_i$ is requests for user $i$ | $N \geq 0$ |
| **Unique User Count (IP-based)** | $U = \vert \text{set}(\text{IP}) \vert$ | Cardinality of distinct IP addresses |
| **Session Duration** | $D_{session} = t_{last} - t_{first}$ | Measured in **seconds** |
| **Average Page View Time** | $\bar{T}_{page} = \frac{D_{session}}{P - 1}$ where $P$ is number of pages in session | $P \geq 2$ |
| **Bounce Rate** | $B\% = \frac{\text{Sessions with 1 page}}{U} \times 100$ | Expressed as percentage |
| **Click-Through Rate (CTR)** | $CTR = \frac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 100$ | Web analytics metric |
| **HTTP Status Class** | $1xx = \text{Info}, \; 2xx = \text{Success}, \; 3xx = \text{Redirect}, \; 4xx = \text{Client Error}, \; 5xx = \text{Server Error}$ | Standard RFC 7231 classification |
| **Time Threshold for Sessionization** | $T_{timeout} = 30 \text{ minutes}$ (industry default) | Configurable parameter |
| **W3C ELF Field Order** | $\text{IP} \to \text{ID} \to \text{User} \to \text{Time} \to \text{Request} \to \text{Status} \to \text{Size} \to \text{Referrer} \to \text{Agent}$ | W3C Working Draft WD-logfile-960323 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-commerce Personalization** — Companies like **Amazon** analyze clickstream data to recommend products in real time.
- **Web Security** — Anomalous access patterns (sudden 404 spikes, unusual referrers) trigger **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)**.
- **SEO and Marketing** — Search engine marketing teams use referrer data to optimize **cost-per-click (CPC)** campaigns.
- **Capacity Planning** — Bytes transferred and request frequency help engineers provision **CDN bandwidth** and **load balancers**.
- **A/B Testing** — Usage logs drive controlled experiments to improve **conversion rates**.
