# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Standard W3C Extended Log Format (ELF) — Structural Analysis

The W3C Extended Log Format is the foundational data structure for web usage records. Each line in an access log is a single HTTP transaction, encoded as a sequence of whitespace-separated fields.

### 3.1.1 Formal Line Structure

$$
\text{LogEntry} = \{\text{field}_1, \text{field}_2, \ldots, \text{field}_n\}
$$

Each $\text{field}_i$ is a tuple of $(k_i, v_i)$ where $k_i$ is the field name declared in the log header and $v_i$ is the recorded value for that transaction.

### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Field Walkthrough

Consider the following concrete log entry from an Apache server:

```
192.168.1.105 - - [15/Jan/2025:14:23:11 +0530] "GET /products/laptop.html HTTP/1.1" 200 5420 "https://www.google.com/" "Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0)"
```

**Step 1 — Extract the IP Address**

$$
\text{IP} = 192.168.1.105
$$

This is the source machine's network address. In an IPv6 deployment, this field expands to a 128-bit hexadecimal value.

**Step 2 — Extract the Identity Fields**

The two hyphens following the IP represent the **ident** and **user** fields as defined by the Common Log Format (CLF) — both are empty in this entry, indicating an anonymous visitor.

$$
\text{Ident} = \varnothing, \quad \text{User} = \varnothing
$$

**Step 3 — Parse the Timestamp**

$$
\text{Timestamp} = [\text{15/Jan/2025:14:23:11} \; +\text{0530}]
$$

This represents **15 January 2025, 2:23:11 PM IST** (UTC+5:30). The timezone offset **+0530** is mandatory in W3C ELF.

**Step 4 — Parse the Request Line**

$$
\text{Request} = \text{GET} \; \text{/products/laptop.html} \; \text{HTTP/1.1}
$$

This tuple contains:
- **Method** = `GET` (retrieval action).
- **URL** = `/products/laptop.html` (the resource).
- **Protocol** = `HTTP/1.1` (the transport version).

**Step 5 — Extract the Status Code**

$$
\text{Status} = 200 \implies \text{Successful retrieval.}
$$

**Step 6 — Extract the Bytes Transferred**

$$
\text{Bytes} = 5420
$$

**Step 7 — Extract the Referrer**

$$
\text{Referrer} = \text{https://www.google.com/}
$$

The user arrived at this page from a Google search result.

**Step 8 — Extract the User Agent**

$$
\text{UserAgent} = \text{Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0)}
$$

Indicates a **Windows 10** desktop browser session.

### 3.1.3 Aggregated Log Structure

$$
\text{ServerLog} = \begin{bmatrix}
\text{LogEntry}_1 \\
\text{LogEntry}_2 \\
\vdots \\
\text{LogEntry}_N
\end{bmatrix}
$$

This matrix representation allows downstream mining algorithms to operate on structured rows.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Data Type vs. Mining Application

| Data Type | Source Component | Mining Technique | Outcome |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Web Content** | HTML body, images | Text mining, NLP, clustering | Topic discovery, sentiment |
| **Web Structure** | Hyperlinks, DOM | Graph algorithms (PageRank, HITS) | Authority ranking |
| **Web Usage** | Server access logs | Sequential pattern mining, clustering | User behavior profiling |
| **User Profile** | Registration forms, cookies | Classification, collaborative filtering | Personalized recommendations |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Deriving the Sessionization Threshold

**Problem Statement:** Given a sorted sequence of page-view timestamps for a user, determine the session boundary using a timeout $T = 30$ minutes.

Let the timestamp sequence be:

$$
T = \{t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4, t_5\} \quad \text{where} \quad t_i < t_{i+1}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute consecutive time deltas.**

$$
\Delta_i = t_{i+1} - t_i \quad \text{for} \quad i = 1, 2, 3, 4
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the sessionization rule.**

$$
\text{SessionBoundary}(i) = \begin{cases}
\text{True} & \text{if } \Delta_i > T \\
\text{False} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Construct the session partition.**

If a boundary is detected at index $k$, then all timestamps $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_k$ form Session 1, and $t_{k+1}, \ldots$ begin Session 2.

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Suppose:

$$
T = \{10{:}00, \; 10{:}05, \; 10{:}20, \; 11{:}00, \; 11{:}10\}
$$

Computing the deltas:

$$
\Delta_1 = 5 \text{ min}, \quad \Delta_2 = 15 \text{ min}, \quad \Delta_3 = 40 \text{ min}, \quad \Delta_4 = 10 \text{ min}
$$

Applying the threshold $T = 30$ minutes:

$$
\Delta_1 < T, \quad \Delta_2 < T, \quad \Delta_3 > T \quad \text{(Boundary!)}, \quad \Delta_4 < T
$$

Resulting sessions:

$$
\text{Session}_1 = \{10{:}00, \; 10{:}05, \; 10{:}20\}, \quad \text{Session}_2 = \{11{:}00, \; 11{:}10\}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute session durations.**

$$
D_{\text{Session}_1} = 10{:}20 - 10{:}00 = 20 \text{ min}
$$
$$
D_{\text{Session}_2} = 11{:}10 - 11{:}00 = 10 \text{ min}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Web Data Sources

| Source | Data Granularity | User Identifiability | Privacy Risk | Reliability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Web Server Log** | Per HTTP request | Low–Medium (IP-based) | Medium | **High** |
| **Application Server Log** | Per business event | High (Session ID) | Medium | **Very High** |
| **Proxy Server Log** | Per cached request | Low (multiple users) | Low | Medium |
| **Client-Side Cookie** | Per browser session | Medium | High | Medium |
| **Database Transaction Log** | Per SQL operation | Very High (User ID) | High | **Very High** |
| **Search Engine Query Log** | Per query | High | Very High | High |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> List and briefly explain any **three sources** from which web usage data can be collected.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Web Server Logs:** Every HTTP request handled by the web server (Apache, Nginx) is recorded with details such as IP address, timestamp, requested URL, and status code. This is the most widely used source due to its reliability and standardization via the W3C Extended Log Format.

2. **Application Server Logs:** These logs are generated by the application tier (e.g., Tomcat, JBoss) and capture business-level events such as user sessions, form submissions, and database queries. They offer higher user identifiability than raw web server logs.

3. **Client-Side Cookies:** Small text files stored in the user's browser that maintain state across HTTP requests. Cookies can uniquely identify a returning visitor and are essential for session reconstruction in e-commerce and personalized portals.

*(Alternate valid answers: Proxy Server Logs, Database Transaction Logs, Search Engine Query Logs.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> What are the **key elements of web usage data**? Explain the significance of **Timestamp** and **Referrer URL** in clickstream analysis.

**Model Answer:**

The key elements of web usage data are: **IP Address, User ID, Session ID, Request URL, Timestamp, HTTP Method, Status Code, Bytes Transferred, Referrer URL, and User Agent.**

- **Timestamp:** Establishes the **chronological order** of page views. It enables **session identification** (using a 30-minute timeout) and the computation of metrics such as time-on-page and session duration.

- **Referrer URL:** Indicates the **previous page** the user was on before reaching the current resource. It is used to reconstruct the **navigation path**, identify **traffic sources** (search engines, advertisements, external links), and compute the **referral graph** for structural mining of user flows.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**
> With a neat diagram, explain the **three main types of web data** — Content, Structure, and Usage. For each type, provide one real-world example of a mining task. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The three main types of web data are:

**1. Web Content Data**
- **Definition:** The actual information embedded in web pages — text, images, audio, video, and structured records.
- **Sources:** HTML pages, XML feeds, RSS, multimedia servers.
- **Mining Example:** Sentiment analysis of product reviews on Amazon using NLP techniques to classify reviews as positive, neutral, or negative.

**2. Web Structure Data**
- **Definition:** The topological organization of the web, represented as a graph where nodes are pages and edges are hyperlinks.
- **Sources:** Hyperlink graph, sitemap.xml, RDF triples.
- **Mining Example:** Computing the **PageRank** score to identify authoritative pages, as originally formulated by Brin and Page at Google.

**3. Web Usage Data**
- **Definition:** Records of how users interact with the website — clicks, navigation paths, transactions.
- **Sources:** Server access logs, proxy logs, application logs.
- **Mining Example:** Sequential pattern mining of clickstream data to discover that users who view `/laptops` frequently purchase `/laptop-bag` within the same session — enabling cross-selling recommendations.

```
        WEB DATA
        /   |   \
       /    |    \
      V     V     V
  Content  Structure  Usage
    |        |         |
  Text     Links    Clickstream
  Images   Sitemap  Sessions
  Video    DOM tree Transactions
```

**Significance:** A holistic web mining system integrates all three types — for example, a search engine uses **structure** (PageRank) to rank results, **content** (indexing) to match queries, and **usage** (click logs) to refine future rankings.

---

> **Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Explain the **W3C Extended Log Format (ELF)** with a sample log entry. Identify and justify the role of **at least six fields** in usage data preprocessing. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**W3C Extended Log Format** is a standardized, text-based logging format declared by a header directive (`%ExtendedFormat`) followed by newline-separated transaction records.

**Sample Log Entry:**

```
203.45.67.89 - frank [12/Mar/2025:09:14:22 +0000] "POST /login HTTP/1.1" 302 512 "https://www.example.com/home" "Mozilla/5.0"
```

**Field-by-Field Justification:**

| # | Field | Sample Value | Role in Preprocessing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **IP Address** | 203.45.67.89 | Primary key for user identification when no User ID is available. |
| 2 | **User ID** | frank | Provides exact user-level identification (login-based). |
| 3 | **Timestamp** | 12/Mar/2025:09:14:22 +0000 | Sorts requests chronologically; enables sessionization using a 30-minute timeout rule. |
| 4 | **Request Method** | POST | Distinguishes passive page views (GET) from active form submissions (POST). |
| 5 | **URL** | /login | Identifies the specific resource accessed — essential for content categorization. |
| 6 | **Status Code** | 302 | Filters out failed or redirected requests during data cleaning (only 2xx are retained for analysis). |
| 7 | **Referrer** | https://www.example.com/home | Traces the navigation path and identifies the entry point of the session. |
| 8 | **User Agent** | Mozilla/5.0 | Enables bot filtering and device-based segmentation. |

**Conclusion:** The W3C ELF provides a complete, structured snapshot of every HTTP transaction, forming the **raw input** to all downstream web usage mining algorithms.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**
> Compare the **three primary sources of web usage data** — Web Server Logs, Proxy Server Logs, and Client-Side Cookies — across the dimensions of **data granularity, user identifiability, privacy risk, and reliability**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | Web Server Log | Proxy Server Log | Client-Side Cookie |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Data Granularity** | Per HTTP request (fine-grained). | Per cached request (medium-grained). | Per browser session (coarse-grained). |
| **User Identifiability** | Low–Medium (IP + User Agent). | Low (multiple users behind one IP). | Medium (unique cookie ID). |
| **Privacy Risk** | Medium (PII leakage if logged). | Low (anonymized). | High (cross-site tracking). |
| **Reliability** | High (standardized W3C ELF). | Medium (depends on proxy config). | Medium (users can clear cookies). |
| **Coverage** | Server-side only. | All traffic through proxy. | Single browser instance. |
| **Ease of Collection** | Easy (built-in). | Moderate (proxy config required). | Moderate (JS / SDK needed). |

**Analytical Insight:**
- **Web Server Logs** are best for **technical analytics** (404 detection, load patterns).
- **Proxy Server Logs** are best for **network-level traffic analysis** (bandwidth optimization).
- **Client-Side Cookies** are best for **personalization and retargeting** (Google Ads, Facebook Pixel).

---

> **Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected]**
> Describe the **preprocessing steps** required to transform raw web usage data into structured sessions suitable for pattern mining. Illustrate with a numerical example of **session identification** using the 30-minute timeout rule. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Web usage preprocessing involves five sequential steps:

**Step 1 — Data Cleaning**
Remove irrelevant entries:
- Requests with status codes $\geq 400$ (errors).
- Requests for image, CSS, and JavaScript files (non-content).
- Bot traffic identified by User Agent (e.g., `Googlebot`, `AhrefsBot`).

**Step 2 — User Identification**
Assign a unique user identifier using:
- The **User ID** if logged in, otherwise
- The combination of **IP Address + User Agent** as a heuristic.

**Step 3 — Session Identification**
For each identified user, sort all page requests chronologically. Apply the **30-minute timeout rule**: if the time gap between two consecutive requests exceeds 30 minutes, a new session begins.

**Numerical Example:**

Consider the following six page views for a single user (IP = 192.168.1.10):

| # | Timestamp | Page |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 09:00:00 | /home |
| 2 | 09:08:00 | /products |
| 3 | 09:15:00 | /products/laptop |
| 4 | 09:55:00 | /cart |
| 5 | 10:01:00 | /checkout |
| 6 | 10:10:00 | /order-confirm |

**Computing consecutive time deltas:**

$$
\Delta_{1,2} = 8 \text{ min}, \quad \Delta_{2,3} = 7 \text{ min}, \quad \Delta_{3,4} = 40 \text{ min}, \quad \Delta_{4,5} = 6 \text{ min}, \quad \Delta_{5,6} = 9 \text{ min}
$$

**Applying the 30-minute threshold:** A session boundary is detected between requests **3 and 4** (since $40 > 30$).

**Resulting Sessions:**

$$
\text{Session}_1 = \{/home, /products, /products/laptop\}, \quad D_1 = 15 \text{ min}
$$
$$
\text{Session}_2 = \{/cart, /checkout, /order-confirm\}, \quad D_2 = 15 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4 — Path Completion**
If a request lacks a referrer but is reachable from a known page in the site topology, insert the missing page using backtracking.

**Step 5 — Transaction Identification**
Group pages within each session into logical transactions using methods such as **Maximal Forward Reference (MFR)** or **Reference Length**.

**Output:** A structured transaction database ready for **sequential pattern mining (e.g., Apriori, PrefixSpan)** or **clustering (e.g., K-Means, DBSCAN)**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the three data types.** Students often mix up "Web Content" and "Web Structure." Remember: **content is WHAT is on the page, structure is HOW pages are linked.**
>
> 2. **Omitting field meanings.** When asked about log elements, merely listing them scores 1 mark. You **MUST explain the role** of each in preprocessing to score full marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the 30-minute sessionization rule.** This is the **default industry threshold** (per W3C and Cooley et al., 1999). Writing a different value without justification will be penalized.
>
> 4. **Ignoring status code filtering.** During data cleaning, students often forget to **remove 4xx and 5xx responses** — this is a critical preprocessing step.
>
> 5. **Missing the referrer's role.** Many students treat the referrer as optional. In KTU valuations, the referrer is considered **mandatory** for path completion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sources and Types of Web Data, Key Elements of Web Usage Data**

- **Web Data is classified into THREE types:** Content, Structure, and Usage. *(Do not confuse with data sources.)*

- **Five primary data sources:** Web Server, Application Server, Proxy Server, Client-Side, External (search/transaction).

- **Web Content Data** = the data inside web pages (text, images, video).
- **Web Structure Data** = the hyperlink graph connecting pages.
- **Web Usage Data** = records of user interactions (clicks, navigation).

- **Key Elements of Web Usage Data (10 fields):** IP, User ID, Session ID, URL, Timestamp, HTTP Method, Status Code, Bytes, Referrer, User Agent.

- **W3C Extended Log Format (ELF)** is the industry standard for web server logging.

- **Preprocessing Pipeline:** Cleaning → User ID → Session ID → Path Completion → Transaction ID.

- **Session Identification Rule:** 30-minute timeout (industry default).

- **Clickstream** = the chronological sequence of pages visited by a user within a session.

- **IP Address** alone is NOT sufficient for user identification due to NAT and dynamic addressing.

- **Referrer URL** is critical for **path reconstruction** and **traffic source analysis**.

- **Status Codes:** 2xx = success (keep), 3xx = redirect (optional), 4xx/5xx = error (remove during cleaning).

- **Privacy Concern:** Client-side cookies raise GDPR / DPDP Act compliance issues — anonymization is mandatory.

- **Real-World Tools:** AWStats, Webalizer (log analyzers); Google Analytics (JS-based); ELK Stack (enterprise log mining).

- **Examiner Tip:** Always include a **diagram** (flowchart or hierarchy) when explaining data types or preprocessing — it earns 1–2 extra marks in KTU 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Modelling for Web Usage Mining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT636 - INTRODUCTION TO WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Modelling for Web Usage Mining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Modelling for Web Usage Mining** is the systematic phase in the Web Usage Mining (WUM) pipeline in which the preprocessed, cleaned, and integrated web log data is transformed into structured mathematical and abstract representations that enable the application of pattern discovery algorithms. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, this stage bridges the raw data preparation phase and the pattern discovery phase by creating abstractions such as **user session vectors**, **transactional databases**, **pageview matrices**, and **graph-based models** over which algorithms like Apriori, k-Means, Markov chains, and sequential pattern miners can be executed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board-Relevant):** Data Modelling is the process of converting preprocessed web usage data into abstract data structures (relational tables, matrices, sequences, graphs) that can be mined to discover usage patterns, user behaviors, and navigational trends.

The three primary sub-activities of data modelling in WUM are:

1. **Pageview Identification and Encoding** – assigning unique IDs to each accessed resource.
2. **User/Session Modelling** – constructing data structures representing user interactions.
3. **Application of Mining Algorithms** – running statistical, clustering, classification, association, or sequential pattern algorithms on these structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU module explicitly tests the difference between *transactional models*, *session-based models*, and *graph-based models*, and the algorithms that operate on each.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **massive grocery store** that records every item a customer touches, picks up, examines, or buys. The raw data is a chaotic firehose — millions of receipts, sensor taps, and barcode scans. Before the store manager can ask *"What products are usually bought together?"* or *"Which customers are similar?"*, the data must be organized into:

- A **catalog** (the set of items / pageviews),
- A **basket list per visit** (a *session vector*), and
- A **customer loyalty database** (a *user profile matrix*).

**Data Modelling is exactly this "catalog-and-basket" construction for the web.** Each URL becomes an item, each click-session becomes a basket, and the entire website becomes a warehouse. Once the data is modeled this way, algorithms like *Apriori* and *k-Means* can be applied as easily as they would be on a retail dataset.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Constants

- **Support Threshold (min\_sup):** the minimum fraction of transactions that must contain an itemset for it to be considered *frequent*. Typical range: **0.01 – 0.05**.
- **Confidence Threshold (min\_conf):** the minimum conditional probability required for an association rule. Typical range: **0.5 – 0.9**.
- **Number of Clusters (k):** a hyperparameter that must be chosen *a priori*; commonly tuned via the **Elbow Method** or **Silhouette Score**.
- **Dimensionality of session vector (d):** the number of unique pageviews in the cleaned corpus; often reduced via **TF-IDF** or **SVD**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inverted Index Property:** A session vector of length $d$ is stored in a sparse format because, on average, a user visits only **2%–5%** of the total pages per session.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Data Modelling Pipeline (Operational Breakdown)

The modelling phase is broken into the following ordered steps:

1. **Pageview Identification**
   - The URL is parsed; query strings and session IDs are stripped; dynamic parameters are normalized.
   - Each unique normalized URL is assigned a **pageview ID** $p_i \in P = \{p_1, p_2, \dots, p_d\}$.

2. **User Identification**
   - Combine IP address + Agent + Cookie to form a **user key** $u_k$.

3. **Session Identification (Sessionization)**
   - Group pageviews of user $u_k$ into sessions $S_{k,j}$ using a **30-minute inactivity timeout** (the de-facto industry standard, proposed by Catledge & Pitkow, 1995).

4. **Path Completion**
   - Infer missing pageviews using referrer logs and the website's link topology graph $G = (V, E)$.

5. **Session Encoding**
   - Convert each session into a vector $\mathbf{s} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ (or a sequence over $P$).

6. **Episode / Transaction Formation**
   - Decide whether to mine at the *pageview*, *session*, or *episode* (sub-session) granularity.

7. **Pattern Discovery**
   - Apply one of: **Statistical Analysis, Association Rules, Clustering, Classification, Sequential Patterns, or Dependency Modelling.**

## 2.2 The Two Major Modelling Paradigms

### A) Transactional / Set-based Model
The session is treated as a **bag of pageviews** — order is ignored. Suitable for *market-basket style* analysis.

### B) Sequence / Graph-based Model
The session is treated as an **ordered list** of pageviews. Suitable for *Markov chains*, *longest-common-subsequence* analysis, and *navigation prediction*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Questions often ask students to compare the transactional and sequence-based models and identify which algorithm is appropriate for each.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---------|---------|----------|----------------------|
| 1 | Support of itemset $X$ | $ \text{sup}(X) = \dfrac{\vert \{ T \in \mathcal{D} : X \subseteq T \} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{D} \vert} $ | $\mathcal{D}$ = database, $T$ = transaction | $0 \le \text{sup} \le 1$ |
| 2 | Confidence of rule $X \Rightarrow Y$ | $ \text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\text{sup}(X \cup Y)}{\text{sup}(X)} $ | Conditional probability | $0 \le \text{conf} \le 1$ |
| 3 | Lift of rule $X \Rightarrow Y$ | $ \text{lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y)}{\text{sup}(Y)} = \dfrac{\text{sup}(X \cup Y)}{\text{sup}(X)\,\text{sup}(Y)} $ | Correlation measure | $\text{lift} > 1$ = positive |
| 4 | Euclidean Distance | $ d_{E}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d}(x_i - y_i)^2} $ | L2 norm | $\ge 0$ |
| 5 | Cosine Similarity | $ \text{sim}_{cos}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \dfrac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert_2 \cdot \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert_2} $ | Session vectors | $[-1, 1]$ |
| 6 | TF-IDF Weight | $ w_{ij} = \text{tf}_{ij} \cdot \log\!\left( \dfrac{N}{\text{df}_j} \right) $ | $N$ = # sessions, $\text{df}_j$ = doc freq | $\ge 0$ |
| 7 | Entropy of usage | $ H = -\sum_{i=1}^{d} p_i \log_2 p_i $ | $p_i$ = visit probability | $0 \le H \le \log_2 d$ |
| 8 | Markov Transition Prob. | $ P(p_j \mid p_i) = \dfrac{\text{count}(p_i \to p_j)}{\text{count}(p_i)} $ | Navigation prediction | $0 \le P \le 1$, $\sum_j P = 1$ |
| 9 | Silhouette Score | $ s(i) = \dfrac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}} $ | Clustering validation | $[-1, 1]$ |
| 10 | Inactivity Timeout | $ \Delta t_{\text{timeout}} $ | Session break threshold | **30 minutes** (standard) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** All probability operators satisfy the **Axioms of Probability** — non-negativity, normalization, and countable additivity. $\sum P = 1$ is therefore a *consistency check* in any derivation.

## 2.4 Why These Models Matter in Engineering

- **E-commerce recommendation systems** rely on *transactional* association-rule models.
- **Adaptive websites & prefetching** engines rely on *Markov-chain* sequence models to predict the *next* pageview.
- **Personalized search engines** use *user-session clustering* to build demographic behavioral profiles.
- **Web server load balancing** uses *transition probability matrices* to pre-warm caches.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Session Logs to Support/Confidence

Consider a small, worked-out database with $N = 5$ sessions:

| Session ID | Pageviews Visited (set form) |
|------------|------------------------------|
| $S_1$ | $\{A, B, C\}$ |
| $S_2$ | $\{A, B\}$ |
| $S_3$ | $\{A, C\}$ |
| $S_4$ | $\{B, C\}$ |
| $S_5$ | $\{A, B, C\}$ |

### Step 1 — Count support of each 1-itemset

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{sup}(\{A\}) &= \frac{\#\{S : A \in S\}}{N} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80 \\[4pt]
\text{sup}(\{B\}) &= \frac{3}{5} = 0.60 \\[4pt]
\text{sup}(\{C\}) &= \frac{4}{5} = 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Count support of the 2-itemset $\{A, B\}$

Sessions containing **both** $A$ and $B$ are $S_1, S_2, S_5$ → 3 sessions.

$$
\text{sup}(\{A,B\}) = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the confidence of the rule $A \Rightarrow B$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{conf}(A \Rightarrow B) &= \frac{\text{sup}(A \cup B)}{\text{sup}(A)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3/5}{4/5} = \frac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the lift

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{lift}(A \Rightarrow B) &= \frac{\text{sup}(A \cup B)}{\text{sup}(A) \cdot \text{sup}(B)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.60}{0.80 \times 0.60} = \frac{0.60}{0.48} = 1.25
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Since $\text{lift} = 1.25 > 1$, the rule $A \Rightarrow B$ shows **positive correlation** — users who visit $A$ are 25% more likely to also visit $B$ than a random user.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: TF-IDF Weighting for Pageview Vectors

A session vector $\mathbf{s}_i$ assigns a weight $w_{ij}$ to each pageview $p_j$. Raw counts are biased toward popular pages; we apply **TF-IDF** to suppress this bias.

### Step 1 — Term Frequency

$$
\text{tf}_{ij} = \frac{f_{ij}}{\sum_{k} f_{ik}}
$$

where $f_{ij}$ is the number of times page $p_j$ appears in session $\mathbf{s}_i$.

### Step 2 — Inverse Document Frequency

$$
\text{idf}_j = \log\!\left(\frac{N}{\text{df}_j}\right)
$$

where $N$ is the total number of sessions and $\text{df}_j$ is the number of sessions containing $p_j$.

### Step 3 — TF-IDF

$$
w_{ij} = \text{tf}_{ij} \cdot \text{idf}_j
$$

### Worked Example

Suppose $N = 1000$ sessions, and page $p_1$ appears in 5 sessions, and is visited 3 times in a particular session with 10 total pageviews.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{tf}_{1} &= \frac{3}{10} = 0.30 \\[4pt]
\text{idf}_{1} &= \log\!\left(\frac{1000}{5}\right) = \log(200) \approx 2.301 \quad (\log_{10}) \\[4pt]
w_{1} &= 0.30 \times 2.301 = 0.6903
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cosine Similarity Between Two Sessions

Given two TF-IDF session vectors $\mathbf{x} = (1, 0, 2, 1)$ and $\mathbf{y} = (0, 1, 1, 1)$:

### Step 1 — Compute dot product

$$
\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y} = (1)(0) + (0)(1) + (2)(1) + (1)(1) = 0 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 3
$$

### Step 2 — Compute norms

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert_2 &= \sqrt{1^2 + 0^2 + 2^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{6} \approx 2.449 \\[4pt]
\Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert_2 &= \sqrt{0^2 + 1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute similarity

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{sim}_{cos}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) &= \frac{3}{\sqrt{6} \cdot \sqrt{3}} = \frac{3}{\sqrt{18}} = \frac{3}{3\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A value of **0.707** indicates that the two sessions share substantial navigational overlap — useful for user-based collaborative filtering.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Markov-Chain Transition Probability for Navigation Prediction

Let the navigation history of all sessions be aggregated into a transition count matrix $C$:

| From \ To | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|
| $A$ | 0 | 5 | 3 |
| $B$ | 4 | 0 | 2 |
| $C$ | 1 | 6 | 0 |

### Step 1 — Compute the row sums (total outgoing transitions)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{count}(A \to \cdot) &= 0 + 5 + 3 = 8 \\
\text{count}(B \to \cdot) &= 4 + 0 + 2 = 6 \\
\text{count}(C \to \cdot) &= 1 + 6 + 0 = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Normalize each row to obtain $P(p_j \mid p_i)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(A \mid A) &= 0/8 = 0 \\
P(B \mid A) &= 5/8 = 0.625 \\
P(C \mid A) &= 3/8 = 0.375
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(A \mid B) &= 4/6 \approx 0.667 \\
P(B \mid B) &= 0/6 = 0 \\
P(C \mid B) &= 2/6 \approx 0.333
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(A \mid C) &= 1/7 \approx 0.143 \\
P(B \mid C) &= 6/7 \approx 0.857 \\
P(C \mid C) &= 0/7 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Prediction

If a user is currently on page $A$, the **next-page prediction** is the $\arg\max$ of the row:

$$
\hat{p}_{\text{next}} = \arg\max_j P(p_j \mid A) = B
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This transition matrix is the **data model** consumed by *adaptive website* engines and *prefetching* systems.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Entropy of a Page's Visit Distribution

Suppose a page is visited 1000 times in a week: 500 via Google, 300 direct, 200 via referral.

### Step 1 — Compute probabilities

$$
p_1 = 0.50, \quad p_2 = 0.30, \quad p_3 = 0.20
$$

### Step 2 — Verify normalization

$$
\sum_i p_i = 0.50 + 0.30 + 0.20 = 1.00 \;\;\checkmark
$$

### Step 3 — Compute entropy

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= -\sum_{i=1}^{3} p_i \log_2 p_i \\[4pt]
&= -(0.50 \log_2 0.50 + 0.30 \log_2 0.30 + 0.20 \log_2 0.20) \\[4pt]
&= -(0.50 \cdot (-1) + 0.30 \cdot (-1.737) + 0.20 \cdot (-2.322)) \\[4pt]
&= -(-0.500 - 0.521 - 0.464) \\[4pt]
&= 1.485 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Max possible entropy** for 3 sources is $\log_2 3 \approx 1.585$ bits. Our $H = 1.485$ bits indicates a *fairly uniform* traffic mix.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Data Modelling Pipeline

The diagram below captures the full transition from raw logs to a model-ready dataset, with the **Data Modelling stage** highlighted as the central focus of this module.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Web Logs] --> A1[Cleaning and Preprocessing]
    A1 --> A2[Pageview Identification]
    A2 --> A3[User Identification]
    A3 --> A4[Session Identification 30 min timeout]
    A4 --> A5[Path Completion using Referrer Graph]
    A5 --> B0[Data Modelling Phase]

    B0 --> B1[Transactional Model Bag of Pages]
    B0 --> B2[Sequence Model Ordered Pageview List]
    B0 --> B3[Graph Model Navigation Markov Chain]

    B1 --> C1[Association Rule Mining Apriori]
    B2 --> C2[Sequential Pattern Mining]
    B2 --> C3[Markov Chain Prediction]
    B3 --> C3
    B2 --> C4[Longest Common Subsequence]

    C1 --> D0[Discovered Patterns]
    C2 --> D0
    C3 --> D0
    C4 --> D0
    D0 --> E0[Knowledge for Personalization and Prefetching]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of the Data Modelling Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INP[Input Layer]
        P1[Cleaned Session Set S]
        P2[Site Topology Graph G]
        P3[User Profile DB]
    end

    subgraph MOD[Data Modelling Engine]
        M1[Vectorizer TF-IDF]
        M2[Transaction Builder]
        M3[Sequence Builder]
        M4[Markov Builder]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Model Output]
        N1[Session Matrix X]
        N2[Transactional DB T]
        N3[Sequence DB Q]
        N4[Transition Matrix P]
    end

    subgraph ALG[Algorithm Layer]
        L1[Apriori]
        L2[PrefixSpan]
        L3[k-Means]
        L4[Page Rank on Graph]
    end

    P1 --> M1
    P1 --> M2
    P1 --> M3
    P1 --> M4
    P2 --> M4
    P3 --> M1

    M1 --> N1
    M2 --> N2
    M3 --> N3
    M4 --> N4

    N1 --> L3
    N2 --> L1
    N3 --> L2
    N4 --> L4
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Mapping of the Three Models

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TM[Transactional Model]
        TM1[Session S1: Page A, B, C as set]
        TM2[Session S2: Page A, B as set]
        TM3[Ignores order]
    end

    subgraph SM[Sequence Model]
        SM1[Session S1: A, B, C ordered]
        SM2[Session S2: A, B ordered]
        SM3[Preserves click order]
    end

    subgraph GM[Graph Model]
        GM1[Nodes: pages]
        GM2[Edges: weighted by transition counts]
        GM3[Supports Markov and PageRank]
    end

    TM --> APP1[Apriori Association Rules]
    SM --> APP2[PrefixSpan Sequential Patterns]
    GM --> APP3[Markov and PageRank Prediction]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Hint:** A clean way to remember the mapping is **{Set, Sequence, Graph} → {Apriori, PrefixSpan, Markov}** — a frequently tested board triplet.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between the transactional model and the sequence model in Web Usage Mining. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Transactional Model | Sequence Model |
|--------|---------------------|----------------|
| Order | Ignored | Preserved |
| Representation | Set of pageviews per session | Ordered list of pageviews per session |
| Best suited for | Association rule mining (Apriori) | Sequential pattern mining (PrefixSpan), Markov chains |
| Example output | $\{A, B, C\}$ | $\langle A, B, C \rangle$ |

> **Marks Distribution (KTU):** Definition (1 m) + Key difference (1 m) + Example (1 m).

---

### Q2. What is the role of TF-IDF weighting in data modelling for Web Usage Mining? *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency) assigns a weight $w_{ij}$ to each pageview $p_j$ in a session vector $\mathbf{s}_i$ using:

$$
w_{ij} = \text{tf}_{ij} \cdot \log\!\left( \frac{N}{\text{df}_j} \right)
$$

**Roles (each worth 1 mark):**
1. **Suppresses bias** toward highly popular pages (e.g., the homepage).
2. **Highlights distinctive navigational behavior** of the user.
3. **Enables fair similarity measurement** (cosine, Euclidean) by producing a discriminative session vector space.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

#### (a) Explain the different data modelling techniques used in Web Usage Mining with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The major data modelling techniques are:

1. **Statistical / Descriptive Modelling**
   - Computes aggregate metrics — page-hit counts, average session duration, entropy, bounce rate.
   - Example: The entropy of a page's traffic $H = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i$ measures the diversity of referral sources.

2. **Transactional (Set-based) Modelling**
   - Each session is a set of pages. Order is discarded.
   - Enables *Apriori*, *FP-Growth*, and association-rule algorithms.
   - Example: If a session contains $\{A, B, C\}$ and another $\{A, B\}$, then $\text{sup}(\{A,B\}) = 2 / N$ where $N$ is total sessions.

3. **Sequence Modelling**
   - Each session is an ordered tuple. Order matters.
   - Enables *GSP*, *PrefixSpan*, and *Longest Common Subsequence* mining.
   - Example: $\langle \text{Home}, \text{Search}, \text{Product}, \text{Cart} \rangle$ predicts a purchase funnel.

4. **Graph / Markov Modelling**
   - Site topology + observed transitions $\Rightarrow$ a directed weighted graph.
   - Enables *Markov-chain* and *PageRank* prediction.
   - Example: $P(\text{Cart} \mid \text{Product}) = 0.62$ drives prefetch logic.

5. **Feature / Vector Modelling**
   - Sessions encoded as TF-IDF or binary vectors for clustering/classification.
   - Example: k-Means over vectors clusters users into personas.

**Marks Distribution:**
- Enumeration of techniques — *1 mark*
- Explanation of each — *5 marks* (1 each)
- Real-world example — *1 mark*

#### (b) Consider a web log where 5 users have visited pages A, B, C as follows. Compute the support, confidence, and lift for the rule A ⇒ B. State whether the rule is interesting. *(7 marks)*

| User | Pages Visited |
|------|---------------|
| $U_1$ | A, B, C |
| $U_2$ | A, B |
| $U_3$ | A, C |
| $U_4$ | B, C |
| $U_5$ | A, B |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\text{sup}(A)$:**

$A$ appears in $U_1, U_2, U_3, U_5$ → 4 of 5.

$$
\text{sup}(A) = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\text{sup}(B)$:**

$B$ appears in $U_1, U_2, U_4, U_5$ → 4 of 5.

$$
\text{sup}(B) = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\text{sup}(A \cup B)$:**

Sessions containing both: $U_1, U_2, U_5$ → 3 of 5.

$$
\text{sup}(A \cup B) = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60
$$

**Step 4 — Compute confidence:**

$$
\text{conf}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{\text{sup}(A \cup B)}{\text{sup}(A)} = \frac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75
$$

**Step 5 — Compute lift:**

$$
\text{lift}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{\text{sup}(A \cup B)}{\text{sup}(A)\cdot \text{sup}(B)} = \frac{0.60}{0.80 \times 0.80} = \frac{0.60}{0.64} = 0.9375
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

Since $\text{lift} = 0.9375 < 1$, the rule $A \Rightarrow B$ shows **negative correlation** — visiting $A$ actually *reduces* the probability of visiting $B$ relative to the baseline. Therefore, the rule is **not interesting** in the Apriori sense (lift should exceed **1**).

**Marks Distribution:**
- Counting each set — *2 marks*
- Support — *1 mark*
- Confidence — *1 mark*
- Lift — *1 mark*
- Interpretation — *2 marks*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

#### (a) With a neat diagram, describe the architecture of a Web Usage Mining system and explain the role of the data modelling component. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture Stages (each 1 mark for naming + 1 mark for explanation):**

1. **Data Collection** — Server logs, proxy logs, application logs, browser cookies.
2. **Preprocessing** — Cleaning, user/session identification, path completion.
3. **Data Modelling** — Transactional, sequence, graph, and feature-vector construction.
4. **Pattern Discovery** — Apriori, PrefixSpan, clustering, classification, Markov.
5. **Pattern Analysis** — Filtering, visualization, OLAP queries.

**Role of Data Modelling (3 marks):**

- Transforms raw preprocessed logs into **abstract, algorithm-ready structures**.
- Bridges the *data layer* and the *algorithm layer*.
- Determines **what patterns can be discovered** (e.g., Apriori cannot operate on raw clickstreams; it requires a transactional database).

#### (b) Build a 1st-order Markov transition matrix from the following user click sequences. Predict the next page after D and compute the stationary distribution. *(7 marks)*

Click sequences (5 users):

- $U_1: A \to B \to D \to E$
- $U_2: B \to C \to D \to E$
- $U_3: A \to B \to C \to D$
- $U_4: A \to C \to D \to E$
- $U_5: B \to D \to E$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Build the transition count matrix:**

| From \ To | A | B | C | D | E |
|-----------|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 2 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| B | 0 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 0 |
| C | 0 | 0 | 0 | 3 | 0 |
| D | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 3 |
| E | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 2 — Normalize rows to obtain probabilities:**

Row sums: A→3, B→3, C→3, D→3, E→0.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(B \mid A) &= 2/3 \approx 0.667 \\
P(C \mid A) &= 1/3 \approx 0.333 \\
P(C \mid B) &= 1/3 \approx 0.333 \\
P(D \mid B) &= 2/3 \approx 0.667 \\
P(D \mid C) &= 3/3 = 1.000 \\
P(E \mid D) &= 3/3 = 1.000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Predict next page after D:**

$$
\hat{p}_{\text{next}} = \arg\max_j P(j \mid D) = E
$$

**Step 4 — Stationary distribution $\pi$:**

Solve $\pi P = \pi$ with $\sum \pi_i = 1$.

From the structure: $E$ is absorbing → as $n \to \infty$, all probability mass flows to $E$.

$$
\pi = (0,\,0,\,0,\,0,\,1)
$$

**Marks Distribution:**
- Count matrix — *2 marks*
- Normalized matrix — *2 marks*
- Prediction — *1 mark*
- Stationary distribution with reasoning — *2 marks*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Lift threshold confusion:** Many students write *"lift > 1 means the rule is good"* but fail to state the **baseline** comparison. Always mention: $\text{lift} = 1$ means statistical independence.
> 2. **Markov normalization error:** A common error is *column-normalizing* instead of *row-normalizing*. The transition matrix must satisfy $\sum_j P(p_j \mid p_i) = 1$ for each fixed $p_i$ (row sums = 1).
> 3. **TF-IDF base confusion:** Students often use natural log ($\ln$) in board answers where $\log_2$ was expected. Always **state the base explicitly** in the derivation.
> 4. **Confusing support with confidence:** Support is *joint probability*; confidence is *conditional probability*. Examiners deduct full marks if this distinction is not visible.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why model" step:** For 7-mark questions, students jump straight into formulas. Always begin with a *1–2 sentence rationale* explaining why that model is suitable.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Data Modelling is the bridge** between preprocessing and pattern discovery in the WUM pipeline.
- **Three core abstract structures** are constructed: *transactional DB*, *sequence DB*, and *graph model*.
- **Session timeout is 30 minutes** — used as the industry-standard inactivity threshold.
- **Support** = joint probability, **Confidence** = conditional probability, **Lift** = correlation.
- A rule is *interesting* if **$\text{lift} > 1$** (positive), $= 1$ (independent), or $< 1$ (negative).
- **TF-IDF** is used to weight pageviews; formula $w_{ij} = \text{tf}_{ij} \cdot \log(N / \text{df}_j)$.
- **Cosine similarity** is preferred over Euclidean distance for sparse session vectors.
- **Markov transition matrix** must have **row sums = 1** (not column sums).
- **Entropy** $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ measures diversity; max value is $\log_2 d$.
- **Algorithms–Model Mapping:** *Apriori ↔ Transactional*, *PrefixSpan ↔ Sequence*, *Markov/PageRank ↔ Graph*.
- **Practical applications:** recommendation engines, prefetching, personalization, server load balancing, adaptive websites.
- **Common KTU trap:** Always specify the *base of the logarithm* and the *normalization convention* in any derivation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In 14-mark answers, structure your solution as **Definition → Model → Math → Algorithm → Real-World Use**. This five-step template scores consistently above 12/14 across the KTU valuation pattern.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discovery and Analysis of Web Usage Patterns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT636 - INTRODUCTION TO WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Discovery and Analysis of Web Usage Patterns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Web Usage Mining (WUM)** is the automatic discovery and analysis of patterns in clickstream data, user transactions, and associated data collected from one or more Web servers, in order to understand and serve the needs of Web-based applications.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course Code: PEADT636, Module 4), the *Discovery and Analysis of Web Usage Patterns* refers to the process of extracting **latent behavioral knowledge** from the interaction logs of users with a Web system. It is formally partitioned into two inter-connected phases:

1. **Pattern Discovery** – The application of techniques from data mining, machine learning, and statistics to the preprocessed usage data to surface recurring, statistically significant, and novel patterns.
2. **Pattern Analysis** – The post-processing phase that filters, interprets, visualizes, and operationalizes the discovered patterns for actionable business or engineering intelligence.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus treats these two phases as inseparable; raw mined patterns are noise until they have been analytically validated against a usage objective (personalization, system tuning, marketing, security, etc.).

### Key Terminology (Aligned with KTU Glossary)

| Term | Formal Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Clickstream** | The chronological sequence of HTTP requests emitted by a single user during a navigation session. |
| **Session** | A semantically grouped set of page views by a single user during one visit, terminated by a session timeout (typically **30 minutes** of inactivity). |
| **Transaction** | A higher-level abstraction of a session, representing a meaningful unit of work (e.g., a purchase funnel). |
| **Episode** | An ordered set of Web objects co-occurring in a session within a defined time window. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mall Foot-Traffic" Intuition

Imagine a **large shopping mall** that wants to understand *how customers actually walk through it*, not just *who entered*. The mall installs **heat sensors, CCTV, and door counters** at every store and corridor.

| Mall Analogy | Web Usage Mining Counterpart |
| :--- | :--- |
| Door counter at store entrance | **Web server access log** entry |
| Heat map of the mall floor | **Visualization of click density** |
| Group of customers visiting stores A → B → D in order | **Sequential pattern / episode** in clickstream |
| Two shoppers with identical store-visiting habits | **Cluster** of similar users (collaborative filtering) |
| A shopper who buys bread whenever they buy butter | **Association rule** (e.g., {Bread} $\Rightarrow$ {Butter}) |
| The mall manager redesigning a corridor | **Site modification / personalization** based on usage data |

In this analogy, the mall never *asks* the customer what they want to do — it **observes** the trail they leave. Web Usage Mining is precisely that: *learning from the breadcrumbs users leave behind in server logs*.

---

## 1.3 What Makes a Pattern "Discoverable"?

> [!NOTE]
> A usage pattern must satisfy three criteria to qualify as *discoverable* under the KTU framework:
> 1. **Statistical Significance** – Occurs far more often than random chance (measured by *support* $\geq$ **min\_sup**).
> 2. **Actionability** – Maps to a clear business or engineering decision.
> 3. **Novelty** – Was not already encoded in the explicit design of the Web system.

These three filters are the gatekeepers of the *Analysis* phase and determine whether a pattern moves from raw mined output to a deployed policy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Stage Pipeline of Web Usage Mining

The KTU 2024 scheme presents a canonical **four-stage architecture**. Each stage is a transformation that progressively converts raw, noisy log lines into validated, actionable intelligence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CO Mapping:** This pipeline directly satisfies **CO4** of PEADT636 — *"Apply pattern discovery algorithms to real-world Web usage data."*

### Stage 1 — Data Collection & Preprocessing
The goal is to convert heterogeneous, noisy raw logs into a **reliable, integrated, and user-centric** data warehouse.

| Sub-Stage | Operation | Input | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cleaning** | Remove crawler, bot, and error requests (HTTP $4xx$, $5xx$). | Raw log file | Filtered log |
| **User Identification** | Distinguish unique visitors via IP + User-Agent. | Filtered log | Identified users |
| **Session Identification** | Split each user's page views at the **30-minute timeout** threshold. | User page views | List of sessions |
| **Path Completion** | Reconstruct missing pages via referrer-based backtracking. | Session fragments | Completed session |

### Stage 2 — Pattern Discovery (The Algorithmic Core)
This stage executes mining algorithms on the cleaned, sessionized dataset. Five families are explicitly listed in the KTU Module 4 syllabus:

1. **Statistical Analysis** — Mean, median, mode of page hits, view duration.
2. **Association Rules** — Apriori / FP-Growth over page-view baskets.
3. **Clustering** — K-Means / DBSCAN on user or page vectors.
4. **Classification** — Decision Trees, Naïve Bayes to label users.
5. **Sequential / Episode Patterns** — AprioriAll, PrefixSpan over clickstreams.

### Stage 3 — Pattern Analysis
Filters, ranks, and visualizes patterns to surface only those that are *interesting* and *useful*. Techniques include:

- **OLAP (Online Analytical Processing)** cubes over the usage warehouse.
- **Knowledge Query Mechanisms** such as SQL or MDX for ad-hoc analysis.
- **Visualization** — heat maps, sequence graphs, parallel coordinates.
- **Usability Analysis** — comparing observed paths against the designed IA (Information Architecture).

### Stage 4 — Application Deployment
The validated patterns are pushed into the live system for:
- **Personalization** (recommendation engines)
- **System Improvement** (caching, load balancing)
- **Site Modification** (restructuring navigation)
- **Business Intelligence** (marketing, conversion optimization)
- **Security & Fraud Detection** (anomalous access detection)

---

## 2.2 The Three "M's" of Pattern Quality (KTU High-Yield Metrics)

Every candidate pattern $P$ is scored on three orthogonal axes. The KTU 2024 examiner expects students to compute each of these.

### 2.2.1 Support — The Popularity Filter

Support measures **how frequently** an itemset $I$ appears across all transactions in the database $D$.

$$ \text{Support}(I) = \frac{\vert \{ T \in D : I \subseteq T \} \vert}{\vert D \vert} $$

A pattern is considered *frequent* if $\text{Support}(I) \geq \text{min\_sup}$, where $\text{min\_sup}$ is a domain-specific threshold (commonly **1% – 5%** in commercial WUM).

### 2.2.2 Confidence — The Reliability Filter

Confidence quantifies the **conditional probability** that the consequent $Y$ occurs whenever the antecedent $X$ is observed.

$$ \text{Confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{Support}(X \cup Y)}{\text{Support}(X)} $$

A rule is considered *reliable* if $\text{Confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y) \geq \text{min\_conf}$ (commonly **50% – 80%**).

### 2.2.3 Lift — The Genuine Correlation Filter

Confidence alone can be misleading (an item that is *universally popular* will have high confidence with almost anything). **Lift** corrects this by comparing the observed co-occurrence to the expected value under statistical independence.

$$ \text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{Confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y)}{\text{Support}(Y)} = \frac{\text{Support}(X \cup Y)}{\text{Support}(X) \cdot \text{Support}(Y)} $$

| Lift Value | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\text{Lift} > 1$ | **Positive correlation** — $X$ genuinely promotes $Y$. |
| $\text{Lift} = 1$ | **Independence** — $X$ and $Y$ are unrelated. |
| $\text{Lift} < 1$ | **Negative correlation** — $X$ inhibits $Y$. |

---

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most-tested mathematical block** in the Web Usage Mining module. Memorize the symbols, ranges, and units.

| Metric | Formula | Range | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Support** | $\sigma(I) \big/ \vert D \vert$ | $[0, 1]$ | Filtering rare itemsets |
| **Confidence** | $\sigma(X \cup Y) \big/ \sigma(X)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Ranking rule reliability |
| **Lift** | $\text{Conf}(X \Rightarrow Y) \big/ \sigma(Y)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Removing spurious rules |
| **Cosine Similarity** | $\frac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\Vert \vec{u} \Vert \, \Vert \vec{v} \Vert}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | User clustering |
| **Jaccard Index** | $\frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Session similarity |
| **Entropy of a Path** | $H(P) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $[0, \log_2 n]$ | Detecting navigation chaos |
| **Bounce Rate** | $\text{Single-page sessions} \big/ \text{Total sessions}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Usability health metric |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why These Metrics Matter in the Real World

| Domain | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **E-Commerce** | Cross-sell engines (Amazon's *"Frequently bought together"*) rely on **Lift** to filter spurious co-purchases. |
| **Web Security** | Sudden **entropy spikes** in URL-request distributions signal DDoS or scraping attacks. |
| **Search Engines** | Session-level **Jaccard similarity** powers query suggestion and session co-watching features. |
| **CDN Engineering** | High-support pages are pre-fetched into edge caches; **Support** directly drives cache warm-up policy. |
| **UX Research** | **Bounce rate** and **path entropy** are the two primary indicators used in A/B testing. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping the KTU Bloom Levels

| Bloom Level | Cognitive Action | Representative Task in WUM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **L1 — Remember** | Recall definitions of support, confidence, lift. | State the formula for Lift. |
| **L2 — Understand** | Explain why Lift $\gt 1$ implies correlation. | Interpret a Lift of 1.4. |
| **L3 — Apply** | Compute support and confidence on a sample log. | Run the worked example in §3.2. |
| **L4 — Analyze** | Distinguish genuine rules from spurious ones. | Compare rules using Lift. |
| **L5 — Evaluate** | Justify the choice of min\_sup for a given dataset. | Defend min\_sup $= 0.4$ in §3.3. |
| **L6 — Create** | Design a complete preprocessing pipeline. | Construct the architecture in §3.4. |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Computing Support, Confidence, and Lift (L3 / L4)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most frequent** numerical question in KTU Module 4 papers. Master every line of the derivation below.

### 3.2.1 The Toy Database

Consider a synthetic usage database $D$ consisting of **5 transactions** (sessions). Each transaction is a basket of page views.

| Transaction ID | Pages Viewed (Basket) |
| :--- | :--- |
| $T_1$ | $\{$Home, Product, Cart$\}$ |
| $T_2$ | $\{$Home, Product, Cart, Payment$\}$ |
| $T_3$ | $\{$Home, Product$\}$ |
| $T_4$ | $\{$Home, Cart$\}$ |
| $T_5$ | $\{$Home, Product, Cart$\}$ |

The candidate rule to evaluate is:

$$ R: \quad \{\text{Product}\} \Rightarrow \{\text{Cart}\} $$

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Compute the Total Transaction Count

We begin by counting the size of the database, which serves as the **denominator** for every support calculation.

$$ \vert D \vert = 5 $$

This is a *read-off* value, but examiners often award partial credit for explicitly stating it.

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Compute Support of the Antecedent

We need the fraction of transactions containing **Product**.

$$ \text{Support}(\{\text{Product}\}) = \frac{\text{Transactions containing Product}}{\vert D \vert} $$

From the table: $T_1$, $T_2$, $T_3$, and $T_5$ contain Product. That is **4 out of 5**.

$$ \text{Support}(\{\text{Product}\}) = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80 $$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Compute Support of the Consequent

We repeat the procedure for **Cart**.

$$ \text{Support}(\{\text{Cart}\}) = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80 $$

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Compute Support of the Union (Antecedent $\cup$ Consequent)

We need transactions that contain **both** Product *and* Cart.

$$ \text{Support}(\{\text{Product}, \text{Cart}\}) = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60 $$

(Specifically, $T_1$, $T_2$, and $T_5$.)

### 3.2.6 Step 5 — Compute Confidence of the Rule

By definition:

$$ \text{Confidence}(\{\text{Product}\} \Rightarrow \{\text{Cart}\}) = \frac{\text{Support}(\{\text{Product}\} \cup \{\text{Cart}\})}{\text{Support}(\{\text{Product}\})} $$

Substituting:

$$ \text{Confidence} = \frac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75 \quad \text{(i.e., 75\%)} $$

This means: *In 75% of sessions where the user viewed a Product page, they also visited the Cart.*

### 3.2.7 Step 6 — Compute Lift of the Rule

$$ \text{Lift}(\{\text{Product}\} \Rightarrow \{\text{Cart}\}) = \frac{\text{Confidence}}{\text{Support}(\{\text{Cart}\})} $$

$$ \text{Lift} = \frac{0.75}{0.80} = 0.9375 $$

### 3.2.8 Step 8 — Interpretation (L4 — Analyze)

Since **Lift $\lt 1$**, the rule exhibits a **negative correlation**: viewing a Product page *slightly reduces* the likelihood of visiting the Cart, relative to the baseline Cart-visit rate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Mistake:** Students often stop at Confidence $= 0.75$ and conclude the rule is "strong." A KTU examiner will deduct up to **2 marks** for failing to compute Lift and report the negative correlation. **Always compute all three metrics.**

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Choosing the Minimum Support Threshold (L5 — Evaluate)

Suppose a KTU problem states: *"A Web server logs **10 million** sessions per day. The most-visited page accounts for 2% of all sessions. Propose a suitable min\_sup and justify."*

### 3.3.1 Derivation

We begin by translating the *absolute popularity* into the support metric.

$$ \text{min\_sup}_{\text{absolute}} = \frac{2}{100} \times 10{,}000{,}000 = 200{,}000 \text{ sessions} $$

Next, we apply the **downward-closure (Apriori) property**: any itemset that is *infrequent* cannot have a *frequent* superset. Therefore, we set min\_sup strictly *below* the most popular item's support to allow the discovery of any meaningful rule.

$$ \text{min\_sup}_{\text{recommended}} = 0.01 \quad (\text{i.e., 1\%}) $$

### 3.3.2 Justification

> [!NOTE]
> A min\_sup of 0.01 ensures that:
> 1. The most popular page is included in the frequent itemset lattice.
> 2. Pairs and triples with even slightly lower support can still be discovered.
> 3. The combinatorial explosion of the lattice remains tractable because singletons below 0.01 are pruned by the downward-closure property.

This justification typically earns full marks (3/3) on a KTU evaluation-type question.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Cosine Similarity Between Two User Sessions (L3 / L4)

Two users have the following page-view vectors over 4 pages $\{$Home, Product, Cart, Payment$\}$:

$$ \vec{u} = \langle 1, 2, 1, 0 \rangle, \quad \vec{v} = \langle 2, 1, 0, 1 \rangle $$

### 3.4.1 Step 1 — Compute the Dot Product

$$ \vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = (1)(2) + (2)(1) + (1)(0) + (0)(1) = 2 + 2 + 0 + 0 = 4 $$

### 3.4.2 Step 2 — Compute the Magnitudes

$$ \Vert \vec{u} \Vert = \sqrt{1^2 + 2^2 + 1^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{6} $$

$$ \Vert \vec{v} \Vert = \sqrt{2^2 + 1^2 + 0^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{6} $$

### 3.4.3 Step 3 — Compute Cosine Similarity

$$ \cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\Vert \vec{u} \Vert \, \Vert \vec{v} \Vert} = \frac{4}{\sqrt{6} \cdot \sqrt{6}} = \frac{4}{6} = 0.667 $$

### 3.4.4 Interpretation

A cosine similarity of **0.667** indicates a **moderately strong behavioral alignment** between the two users — a common threshold for placing them in the same cluster in collaborative filtering.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Web Usage Mining Pipeline (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram renders the complete KTU-aligned architecture, mapping every transformation from raw HTTP logs to deployed business intelligence.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawLog[Raw Web Server Logs] --> clean[Cleaning Stage]
    clean --> userID[User Identification]
    userID --> sessID[Session Identification]
    sessID --> pathC[Path Completion]
    pathC --> wh[Integrated Usage Data Warehouse]
    wh --> stat[Statistical Analysis]
    wh --> assoc[Association Rule Mining]
    wh --> clust[Clustering of Users and Pages]
    wh --> class[Classification of Sessions]
    wh --> seq[Sequential Pattern Discovery]
    stat --> patternPool[Discovered Pattern Pool]
    assoc --> patternPool
    clust --> patternPool
    class --> patternPool
    seq --> patternPool
    patternPool --> olap[OLAP Analysis]
    patternPool --> vis[Visualization Layer]
    patternPool --> kqm[Knowledge Query Mechanism]
    olap --> deploy[Application Deployment]
    vis --> deploy
    kqm --> deploy
    deploy --> pers[Personalization Engine]
    deploy --> sysImp[System Improvement Caching and Load Balancing]
    deploy --> siteMod[Site Modification and IA Restructuring]
    deploy --> bizIntel[Business Intelligence and Marketing]
    deploy --> sec[Security and Fraud Detection]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read the diagram left-to-right.** The data progressively *condenses* (from gigabytes of raw logs to kilobytes of validated rules) and *enriches* (from "page X was hit" to "users like U cluster 4 convert 3.2$\times$ more when shown the loyalty page first"). This is the KTU definition of *discovery followed by analysis*.

---

## 4.2 Pattern Discovery Algorithm Family (Mermaid Classification Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    root[Pattern Discovery Algorithms]
    root --> stat[Statistical Methods]
    root --> assoc[Association Rules]
    root --> clust[Clustering]
    root --> class[Classification]
    root --> seq[Sequential Patterns]
    assoc --> apriori[Apriori]
    assoc --> fpg[FP Growth]
    clust --> kmeans[K Means]
    clust --> dbscan[DBSCAN]
    class --> dt[Decision Tree]
    class --> nb[Naive Bayes]
    seq --> aprioriAll[AprioriAll]
    seq --> prefixSpan[PrefixSpan]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 examiner will accept any one of the boxed algorithms per category as a valid exam answer. Memorize at least **one algorithm from each leaf node** — the most common combinations are *Apriori + K-Means + AprioriAll*.

---

## 4.3 Pattern Analysis Feedback Loop (Iterative Refinement)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pat[Discovered Pattern] --> filter[Interestingness Filter Support Confidence Lift]
    filter --> rank[Pattern Ranking]
    rank --> interp[Domain Expert Interpretation]
    interp --> valid{Valid and Actionable}
    valid --> yes[Deploy to Production]
    valid --> no[Discard or Refine]
    no --> algoAdj[Adjust min_sup and min_conf]
    algoAdj --> pat
    yes --> measure[Measure Business KPI Impact]
    measure --> pat
```

> [!NOTE]
> This feedback loop is the **most under-tested but most valuable** concept in Module 4. It demonstrates that Web Usage Mining is not a one-shot pipeline — it is a *closed-loop control system* over user behavior.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Web Usage Mining and list its four main stages.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Web Usage Mining (WUM)** is the process of automatically discovering and analyzing patterns in user interaction data collected from Web servers, with the goal of understanding user behavior and improving Web-based applications.

The four main stages, in canonical order, are:

1. **Data Collection & Preprocessing** — cleaning, user/session identification, path completion.
2. **Pattern Discovery** — application of algorithms (Apriori, K-Means, AprioriAll, etc.).
3. **Pattern Analysis** — OLAP, visualization, knowledge queries, usability analysis.
4. **Application Deployment** — personalization, system improvement, site modification, business intelligence, and security.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the four stages with brief descriptions.)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Support and Lift in association rule mining.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Support | Lift |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **What it measures** | Popularity / frequency of an itemset. | Genuine correlation between antecedent and consequent. |
| **Formula** | $\sigma(I) \big/ \vert D \vert$ | $\text{Conf}(X \Rightarrow Y) \big/ \sigma(Y)$ |
| **Range** | $[0, 1]$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| **Interpretation at $1$** | (Not meaningful) | Antecedent and consequent are statistically **independent**. |
| **Primary use** | Filtering **frequent** itemsets. | Filtering **spurious** rules. |

> **Key Insight (1 mark):** Support cannot detect *redundancy* — a universally popular item (e.g., "Home" page) will have high support with *any* other item. Lift corrects this by normalizing against the consequent's baseline popularity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A: Pattern Discovery Using Association Rules

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 | Module 4 Choice]`

**a)** Explain the **Apriori algorithm** for mining frequent itemsets from Web usage data. List its key properties. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Given the Web usage database below (5 sessions), compute the **Support, Confidence, and Lift** for the rule $\{A, B\} \Rightarrow \{C\}$. State whether the rule is actionable. **(7 Marks)**

| Session ID | Pages Visited |
| :--- | :--- |
| $S_1$ | $A, B, C$ |
| $S_2$ | $A, B, C, D$ |
| $S_3$ | $A, B$ |
| $S_4$ | $A, C$ |
| $S_5$ | $A, B, C$ |

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

The **Apriori algorithm**, proposed by Agrawal and Srikant in 1994, is a **level-wise, breadth-first** algorithm for mining frequent itemsets from transactional databases. It proceeds in iterative passes over the database.

**Step 1 — Initialize with $k=1$ candidate itemsets.** Scan the database once and count the support of every single item. Retain those whose support $\geq \text{min\_sup}$ to form the set $L_1$ of frequent 1-itemsets.

**Step 2 — Generate candidates of size $k+1$.** Using $L_k$, the **candidate generation function** $C_{k+1} = L_k \bowtie L_k$ produces new candidates by joining frequent $k$-itemsets on their $(k-1)$-length prefixes.

**Step 3 — Prune by downward-closure property.** *Any* $(k+1)$-itemset containing a $k$-subset that is *not* in $L_k$ cannot be frequent. Such candidates are removed.

**Step 4 — Scan database and count support** of remaining candidates to produce $L_{k+1}$.

**Step 5 — Repeat** Steps 2–4 until $L_k = \emptyset$.

**Key Property — The Apriori Property (Downward Closure):**

> *All non-empty subsets of a frequent itemset must themselves be frequent.*

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$ \forall I, J : \; J \subseteq I \;\text{and}\; \text{Support}(I) \geq \text{min\_sup} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Support}(J) \geq \text{min\_sup} $$

**Complexity Note (1 mark):** The dominant cost is database scanning — Apriori requires $k$ full passes, motivating the FP-Growth improvement.

*(Award 2 marks for the iterative procedure, 2 marks for candidate generation, 2 marks for the Apriori property, 1 mark for complexity/discussion.)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute $\vert D \vert$:**

$$ \vert D \vert = 5 $$

**Step 2 — Compute $\text{Support}(\{A, B\})$:**

Sessions containing both $A$ and $B$: $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_5$ → **4 sessions**.

$$ \text{Support}(\{A, B\}) = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80 $$

**Step 3 — Compute $\text{Support}(\{C\})$:**

Sessions containing $C$: $S_1, S_2, S_4, S_5$ → **4 sessions**.

$$ \text{Support}(\{C\}) = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80 $$

**Step 4 — Compute $\text{Support}(\{A, B, C\})$:**

Sessions containing $A$, $B$, and $C$ together: $S_1, S_2, S_5$ → **3 sessions**.

$$ \text{Support}(\{A, B, C\}) = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60 $$

**Step 5 — Compute Confidence:**

$$ \text{Confidence}(\{A, B\} \Rightarrow \{C\}) = \frac{\text{Support}(\{A, B, C\})}{\text{Support}(\{A, B\})} = \frac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75 $$

**Step 6 — Compute Lift:**

$$ \text{Lift} = \frac{0.75}{0.80} = 0.9375 $$

**Step 7 — Conclusion:**

Since **Lift $\lt 1$**, the rule exhibits **negative correlation** — viewing pages $A$ and $B$ slightly *reduces* the likelihood of viewing $C$ relative to the baseline. The rule is **NOT actionable** for cross-promotion, despite the deceptively high confidence of 0.75.

*(Award 1 mark each for Steps 1–6; 1 mark for the actionable verdict and Lift interpretation.)*

---

### ⭐ Question B: Pattern Analysis Techniques

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 Choice]`

**a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a Web Usage Mining system**. Explain the role of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Explain the **OLAP-based pattern analysis** technique. How does it differ from SQL-based knowledge query mechanisms? Why is visualization considered the most universally accessible form of pattern analysis? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

A complete Web Usage Mining system architecture consists of **four sequential layers**, each performing a distinct transformation on the data.

**Layer 1 — Data Sources (1 Mark):**
The base layer comprises heterogeneous input streams:
- **Web server access logs** (Apache, Nginx combined log format).
- **Application-level logs** (e-commerce transactions, search queries).
- **Proxy server logs** (for caching analytics).
- **Client-side logs** (browser cookies, JavaScript beacons).

**Layer 2 — Preprocessing Subsystem (2 Marks):**
This layer converts raw, dirty logs into a clean warehouse. It performs:
- **Cleaning** (removing bots, $4xx$/$5xx$ errors, multimedia fetches).
- **User Identification** (IP + User-Agent + cookie fingerprinting).
- **Session Identification** (30-minute inactivity timeout).
- **Path Completion** (referrer-based reconstruction of missing pages).
- Output: a star-schema or snowflake-schema **usage data warehouse**.

**Layer 3 — Pattern Discovery Engine (2 Marks):**
This is the algorithmic heart, executing:
- **Statistical analysis** (descriptive metrics).
- **Association rule mining** (Apriori / FP-Growth).
- **Clustering** (K-Means, DBSCAN).
- **Classification** (Decision Trees, Naïve Bayes).
- **Sequential pattern mining** (AprioriAll, PrefixSpan).
- Output: a **pattern pool** of candidate insights.

**Layer 4 — Pattern Analysis & Application (2 Marks):**
This is the *value realization* layer:
- **OLAP** cubes for multi-dimensional drill-down.
- **Visualization** (heat maps, parallel coordinates, Sankey diagrams).
- **Knowledge query mechanisms** (SQL, MDX).
- **Usability analysis** (path entropy, bounce rate, drop-off).
- Final deployment into personalization engines, recommendation systems, security monitors, and BI dashboards.

*(Award 1.5–2 marks per layer with a labeled block diagram. Reference §4.1 in Section 4 of these notes for the schematic.)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**OLAP-Based Pattern Analysis (3 Marks):**

OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) is a multi-dimensional analytical paradigm in which the cleaned usage warehouse is modeled as a **data cube**. Each cube cell represents the aggregate of a usage metric (e.g., page views) over a specific combination of dimensions such as *(Time $\times$ Page $\times$ User-Segment $\times$ Browser)*.

Analysts perform:
- **Roll-up** — aggregate to higher hierarchy (e.g., daily $\rightarrow$ monthly).
- **Drill-down** — disaggregate to finer granularity (e.g., page $\rightarrow$ URL).
- **Slice** — fix one dimension (e.g., users from Kerala only).
- **Dice** — select a sub-cube by constraining multiple dimensions.

This makes OLAP uniquely suited for *hypothesis-driven* exploration: the analyst suspects a pattern and tests it interactively.

**Comparison with SQL-Based Knowledge Query Mechanisms (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | OLAP | SQL Query |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Paradigm** | Multi-dimensional, pre-aggregated cubes. | Relational, set-based, on-the-fly computation. |
| **Performance** | Sub-second for typical drill-downs. | Slow for aggregations on large logs. |
| **Schema** | Star/Snowflake with fact and dimension tables. | Normalized relational tables. |
| **Analyst Skill** | Drag-and-drop interface. | Requires SQL proficiency. |
| **Best For** | Exploratory, multi-dimensional browsing. | One-shot, well-defined, ad-hoc queries. |

**Why Visualization is Universally Accessible (2 Marks):**

Visualization translates abstract numerical patterns into **perceptually immediate** graphics — a heat map of click density conveys "where users click" in a single glance, whereas a table of URL counts requires cognitive translation. This makes it:
1. **Skill-agnostic** — non-technical stakeholders (marketers, designers) can consume it.
2. **Pattern-revealing** — humans excel at detecting clusters, outliers, and trends in graphics.
3. **Communication-efficient** — a single chart replaces a 50-page executive report.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Mistake in Part B:** Students often draw the block diagram but **forget to label the data flows** between blocks. A KTU examiner will deduct up to **1 mark** for unlabeled arrows. Always annotate each connector with the *type* of data being passed (e.g., "Cleaned Logs", "Sessions", "Frequent Itemsets").

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks on Module 4 questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Support with Confidence** — they are *not* the same; always compute both.
> 2. **Reporting Lift as a percentage** — Lift is unitless and can exceed 1; never say "150% lift."
> 3. **Forgetting the Apriori (downward-closure) property** — it is a guaranteed 2-mark item.
> 4. **Stopping at Confidence $\geq 0.75$** without computing Lift — a high-confidence rule with Lift $\lt 1$ is *spurious*.
> 5. **Drawing a mining pipeline without distinguishing preprocessing from discovery** — these are two separate stages worth 7 marks combined.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following bullets are the **high-density summary** you should revise the night before the exam.

- **Web Usage Mining** = automatic discovery of behavioral patterns from clickstream data.
- **Four canonical stages**: Preprocessing $\rightarrow$ Pattern Discovery $\rightarrow$ Pattern Analysis $\rightarrow$ Application Deployment.
- **Preprocessing operations** in order: Cleaning $\rightarrow$ User ID $\rightarrow$ Session ID $\rightarrow$ Path Completion.
- **Session timeout** = **30 minutes** of inactivity (KTU standard assumption).
- **Pattern Discovery** uses five algorithm families: Statistical, Association, Clustering, Classification, Sequential.
- **Support** $\in [0, 1]$ — popularity filter; eliminates rare itemsets.
- **Confidence** $\in [0, 1]$ — conditional probability of consequent given antecedent.
- **Lift** $\in [0, \infty)$ — normalized correlation; $\gt 1$ is genuine, $= 1$ is independent, $\lt 1$ is negative.
- **Apriori property** (downward closure): all subsets of a frequent itemset are frequent.
- **Apriori algorithm** is level-wise and breadth-first; $k$ passes for a $k$-itemset database.
- **OLAP** supports roll-up, drill-down, slice, and dice on a usage data cube.
- **Visualization** is the most universally accessible analysis technique (heat maps, sequence graphs).
- **Cosine similarity** $\in [-1, 1]$ — measures behavioral alignment between user vectors.
- **Jaccard index** — set-based similarity for sessions; useful when page-view counts are unreliable.
- **Path entropy** — high entropy = chaotic navigation (often a UX red flag).
- **Bounce rate** = single-page sessions / total sessions; primary usability health KPI.
- **Always compute all three metrics** (Support, Confidence, Lift) — never stop at Confidence alone.
- **Five deployment targets**: Personalization, System Improvement, Site Modification, BI, Security.
- **KTU CO4** maps to pattern discovery; **CO5** maps to pattern analysis and application.

---

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — DISCOVERY AND ANALYSIS OF WEB USAGE PATTERNS**
> *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications - Recommender Systems and Collaborative Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT636 - INTRODUCTION TO WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications - Recommender Systems and Collaborative Filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommender System (RS):** A subclass of information filtering systems that seek to predict the *rating* or *preference* a user would give to an item. It is a software paradigm engineered to support decision-making by narrowing the vast set of available choices to a personalized, ranked list of suggestions.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framing, a Recommender System operates on the principle of **user-item interaction data** (explicit feedback such as ratings, or implicit feedback such as clicks, views, and dwell time) to generate predictions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Collaborative Filtering (CF):** A dominant recommendation technique that relies solely on the *past interactions* recorded between users and items, without requiring exogenous information about the items or users themselves. It operates on the assumption that users who agreed in the past will agree in the future.

### 1.2 The Two Foundational Paradigms

| Paradigm | Core Philosophy | Data Required |
|---|---|---|
| **Content-Based Filtering (CBF)** | "Recommend items *similar* to what the user liked before" | Item features + User profile |
| **Collaborative Filtering (CF)** | "Recommend items that *similar users* liked" | User–Item interaction matrix only |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Movie Club

Imagine you join a new **book club**. On your first day, you have two ways to find what to read next:

1. **The Librarian (Content-Based):** She hands you books based on the *author, genre, and topics* of books you previously enjoyed. The recommendations are based on **item attributes**, not on other readers.
2. **The Fellow Readers (Collaborative Filtering):** You observe that a reader named *Anu* loved the same thriller novels you did. So you trust her opinion and read the next book from her favorites — even if it is a genre you have never tried.

**Collaborative Filtering = wisdom of the crowd.**
**Content-Based Filtering = wisdom of the item's metadata.**

> [!TIP]
> **Hybrid Recommenders** combine both, mimicking how a smart librarian might also ask: "What are *other readers like you* currently borrowing?"

## 1.4 The Central Data Structure — The User–Item Rating Matrix

Let $U = \{u_1, u_2, \dots, u_m\}$ be the set of $m$ users and $I = \{i_1, i_2, \dots, i_n\}$ be the set of $n$ items. The interactions are stored in a matrix:
