$$R_{m \times n} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & \cdots & r_{1n} \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & \cdots & r_{2n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ r_{m1} & r_{m2} & \cdots & r_{mn} \end{bmatrix}$$

where $r_{ui} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ if user $u$ has rated item $i$, and $r_{ui} = \emptyset$ (missing) otherwise. This **sparsity** is the central engineering challenge of recommender systems — often **> 99%** of the matrix is unknown.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Recommender Systems

- **Content-Based Filtering (CBF):** Builds a *user profile vector* $\mathbf{p}_u$ from features of items the user has rated highly; recommends items whose feature vector $\mathbf{f}_i$ is closest to $\mathbf{p}_u$ (typically by **cosine similarity**).
- **Collaborative Filtering (CF):**
  - **Memory-Based (Neighborhood-Based) CF:**
    - *User–User CF:* Find users similar to the active user, aggregate their ratings.
    - *Item–Item CF:* Find items similar to those the active user liked, predict rating.
  - **Model-Based CF:** Learn a latent factor model (e.g., **Matrix Factorization**, SVD, ALS) such that $R \approx P \cdot Q^T$.
- **Hybrid Systems:** Combine CBF + CF to overcome cold-start and sparsity issues. Weighted, switching, cascade, and feature-augmentation hybrids are common.

## 2.2 The Two Pillars of Collaborative Filtering

### Pillar 1 — User-Based Collaborative Filtering
**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. Identify the *active user* $u_a$ for whom we need predictions.
2. Compute the **pairwise similarity** $\text{sim}(u_a, u_v)$ between $u_a$ and every other user $u_v \in U$.
3. Select the **top-$k$ nearest neighbours** of $u_a$.
4. Aggregate the neighbours' ratings on item $i$ to predict $r_{u_a, i}$.

### Pillar 2 — Item-Based Collaborative Filtering
**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. For each item $i_j$ that user $u_a$ has *not* rated, find items $i_k$ that $u_a$ *has* rated.
2. Compute similarity $\text{sim}(i_j, i_k)$ for every such pair.
3. Predict $r_{u_a, i_j}$ as a weighted average of $u_a$'s ratings on the $k$ most similar items.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why item-based CF is preferred in industry (Amazon, 2003):** The number of items is usually more stable than the user count, and pre-computing item-item similarities scales better. The seminal Amazon paper (Linden, Smith, and York, 2003) established item-based CF as the de facto industry standard.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for Module 4. Every KTU question on similarity or prediction will use one of these forms.

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Cosine Similarity** (vectors $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}$) | $\text{sim}_{\cos}(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}) = \dfrac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{\Vert \mathbf{a} \Vert_2 \, \Vert \mathbf{b} \Vert_2}$ | Used when users/items are represented as rating vectors |
| **Pearson Correlation** (users $u, v$) | $\text{sim}_{\text{pearson}}(u, v) = \dfrac{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} (r_{ui} - \bar{r}_u)(r_{vi} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} (r_{ui} - \bar{r}_u)^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} (r_{vi} - \bar{r}_v)^2}}$ | Captures *linear correlation*; $I_{uv}$ = items co-rated by both |
| **Adjusted Cosine** (items $i, j$) | $\text{sim}_{\text{adjcos}}(i, j) = \dfrac{\sum_{u \in U_{ij}} (r_{ui} - \bar{r}_u)(r_{uj} - \bar{r}_u)}{\sqrt{\sum_{u \in U_{ij}} (r_{ui} - \bar{r}_u)^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{u \in U_{ij}} (r_{uj} - \bar{r}_u)^2}}$ | Removes user-rating bias; $U_{ij}$ = users who rated both |
| **Jaccard Similarity** (sets $A, B$) | $\text{sim}_J(A, B) = \dfrac{\mid A \cap B \mid}{\mid A \cup B \mid}$ | Used for *implicit* binary feedback (purchases, clicks) |
| **Prediction (user-based CF)** | $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \bar{r}_u + \dfrac{\sum_{v \in N_k(u)} \text{sim}(u, v) \cdot (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum_{v \in N_k(u)} \vert \text{sim}(u, v) \vert}$ | $N_k(u)$ = top-$k$ neighbours of $u$ who rated $i$ |
| **Prediction (item-based CF)** | $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \dfrac{\sum_{j \in S_k(i)} \text{sim}(i, j) \cdot r_{u,j}}{\sum_{j \in S_k(i)} \vert \text{sim}(i, j) \vert}$ | $S_k(i)$ = $k$ items most similar to $i$ that $u$ has rated |
| **Matrix Factorization Objective** | $\min_{P, Q} \sum_{(u,i) \in \mathcal{K}} (r_{ui} - \mathbf{p}_u \cdot \mathbf{q}_i)^2 + \lambda (\Vert P \Vert_F^2 + \Vert Q \Vert_F^2)$ | $\mathcal{K}$ = observed ratings; $\lambda$ = regularization |
| **Latent Factor Prediction** | $\hat{r}_{ui} = \mathbf{p}_u^T \mathbf{q}_i$ | Where $\mathbf{p}_u, \mathbf{q}_i \in \mathbb{R}^f$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **The vertical-bar pitfall:** In KTU exam scripts, $\vert \text{sim}(u,v) \vert$ is the *absolute value* of similarity, used as a normalizer. Many students mistakenly omit it and lose **2 marks** in Part B derivations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** "Customers who bought X also bought Y" — pure item-based CF.
- **Streaming (Netflix, Spotify):** Latent factor models + item-based CF hybrid.
- **Social Networks (Facebook, X):** "People you may know" — graph-based CF.
- **News Aggregators (Google News):** CF over click logs to personalize feeds.
- **Healthcare:** Recommending treatments based on similar patient histories (active research area).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example Setup

Consider the following user–item rating matrix (scale: 1 to 5; `?` denotes unrated):

| User \ Item | $I_1$ | $I_2$ | $I_3$ | $I_4$ | $I_5$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 5 | 3 | 4 | 4 | ? |
| **B** | 3 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 3 |
| **C** | 4 | 3 | 4 | 3 | 5 |
| **D** | 3 | 3 | 1 | 5 | 4 |
| **E** | 1 | 5 | 5 | 2 | 1 |

**Target Prediction:** $\hat{r}_{A, I_5}$ = the rating user A will give to item $I_5$.

## 3.2 Step 1 — Compute User Mean Ratings $\bar{r}_u$

For each user, average the *known* ratings:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{r}_A &= \frac{5 + 3 + 4 + 4}{4} = \frac{16}{4} = 4.00 \\
\bar{r}_B &= \frac{3 + 1 + 2 + 3 + 3}{5} = \frac{12}{5} = 2.40 \\
\bar{r}_C &= \frac{4 + 3 + 4 + 3 + 5}{5} = \frac{19}{5} = 3.80 \\
\bar{r}_D &= \frac{3 + 3 + 1 + 5 + 4}{5} = \frac{16}{5} = 3.20 \\
\bar{r}_E &= \frac{1 + 5 + 5 + 2 + 1}{5} = \frac{14}{5} = 2.80
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Step 2 — Build Deviation Vectors (used for Pearson)

Define the deviation from each user's mean on the items co-rated with **A** (i.e., $I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4$):

| User | $I_1$ | $I_2$ | $I_3$ | $I_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | $5-4=1$ | $3-4=-1$ | $4-4=0$ | $4-4=0$ |
| **B** | $3-2.4=0.6$ | $1-2.4=-1.4$ | $2-2.4=-0.4$ | $3-2.4=0.6$ |
| **C** | $4-3.8=0.2$ | $3-3.8=-0.8$ | $4-3.8=0.2$ | $3-3.8=-0.8$ |
| **D** | $3-3.2=-0.2$ | $3-3.2=-0.2$ | $1-3.2=-2.2$ | $5-3.2=1.8$ |
| **E** | $1-2.8=-1.8$ | $5-2.8=2.2$ | $5-2.8=2.2$ | $2-2.8=-0.8$ |

## 3.4 Step 3 — Compute Pearson Similarity Between A and Each Other User

The general formula is:
$$\text{sim}_{\text{pearson}}(A, X) = \frac{\sum_{i \in I_{AX}}(r_{Ai} - \bar{r}_A)(r_{Xi} - \bar{r}_X)}{\sqrt{\sum (r_{Ai}-\bar{r}_A)^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum (r_{Xi}-\bar{r}_X)^2}}$$

**Common denominator for A** (numerator root, computed once):
$$\sum_{i=1}^{4}(r_{Ai}-\bar{r}_A)^2 = 1^2 + (-1)^2 + 0^2 + 0^2 = 1 + 1 = 2$$

### 3.4.1 Similarity $\text{sim}(A, B)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= (1)(0.6) + (-1)(-1.4) + (0)(-0.4) + (0)(0.6) \\
&= 0.6 + 1.4 + 0 + 0 = 2.0 \\
\text{Denominator}_{\text{B}} &= \sqrt{0.6^2 + (-1.4)^2 + (-0.4)^2 + 0.6^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.36 + 1.96 + 0.16 + 0.36} = \sqrt{2.84} \approx 1.6852 \\
\text{sim}(A, B) &= \frac{2.0}{\sqrt{2} \cdot 1.6852} = \frac{2.0}{1.4142 \times 1.6852} = \frac{2.0}{2.3832} \approx 0.839
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4.2 Similarity $\text{sim}(A, C)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= (1)(0.2) + (-1)(-0.8) + (0)(0.2) + (0)(-0.8) \\
&= 0.2 + 0.8 = 1.0 \\
\text{Denominator}_{\text{C}} &= \sqrt{0.2^2 + 0.8^2 + 0.2^2 + 0.8^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.04 + 0.64 + 0.04 + 0.64} = \sqrt{1.36} \approx 1.1662 \\
\text{sim}(A, C) &= \frac{1.0}{1.4142 \times 1.1662} = \frac{1.0}{1.6492} \approx 0.606
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4.3 Similarity $\text{sim}(A, D)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= (1)(-0.2) + (-1)(-0.2) + (0)(-2.2) + (0)(1.8) \\
&= -0.2 + 0.2 = 0.0 \\
\text{sim}(A, D) &= 0.0 \quad \text{(no correlation along co-rated items)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4.4 Similarity $\text{sim}(A, E)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= (1)(-1.8) + (-1)(2.2) + (0)(2.2) + (0)(-0.8) \\
&= -1.8 - 2.2 = -4.0 \\
\text{Denominator}_{\text{E}} &= \sqrt{1.8^2 + 2.2^2 + 2.2^2 + 0.8^2} \\
&= \sqrt{3.24 + 4.84 + 4.84 + 0.64} = \sqrt{13.56} \approx 3.6824 \\
\text{sim}(A, E) &= \frac{-4.0}{1.4142 \times 3.6824} = \frac{-4.0}{5.2074} \approx -0.768
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Step 4 — Rank the Neighbours

| Neighbour | $\text{sim}(A, X)$ | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| **B** | $+0.839$ | 1 |
| **C** | $+0.606$ | 2 |
| **D** | $\;\;0.000$ | 3 |
| **E** | $-0.768$ | 4 |

To predict $r_{A, I_5}$, we need neighbours who have rated $I_5$. From the matrix, **B = 3, C = 5, D = 4, E = 1** — all four have rated $I_5$. We will use the top-3 (B, C, D) to avoid the negative pull from E, though we show the full formula below for completeness.

## 3.6 Step 5 — Apply the User-Based CF Prediction Formula

$$\hat{r}_{A, I_5} = \bar{r}_A + \frac{\sum_{v \in N_k(A)} \text{sim}(A, v) \cdot (r_{v, I_5} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum_{v \in N_k(A)} \vert \text{sim}(A, v) \vert}$$

### 3.6.1 Using top-3 neighbours {B, C, D}

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 0.839 \cdot (3 - 2.4) + 0.606 \cdot (5 - 3.8) + 0.0 \cdot (4 - 3.2) \\
&= 0.839 \cdot 0.6 + 0.606 \cdot 1.2 + 0 \\
&= 0.5034 + 0.7272 + 0 = 1.2306 \\
\text{Denominator} &= \vert 0.839 \vert + \vert 0.606 \vert + \vert 0.0 \vert = 0.839 + 0.606 + 0.0 = 1.445 \\
\hat{r}_{A, I_5} &= 4.00 + \frac{1.2306}{1.445} = 4.00 + 0.8516 = 4.8516 \approx 4.85
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.6.2 Using all 4 neighbours {B, C, D, E} for verification

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 0.839(0.6) + 0.606(1.2) + 0.0(0.8) + (-0.768)(1-2.8) \\
&= 0.5034 + 0.7272 + 0 + (-0.768)(-1.8) \\
&= 0.5034 + 0.7272 + 0 + 1.3824 = 2.6130 \\
\text{Denominator} &= 0.839 + 0.606 + 0.0 + 0.768 = 2.213 \\
\hat{r}_{A, I_5} &= 4.00 + \frac{2.6130}{2.213} = 4.00 + 1.1808 = 5.1808
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The value **5.18** is **invalid** (exceeds the rating ceiling of 5). This demonstrates why *negative-similarity neighbours* are typically excluded or weighted down in practice — a frequent KTU exam subtlety.

## 3.7 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Operation | Formula Used | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean rating | $\bar{r}_u = \frac{1}{\mid I_u \mid}\sum r_{ui}$ | $\{\bar{r}_A,\dots,\bar{r}_E\}$ |
| 2 | Deviation vector | $d_{ui} = r_{ui} - \bar{r}_u$ | Per-user deviation table |
| 3 | Pearson similarity | $\text{sim}_{pearson}$ formula | $\text{sim}(A, X) \in [-1, +1]$ |
| 4 | Neighbour selection | Sort by $\text{sim}$, pick top-$k$ | $N_k(A) = \{B, C, D\}$ |
| 5 | Weighted prediction | User-based CF formula | $\hat{r}_{A,I_5} \approx 4.85$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Recommender System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userInput["Active User Query"] --> dataLayer["User Item Interaction Matrix R"]
    contentFeat["Item Feature Database"] --> dataLayer
    userProf["User Profile Store"] --> dataLayer

    dataLayer --> filterMod{"Filtering Paradigm"}

    filterMod --> cbfPath["Content Based Filtering"]
    filterMod --> cfPath["Collaborative Filtering"]
    filterMod --> hybridPath["Hybrid Engine"]

    cbfPath --> cbfOut["Item Feature Similarity Score"]
    cfPath --> cfOut["Neighborhood or Latent Score"]
    hybridPath --> hybridOut["Weighted Combined Score"]

    cbfOut --> ranker["Top K Ranking and Sorting"]
    cfOut --> ranker
    hybridOut --> ranker

    ranker --> recList["Final Recommendation List for User"]
    userProf --> ranker

    recList --> feedbackLoop["Implicit and Explicit Feedback"]
    feedbackLoop --> dataLayer
```

## 4.2 Collaborative Filtering Sub-Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawMatrix["Sparse User Item Matrix R"] --> splitMod{"Memory Based or Model Based"}

    splitMod --> memPath["Memory Based CF"]
    splitMod --> modelPath["Model Based CF"]

    memPath --> uucf["User User Similarity"]
    memPath --> iicf["Item Item Similarity"]

    uucf --> neigh["Top K Neighbour Selection"]
    iicf --> neigh
    neigh --> weightedPred["Weighted Rating Prediction"]

    modelPath --> mf["Matrix Factorization SVD or ALS"]
    modelPath --> nnmf["Neural Collaborative Filtering"]

    mf --> latentPred["Latent Factor Prediction"]
    nnmf --> latentPred

    weightedPred --> finalOut["Predicted Rating r hat"]
    latentPred --> finalOut

    finalOut --> topKrec["Top N Item Recommendations"]
```

## 4.3 Cold-Start Problem Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode["New User or New Item Enters"] --> typeCheck{"What is Missing?"}

    typeCheck -->|"New User"| newUser["Cold Start: New User"]
    typeCheck -->|"New Item"| newItem["Cold Start: New Item"]
    typeCheck -->|"Both Exist"| normal["Standard CF Path"]

    newUser --> newUserSol["Solution: Onboarding Questionnaire, Demographic Filter, Popular Items Baseline"]
    newItem --> newItemSol["Solution: Content Features, Hybrid CBF plus CF, Tag Based Bootstrap"]

    newUserSol --> hybridRec["Hybrid Recommendation Engine"]
    newItemSol --> hybridRec
    normal --> hybridRec

    hybridRec --> outputRec["Final Ranked Recommendations"]
```

## 4.4 Evaluation Pipeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    dataset["Holdout Dataset D train and D test"] --> splitOps{"Split Strategy"}

    splitOps --> randomSplit["Random K Fold Cross Validation"]
    splitOps --> temporalSplit["Temporal Split for Time Aware RS"]
    splitOps --> leaveOneOut["Leave One Out Evaluation"]

    randomSplit --> metricCalc["Metric Computation Module"]
    temporalSplit --> metricCalc
    leaveOneOut --> metricCalc

    metricCalc --> maeNode["MAE Mean Absolute Error"]
    metricCalc --> rmseNode["RMSE Root Mean Square Error"]
    metricCalc --> precNode["Precision at K"]
    metricCalc --> recallNode["Recall at K"]
    metricCalc --> ndcgNode["NDCG Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain"]
    metricCalc --> mapNode["MAP Mean Average Precision"]

    maeNode --> report["Evaluation Report and Model Selection"]
    rmseNode --> report
    precNode --> report
    recallNode --> report
    ndcgNode --> report
    mapNode --> report
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **Content-Based Filtering** and **Collaborative Filtering**. State one key limitation of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Content-Based Filtering (CBF) | Collaborative Filtering (CF) |
|---|---|---|
| **Recommendation basis** | Item *features* and user profile | Behaviour of *similar users/items* |
| **Data needed** | Item attributes + user history | Only user–item interaction matrix |
| **Limitations** | *Overspecialization* (recommends only items very similar to past ones; cannot perform *serendipity*) | *Cold-start* (cannot recommend for new users or new items with no interaction history) |

*Marking note:* 1 mark for differentiation, 1 mark for limitation of CBF, 1 mark for limitation of CF.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** What is the **cold-start problem** in recommender systems? List **two** practical techniques to mitigate it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** The cold-start problem occurs when a recommender system has *insufficient interaction data* to compute reliable similarities or latent factors — typically when a **new user** has rated very few items, or a **new item** has been rated by very few users.
- **Techniques (1.5 marks):**
  1. **Onboarding questionnaire / demographic-based bootstrapping** for new users.
  2. **Hybrid Content-Based + Collaborative filtering**, using item features (genre, tags) until enough ratings accumulate.
  3. *(alternative)* Default to **popularity-based** recommendations until interaction data builds up.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **ESE Module 4 — Internal Choice between Question A and Question B**
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

---

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[A] (a)** Explain the **User-Based Collaborative Filtering** algorithm in detail. Discuss the role of the *Pearson correlation coefficient* and the *prediction formula* used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Outline (2 marks):**
User-based CF works in two phases: (i) **similarity computation** between the active user $u_a$ and all other users, and (ii) **rating prediction** for an unseen item $i$ by aggregating the ratings of the top-$k$ most similar users.

**Step 2 — Pearson Correlation Similarity (3 marks):**
The similarity between users $u$ and $v$ over their co-rated item set $I_{uv}$ is given by:

$$\text{sim}_{\text{pearson}}(u, v) = \frac{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} (r_{ui} - \bar{r}_u)(r_{vi} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}}(r_{ui}-\bar{r}_u)^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}}(r_{vi}-\bar{r}_v)^2}}$$

- It lies in $[-1, +1]$.
- The $(r - \bar{r})$ centering removes **user-rating bias** (e.g., a user who always rates 5/5 is a *generous rater*).
- It captures **linear co-variation**, not magnitude similarity.

**Step 3 — Prediction Formula (2 marks):**
$$\hat{r}_{u,i} = \bar{r}_u + \frac{\sum_{v \in N_k(u)} \text{sim}(u, v) \cdot (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum_{v \in N_k(u)} \vert \text{sim}(u, v) \vert}$$

Where $N_k(u)$ is the set of $k$ nearest neighbours who have rated item $i$.

---

> **[A] (b)** Consider the following **user–item rating matrix** where `?` denotes an unknown rating. Compute the **Pearson similarity** between users **U1 and U3**, and predict $\hat{r}_{U1, I_3}$ using a **user-based CF** with $k = 2$ neighbours chosen from the remaining users. **(7 marks)**

| | $I_1$ | $I_2$ | $I_3$ | $I_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **U1** | 5 | 3 | ? | 4 |
| **U2** | 4 | 2 | 3 | 5 |
| **U3** | 2 | 4 | 5 | 1 |
| **U4** | 3 | 3 | 4 | 4 |

**Model Solution:**

**Mean ratings:**
$$\bar{r}_{U1} = \frac{5+3+4}{3} = 4.00,\quad \bar{r}_{U2} = \frac{4+2+3+5}{4} = 3.50$$
$$\bar{r}_{U3} = \frac{2+4+5+1}{4} = 3.00,\quad \bar{r}_{U4} = \frac{3+3+4+4}{4} = 3.50$$

**Step 1 — Similarity $\text{sim}(U1, U3)$ (4 marks):**
Co-rated items: $I_1, I_2, I_4$ (since $I_3$ is missing for U1).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Num} &= (5-4)(2-3) + (3-4)(4-3) + (4-4)(1-3) \\
&= (1)(-1) + (-1)(1) + (0)(-2) = -1 - 1 + 0 = -2.0 \\
\text{Denom}_{U1} &= \sqrt{1^2 + (-1)^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \\
\text{Denom}_{U3} &= \sqrt{(-1)^2 + 1^2 + (-2)^2} = \sqrt{1+1+4} = \sqrt{6} \approx 2.449 \\
\text{sim}(U1, U3) &= \frac{-2.0}{1.414 \times 2.449} = \frac{-2.0}{3.464} \approx -0.577
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Similarities with U2 and U4 (for $k=2$ selection) (1.5 marks):**

$\text{sim}(U1, U2)$ (co-rated: $I_1, I_2, I_4$):
$$\text{Num} = (1)(0.5) + (-1)(-1.5) + (0)(1.5) = 0.5 + 1.5 = 2.0$$
$$\text{Denom}_{U2} = \sqrt{0.5^2 + 1.5^2 + 1.5^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 2.25 + 2.25} = \sqrt{4.75} \approx 2.179$$
$$\text{sim}(U1, U2) = \frac{2.0}{1.414 \times 2.179} = \frac{2.0}{3.081} \approx 0.649$$

$\text{sim}(U1, U4)$ (co-rated: $I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4$):
$$\text{Num} = (1)(-0.5) + (-1)(-0.5) + (0)(0.5) + (0)(0.5) = -0.5 + 0.5 = 0$$
$$\text{sim}(U1, U4) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Top-2 neighbours and prediction (1.5 marks):**
By absolute similarity: U2 (0.649) and U3 (0.577) → use U2 and U3.
- $r_{U2, I_3} = 3,\quad \bar{r}_{U2} = 3.50$
- $r_{U3, I_3} = 5,\quad \bar{r}_{U3} = 3.00$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{r}_{U1, I_3} &= 4.00 + \frac{0.649(3 - 3.5) + (-0.577)(5 - 3.0)}{0.649 + 0.577} \\
&= 4.00 + \frac{0.649(-0.5) + (-0.577)(2.0)}{1.226} \\
&= 4.00 + \frac{-0.3245 - 1.154}{1.226} \\
&= 4.00 + \frac{-1.4785}{1.226} \\
&= 4.00 - 1.206 \approx 2.79
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Examiners expect students to *show the deviation table* before plugging into Pearson. Skipping the explicit numerator/denominator computation leads to **−2 marks**. Also, students often forget to include the **mean-correction term** $\bar{r}_u$ in the prediction formula.

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[B] (a)** Explain **Item-Based Collaborative Filtering** with the **adjusted cosine similarity** measure. How does it differ from user-based CF? Mention any **two advantages** of item-based CF in production systems. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Item-Based CF (3 marks):**
In item-based CF, we pre-compute similarities between *items* (not users) using the ratings they have received from all users. For a target user $u_a$, the prediction for an unseen item $i$ is a weighted average of $u_a$'s ratings on items *similar to* $i$.

**Adjusted Cosine Similarity (3 marks):**
Standard cosine treats all users equally, ignoring that some users rate systematically higher. Adjusted cosine *centers* each user's ratings by subtracting their mean $\bar{r}_u$:

$$\text{sim}_{\text{adjcos}}(i, j) = \frac{\sum_{u \in U_{ij}} (r_{ui} - \bar{r}_u)(r_{uj} - \bar{r}_u)}{\sqrt{\sum_{u \in U_{ij}}(r_{ui}-\bar{r}_u)^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{u \in U_{ij}}(r_{uj}-\bar{r}_u)^2}}$$

where $U_{ij}$ is the set of users who have rated *both* items $i$ and $j$.

**Differences & Advantages (1 mark):**

| Aspect | User-Based CF | Item-Based CF |
|---|---|---|
| Similarity between | Users | Items |
| Computational cost at query time | High (must scan all users) | Low (item similarities pre-computed) |
| Stability | Lower (user base churns) | Higher (item catalog more stable) |

**Two advantages:** (i) **Scalability** — pre-computed item-item matrix scales to millions of users; (ii) **Stability and interpretability** — item relationships are more meaningful and explainable ("because you watched Inception…").

---

> **[B] (b)** Given the following user–item matrix, compute the **cosine similarity** between items $I_1$ and $I_2$ using both **raw cosine** and **adjusted cosine** forms. Comment on the difference. **(7 marks)**

| | $I_1$ | $I_2$ | $I_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **U1** | 5 | 4 | 1 |
| **U2** | 3 | 2 | 4 |
| **U3** | 4 | 5 | 2 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Raw Cosine (3 marks):**
Item vectors (ratings across users):
- $\mathbf{I_1} = (5, 3, 4)$
- $\mathbf{I_2} = (4, 2, 5)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{I_1} \cdot \mathbf{I_2} &= 5 \cdot 4 + 3 \cdot 2 + 4 \cdot 5 = 20 + 6 + 20 = 46 \\
\Vert \mathbf{I_1} \Vert &= \sqrt{25 + 9 + 16} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.071 \\
\Vert \mathbf{I_2} \Vert &= \sqrt{16 + 4 + 25} = \sqrt{45} \approx 6.708 \\
\text{sim}_{\cos}(I_1, I_2) &= \frac{46}{7.071 \times 6.708} = \frac{46}{47.43} \approx 0.970
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — User Means (1 mark):**
$$\bar{r}_{U1} = \frac{5+4+1}{3} = 3.33,\quad \bar{r}_{U2} = \frac{3+2+4}{3} = 3.00,\quad \bar{r}_{U3} = \frac{4+5+2}{3} = 3.67$$

**Step 3 — Adjusted Cosine (3 marks):**
Deviation vectors:
- $\mathbf{I_1'} = (5-3.33, 3-3.00, 4-3.67) = (1.67, 0, 0.33)$
- $\mathbf{I_2'} = (4-3.33, 2-3.00, 5-3.67) = (0.67, -1.00, 1.33)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{I_1'} \cdot \mathbf{I_2'} &= (1.67)(0.67) + (0)(-1.00) + (0.33)(1.33) \\
&= 1.119 + 0 + 0.439 = 1.558 \\
\Vert \mathbf{I_1'} \Vert &= \sqrt{1.67^2 + 0^2 + 0.33^2} = \sqrt{2.789 + 0 + 0.109} = \sqrt{2.898} \approx 1.703 \\
\Vert \mathbf{I_2'} \Vert &= \sqrt{0.67^2 + 1.00^2 + 1.33^2} = \sqrt{0.449 + 1.000 + 1.769} = \sqrt{3.218} \approx 1.794 \\
\text{sim}_{\text{adjcos}}(I_1, I_2) &= \frac{1.558}{1.703 \times 1.794} = \frac{1.558}{3.055} \approx 0.510
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment (in-built marks within 7):**
The **raw cosine (0.970)** is very high because U2 rates everything low and U3 rates high, but the *direction* of the vectors is similar. The **adjusted cosine (0.510)** is lower because it correctly accounts for user-rating bias: U2's low ratings for $I_2$ are not because $I_2$ is unlike $I_1$, but because U2 is a strict rater. Adjusted cosine is therefore the **more honest** similarity in the presence of heterogeneous user behaviour.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly use $\bar{r}_i$ (item mean) instead of $\bar{r}_u$ (user mean) in the adjusted cosine formula. The **mean used is the user's**, not the item's — losing this distinction costs **2 marks**. Also, U2's item $I_2$ rating is **2**, not the user mean — students often confuse directionality.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning Summary

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Patterns in CF Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $\bar{r}_u$ and $\bar{r}_i$:** Pearson uses *user* mean; adjusted cosine uses *user* mean in deviation. Item-mean is **not** used in either.
> 2. **Omitting the absolute-value normalizer** $\vert \text{sim}(u,v) \vert$ in the prediction formula.
> 3. **Failing to define $I_{uv}$** explicitly — examiners demand clarity on which items are *co-rated*.
> 4. **Including negative-similarity neighbours** without comment — predict ratings can exceed the valid range.
> 5. **Skipping intermediate deviation tables** — a 7-mark question typically has 2 marks reserved for showing the deviation matrix.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> **Important Things to Remember — Module 4 / Recommender Systems & CF**

- **Recommender System = information filter** that predicts user preferences.
- **Two main families:** Content-Based (item features) vs Collaborative (interaction matrix only).
- **Collaborative Filtering has two flavours:** **Memory-based** (neighbourhood) and **Model-based** (latent factor / matrix factorization).
- **Memory-based CF has two sub-flavours:** **User–User** and **Item–Item** (item-based is preferred in industry).
- **Three similarity measures to master:**
  - **Cosine** for raw rating vectors.
  - **Pearson Correlation** for user–user similarity with bias correction.
  - **Adjusted Cosine** for item–item similarity with user-bias correction.
- **Jaccard** is used for **binary implicit feedback** (clicks, purchases).
- **Prediction formulas:**
  - User-based: $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \bar{r}_u + \frac{\sum \text{sim}(u,v) \cdot (r_{vi} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum \vert \text{sim}(u,v) \vert}$
  - Item-based: $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \frac{\sum \text{sim}(i,j) \cdot r_{uj}}{\sum \vert \text{sim}(i,j) \vert}$
- **Latent factor model:** $\hat{r}_{ui} = \mathbf{p}_u^T \mathbf{q}_i$ learned by minimizing the regularized squared error.
- **Cold-start problem** affects new users (no history) and new items (no ratings); mitigated by **hybrid methods** and **onboarding**.
- **Sparsity** of the user–item matrix (>99% missing) is the central engineering challenge.
- **Evaluation metrics:** MAE, RMSE (rating accuracy); Precision@K, Recall@K, MAP, NDCG (ranking quality).
- **Hybrid recommenders** (weighted / switching / cascade / feature-combination) combine CBF + CF to overcome the limitations of either alone.
- **Exam tip:** Always explicitly state $I_{uv}$ or $U_{ij}$ before plugging into similarity formulas — KTU examiners award 1 mark for this clarity.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Query Log Mining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT636 - INTRODUCTION TO WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Query Log Mining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Query Log Mining (QLM)** is a specialized sub-discipline of **Web Usage Mining** that focuses on the systematic discovery, extraction, and analysis of patterns, structures, and actionable intelligence from search engine query logs. A query log is a chronologically ordered record of user interactions with a search engine, capturing fields such as *User Identifier (UserID / CookieID)*, *Query String (Q)*, *Timestamp (T)*, *Result Page (Rank)*, and *Clicked URL (ClickURL)*.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Web Mining, Query Log Mining is positioned at the intersection of **Information Retrieval (IR)**, **User Behavior Modeling**, and **Data Mining**. It serves as the foundational data layer for *Learning to Rank (LTR)* algorithms, *Query Recommendation Systems*, *Search Personalization*, and *Sponsored Search Optimization*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Query Log Mining is treated as the **primary data source** for understanding *collective user intelligence* and *individual search intent*. The 2024 Scheme explicitly emphasizes its role in deriving **session boundaries**, **query reformulation chains**, and **click-through behavior**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **massive library** where every visitor is given a small card. On the card, the librarian notes down:

1. *Who* came in (Visitor ID)
2. *What* book they asked for (Query)
3. *When* they asked (Time)
4. *Which books* the librarian suggested back (Search Results)
5. *Which* suggested book the visitor actually picked up (Click)

If the librarian collects **millions** of such cards over years, they can deduce remarkable patterns:
- "Visitors searching for `python tutorial` usually end up clicking the **4th result**, not the 1st" → *Ranking Insight*
- "Visitors who type `java` usually follow up with `coffee` or `programming`" → *Query Suggestion*
- "After typing `iphone 15 review`, visitors often re-query as `iphone 15 vs samsung s24`" → *Query Reformulation*

This is exactly what **Query Log Mining** does, but at web-scale (Google processes **~8.5 billion searches/day** ≈ **3.1 trillion/year** as of recent estimates).

> [!NOTE]
> **The 3 V's of Query Logs (Big Data Perspective):**
> - **Volume:** Petabytes per year
> - **Velocity:** Real-time streams from millions of concurrent users
> - **Variety:** Multilingual, mixed intent (navigational, informational, transactional)

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Bolded)

- **Query Session Timeout Threshold:** **30 minutes** (industry standard; Microsoft Research, Jansen et al.)
- **Average Query Length:** **2.4 words** (Jansen, Spink, Taksa 2008 benchmark)
- **Click-Through Rate (CTR) Calculation Base:** Position-weighted
- **Duplicated Query Ratio in Logs:** **31% – 60%** depending on search engine
- **Average Session Length:** **3 – 5 queries per session**

---

## 1.4 Typical Query Log Schema (KTU Standard)

| Field Name | Symbol | Type | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| User Identifier | UID | String/Int | `User_238947` |
| Query | Q | String | `"web mining ktu notes"` |
| Timestamp | T | Long (Unix) | `1717654321` |
| Result Rank Clicked | R | Int | `3` |
| Clicked URL | URL | String | `https://...` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Privacy Note (GDPR / KTU Ethical Computing):** Anonymization of `UID` and IP is **mandatory** before mining. Differential privacy techniques must be applied to avoid re-identification attacks.

---

## 1.5 Why Query Log Mining Matters (Engineering Utility)

1. **Search Engine Result Page (SERP) Optimization** — Direct feedback on ranking quality.
2. **Query Auto-Completion (QAC)** — Powers Google Suggest / Bing Autosuggest.
3. **Personalized Search** — Re-ranking based on user history.
4. **Trending Topic Detection** — Real-time news aggregation.
5. **Ad Targeting (Sponsored Search)** — Higher CTR for contextually matched ads.
6. **Spam & Click-Fraud Detection** — Identifies bot-driven click patterns.
7. **Cross-lingual Information Retrieval** — Identifies translation pairs from user behavior.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of Query Log Mining

The pipeline follows a strict **5-stage sequential architecture**:

### Stage 1 — **Data Acquisition**
- Raw logs from search engine front-end (Apache Kafka streaming).
- Fields: `UID, Q, T, R, URL, DeviceInfo, GeoIP`.
- Storage: HDFS / S3 / BigQuery (columnar format: Parquet / ORC).

### Stage 2 — **Preprocessing & Cleaning**
- **Tokenization:** Split queries by whitespace/punctuation.
- **Stopword Removal:** Filter articles, prepositions.
- **Stemming / Lemmatization:** Porter Stemmer, WordNet Lemmatizer.
- **Anonymization:** Hashing `UID` with salted SHA-256.
- **Bot Filtering:** Remove crawlers via User-Agent and timing regularity.

### Stage 3 — **Session Identification**
A **query session** is the sequence of queries issued by a single user within a bounded time window.
- **Time-Based Heuristic (Jansen et al.):** New session if $\Delta T > 30 \text{ min}$.
- **Query-Similarity Heuristic:** New session if semantic similarity drops below threshold $\theta$.

### Stage 4 — **Pattern Discovery**
- **Query Reformulation Chains:** Generalization, Specialization, Parallel, Reformulation.
- **Click Graph Construction:** Bipartite graph $(Q \leftrightarrow URL)$.
- **Association Rule Mining:** Apriori / FP-Growth over query itemsets.

### Stage 5 — **Application & Evaluation**
- Recommendation, Personalization, Re-ranking.
- Metrics: MRR, NDCG@k, CTR@k, Session Satisfaction Score.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute value bars `|x|` are replaced with `\vert x \vert` to prevent markdown table corruption.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Click-Through Rate (Position-k)** | $CTR@k = \dfrac{\text{Clicks on Rank} \leq k}{\text{Impressions at Rank} \leq k}$ | Ratio ∈ [0, 1] | Evaluates ranking quality |
| 2 | **Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR)** | $MRR = \dfrac{1}{\vert S \vert} \sum_{i=1}^{\vert S \vert} \dfrac{1}{rank_i}$ | $rank_i$ = first relevant URL position | Session-level satisfaction |
| 3 | **Normalized DCG @ k** | $NDCG@k = \dfrac{DCG@k}{IDCG@k}$ | Discounted gain | Multi-query ranking eval |
| 4 | **DCG Formula** | $DCG@k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{rel_i}{\log_2(i+1)}$ | $rel_i$ = relevance grade | Cumulative gain |
| 5 | **Cosine Similarity (Query-Query)** | $\cos(Q_1, Q_2) = \dfrac{\vec{Q_1} \cdot \vec{Q_2}}{\vert \vec{Q_1} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{Q_2} \vert}$ | Vector in $\mathbb{R}^n$ | Query suggestion |
| 6 | **TF-IDF (Term Weighting)** | $w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot \log \dfrac{\vert D \vert}{\vert \{d : t \in d\} \vert}$ | $tf$ = term freq, $df$ = doc freq | Query representation |
| 7 | **Jaccard Similarity (Query Set)** | $J(Q_1, Q_2) = \dfrac{\vert Q_1 \cap Q_2 \vert}{\vert Q_1 \cup Q_2 \vert}$ | Set cardinality | Reformulation detection |
| 8 | **Session Boundary (Time)** | $Session_{new} \iff \Delta T_i > 30 \text{ min}$ | $\Delta T$ in minutes | Session segmentation |
| 9 | **Query Reformulation Entropy** | $H(R) = -\sum_{i} p(r_i) \log_2 p(r_i)$ | $p(r_i)$ = reformulation type prob | User behavior complexity |
| 10 | **Click Entropy (URL)** | $H(C \vert Q) = -\sum_{u} p(u \vert Q) \log_2 p(u \vert Q)$ | $u$ = URL clicked | Query ambiguity measure |

---

## 2.3 Theoretical Foundation: The Click Model

The **Examination Hypothesis (PBM — Position-Based Model)** by Craswell et al. states:

$$P(\text{click} \vert \text{rank}=k) = P(\text{examine} \vert \text{rank}=k) \times P(\text{relevant} \vert \text{examined})$$

The DBN (Dynamic Bayesian Network) model extends this to incorporate **continuation** behavior:

$$P(c_i, E_i \vert c_{i-1}, A_{i-1}) = P(A_i \vert c_{i-1}) \cdot P(E_i \vert A_i) \cdot P(c_i \vert A_i, E_i)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters for KTU:** Click models form the **mathematical backbone** of modern search ranking. Understanding PBM and DBN is essential for Module 4 viva and Part B derivations.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Use of Query Log Mining |
|---|---|

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Click-Through Rate at Position k

> **Problem:** A search engine shows **1000 users** a query result page. The number of clicks observed at ranks 1, 2, 3 are 450, 200, 100 respectively. Compute CTR@1, CTR@2, CTR@3 and the *expected clicks per query* using the Position-Based Model assumption.

### Step 1 — Define Impressions
At rank $k$, the impression count is the total number of users who **saw** rank $k$.

$$I_k = 1000 \quad \forall k \in \{1, 2, 3\}$$

### Step 2 — Define Clicks
Clicks observed at each rank:

$$C_1 = 450, \quad C_2 = 200, \quad C_3 = 100$$

### Step 3 — Compute CTR@k

$$
\begin{aligned}
CTR@1 &= \frac{C_1}{I_1} = \frac{450}{1000} = 0.450 \\
CTR@2 &= \frac{C_1 + C_2}{I_1} = \frac{450 + 200}{1000} = 0.650 \\
CTR@3 &= \frac{C_1 + C_2 + C_3}{I_1} = \frac{450 + 200 + 100}{1000} = 0.750
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A user who clicks rank 2 *must* have seen rank 1 (cascading click model). Hence cumulative numerator.

### Step 4 — Position-Based Model Probability

The PBM assumes examination probability decays by rank:

$$P(\text{examine} \vert \text{rank}=k) = \alpha_k, \quad \alpha_1 > \alpha_2 > \alpha_3$$

If we set $P(\text{relevant}) = 0.8$ constant (only as illustrative assumption), then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_1 &= \frac{C_1}{I_1 \times 0.8} = \frac{450}{800} = 0.5625 \\
\alpha_2 &= \frac{C_2}{I_2 \times 0.8} = \frac{200}{800} = 0.2500 \\
\alpha_3 &= \frac{C_3}{I_3 \times 0.8} = \frac{100}{800} = 0.1250
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Why these values make sense:** $\alpha_1 \gg \alpha_3$ confirms the **positional bias** — users are far more likely to examine top results.

### Step 5 — Final Analytical Conclusion
The CTR drops sharply with rank. For KTU 14-mark answers, **always show both cumulative and per-position CTR** and comment on the *exponential decay trend*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Session Identification via Time Threshold

> **Problem:** Given the following query log for User U1, identify sessions using a **30-minute timeout rule**.

| # | Query | Time (HH:MM) | ΔT (min) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `ktu s3 syllabus` | 09:00 | — |
| 2 | `btech notes pdf` | 09:18 | 18 |
| 3 | `web mining module 4` | 09:45 | 27 |
| 4 | `pizza near me` | 11:02 | **77** |
| 5 | `best pizza kochi` | 11:10 | 8 |
| 6 | `data science jobs` | 14:05 | **175** |
| 7 | `ktu result 2024` | 14:25 | 20 |

### Step 1 — Compute ΔT
$\Delta T_i = T_i - T_{i-1}$

### Step 2 — Apply Boundary Rule
New session if $\Delta T > 30$ min.

### Step 3 — Resulting Sessions

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= \{Q_1, Q_2, Q_3\} \quad \text{(Academic session, 09:00-09:45)} \\
S_2 &= \{Q_4, Q_5\} \quad \text{(Lunch search, 11:02-11:10)} \\
S_3 &= \{Q_6, Q_7\} \quad \text{(Career + Academic, 14:05-14:25)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Analytical Observation
The user U1 demonstrates **multi-intent** behavior across the day. Session 3 mixes informational and transactional intent, which is **statistically common** in real logs (called *intent switching*).

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Cosine Similarity Between Two Queries

> **Problem:** Given query vocabulary $V = \{$`web`, `mining`, `notes`, `ktu`, `exam`$\}$ and term-frequency vectors:
> $Q_1$ = "web mining notes" → $\vec{Q_1} = (1, 1, 1, 0, 0)$
> $Q_2$ = "web mining exam" → $\vec{Q_2} = (1, 1, 0, 0, 1)$

Compute cosine similarity.

### Step 1 — Compute Dot Product

$$
\vec{Q_1} \cdot \vec{Q_2} = (1)(1) + (1)(1) + (1)(0) + (0)(0) + (0)(1) = 2
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Magnitudes

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert \vec{Q_1} \vert &= \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2 + 0^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732 \\
\vert \vec{Q_2} \vert &= \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 0^2 + 0^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Cosine Similarity

$$
\cos(Q_1, Q_2) = \frac{\vec{Q_1} \cdot \vec{Q_2}}{\vert \vec{Q_1} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{Q_2} \vert} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \sqrt{3}} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
A cosine similarity of **0.667** indicates **moderate overlap** — the queries share 2 out of 3 unique terms. For threshold $\theta = 0.5$, they would be flagged as **similar enough for suggestion** (e.g., after searching "web mining notes", suggest "web mining exam").

---

## 3.4 Derivation #4: TF-IDF Vector for Query Representation

> **Problem:** Compute TF-IDF weight of term `mining` in a query $Q$ = "web mining notes" from a corpus of $\vert D \vert = 1000$ unique queries, where the term `mining` appears in 50 queries. Assume $tf_{\text{mining}} = 1$.

### Step 1 — Term Frequency
$tf_{\text{mining}} = 1$ (appears once in $Q$).

### Step 2 — Inverse Document Frequency

$$
idf_{\text{mining}} = \log \frac{\vert D \vert}{\vert \{d : \text{mining} \in d\} \vert} = \log \frac{1000}{50} = \log(20) \approx 2.996
$$

### Step 3 — TF-IDF Weight

$$
w_{\text{mining}, Q} = tf \times idf = 1 \times 2.996 \approx 2.996
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Insight
The high weight (**≈ 3.0**) confirms `mining` is a **discriminative term** — it appears in only 5% of queries, making it semantically rich. Conversely, common terms like `web` would have very low TF-IDF.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Query Reformulation Types

| Reformulation Type | Pattern | Example | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Generalization** | Specific → Broad | `iphone 15 pro max review` → `iphone reviews` | ~18% |
| **Specialization** | Broad → Specific | `python tutorial` → `python pandas tutorial pdf` | ~22% |
| **Parallel Move** | Similar concept | `java tutorial` → `python tutorial` | ~14% |
| **Reformulation** | Re-worded | `how to bake cake` → `cake recipe without oven` | ~31% |
| **New Topic** | Unrelated | `laptop under 50000` → `weather in kerala` | ~15% |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always show a *tabular comparison* of reformulation types in 7-mark answers. Examiners award full marks for **clear typology + examples + percentages**.

---

## 3.6 Derivation #5: Click Entropy (Query Ambiguity)

> **Problem:** For query `apple`, the click distribution is: URL₁ (apple.com) = 0.7, URL₂ (wikipedia/apple) = 0.2, URL₃ (apple store) = 0.1. Compute click entropy.

### Step 1 — Entropy Formula

$$
H(C \vert Q) = -\sum_{u} p(u \vert Q) \log_2 p(u \vert Q)
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(C \vert \text{apple}) &= -[0.7 \log_2 0.7 + 0.2 \log_2 0.2 + 0.1 \log_2 0.1] \\
&= -[(0.7)(-0.515) + (0.2)(-2.322) + (0.1)(-3.322)] \\
&= -[-0.360 - 0.464 - 0.332] \\
&= 1.156 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation
A click entropy of **1.156 bits** indicates **moderate ambiguity** for `apple`. Queries with $H > 2$ bits are considered **highly ambiguous** (need disambiguation like Google "did you mean: apple fruit, apple inc, apple store?").

---

## 3.7 Comprehensive Step-by-Step Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem Statement:** A query log contains 5 sessions. Compute:
> (a) Total queries, unique queries, repeated query ratio.
> (b) Average session length.
> (c) Apply the 30-minute rule to re-segment and verify.
> (d) Compute the most frequent query (Top-1) and its reformulation entropy.

### Given Data
| SessionID | UID | Query | Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | U1 | `ktu notes` | 10:00 |
| S1 | U1 | `ktu notes` | 10:10 |
| S1 | U1 | `web mining` | 10:25 |
| S2 | U2 | `java tutorial` | 11:00 |
| S2 | U2 | `python tutorial` | 11:08 |
| S2 | U2 | `ktu notes` | 11:20 |
| S3 | U1 | `weather kerala` | 12:00 |

### Solution

**(a) Total Queries:** 7. **Unique Queries:** 6 (`ktu notes` repeats). **Repeated Query Ratio:**

$$
R_{rep} = \frac{\text{repeated queries}}{\text{total queries}} = \frac{1}{7} \approx 14.3\%
$$

**(b) Average Session Length:**

$$
L_{avg} = \frac{|S_1| + |S_2| + |S_3|}{3} = \frac{3 + 3 + 1}{3} = 2.33 \text{ queries/session}
$$

**(c) 30-Minute Rule Check:**
All $\Delta T \leq 30$ min, so segmentation is valid.

**(d) Top-1 Frequent Query:** `ktu notes` (frequency = 2). Reformulation entropy is low since query is repeated verbatim (specialization factor = 0).

## 📘 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define Query Log Mining. List any four typical fields in a search engine query log with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
*Query Log Mining is the process of extracting meaningful patterns, user behavior insights, and actionable intelligence from search engine query logs to improve search quality, personalization, and recommendation systems.*

Four typical fields:
1. **UserID (UID):** Unique identifier (anonymized) of the user issuing the query.
2. **Query (Q):** The text string typed by the user.
3. **Timestamp (T):** Unix time of query submission (used for session detection).
4. **ClickedURL:** The URL the user clicked from the SERP (used for relevance feedback).

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + Four fields with descriptions (2 × 0.5 each).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**What is a query session? State the standard timeout threshold used in industry for session segmentation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A *query session* is a **contiguous sequence of queries** issued by a single user to satisfy a unified information need, separated from the next session by a long pause (no user activity) or a topic shift.

The industry-standard timeout is **30 minutes** (Jansen, Spink & Taksa, 2008), originally derived from empirical analysis of AltaVista and Excite logs.

> **Mark Distribution:** Session definition (2) + Timeout (1).

## 📕 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern:** Students answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Each question has sub-parts (a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks.

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Position-Based Model (PBM)** of click behavior with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for click probability at rank $k$ and comment on **positional bias**.

**Model Answer:**

**Position-Based Model (Craswell et al., 2008):**

The PBM assumes that for a query $Q$ with a ranked result list, a user:
1. **Examines** rank 1 with probability $\alpha_1$.
2. If examined AND relevant, **clicks** with probability $s$ (relevance satisfaction).
3. After clicking, may continue down the list with probability $\gamma$ (continuation).

**Click Probability Formula:**

$$
P(C_k \vert Q) = \alpha_k \cdot s_r
$$

where:
- $C_k$ = click on rank $k$
- $\alpha_k$ = examination probability at rank $k$ (decreasing function of $k$)
- $s_r$ = probability of click given relevance (satisfaction parameter)

**Positional Bias Comment:**

Since $\alpha_1 > \alpha_2 > \alpha_3 > \ldots > \alpha_k$, top-ranked results receive **disproportionately more clicks** even if relevance is identical. Empirical logs confirm this — for the same URL placed at rank 1 vs rank 10, the CTR ratio is approximately **5:1 to 10:1** depending on query type.

> **Mark Distribution:** PBM explanation (3) + Diagram (1) + Formula derivation (2) + Positional bias comment (1).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given the click data below for the query "machine learning":

| Rank | URL | Impressions | Clicks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | wikipedia.org | 1000 | 600 |
| 2 | coursera.org | 1000 | 250 |
| 3 | ktu.edu.in | 1000 | 100 |

Compute:
1. CTR@1, CTR@2, CTR@3
2. Examination probability $\alpha_k$ assuming $s_r = 0.8$ (relevance)
3. The Click Entropy $H(C \vert Q)$ to assess query ambiguity.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: CTR Calculations (cumulative) — 3 Marks**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CTR@1 &= \frac{600}{1000} = 0.60 \\
CTR@2 &= \frac{600 + 250}{1000} = 0.85 \\
CTR@3 &= \frac{600 + 250 + 100}{1000} = 0.95
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Examination Probability — 2 Marks**

Using PBM: $P(\text{click at } k) = \alpha_k \cdot s_r$, so $\alpha_k = \dfrac{C_k}{I_k \cdot s_r}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_1 &= \frac{600}{1000 \times 0.8} = 0.75 \\
\alpha_2 &= \frac{250}{1000 \times 0.8} = 0.3125 \\
\alpha_3 &= \frac{100}{1000 \times 0.8} = 0.125
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Click Entropy — 2 Marks**

Click probability distribution $p(u_k \vert Q) = C_k / \sum C_i$:

$$
p_1 = \frac{600}{950} = 0.632, \quad p_2 = \frac{250}{950} = 0.263, \quad p_3 = \frac{100}{950} = 0.105
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(C \vert Q) &= -[0.632 \log_2 0.632 + 0.263 \log_2 0.263 + 0.105 \log_2 0.105] \\
&= -[(0.632)(-0.661) + (0.263)(-1.927) + (0.105)(-3.252)] \\
&= -[-0.418 - 0.507 - 0.341] \\
&= 1.266 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $H = 1.266$ bits indicates **moderate ambiguity** for the query "machine learning" — multiple reasonable interpretations exist (Wikipedia overview, course enrollment, KTU syllabus).

> **Mark Distribution:** CTR (3) + Examination (2) + Entropy (2).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Alternative Set]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **30-minute session identification rule** with an example. What are its **limitations** and how can the **query-similarity heuristic** address them?

**Model Answer:**

**30-Minute Rule (Jansen et al., 2008):**
A new session is initiated when the time gap $\Delta T$ between two consecutive queries from the same user exceeds **30 minutes**.

**Example:** User issues `python tutorial` (10:00) → `pandas dataframe` (10:10) → `laptop under 50000` (12:05). Here, $\Delta T_{2 \to 3} = 115$ min $> 30$ min, so a new session begins at 12:05.

**Limitations (3 Marks):**
1. **Topic Switching Within 30 min:** A user may research `weather` (10:00) and then `python` (10:15) — same session incorrectly!
2. **Idle Users:** A user opens browser, gets distracted for 35 min, then resumes — split incorrectly.
3. **Long-Form Research:** Genuine research sessions (PhD students, analysts) may exceed 30 min but belong to one logical task.

**Query-Similarity Heuristic (4 Marks):**
A new session is declared if the **cosine similarity** between consecutive query vectors drops below threshold $\theta$ (typically 0.3).

$$
\text{New Session} \iff \cos(Q_i, Q_{i+1}) < \theta
$$

This addresses limitation #1 — `weather` and `python` have low semantic overlap, hence cosine similarity is low, correctly triggering a session split even within 30 minutes.

> **Mark Distribution:** Rule explanation (2) + Example (1) + Limitations (2) + Heuristic solution (2).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given the following 3 queries:
- $Q_1$ = "data science tutorial"
- $Q_2$ = "data science course kerala"
- $Q_3$ = "machine learning basics"

Compute the **cosine similarity matrix** using the vocabulary $V = \{$`data`, `science`, `tutorial`, `course`, `kerala`, `machine`, `learning`, `basics`$\}$ and TF representation.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: TF Vectors (2 Marks)**

$$
\vec{Q_1} = (1,1,1,0,0,0,0,0)
$$
$$
\vec{Q_2} = (1,1,0,1,1,0,0,0)
$$
$$
\vec{Q_3} = (0,0,0,0,0,1,1,1)
$$

**Step 2: Magnitudes (1 Mark)**

$$
\vert \vec{Q_1} \vert = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732, \quad \vert \vec{Q_2} \vert = \sqrt{4} = 2, \quad \vert \vec{Q_3} \vert = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732
$$

**Step 3: Pairwise Cosine Similarity (3 Marks)**

**Between Q₁ and Q₂:**

$$
\cos(Q_1, Q_2) = \frac{(1)(1) + (1)(1) + 0 + 0 + 0}{\sqrt{3} \times 2} = \frac{2}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577
$$

**Between Q₁ and Q₃:**

$$
\cos(Q_1, Q_3) = \frac{0 + 0 + 0}{\sqrt{3} \times \sqrt{3}} = \frac{0}{3} = 0
$$

**Between Q₂ and Q₃:**

$$
\cos(Q_2, Q_3) = \frac{0}{2 \times \sqrt{3}} = 0
$$

**Step 4: Cosine Similarity Matrix (1 Mark)**

| | $Q_1$ | $Q_2$ | $Q_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Q_1$ | 1.000 | 0.577 | 0.000 |
| $Q_2$ | 0.577 | 1.000 | 0.000 |
| $Q_3$ | 0.000 | 0.000 | 1.000 |

> **Mark Distribution:** TF vectors (2) + Magnitudes (1) + 3 cosine calcs (3) + Matrix (1).

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in Query Log Mining Answers:**
> 1. **Confusing CTR (cumulative) with per-position CTR** — Examiners deduct 1 mark if you compute CTR@2 as `200/1000` instead of `(450+200)/1000`.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — Always mention bits for entropy, ratio ∈ [0,1] for CTR.
> 3. **Not stating the 30-minute rule origin** — Credit is reserved for the Jansen et al. (2008) attribution.
> 4. **Skipping the relevance parameter $s_r$** in PBM derivations — Show the assumption explicitly.
> 5. **Mixing log₂ with log** — Click entropy MUST use base 2; using natural log loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Skipping diagrams in 14-mark answers** — PBM, session segmentation, click graph all expect a visual aid. Missing diagram = up to **2-mark penalty**.

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultra-High-Density Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Night Rev):**

- ✅ **Query Log Mining = Web Usage Mining + IR + User Modeling**
- ✅ **Standard Log Schema:** `UID, Q, T, R, URL` (5 fields — KTU favourite)
- ✅ **Session Timeout = 30 minutes** (Jansen, Spink, Taksa 2008)
- ✅ **Average Query Length = 2.4 words**; Average Session = 3-5 queries
- ✅ **CTR@k (cumulative):** $\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} C_i}{I}$
- ✅ **PBM Formula:** $P(C_k \vert Q) = \alpha_k \cdot s_r$
- ✅ **Positional Bias:** $\alpha_1 \gg \alpha_{10}$ (exponential decay)
- ✅ **Cosine Similarity Range:** $[-1, +1]$; for TF vectors: $[0, 1]$
- ✅ **Jaccard vs Cosine:** Jaccard is set-based; Cosine is vector-based (handles repetition)
- ✅ **Click Entropy Interpretation:** $H < 1$ = clear intent, $H > 2$ = ambiguous
- ✅ **Query Reformulation Types:** Generalization, Specialization, Parallel, Reformulation, New Topic (5 types)
- ✅ **Privacy:** Anonymize UID + IP via salted SHA-256; apply k-anonymity or differential privacy
- ✅ **Top 3 Applications:** Query Suggestion, Personalized Ranking, Ad CTR Prediction
- ✅ **KTU 14-Mark Must-Include:** Diagram + Formula + Numerical example + Conclusion
- ✅ **TF-IDF Formula:** $w = tf \cdot \log(\vert D \vert / df)$ — always use $\log$ base 2 for entropy
- ✅ **MRR for sessions:** $\dfrac{1}{\vert S \vert} \sum \dfrac{1}{rank_i}$ — first-click-only evaluation

> **One-Line Mantra for KTU Exam:**
> *"Query Log Mining turns the noise of billions of searches into the signal of user intent — and that signal powers every smart search box you've ever used."*
